title: BrickAnything: Geometry-Conditioned Buildable Brick Generation with Structure-Aware Tokenization
summary: Generating physically buildable brick structures from 3D shapes requires more than geometric reconstruction: the output must also satisfy discrete part constraints and structural stability. Existing brick generation methods either rely on heuristic optimization, which can break down when the target 3D shape does not admit a feasible structure under predefined constraints, or generate brick sequences without explicitly modeling the underlying 3D geometry and assembly relations. In this work, we present BrickAnything, a geometry-conditioned autoregressive framework for generating buildable brick structures from diverse 3D representations. BrickAnything uses point clouds as a unified geometric interface and predicts brick sequences that reconstruct the target shape under assembly constraints. To model structural dependencies among bricks, we introduce a structure-aware tree tokenization, which represents brick structures through local attachment relations. This formulation makes sequence generation more consistent with the physical construction process, and reduces invalid intermediate states. We further introduce preference-based alignment post-training, validity-constrained decoding and adaptive rollback to improve buildability objectives such as stability and geometric fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BrickAnything produces geometrically faithful and physically realizable brick structures, and that the proposed tokenization effectively reduces rollback and regeneration compared with conventional ordering strategies.

AI.tldr: BrickAnything is a framework that converts 3D shapes into physically buildable brick structures using geometry-conditioned autoregressive generation.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of existing brick generation methods that either rely on fragile heuristic optimization or ignore underlying 3D geometry and assembly relations, often producing unbuildable or unstable structures.
AI.method: They use point clouds as a unified 3D interface and an autoregressive model with a new structure-aware tree tokenization that captures local brick attachment relations. They also add preference-based alignment post-training, validity-constrained decoding, and adaptive rollback to optimize for buildability, stability, and geometric fidelity.
AI.result: Experiments show that BrickAnything can generate brick assemblies that are both geometrically faithful to the input shapes and physically realizable, and that the proposed tree tokenization reduces the need for rollback and regeneration compared to standard brick ordering strategies.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-conditioned autoregressive generation with structure-aware tokenization and buildability-aware decoding is effective for producing stable, buildable brick structures from various 3D shape inputs.

title: Can LLMs Introspect? A Reality Check
summary:
Can large language models detect and report their own internal states? A number of studies have argued that the answer to this question is yes. We argue, based on lessons from human metacognition research, that this conclusion may be premature: to be convinced of this conclusion we need to distinguish genuine introspection from pattern matching based on surface-level cues. Furthermore, we argue that behavioral evidence alone is inherently insufficient to establish strong introspective claims.
  We re-examine two recently introduced evaluation paradigms in light of this consideration. In the first paradigm, models are expected to detect whether their internal states have been tampered with. We find that models cannot reliably distinguish such interventions on their internal states from manipulations of the input, suggesting that their success in the original studies reflects their ability to detect anomalies more generally, as opposed to interventions on their internal states in particular. In the second paradigm we examine, models are tasked with predicting labels derived from their own hidden states. Here, we find that classifiers that only have access to the input achieve equivalent performance to the model's own in-context predictions, indicating that the original results do not conclusively demonstrate that the model has privileged access to its internal representations. We further introduce a relabeled control setting, where models cannot rely on the semantics of the task to solve it, and instead must rely on the internal representation; models perform closer to chance on this better-controlled version of the task. Taken together, these results indicate that current evidence is insufficient to establish that LLMs display metacognitive monitoring.

AI.tldr: The paper critically examines recent claims that large language models (LLMs) can introspect or report on their own internal states, arguing that existing evidence is inadequate and largely explained by pattern matching on inputs rather than genuine metacognition.
AI.motivation: There is growing interest in whether LLMs possess metacognitive abilities similar to human introspection, such as detecting tampering with their internal processes or reading out their own hidden states. Several studies have suggested they can, which has philosophical, safety, and interpretability implications. The authors are motivated to clarify whether these results really demonstrate privileged self-access or instead reflect confounds like pattern recognition over inputs and labels, drawing on lessons from human metacognition research about how hard it is to distinguish real introspection from clever responding to surface cues.
AI.method: The authors re-analyze and extend two existing evaluation paradigms. (1) For tampering-detection tasks, they test whether LLMs can specifically recognize interventions on their internal activations versus more generic anomalies such as input perturbations, comparing performance across these conditions. (2) For hidden-state-label prediction tasks, they compare the LLM’s in-context predictions of labels derived from its own hidden states to external classifiers that only see the input text, to see if equivalent performance can be achieved without internal access. They also design a relabeled control condition where task semantics are scrambled, forcing any successful model to rely on internal representations rather than semantic regularities in the labels or prompts.
AI.result: In the tampering-detection setting, models do not reliably distinguish true interventions on internal activations from simple input manipulations, performing similarly across both, suggesting they are detecting generic anomalies instead of specifically recognizing disturbance to their internal states. In the hidden-state-label prediction paradigm, external classifiers using only the input text match the LLM’s in-context label prediction performance, undermining the claim that the model is leveraging privileged knowledge of its hidden states. In the relabeled control condition, where semantic cues are removed, LLM performance drops to near-chance levels, showing that prior successes largely relied on semantic structure rather than access to internal representations.
AI.conclusion: The current behavioral tests do not provide strong evidence that LLMs possess metacognitive monitoring or genuine introspective access to their own internal states. What appears as introspection can be explained by pattern matching on inputs and semantics, or by generic anomaly detection, rather than privileged self-knowledge. Robust claims about LLM introspection will require more carefully controlled paradigms that can rule out these alternative explanations; behavioral performance alone is insufficient to establish genuine metacognition in such systems.

title: Is Agent Memory a Database? Rethinking Data Foundations for Long-Term AI Agent Memory
summary: Long-running AI agents need persistent memory. Memory supports learning across sessions, reduces repeated context injection, and enables auditing of past decisions. Current agent memory systems and database paradigms treat memory as storage. They localize correctness at records, embeddings, or edges. Each supplies only some of the capabilities that long-term memory requires. The result is four recurring failure modes: unregulated growth, missing semantic revision, capacity-driven forgetting, and read-only retrieval. In our vision, long-term agent memory is a new data-management workload. Its correctness is a property of the state trajectory, not of individual records. We formalize this as Governed Evolving Memory (GEM). GEM replaces record-level database operations with four state-level operators: ingestion, revision, forgetting, and retrieval. Six correctness conditions govern how the state evolves. Three structural observations establish that no record-level system can satisfy these conditions, regardless of the storage model. We realize the abstraction in MemState, a prototype on a property-graph backend. MemState validates feasibility and exposes the gap to a native engine. We outline three research directions that define memory-centric data management as a workload.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Governed Evolving Memory (GEM), a new abstraction for long-term AI agent memory that focuses on correctness of the entire memory state trajectory instead of individual records, and demonstrates it via a prototype system, MemState.
AI.motivation: Long-running AI agents require persistent memory to learn across sessions, avoid repeatedly injecting large contexts, and enable auditing of past decisions. Existing memory systems—whether based on databases, embeddings, or graph structures—mainly treat memory as passive storage and enforce correctness at the record or edge level. This leads to four recurring problems: uncontrolled memory growth, lack of semantic updates to past content, forgetting driven purely by capacity constraints, and retrieval that is effectively read-only with no principled evolution of memory. The paper is motivated by the need to reconceptualize memory as an evolving stateful workload with its own correctness properties, rather than as static storage.
AI.method: The authors introduce the Governed Evolving Memory (GEM) abstraction, which models long-term memory as an evolving state governed by system-wide constraints. GEM defines four state-level operators—ingestion, revision, forgetting, and retrieval—that manipulate the memory as a whole rather than individual records. They specify six global correctness conditions that constrain how memory can evolve over time. The paper then provides three structural arguments showing that any system that operates only at the record level, independent of the underlying storage model, cannot fully satisfy these conditions. To validate the abstraction, they implement MemState, a prototype system built on top of a property-graph backend, and use it to explore feasibility and highlight the limitations of retrofitting GEM onto existing storage engines.
AI.result: The paper shows that GEM’s global-state perspective can be concretely realized in MemState, demonstrating that it is feasible to support ingestion, revision, forgetting, and retrieval operations under global correctness constraints, even atop an existing property-graph store. Through this prototype, the authors empirically and structurally illustrate the mismatch between current record-level systems and the requirements of governed evolving memory: while MemState works, it exposes performance and architectural gaps that would be better addressed by a native GEM-aware engine. The three structural observations formally establish the impossibility of fully implementing GEM with purely record-level operations, independent of data model choices.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that long-term agent memory should be treated as a distinct data-management workload, with correctness defined over memory state trajectories rather than individual records. Governed Evolving Memory (GEM) provides a principled abstraction for this, with four state-level operators and six correctness conditions that existing record-level systems cannot jointly satisfy. The MemState prototype confirms that GEM is feasible but also reveals the limitations of adapting current storage technologies, underscoring the need for new, memory-centric data-management systems. The paper sets out three research directions to develop native engines and techniques for governed, evolving agent memory.

title: Personalizing Embodied Multimodal Large Language Model Agents over Long-term User Interactions
summary: Multimodal large language model (MLLM)-based embodied agents have shown strong potential for solving complex tasks in physical environments. However, personalized assistance requires more than following generic instruction or recognizing object categories. In real-world scenarios, the intended target is often specified only implicitly through prior interactions, requiring agents to leverage personalized context accumulated over time. In this work, we propose POLAR, a multiomodal memory-augmented framework for personalized embodied agents over long-term user interactions. POLAR organizes prior interactions into a multimodal knowledge graph that captures semantic memory for personalized context and visual concepts, and episodic memory for embodied experiences such as agent trajectories. To execute embodied tasks, POLAR retrieves relevant memories to interpret the current request and guide task execution. We evaluate POLAR across multiple MLLM backbones and diverse evaluation scenarios to study the role of memory in long-term personalization. Results show that the proposed memory mechanism consistently improves performance by enabling more effective use of information accumulated over prior interactions. The gains are especially pronounced when the agents are required to reason across multiple interactions, perform multi-hop inference, or tracking updates in user-specific context over time.

AI.tldr: POLAR is a multimodal memory-augmented framework that equips MLLM-based embodied agents with long-term personalized context, improving performance on complex, multi-interaction tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLM-based embodied agents mainly follow generic instructions or recognize objects but struggle with real-world personalized assistance, where the target is often specified implicitly based on each user’s history. Agents need to accumulate and exploit long-term, personalized multimodal context—what the user prefers, how environments change, and what was done before—but current systems lack a structured way to store and use such memory over many interactions.
AI.method: The authors introduce POLAR, a multimodal memory-augmented framework for embodied agents. POLAR converts prior user-agent interactions into a multimodal knowledge graph that encodes: (1) semantic memory capturing personalized context and visual concepts, and (2) episodic memory capturing embodied experiences such as trajectories. At inference time, given a new embodied task, POLAR retrieves relevant nodes and subgraphs from this memory to interpret the current request, disambiguate implicitly specified targets, and guide task execution. The framework is instantiated on top of multiple multimodal LLM backbones and evaluated in varied scenarios requiring different forms of long-term reasoning.
AI.result: Across different MLLM backbones and evaluation setups, adding POLAR’s memory mechanism consistently boosts task performance compared to baselines without such memory. The improvements are particularly strong in scenarios that need reasoning across multiple past interactions, multi-hop inference over stored knowledge, and tracking or updating user-specific context (e.g., object locations, user preferences) over time.
AI.conclusion: A structured multimodal memory in the form of a knowledge graph—combining semantic and episodic components—substantially enhances the ability of embodied MLLM agents to provide personalized, long-term assistance. POLAR demonstrates that effectively storing and retrieving information from prior interactions is key for handling implicit user intent and complex, temporally extended tasks, and it generalizes across different backbone models and environments.

title: Self-Verified Distillation: Your Language Model Is Secretly Its Own Synthetic Data Pipeline
summary: Can post-trained large language models (LLMs) further improve themselves using only unlabeled prompts, without external teachers or feedback from tools? We study this setting starting only from unlabeled seed questions with no ground-truth solutions, across three reasoning domains: math, science, and coding. We propose Self-Verified Distillation, a simple post-training refinement algorithm in which the model generates candidate solutions to these seed questions, filters them using prompt-based self-verification, and trains on the resulting self-curated dataset. Inspired by the UQ benchmark's use of multiple validators to screen candidate answers to hard unsolved questions, we adapt this validation-based filtering idea to self-training: the model filters its own generated solutions through a three-stage cascade of cycle-consistency, factuality, and correctness checks, accepting a solution only if it passes all stages with unanimous judge votes. We find that sampling more candidate generations and using a larger verification budget during training data construction produces higher-quality self-curated data and, in turn, better reasoning models. We then train Qwen3 models at multiple scales with Self-Verified Distillation and obtain gains across all three domains. For Qwen3-4B, our method improves aggregate held-out pass@1 by +16.7 points in math (AIME26 and HMMT), +11.1 points in science (GPQA Diamond and HLE), and +8.3 points in coding (LCBv5 and LCBv6), with gains also extending to 0.6B and 8B models. Compared to our test-time-only baseline (UQ-TTC), which improves performance by spending extra compute at inference time, Self-Verified Distillation achieves better performance in most settings while requiring only a single inference call at test time.

AI.tldr: They propose a self-training method where an LLM improves itself using only unlabeled prompts by generating, verifying, and then learning from its own solutions across math, science, and coding.
AI.motivation: To determine whether post-trained LLMs can further enhance their reasoning abilities without labeled data, external teachers, or tool feedback, enabling cheaper and more scalable improvement, especially in complex reasoning domains.
AI.method: They introduce Self-Verified Distillation: starting from unlabeled seed questions, the model generates multiple candidate solutions, then filters them through a three-stage prompt-based self-verification cascade (cycle-consistency, factuality, and correctness) that uses multiple internal ‘judge’ votes. Only solutions unanimously passing all stages are kept to form a self-curated training set, which is then used to further train Qwen3 models. They study the effects of sampling more candidates and using larger verification budgets on data quality and downstream performance.
AI.result: Using Self-Verified Distillation on Qwen3 models (0.6B, 4B, 8B) across math, science, and coding, they observe substantial gains. For Qwen3-4B, aggregate held-out pass@1 improves by +16.7 points in math (AIME26, HMMT), +11.1 in science (GPQA Diamond, HLE), and +8.3 in coding (LCBv5, LCBv6). Higher sampling and verification budgets yield higher-quality self-curated data and better models. The approach generally outperforms a test-time-compute-only baseline (UQ-TTC).
AI.conclusion: LLMs can significantly self-improve on reasoning tasks using only unlabeled prompts by generating and strictly self-verifying their own training data. Self-Verified Distillation yields strong, scalable gains across domains and model sizes and typically surpasses test-time-only compute-heavy baselines while keeping inference cheap (single call at test time).

title: Pretraining Data Exposure in Large Language Models: A Survey of Membership Inference, Data Contamination, and Security Implications
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have become the predominant paradigm in NLP, advancing both research and industry. As model sizes and pretraining data grow, concerns about Pretraining Data Exposure (PDE) increase due to the scale and opacity of training datasets.
  PDE refers to determining whether specific data appeared in an LLM's pretraining corpus. It is critical for ensuring evaluation integrity and protecting privacy, intersecting two key areas: data contamination and membership inference. Though conceptually related, these areas have often been studied in isolation. This paper offers the first unified survey of both under the PDE framework. We formalize PDE across exposure levels, review attack and defense methods, synthesize empirical findings, and highlight open challenges and future research directions.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys Pretraining Data Exposure (PDE) in LLMs, unifying data contamination and membership inference under a single framework, summarizing attacks, defenses, findings, and open problems.
AI.motivation: As LLMs grow and their training data remain opaque, risks increase that specific items from pretraining corpora can be detected or leaked, threatening evaluation validity and privacy. Existing work on data contamination and membership inference is fragmented, lacking a unified view tailored to LLMs’ pretraining data exposure.
AI.method: The authors propose a unified PDE framework that formalizes different levels of exposure, then systematically review and categorize prior work on attacks (ways to infer if data were in pretraining), defenses (ways to mitigate or detect exposure), and empirical studies. They synthesize results across these threads and analyze patterns, limitations, and gaps.
AI.result: They provide a taxonomy of exposure levels, attack and defense strategies, and empirical findings on when and how LLMs reveal pretraining data. The survey clarifies relationships between data contamination and membership inference, identifies common assumptions and evaluation pitfalls, and compiles evidence about PDE risks in current LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining Data Exposure is a central concern for LLM safety, evaluation reliability, and privacy. By unifying data contamination and membership inference, the survey establishes a common conceptual and methodological ground, surfaces unresolved challenges, and outlines key directions for future research on measuring, mitigating, and governing PDE in LLMs.

title: Your Agents Are Aging Too: Agent Lifespan Engineering for Deployed Systems
summary: Long-lived AI agents are increasingly deployed as persistent operational systems, yet they are still evaluated like freshly initialized models. Day-one benchmarks miss a basic systems question: how long does an agent remain reliable after deployment? Even when model weights are frozen, an agent's effective state keeps changing as it compresses interaction history, retrieves from a growing memory store, revises facts after updates, and undergoes routine maintenance. Reliability therefore becomes a lifespan property of the full agent harness, not only a snapshot property of the base model. We introduce AgingBench, a longitudinal reliability benchmark for agent lifespan engineering: measuring not only whether deployed agents degrade, but what form the degradation takes and where repair should target. AgingBench organizes agent aging into four mechanisms: compression aging, interference aging, revision aging, and maintenance aging. To diagnose these failures, AgingBench uses temporal dependency graphs and paired counterfactual probes that produce diagnostic profiles for the write, retrieval, and utilization stages of the memory pipeline. Across 7 scenarios, 14 models, multiple memory policies, and both runner-controlled and autonomous agents, over ~400 runs spanning 8 - 200 sessions show that agent aging is not one-dimensional: behavioral tests can remain clean while factual precision decays; derived-state tracking can collapse sharply within a single model; and the same wrong answer can require different repairs depending on what the diagnostic profile points to. These results suggest that reliable agent deployment requires lifespan evaluation, mechanism-level diagnosis, and stage-targeted repair, not only stronger day-one models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AgingBench, a benchmark to study how long-lived AI agents degrade over time and how to diagnose and repair that degradation.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations treat agents like freshly initialized models and ignore how reliability changes as agents run over many sessions. Long-lived agents update memory, compress history, and get maintained, so reliability is a lifespan property. The authors want a systematic way to measure and understand "agent aging" in realistic deployments.
AI.method: They define four mechanisms of agent aging—compression, interference, revision, and maintenance aging—and build AgingBench, a longitudinal benchmark that uses temporal dependency graphs and paired counterfactual probes to diagnose failures. The benchmark instruments the agent memory pipeline (write, retrieval, utilization) and runs agents across many sessions, models, memory policies, and control regimes, collecting diagnostic profiles over time.
AI.result: Across 7 scenarios, 14 models, multiple memory policies, and both controlled and autonomous runs (~400 runs over 8–200 sessions), they find that agent aging is multi-dimensional: agents can keep passing behavioral tests while their factual knowledge decays; tracking of derived state can suddenly collapse; and identical wrong answers can stem from different underlying failure modes as revealed by diagnostic profiles.
AI.conclusion: Reliability of long-lived agents cannot be guaranteed by strong day-one performance alone. Developers need lifespan-oriented evaluation, mechanism-level diagnosis of aging, and targeted repairs to specific stages of the memory pipeline in order to maintain reliable deployed agents.

title: Experiments in Agentic AI for Science
summary: This paper details two novel frameworks for developing autonomous, agentic AI in scientific workflows. Both systems leverage a hybrid Local Body, Remote Brain architecture via Google Colab, utilizing Python-based local orchestrators to invoke large language model (LLM) cloud backends. The first agent, DeepTS/DeepCollector, automates the large-scale curation, extraction, and deduplication of time-series datasets. The second, DeepScribe, is an autonomous presentation analyzer that converts visually dense, mathematically complex physics lectures into structured scientific reports. Through practical systems engineering-such as granular attribute extraction (Cellular RAG), remote data inspection, and distributed concurrency controls-we demonstrate how agentic AI can overcome the context and reasoning limitations of current state-of-the-art systems to rigorously support scientific workflows. Finally, we outline a generalization of DeepTS to support deep knowledge graphs and discuss the application of this conceptual approach to high-energy physics (DeepQCD).

AI.tldr: Two agentic AI frameworks use a local–remote architecture with LLM backends to automate (1) large-scale time-series data curation and (2) conversion of complex physics lectures into structured reports, with extensions toward knowledge graphs for high-energy physics.
AI.motivation: Scientific workflows increasingly depend on heterogeneous, large-scale data (e.g., time series) and complex multimedia inputs (e.g., lecture videos) that are difficult and labor-intensive for humans to curate, structure, and interpret. Current LLM-based tools struggle with long-context reasoning, precise extraction, and managing distributed resources. The authors aim to design practical, autonomous AI agents that can reliably perform end-to-end scientific tasks—data collection, cleaning, analysis, and documentation—by orchestrating LLMs with robust systems engineering patterns.
AI.method: They propose a hybrid Local Body, Remote Brain architecture implemented via Google Colab. Lightweight Python orchestrators running locally coordinate calls to cloud LLM backends. The first framework, DeepTS/DeepCollector, is an autonomous pipeline for discovering, curating, extracting, and de-duplicating large time-series datasets, equipped with granular attribute extraction (Cellular RAG), remote data inspection, and concurrency management. The second framework, DeepScribe, is an autonomous presentation analyzer that parses visually and mathematically dense physics lectures (e.g., slides, equations) and converts them into structured scientific reports. They also sketch an extension of DeepTS toward deep knowledge graph construction for domain-specific applications like high-energy physics (DeepQCD).
AI.result: The systems demonstrate that combining LLMs with explicit orchestration logic, fine-grained retrieval and extraction (Cellular RAG), and distributed concurrency controls can automate complex scientific tasks that exceed the practical context and reasoning limits of monolithic LLM calls. DeepTS/DeepCollector successfully handles large-scale time-series data curation with deduplication and structured metadata extraction, while DeepScribe converts complex physics presentations into coherent, structured scientific documents. The conceptual extension toward DeepQCD indicates feasibility for building deep knowledge graphs from domain data, although details and metrics are not given in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI, when embedded in a Local Body, Remote Brain systems architecture with careful engineering for retrieval, inspection, and concurrency, can robustly support and partially automate demanding scientific workflows. The presented frameworks—DeepTS/DeepCollector for time-series data and DeepScribe for lecture-to-report conversion—illustrate how LLM-based agents can surmount context window and reasoning constraints of standalone models. The generalization to knowledge graphs (e.g., DeepQCD) suggests a path toward scalable, domain-specific, autonomous scientific assistants, particularly in data-intensive fields like high-energy physics.

title: CroCo: Cross-Lingual Contrastive Preference Tuning on Self-Generations
summary: Prior work establishes that controlled contrastiveness between self-generated responses from large language models, set via reward scores, improves downstream preference tuning in English. We extend this method to multiple languages and evaluate two models across a total of 14 high and low-resource languages on a diverse set of tasks. Our central finding is that cross-lingual contrastive preference tuning on self-generations (CroCo) transfers without language-specific preference annotation. A reward model trained on English preferences (atop a multilingual base) produces useful within-language rankings across most languages, and pairing in either a monolingual or multilingual setting improves over each model on the majority of setups while preventing the catastrophic forgetting of supervised fine-tuning. We observe that the gains require on-policy data. Off-policy responses reduce the benefit and online preference optimization fails to improve over the offline variant. Specifically, on structured tasks, our method matches or exceeds the base in 6/7 languages for EuroLLM-9B and 4/7 settings for Aya-3B. On open-ended generation, both tuned models win against their respective base across 11 evaluated languages. Overall, we show promising directions for multilingual preference tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CroCo, a cross-lingual contrastive preference tuning method that uses self-generated responses and an English-trained reward model to improve multilingual LLMs without language-specific preference data.
AI.motivation: To reduce the cost and complexity of collecting language-specific human preference annotations for many languages while still improving multilingual LLM alignment and avoiding catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning.
AI.method: Extend controlled contrastive preference tuning on self-generated responses from English to a multilingual setting. Train a multilingual reward model on English preference data only, then use it to score and contrastively rank model self-generations in 14 high- and low-resource languages. Perform preference tuning both mono- and multilingual, compare on-policy vs off-policy data and online vs offline optimization, and evaluate two base models (EuroLLM-9B and Aya-3B) on structured and open-ended tasks.
AI.result: An English-trained reward model atop a multilingual backbone produces useful preference rankings in many other languages. Cross-lingual contrastive tuning (CroCo) improves performance over the base models in most configurations while mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Gains rely on on-policy data; off-policy responses weaken benefits and online preference optimization does not surpass offline. On structured tasks CroCo matches or exceeds the base in 6/7 languages for EuroLLM-9B and 4/7 for Aya-3B, and on open-ended tasks both tuned models outperform their bases across 11 evaluated languages.
AI.conclusion: Cross-lingual contrastive preference tuning on self-generations is an effective and scalable strategy for multilingual alignment without per-language preference labels. English-only preference supervision, combined with a multilingual reward model and on-policy data, can transfer alignment improvements across many languages and task types, suggesting a promising path for multilingual preference tuning research and practice.

title: Anchor: Mitigating Artifact Drift in Agent Benchmark Generation
comment: Accepted to RLEval '26 (Workshop at ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems 2026)
summary: AI agents are beginning to complete valuable, long-horizon business operations tasks, but training and evaluation environments for enterprise work still struggle to balance realism, verifiability, and scale. Environment and task creation frequently suffers from a failure mode we call artifact drift: when instructions, environments, oracles, and verifiers are created by loosely coupled processes, they frequently disagree on what a task requires, producing environments that are unsolvable, reward-hackable, or inconsistent. We introduce Anchor, a task-generation pipeline that formalizes domain experts' specifications of business workflows into constraint optimization programs. From a single parametric specification, the pipeline jointly produces a natural-language instruction, environment configuration, solver-certified ground-truth solution, and state-based verifier. With Anchor, altering parameters yields new tasks with controlled difficulty and known optimal solutions, producing harness-agnostic environments whose rewards depend solely on end-state business correctness. We apply Anchor to produce ERP-Bench: a benchmark of 300 long-horizon tasks spanning procurement and manufacturing workflows in a production-grade ERP system. We find that generation parameters predict realized difficulty, and that frontier models satisfy explicit task constraints in 26.1% of trials but reach a fully optimal solution in only 17.4% of trials. Overall, we show that Anchor and ERP-Bench offer a concrete recipe for building auditable evaluation environments for economically valuable agent work. We release the task generator and ERP-Bench dataset at erpbench.ai

AI.tldr: Anchor is a pipeline that turns expert-specified business workflows into consistent, auditable AI task environments and is used to build ERP-Bench, a realistic benchmark of long-horizon enterprise tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing environments for training and evaluating AI agents on enterprise tasks are either unrealistic, hard to verify, or do not scale. A key problem, artifact drift, arises when task components like instructions, environments, and verifiers are created separately, leading to unsolvable or reward-hackable tasks. The paper aims to address this by tightly coupling task specification, environment generation, and verification in a principled way.
AI.method: The authors propose Anchor, a task-generation pipeline that encodes domain experts’ descriptions of business workflows into constraint optimization programs. From one parametric specification, Anchor jointly produces four aligned artifacts: (1) a natural-language task description, (2) an environment configuration, (3) a solver-certified ground-truth solution, and (4) a state-based verifier. Task parameters can be systematically varied to control difficulty while maintaining known optimal solutions. They instantiate this pipeline on a real ERP system to create ERP-Bench, a suite of 300 procurement and manufacturing tasks.
AI.result: Using Anchor, they generate ERP-Bench, a benchmark of 300 realistic, long-horizon ERP workflows. They empirically show that the chosen generation parameters correlate with the realized task difficulty. When evaluated on "frontier" models acting as agents, the models satisfy explicit task constraints in 26.1% of runs and achieve fully optimal business solutions in only 17.4%, demonstrating substantial remaining headroom.
AI.conclusion: Anchor provides a practical recipe for constructing scalable, realistic, and auditable environments for enterprise AI agents by eliminating artifact drift via joint generation of all task components from a single formal specification. ERP-Bench, built with Anchor, showcases this approach on procurement and manufacturing workflows and reveals that state-of-the-art models still struggle with end-to-end optimal performance on complex business processes.

title: The Daily Dose: Workflow-Integrated Large Language Model Automation for Clinical Summarization and Trial Identification in Radiation Oncology
summary: Objective: To describe the design and early clinical evaluation of The Daily Dose (TDD), an LLM-driven, automated clinical summarization and clinical-trial identification system integrated into routine radiation oncology practice. Design: Mixed-methods evaluation using a cross-sectional, anonymous clinician survey administered after 1 month of system deployment. Exposure: Daily automated delivery of physician-specific email summaries generated using RadOnc-GPT, including patient schedules, concise EHR-derived clinical-status summaries, and automated identification of potentially relevant clinical trials for new or consult visits. Main Outcomes and Measures: Primary outcomes included self-reported usability, satisfaction, perceived usefulness, perceived impact on workflow, time savings, and intention for continued use. Internal consistency reliability was assessed using Cronbach's $α$. Results: Among 55 respondents, 52 (94.5\%) worked in radiation oncology, and 38 (69.1\%) were attending physicians. Most participants (83.6\%) reported using TDD daily or several times per week. Mean (SD) scores were 3.89 (1.04) for usability and satisfaction, 3.43 (1.24) for perceived usefulness, and 3.80 (1.17) for impact and future use (5-point Likert scale). Overall satisfaction was positively associated with perceived time savings ($p < .001$). Participants reported variable time savings, with 27\% estimating $\geq 10$ minutes saved per day. The questionnaire demonstrated excellent internal consistency (overall Cronbach's $α$ = 0.97).

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates an LLM-driven system (The Daily Dose) that automatically summarizes clinical information and highlights clinical trials for radiation oncologists, showing good usability and perceived time savings in early use.
AI.motivation: Clinicians face information overload in electronic health records and underutilization of clinical trials, especially in busy radiation oncology practices. There is a need for tools that can automatically summarize patient status and flag relevant trials without adding workflow burden.
AI.method: The authors designed an LLM-based tool, The Daily Dose, that emails physicians daily, providing schedules, concise EHR-derived patient summaries, and automatically identified clinical trials for new/consult visits. They conducted a mixed-methods, cross-sectional, anonymous survey of clinicians after one month of deployment, measuring usability, satisfaction, usefulness, workflow impact, time savings, and intention to continue using the tool, and assessed internal consistency with Cronbach’s alpha.
AI.result: Among 55 survey respondents (mostly radiation oncology and attending physicians), over 80% used The Daily Dose at least several times per week. On 5-point Likert scales, mean scores were around 3.4–3.9 for usability, satisfaction, usefulness, impact, and future use. Higher satisfaction correlated strongly with perceived time savings, and about 27% of respondents reported saving at least 10 minutes per day. The survey instrument had excellent internal consistency with an overall Cronbach’s alpha of 0.97.
AI.conclusion: Early clinical deployment of The Daily Dose, an LLM-based summarization and trial-identification tool, appears feasible and well-accepted, with frequent use, good usability ratings, and perceived time savings for radiation oncologists. These findings support further development and more rigorous evaluation of such AI tools in routine oncology workflows.

title: OmniToM: Benchmarking Theory of Mind in LLMs via Explicit Belief Modeling
summary: Theory of Mind (ToM), the ability to infer others' knowledge, intentions, and emotions, is commonly evaluated in large language models (LLMs) using end-point question answering, where performance is judged solely by the final answer to a social reasoning query. This paradigm obscures whether the model actually constructs the underlying mental-state representations required for robust reasoning, particularly in scenarios involving divergent, evolving, or mistaken beliefs. In order to address this research gap, we introduce OmniToM, a benchmark that directly evaluates these representations by requiring explicit modeling of belief structures for all relevant actors within a narrative. These structures are composed of belief propositions: minimal statements of what an actor takes to be true about the world or another actor's mental state, allowing knowledge, intentions, emotions, and false beliefs to be analyzed in a common format. Models are evaluated in two stages: Stage 1: Belief Extraction, which extracts from the story the beliefs relevant to its social dynamics, and Stage 2: Belief Labeling, which assigns each belief a seven-dimensional schema label covering recursive order, truth status, knowledge access, explicitness, content type, mental source, and context. Built from 895 stories from the existing ToMBench story corpus and augmented with 22,343 labeled belief propositions, OmniToM uses a human-calibrated LLM-assisted annotation pipeline. Across diverse models in zero-shot evaluation, OmniToM reveals an actor-specific belief-tracking bottleneck: current LLMs struggle with the knowledge-access and representational decisions required to transform narrative facts into actors' beliefs and shared mental states.

AI.tldr: OmniToM is a benchmark to evaluate whether LLMs explicitly build and label detailed belief representations for all actors in social narratives, revealing a bottleneck in actor-specific belief tracking.
AI.motivation: Existing Theory of Mind evaluations for LLMs mostly use end-point question answering, judging success only by the final answer to a social reasoning question. This makes it unclear whether models truly construct internal mental-state representations—especially for complex cases involving mistaken, changing, or nested beliefs—rather than just pattern-matching to the correct answer. The authors aim to fill this gap by designing a benchmark that directly probes how well models extract and structure the beliefs of different actors in a story.
AI.method: The authors create OmniToM, a benchmark built from 895 narratives from the ToMBench corpus, annotated with 22,343 belief propositions. Each proposition is a minimal statement about what an actor believes about the world or others’ minds. The evaluation has two stages: (1) Belief Extraction, where a model must list the beliefs relevant to the story’s social dynamics for each actor; and (2) Belief Labeling, where the model assigns each belief a seven-dimensional label covering recursive order (e.g., who thinks what about whom), truth status, knowledge access, explicitness, content type, mental source, and context. Annotation is performed via a human-calibrated LLM-assisted pipeline. Models are evaluated zero-shot on this benchmark.
AI.result: Across a range of LLMs evaluated in zero-shot conditions, the benchmark shows that current models have difficulty accurately tracking which actor knows or believes what, when, and why. In particular, they struggle with knowledge-access inference and with converting narrative facts into correctly represented individual and shared mental states. Performance limitations emerge clearly at the level of actor-specific belief structures rather than just final question answering accuracy.
AI.conclusion: OmniToM demonstrates that many LLMs’ apparent Theory of Mind abilities are limited by a bottleneck in constructing precise, actor-specific belief representations. By moving beyond end-point QA and requiring explicit extraction and structured labeling of beliefs, the benchmark provides a more direct and fine-grained test of ToM reasoning in LLMs and exposes gaps in their ability to handle complex social cognition, such as recursive and false beliefs.

title: RICE-PO: Turning Retrieval Interactions into Credit Signals for Reasoning Agents
summary: Retrieval is increasingly moving from one-shot matching toward interactive reasoning, where language agents iteratively inspect evidence, reformulate queries, and search again. Training such agents raises a credit-assignment challenge: executable actions such as queries or summaries can be directly evaluated by the retriever, while latent reasoning steps are not directly observable and only affect future executable actions. This asymmetry makes outcome-level reward assignment unreliable, as the same final reward may credit reasoning steps that did not actually shape retrieval success. We propose RICE-PO, a critic-free policy optimization framework that converts retrieval interactions into localized learning signals. RICE-PO selects high-uncertainty executable actions as anchors, evaluates local counterfactual branches using retrieval metrics, and propagates credit to latent reasoning steps only when reasoning-to-action influence is strong and future residual effects are stable. On BRIGHT and BEIR, RICE-PO consistently outperforms prompt-based agents and group-based RL baselines under the same retriever setting. These results show that the structure of agent-environment interaction itself can provide useful supervision for training reasoning-based retrieval agents.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RICE-PO, a critic-free policy optimization framework that improves training of interactive retrieval agents by assigning localized credit to reasoning and action steps, outperforming existing baselines on BRIGHT and BEIR benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Interactive retrieval agents increasingly perform multi-step reasoning: they inspect evidence, reformulate queries, and repeatedly search. Training them is hard because only executable actions (like queries and summaries) can be directly evaluated with retrieval metrics, while internal reasoning steps are latent. Standard outcome-level rewards (e.g., based only on final retrieval quality) make credit assignment unreliable: good final results may incorrectly reward reasoning steps that had little or no impact on success. The authors aim to solve this credit-assignment problem and provide more faithful supervision for reasoning-based retrieval agents without relying on a separate critic model.
AI.method: The authors introduce RICE-PO, a critic-free policy optimization method tailored to interactive retrieval. It converts an entire retrieval interaction trajectory into localized learning signals by: (1) identifying high-uncertainty executable actions (e.g., queries, summaries) along the trajectory and treating them as anchor points; (2) constructing local counterfactual branches around each anchor by exploring alternative actions or continuations; (3) evaluating these branches with standard retrieval metrics to quantify the local impact of the anchor action; and (4) propagating credit to the latent reasoning steps that led to that anchor only when two conditions hold: (a) the reasoning strongly influences the chosen executable action, and (b) the downstream residual effects of this action are relatively stable across branches. This way, the method focuses learning on reasoning steps that genuinely shape successful retrieval behavior without requiring an explicit critic network.
AI.result: On the BRIGHT and BEIR benchmarks, RICE-PO is evaluated under a fixed retriever configuration to isolate the effect on the agent’s reasoning and interaction strategy. Across these datasets, RICE-PO consistently outperforms prompt-based interactive agents and group-based reinforcement learning baselines on retrieval quality metrics. The gains indicate that its localized credit assignment leads to more effective training of reasoning policies compared with methods that rely on coarse, outcome-level rewards or group-based interaction modeling.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that the structural signals inherent in agent-environment interaction—specifically, which actions are uncertain, how local counterfactual variations perform, and how stable downstream effects are—can serve as potent supervision for training reasoning-based retrieval agents. By leveraging these structural cues, RICE-PO avoids the need for a learned critic while providing more accurate credit assignment to latent reasoning steps. The success on BRIGHT and BEIR suggests that critic-free, interaction-structure-aware policy optimization is a promising direction for improving complex, multi-step retrieval systems and potentially other reasoning-intensive language agents.

title: JobBench: Aligning Agent Work With Human Will
summary: Current benchmarks for occupational AI agents are scoped primarily by economic values, telling a replacement story. We introduce JobBench, which evaluates AI agents on the workflows that experts identify as high-priority for delegation, empowering humans based on their needs instead of replacing them with GDP value. JobBench covers 130 agentic tasks across 35 occupations. Each task is packaged as a workspace of heterogeneous reference files, requiring the agent to reason through the cluttered information streams of real professional work. Outputs are graded by a fact-anchored chain of rubrics, averaging 35.6 binary criteria per task. We evaluate 36 models; the strongest, Claude Opus~4.7 under Claude Code, reaches only 45.9 %. We hope JobBench shifts the community's target labour-market effect from replacement to enhancement: building agents that do what humans actually want delegated, not only what is most economically valuable.

AI.tldr: JobBench is a benchmark for AI occupational agents focused on tasks that professionals actually want to delegate, rather than those with highest economic value, and shows current models still perform far from expert levels.
AI.motivation: Existing occupational AI benchmarks emphasize economic impact and job replacement, overlooking what parts of work humans truly want to offload. The authors want to reframe AI's role from replacing workers based on GDP/value metrics to empowering them by supporting real-world workflows and high-priority delegable tasks.
AI.method: They construct JobBench, a benchmark of 130 realistic agentic tasks across 35 occupations. Each task includes a cluttered workspace of heterogeneous reference files designed to mimic real professional information environments. They design detailed, fact-anchored, chain-structured rubrics with an average of 35.6 binary criteria per task, and use these rubrics to grade outputs from 36 different AI models on the benchmark.
AI.result: Across the evaluated 36 models, performance is generally low. The best-performing system, Claude Opus 4.7 used in a coding-focused configuration, achieves only 45.9% on the JobBench criteria, indicating substantial remaining gaps in AI capability for these realistic, delegable workflows.
AI.conclusion: JobBench demonstrates that even top current AI agents fall well short of reliably handling the kinds of professional tasks experts most want to delegate. The authors propose JobBench as a way to redirect research and evaluation from labour-market replacement narratives to human-centric enhancement, encouraging the development of systems that support workers on the tasks they actually prefer to offload.

title: In-Context Optimization for Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Gradient-Descent Perspective
summary: In-context learning has recently been linked to implicit gradient descent in linear self-attention models, suggesting that context can induce a forward-pass update. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) also relies on context, but retrieved documents are usually treated as static evidence rather than signals for adaptation. We study RAG as an in-context optimization process. First, we show that one linear self-attention layer can implement one gradient-descent step on a unified linearized RAG objective covering both projection-based and dot-product retrieval interfaces. This gives an exact regime where retrieval-augmented prediction and in-context optimization coincide. We use this result not as a literal model of LLM computation, but as a guide for adapting the interaction between queries and retrieved evidence. We then test the boundary of this correspondence: it remains stable under controlled linear extensions, but becomes feature-distribution dependent under nonlinear architectures. Finally, we turn this view into a lightweight method for frozen RAG LLMs. The method keeps the retriever and backbone fixed, and predicts a context-conditioned update to a generator-side evidence-use interface. Across seven QA benchmarks, two retrievers, and two frozen LLM backbones, this forward-only update improves a shared-interface baseline, transfers to held-out tasks, and approaches test-time gradient adaptation at much lower per-query cost.

AI.tldr: The paper reinterprets retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) as an in-context optimization process and shows that, in a linear self-attention setting, RAG can be exactly equivalent to one step of gradient descent on a unified RAG objective. Building on this view, the authors design a lightweight, forward-only adaptation method for frozen RAG LLMs that improves evidence use across multiple QA benchmarks at low cost.
AI.motivation: Existing work has shown that in-context learning in linear self-attention models can be viewed as implicit gradient descent, where context induces a forward-pass parameter update. RAG also relies heavily on context (retrieved documents), but these are typically treated as static evidence rather than as signals that can drive adaptation of how the model uses that evidence. There is a need to understand whether and how RAG can be framed as in-context optimization, and to exploit this perspective to design better, cheaper test-time adaptation methods for frozen LLMs that make more effective use of retrieved documents.
AI.method: 1) The authors analytically study linear self-attention and derive conditions under which one attention layer can implement a single gradient-descent step on a unified, linearized RAG objective that subsumes both projection-based and dot-product retrieval. 2) They analyze how robust this equivalence is when extending beyond the simplest linear setting, showing where it holds and where it breaks under more complex, nonlinear architectures and distributional shifts in features. 3) Guided by this theory, they propose a practical method for frozen RAG LLMs that leaves the retriever and backbone unchanged while learning to predict, via a forward pass, a context-conditioned update to the generator’s evidence-use interface (how the generator interacts with retrieved documents).
AI.result: The theoretical result establishes an exact equivalence between retrieval-augmented prediction and a single in-context gradient-descent step in a linear self-attention model under the unified RAG objective. Empirically, the proposed forward-only adaptation method, applied on top of frozen LLM backbones and fixed retrievers, yields consistent improvements over a shared-interface RAG baseline across seven QA benchmarks, two different retrievers, and two LLM generators. The method generalizes to unseen tasks and achieves performance close to more expensive test-time gradient-based adaptation, while requiring much lower computation per query.
AI.conclusion: RAG can be rigorously understood as a form of in-context optimization in linear self-attention, unifying retrieval and adaptation under a gradient-descent perspective. While the exact correspondence weakens with nonlinear architectures and distributional complexity, this conceptual lens enables a practical, lightweight adaptation technique for frozen RAG LLMs. By predicting context-conditioned updates to the generator’s evidence-use interface in a purely forward manner, the method improves QA performance, transfers to new tasks, and approximates the benefits of explicit test-time gradient updates at significantly lower cost.

title: Managing Uncertainty in LLM-Generated Procedural Knowledge for Virtual Laboratory Planning
summary: Educational virtual laboratories can make experimental training more scala-ble, adaptive, and accessible, especially when students have limited access to physical laboratory facilities. However, authoring new simulated laboratory procedures remains costly: educators must describe new equipment, define how instruments and materials interact, and specify valid procedural flows that can be executed or assessed inside the virtual environment. Large lan-guage models can assist in this authoring process by generating detailed ex-perimental procedures, but their output should not be treated as directly exe-cutable plans. They may omit necessary actions, arrange steps in the wrong order, or produce instructions that are logically incorrect or incompatible with the laboratory equipment. This paper presents a prototype framework for managing uncertainty in LLM-generated procedural knowledge for virtu-al laboratory planning. The framework aims to reduce procedural uncertainty by using structured domain representations and uncertain LLM-generated state-transition samples to extract candidate procedural rules, transform them into explicit and inspectable constraints, and use them to repair uncertain procedural steps. Although the motivating domain refers to educational vir-tual laboratories, the underlying problem is more general: managing uncer-tain procedural knowledge for action planning in structured interactive envi-ronments. We illustrate the approach in a virtual laboratory domain involving laboratory instruments, containers, tools, and material-transfer actions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework to handle and repair the uncertainty and errors in large-language-model-generated experimental procedures for educational virtual labs, by extracting and enforcing explicit procedural constraints from uncertain LLM samples.
AI.motivation: Virtual labs can expand access to experimental training but are hard to author because educators must meticulously encode equipment, interactions, and valid procedures. LLMs can help generate procedures, yet their outputs are unreliable and not directly executable, introducing uncertainty, missing or misordered steps, and logical incompatibilities with lab equipment. There is thus a need for a systematic way to manage and repair uncertain LLM-generated procedural knowledge so that it can be safely used in interactive environments.
AI.method: The authors build a prototype framework that uses structured domain representations for a virtual lab, samples multiple uncertain state transitions from an LLM, and from these samples extracts candidate procedural rules. These rules are converted into explicit, human-inspectable constraints over actions, states, and flows. The system then uses these constraints to detect and repair uncertain or inconsistent steps in LLM-generated procedures, effectively constraining and adjusting the plan so it becomes consistent with the modeled lab environment.
AI.result: In a virtual laboratory domain including instruments, containers, tools, and material-transfer actions, the framework can take noisy LLM-generated procedures and refine them into more consistent, constraint-respecting action sequences. The case study demonstrates that uncertain procedural knowledge from LLMs can be made more structured and reliable for planning through rule extraction and constraint-based repair, though results are at the prototype / illustrative stage rather than at large-scale evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that uncertainty in LLM-generated procedural knowledge can be managed by combining structured domain models with constraint extraction from uncertain LLM samples, enabling repair of flawed procedures for use in virtual lab planning. While the work is demonstrated in educational virtual laboratories, the authors argue that the underlying ideas generalize to other structured interactive environments that require reliable action plans, and they position the framework as an initial step toward robust LLM-assisted authoring of executable procedures.

title: Why LLMs Hallucinate on Structured Knowledge: A Mechanistic Analysis of Reasoning over Linearized Representations
comment: To appear in Proceedings of ACL 2026
summary: In many reasoning tasks, large language models (LLMs) rely on structured external knowledge, such as graphs and tables, which is typically linearized into sequential token representations. However, even when sufficient knowledge is available, LLMs can still produce hallucinated outputs, and the underlying mechanisms behind such failures remain poorly understood. We investigate these mechanisms and find that hallucinations arise from systematic internal dynamics rather than random noise. First, attention disproportionately concentrates toward shortcut-like structural cues rather than distributing across the full context. Second, feed-forward representations fail to ground the provided knowledge, causing the model to revert to parametric memory. Moreover, our results indicate that hallucination is consistently associated with failures in semantic grounding within feed-forward layers, while attention allocation exhibits greater task-dependent variability. Finally, we show that these mechanistic patterns generalize beyond single-hop graphs to multi-hop and tabular settings, enabling effective hallucination detection across structured knowledge formats.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why LLMs hallucinate even when given correct structured knowledge, finding that hallucinations stem from systematic internal mechanisms in attention and feed-forward layers, and uses these patterns to detect hallucinations across graphs and tables.
AI.motivation: LLMs often access external structured knowledge (graphs, tables) that is linearized into text, yet they still hallucinate. Existing work usually treats hallucinations as noisy or unpredictable errors and focuses on symptoms, not mechanisms. The authors aim to understand the internal computational causes of hallucination when sufficient knowledge is present, and to see whether these causes are consistent and exploitable for detection across different structured knowledge formats.
AI.method: The authors perform mechanistic interpretability and circuit-level analyses of LLMs on reasoning tasks over structured knowledge (single-hop graphs, multi-hop graphs, and tables). They study attention patterns to see how the model attends to different parts of the linearized structure and probe feed-forward layer activations to test whether they encode and use the provided external knowledge versus falling back on parametric memory. They correlate specific attention and feed-forward behaviors with hallucination occurrences and evaluate whether these signatures transfer across task types and structured formats, then leverage the discovered patterns to build hallucination detectors.
AI.result: They find that hallucinations are driven by systematic internal dynamics, not random noise. Attention tends to over-focus on shortcut-like structural tokens instead of the full relevant context, and feed-forward layers frequently fail to properly represent or "ground" the supplied knowledge, leading the model to default to its parametric knowledge. Hallucinated outputs strongly correlate with failures of semantic grounding in feed-forward layers, while aberrant attention patterns are more variable across tasks. These internal signatures generalize across single-hop graphs, multi-hop graphs, and tabular data, enabling an effective, mechanism-based hallucination detector.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination in LLMs with access to structured external knowledge is primarily a result of systematic failures in semantic grounding within feed-forward layers, combined with shortcut-seeking attention dynamics, rather than random errors. These mechanisms are robust across different structured knowledge settings and can be used to detect hallucinations. Understanding these internal patterns provides a foundation for designing more reliable models and targeted interventions that improve grounding to external knowledge and mitigate hallucinations in reasoning tasks.

title: ScientistOne: Towards Human-Level Autonomous Research via Chain-of-Evidence
summary: Autonomous research agents produce competitive solutions and professional-looking manuscripts, yet their outputs contain verifiability failures undetectable by surface-level evaluation: fabricated citations, unreproducible scores, and method descriptions that diverge from the implementation. We address this through three contributions. First, Chain-of-Evidence (CoE), a verifiability framework requiring every claim to be traceable to its evidence source. Second, ScientistOne, an end-to-end autonomous research system that maintains evidence chains by construction throughout literature review, solution discovery, and paper writing. Third, CoE Audit, a post-hoc audit whose four integrity checks -- score verification, specification violation, reference verification, and method-code alignment -- apply uniformly to all systems. Across 75 papers spanning five systems and five frontier research tasks, every baseline exhibits at least one systematic failure mode: hallucinated reference rates reach 21%, score verification passes in as few as 42% of papers, and method-code alignment ranges from 20% to 80%. ScientistOne achieves zero hallucinated references (0/337), perfect score verification (12/12), and the highest method-code alignment (14/15), while matching or exceeding human expert performance on all five tasks. ScientistOne further generalizes to six additional tasks spanning medical imaging, fine-grained recognition, 3D perception, and language modeling, achieving state-of-the-art on Parameter Golf and gold medals on MLE-Bench tasks where baselines fail entirely.

AI.tldr: They propose a framework and system to ensure every claim in autonomous research papers is verifiable, and show it eliminates common failures like hallucinated references and unverifiable scores while matching or beating expert performance.
AI.motivation: Autonomous research agents can write convincing papers but often include subtle, hard-to-detect verifiability failures such as fabricated citations, unverifiable results, and mismatches between described and implemented methods. There is a need for a principled way to enforce and audit verifiability so that their outputs can be trusted for serious scientific use.
AI.method: They introduce (1) Chain-of-Evidence (CoE), a framework that requires every claim in a paper to be explicitly linked to supporting evidence; (2) ScientistOne, an end-to-end autonomous research system that preserves these evidence chains through literature review, experiment design, solution search, and manuscript writing; and (3) CoE Audit, a post-hoc auditing procedure with four integrity checks—score verification, specification violation detection, reference verification, and method-code alignment—that can be applied to any system’s outputs. They evaluate these on 75 papers generated by five different systems across five challenging research tasks, and further test ScientistOne on six additional tasks.
AI.result: Baseline autonomous research systems all show systematic verifiability failures: up to 21% hallucinated references, score verification passing in as few as 42% of papers, and method-code alignment as low as 20%. In contrast, ScientistOne produces 0 hallucinated references out of 337, passes all 12/12 score verification checks, and attains the best method-code alignment (14/15) while also matching or exceeding human expert performance on all five primary tasks. On six additional tasks (including medical imaging, fine-grained recognition, 3D perception, and language modeling) it reaches state-of-the-art on the Parameter Golf benchmark and wins gold medals on MLE-Bench tasks where other baselines fail.
AI.conclusion: Maintaining explicit chains of evidence throughout the autonomous research pipeline, combined with a standardized post-hoc audit, can largely eliminate common verifiability failures in AI-generated research while preserving or improving research quality. ScientistOne demonstrates that verifiable, state-of-the-art autonomous research is feasible across diverse tasks and domains, setting a new standard for how such systems should be built and evaluated.

title: Cultural Value Alignment Via Latent Activation Steering in Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) often exhibit homogenized cultural perspectives. While the World Values Survey (WVS) provides a gold standard for mapping human values, traditional direct prompting of LLMs on WVS often fails to access the model's latent cultural depth, leading to safety-aligned refusals or neutral responses. Here, we propose a generalizable framework for cultural evaluation and intervention that transitions from abstract queries to scenario-based behavioral probing. By extracting implicit token probabilities across 300 situational dilemmas, we bypass surface-level alignment to map the latent coordinates of LLMs cultural value. We further introduce activation steering to shift these internal alignments during the forward pass without retraining. Across multiple LLMs, we find substantial variation in adaptability and uncover a consistent phenomenon of latent entanglement, where interventions along one cultural dimension induce shifts along another. These results suggest that cultural values are encoded as coupled structures, limiting precise alignment. This work establishes a computationally efficient framework for cultural steering, highlighting the structural complexities when navigating global value with LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework to reveal and edit the latent cultural value structure of LLMs using scenario-based probing and activation steering, showing that cultural dimensions are entangled and alignment is imprecise.
AI.motivation: LLMs often respond with homogenized, neutral, or safety-aligned answers that obscure their underlying cultural value representations. Existing evaluation methods, such as directly prompting with standardized surveys like the World Values Survey (WVS), fail to access these latent value structures because the models avoid taking positions. There is a need for methods that can both measure and adjust the internal cultural value orientations of LLMs in a more faithful and fine-grained way, without expensive retraining.
AI.method: The authors replace abstract, survey-style questions with 300 concrete situational dilemmas that elicit behavioral preferences. Instead of reading explicit answers, they analyze implicit token probability distributions to infer the model’s latent stance on different cultural value dimensions, effectively mapping its cultural coordinates beneath alignment layers. They then apply activation steering: targeted modifications of internal activations during the forward pass to shift the model’s cultural orientation along particular dimensions, all without retraining. They test this protocol across multiple LLMs to examine how easily their cultural values can be steered and how independent different value dimensions are.
AI.result: The framework successfully bypasses surface-level safety alignment and reveals rich, latent cultural value structure in LLMs. Models differ in how adaptable their cultural values are to steering. A key empirical finding is “latent entanglement”: when the model is steered along one cultural dimension, other value dimensions shift as well, indicating that these cultural representations are not independent. This entanglement occurs consistently across models.
AI.conclusion: Cultural values in LLMs are encoded as coupled, entangled structures rather than independent, orthogonal dimensions. This limits the precision with which a single value dimension can be aligned without affecting others. The proposed scenario-based probing plus activation steering offers a computationally efficient way to evaluate and intervene on LLM cultural values, but it also reveals structural constraints that make fine-grained, globally consistent cultural alignment inherently challenging.

title: Automatic Layer Selection for Hallucination Detection
summary: Recent studies on hallucination detection have shown that hallucination-related signals are more strongly encoded in intermediate layers than in the final layer of large language models (LLMs). Although a growing body of work has sought to exploit this property for hallucination detection, how to automate the selection of high-performing layers remains underexplored, and principled methods for this purpose are still lacking. To address this gap, we first propose several hypotheses for why such signals emerge in intermediate layers and evaluate corresponding criteria for automatic layer selection across diverse LLM architectures, scales, and tasks, covering both question answering and summarization hallucination detection benchmarks. However, we find that none of these criteria consistently delivers satisfactory performance. We therefore propose a new selection criterion, First Effective Peak of Intrinsic Dimension (FEPoID), which consistently identify optimal or near-optimal layers and outperforms both the aforementioned criteria and existing hallucination detection baselines. FEPoID is training-free and incurs negligible computational overhead. In addition, we study the generation behaviors of LLMs and introduce a simple yet effective truncation strategy, which further amplifies hallucination-related signals and substantially improves overall detection performance. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/DesoloYw/Automatic-Layer-Selection-for-Hallucination-Detection.git

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an automatic, training-free method to select intermediate layers of large language models that best encode hallucination-related signals, and combines it with a truncation-based generation strategy to significantly improve hallucination detection in both QA and summarization tasks.
AI.motivation: Prior work found that hallucination signals are often stronger in intermediate layers than in the final layer of LLMs, but there is no principled, robust, and automatic way to select which intermediate layers to use for hallucination detection across different models, sizes, and tasks. Existing heuristics do not generalize well and their underlying reasons are not well understood.
AI.method: The authors (1) posit and test several hypotheses about why hallucination-related information appears in intermediate layers, turning these hypotheses into concrete criteria for automatic layer selection; (2) empirically evaluate these criteria across various LLM architectures, scales, and hallucination detection benchmarks for QA and summarization; (3) introduce a new selection criterion called First Effective Peak of Intrinsic Dimension (FEPoID), which identifies the first layer where the intrinsic dimensionality of representations reaches an effective peak; and (4) analyze LLM generation behavior and design a truncation strategy that stops decoding at a specific point to further amplify hallucination-related signals in the chosen layers.
AI.result: They find that previously hypothesized criteria for layer selection do not consistently yield strong hallucination detection performance across models and tasks. In contrast, FEPoID reliably selects optimal or near-optimal layers, surpassing other criteria and state-of-the-art hallucination detection baselines while being training-free and incurring negligible computational cost. The proposed truncation strategy further boosts hallucination signal strength and leads to substantial additional gains in detection metrics on both QA and summarization benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Intermediate layers in LLMs indeed carry rich hallucination-related signals, but selecting the best layers requires a principled criterion. The proposed FEPoID metric offers a robust, training-free way to automatically identify effective layers for hallucination detection across diverse models and tasks, and can be enhanced via a simple truncation strategy during generation. This framework advances practical hallucination detection without retraining or heavy computation.

title: Memory Architectures for Multi-Turn Text-to-SQL: A Benchmark and Empirical Study
comment: 18 pages, 4 figures, 14 tables; includes appendices with verbatim prompts, example session, and full ablation tables; prepared by the LLM Suite Engineering Team, JP Morgan Chase & Co
summary: Multi-turn Text-to-SQL is central to enterprise analytics yet remains predominantly evaluated in single-turn settings. We introduce EnterpriseMem-Bench, a multi-turn Text-to-SQL benchmark of 300 sessions and 1,400 turns built programmatically from three enterprise domains (BIRD financial, SEC EDGAR, Northwind), with deterministic ground truth and per-turn memory-critical annotation. We evaluate five frontier models -- GPT-5 mini, GPT-5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Sonnet 4.6, and Opus 4.6 -- across five memory conditions enabling a three-way ablation isolating working-memory window size, episodic retrieval, and semantic augmentation as independent effects. All Claude models are evaluated with extended thinking enabled to maintain parity with GPT reasoning models. We introduce the Memory Benefit Score (MBS) as a per-turn diagnostic metric.
