Four findings emerge: (1) stateless multi-turn Text-to-SQL collapses to zero execution accuracy by Turn 3 across all five models, even under reasoning; (2) memory-architecture complexity does not monotonically improve accuracy -- working memory dominates, and additional components produce model- and dataset-dependent effects from +14 to -16 percentage points; (3) Claude Sonnet 4.6 underperforms Sonnet 4.5 by 17-33pp on SEC EDGAR across conditions, a generational regression persisting under reasoning; (4) under reasoning, Claude error distributions become mono-modal -- every non-correct turn is a wrong-result error. We release the benchmark, agent, and evaluation code.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EnterpriseMem-Bench, a multi-turn Text-to-SQL benchmark focused on memory and evaluates several large models under different memory conditions, finding that working memory is critical, more complex memory architectures are not always better, and that newer models can regress.
AI.motivation: Existing Text-to-SQL evaluations are mostly single-turn, which does not reflect realistic enterprise analytics workflows that involve multi-turn dialogues and require memory over interaction history. There is a need for a controlled, deterministic benchmark to diagnose how different memory architectures affect model performance in multi-turn Text-to-SQL, and to understand failure modes and potential regressions of new model generations.
AI.method: The authors programmatically construct a benchmark of 300 multi-turn sessions (1,400 turns) spanning three enterprise-like databases (BIRD financial, SEC EDGAR, Northwind). Each turn has deterministic ground-truth SQL and annotations indicating whether access to past turns is memory-critical. They design five memory conditions that independently vary (a) working-memory window size, (b) episodic retrieval of past context, and (c) semantic augmentation, enabling a three-way ablation. Five state-of-the-art LLMs (two GPT-5 variants and three Claude Sonnet variants) are evaluated on execution accuracy per turn, with Claude models run in an extended-thinking mode comparable to GPT’s reasoning mode. They introduce Memory Benefit Score (MBS) as a per-turn metric quantifying the benefit from added memory. Error distributions are analyzed by type.
AI.result: The study shows that without statefulness, all examined models’ execution accuracy drops to near zero by the third turn, even with reasoning enabled. Increasing memory-architecture complexity does not consistently improve performance; larger or more elaborate memory systems sometimes help and sometimes hurt, with effects ranging from +14 to -16 percentage points depending on model and dataset. Working memory (context window) is found to be the dominant factor in performance. Surprisingly, Claude Sonnet 4.6 performs 17–33 percentage points worse than Sonnet 4.5 on SEC EDGAR across all memory conditions, demonstrating a significant generational regression that persists when reasoning is enabled. Under reasoning, Claude models’ errors become mono-modal: non-correct turns are overwhelmingly “wrong-result” rather than other error types.
AI.conclusion: EnterpriseMem-Bench offers a controlled, deterministic environment to systematically study memory in multi-turn Text-to-SQL, revealing that (1) stateless use of LLMs is inadequate for realistic multi-turn querying, (2) simply adding complex memory modules is not a guaranteed path to better performance, as working memory is the main driver of gains, and (3) model updates can regress substantially in specific domains. The proposed Memory Benefit Score provides a fine-grained diagnostic of memory utility. The authors release benchmark, agent, and evaluation code to support further research on memory architectures for enterprise analytics.

title: Exploiting Local Dynamics Regularity for Reusable Skills in Offline Hierarchical RL
summary: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) promises to solve long-horizon Reinforcement Learning (RL) tasks more efficiently than non-hierarchical counterparts by discovering and reusing temporally-extended skills. However, obtaining skills that are actually reusable remains an open challenge. Towards this end, we focus on abstractions that exploit the intuition of local dynamics: local transitions in different global contexts require similar kinds of action sequences. By aligning these contexts with the action sequences they require, we are able to learn which skills to reuse and where to reuse them. In principle, this information should benefit many HRL algorithms, where high-level policies have to reason about the low-level skills they use. The resulting algorithm CARL (Contrastive Action-based Representations for Reusable Local Control) shows both qualitative clustering of meaningful skills in complex humanoid environments and improved downstream performance on the OGBench benchmark when integrated with HIQL.

AI.tldr: CARL proposes a contrastive representation-learning method to discover reusable, temporally-extended skills for hierarchical RL, leading to better long-horizon control.
AI.motivation: Hierarchical RL should in principle handle long-horizon control more efficiently by discovering skills, but existing methods struggle to learn skills that transfer and are truly reusable across different parts of a task or across tasks. The authors leverage the observation that locally similar dynamics may require similar action sequences even when the global context differs, and want to exploit this for better skill discovery and reuse.
AI.method: They learn action-conditioned representations that align similar local transition contexts (state changes) with the similar action sequences that produce them, using a contrastive learning objective. These representations induce clusters that correspond to reusable local control skills. CARL can then plug into existing HRL frameworks, informing the high-level policy about which low-level skill to invoke where. They demonstrate this by integrating CARL with the HIQL hierarchical RL algorithm.
AI.result: In complex humanoid control environments, the learned representations cluster into semantically meaningful skills, as shown qualitatively. Quantitatively, when used within HIQL, CARL improves performance on the OGBench benchmark compared to baselines without this contrastive skill representation.
AI.conclusion: By exploiting local dynamics and contrastive learning, CARL discovers representations of reusable local control skills that integrate cleanly with hierarchical RL algorithms and improve performance on long-horizon benchmarks, providing evidence that better skill abstractions can make HRL more effective in practice.

title: Annotator Positionality as Signal: Psychometric Weighting for Anti-Autistic Ableism Detection
comment: main paper: 8 pages; total: 18 pages; 2 figures
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in decision-making tasks where they can amplify or suppress perspectives, raising concerns in high-stakes settings affecting autistic communities. While previous research has identified disability-related biases in LLMs, it remains unclear how they conceptualize ableism or detect it in text. We introduce a bias-aware evaluation framework targeting anti-autistic ableist language with a psychometrically-weighted, community-proximate ground truth anchored in annotator positionality. This framework constitutes a stricter standard than conventional majority-vote aggregation which significantly and consistently underweights autistic and autism-accepting perspectives. We find that LLMs frequently produce harmful outputs, mislabel community-reclaimed language as ableist, and express more negative attitudes toward autistic people when assessment instruments are masked. Our error analysis reveals that models rely on surface-level keyword matching rather than contextual factors such as speaker identity, and whether the language fosters in-group solidarity or inflicts out-group harm.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how large language models handle anti-autistic ableist language using a bias-aware, community-grounded framework, revealing that models often misidentify reclaimed terms as harmful and rely on shallow keyword cues instead of social context.
AI.motivation: To understand and rigorously evaluate how LLMs conceptualize and detect ableism, specifically anti-autistic ableist language, in order to assess and mitigate the risks these models pose to autistic communities in high-stakes decision-making contexts.
AI.method: The authors design a bias-aware evaluation framework focused on anti-autistic ableist language. They build a ground truth that is psychometrically weighted and aligned with autistic and autism-accepting community perspectives, explicitly modeling annotator positionality instead of using simple majority-vote labels. They then test LLMs on this benchmark and conduct error analysis of model outputs and classifications.
AI.result: Using the new framework, they find that LLMs often generate harmful content, incorrectly flag community-reclaimed language as ableist, and show more negative attitudes toward autistic people when diagnostic assessment cues are hidden. Error analysis shows that models primarily rely on surface-level keyword matching and fail to account for contextual features like speaker identity or whether the language promotes in-group solidarity versus out-group harm.
AI.conclusion: Conventional evaluation and aggregation methods underestimate ableist harms toward autistic people and systematically downweight autistic and autism-accepting perspectives. The proposed bias-aware, community-proximate framework reveals that current LLMs inadequately handle nuanced anti-autistic ableism, misclassify reclaimed language, and depend on shallow lexical cues rather than social context, highlighting the need for more context-sensitive and community-grounded model evaluation and development.

title: Advancing Creative Physical Intelligence in Large Multimodal Models
comment: 51 Pages, 9 Figures, 7 Tables, Previous Work CreativityBench: arXiv:2605.02910
summary: Large multimodal models (LMMs) have rapidly advanced in perception and reasoning; however, it remains unclear whether these capabilities generalize to discovering visually grounded solutions in open-ended environments, beyond pattern recognition. In such settings, intelligence requires more than answering well-posed questions: it involves identifying how elements in a scene can be repurposed in non-obvious yet physically feasible ways. This form of creative problem-solving is central to human intelligence, but remains largely untested in current benchmarks. To evaluate this ability, we introduce MM-CreativityBench, a benchmark for affordance-grounded creative tool use in visually rich, physically constrained environments. Each instance presents a scenario image with structured views of candidate entities and their parts, enabling fine-grained, interactive evaluation of how models iteratively inspect the scene, identify relevant affordances, and compose visually and physically grounded solutions. Our experiments show that current LMMs often fall short, not due to lack of generative capability, but because they do not sustain grounded exploration. Models often overlook relevant entities, under-examine critical parts, or hallucinate attributes not grounded in the image. Motivated by this failure mode, we propose affordance-grounded alignment, which casts creative tool use as a preference learning problem. Using Direct Preference Optimization, we encourage models to prefer attribute-affordance reasoning grounded in visual evidence over hallucinated alternatives. In addition, we incorporate supervision derived from an affordance knowledge base to guide broader entity exploration and multi-turn planning. Our results show consistent gains in selecting the correct entities and parts, while substantially reducing hallucination and grounding-related errors.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MM-CreativityBench to test whether large multimodal models can creatively use visual affordances in complex scenes, and proposes an affordance-grounded alignment method using preference optimization and an affordance knowledge base to reduce hallucinations and improve grounded, multi-step tool-use reasoning.
AI.motivation: While large multimodal models perform well on perception and structured reasoning tasks, it is unclear if they can engage in human-like creative problem-solving that requires discovering non-obvious but physically feasible uses of objects in open-ended visual environments. Existing benchmarks mostly test pattern recognition or answering well-posed questions, not the ability to explore a scene, identify actionable affordances, and repurpose objects as tools under physical constraints. There is therefore a need for a benchmark and methods that specifically target affordance-grounded creativity and reduce hallucinations in such settings.
AI.method: The authors design MM-CreativityBench, a benchmark containing visually rich, physically constrained scenarios where models must creatively select and compose entities and their parts as tools to solve problems. Each instance provides a main scenario image plus structured views of candidate objects and their parts, allowing fine-grained, interactive evaluation of how models inspect, reason about, and reuse these elements. They analyze existing LMMs and identify a key failure mode: lack of sustained, grounded exploration leading to missed entities, under-examined parts, and hallucinated attributes. To address this, they formulate creative tool use as a preference learning problem and introduce affordance-grounded alignment. Using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), they train models to prefer solutions that base attribute–affordance reasoning on visual evidence rather than hallucinations, and they incorporate supervision from an affordance knowledge base to nudge models toward broader entity exploration and multi-turn planning.
AI.result: On MM-CreativityBench, current LMMs show significant shortcomings in creative, visually grounded problem-solving, frequently failing to explore the scene thoroughly or hallucinating non-existent properties. After applying affordance-grounded alignment with DPO and affordance-knowledge supervision, models achieve consistent improvements in selecting the appropriate entities and parts to use as tools. They also exhibit fewer hallucinations and make fewer grounding-related errors across multiple tasks and turns of interaction, demonstrating better alignment between visual evidence, affordance reasoning, and generated solutions.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that existing LMMs, despite strong perceptual and generative skills, struggle with affordance-grounded creative tool use in open-ended visual environments because they do not maintain systematic, grounded exploration. MM-CreativityBench provides a targeted benchmark to expose these limitations. The proposed affordance-grounded alignment framework, combining DPO-based preference learning with affordance knowledge supervision, substantially improves models’ ability to ground their creative reasoning in visual evidence, select appropriate entities and parts, and reduce hallucinations. This suggests that aligning models around affordance-centric exploration and evidence-based preferences is a promising direction for advancing multimodal creative problem-solving.

title: Towards Just-in-Time Adaptive Feedback: Enhancing Student Learning via Knowledge-Grounded LLM
comment: 8 pages, Accepted to 21st Workshop on Innovative Use of NLP for Building Educational Applications (BEA 2026)
summary: Educational interventions are effective tools for enhancing student learning. While Large Language Models (LLMs) allow for generating adaptive feedback at scale, current studies lack clear methodologies for providing Just-in-Time (JiT) feedback in authentic instructional settings. In this paper, we present a framework that provides adaptive feedback by grounding LLMs with domain-specific expert knowledge. Our approach collects written reasoning logic (strategy essays) from students, analyzes potential error types based on the content of that reasoning, and delivers non-intrusive feedback designed to clarify missing or incorrect concepts. We deploy this framework in a large-scale university course (N > 1000), where it improved student performance by over 80% compared to previous semesters. Lastly, we validate the framework's pedagogical utility by analyzing the learning trajectories; we demonstrate how iterative conversations with LLM facilitate shifting one's misconception to correct understanding.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework that uses LLMs grounded in expert knowledge to provide just-in-time, adaptive feedback on students’ written reasoning, significantly improving performance in a large university course.
AI.motivation: Educational interventions improve learning, but there is a methodological gap in how to deliver Just-in-Time feedback using LLMs in real, large-scale instructional settings. Existing work does not clearly show how to ground LLM-generated feedback in domain expertise or how to embed it seamlessly in courses.
AI.method: The authors build a framework that: (1) collects students’ written strategy essays capturing their reasoning process; (2) analyzes these essays to detect error types and misconceptions using LLMs grounded in domain-specific expert knowledge; and (3) generates non-intrusive, adaptive feedback aimed at clarifying missing or incorrect concepts. They deploy this system in a large-enrollment university course and track learning trajectories through iterative LLM–student interactions.
AI.result: In a deployment with over 1000 university students, the framework led to an improvement in student performance of more than 80% compared to previous semesters without the system. Analysis of interaction logs and learning trajectories shows students progressively corrected misconceptions through repeated LLM-guided feedback.
AI.conclusion: Grounding LLMs with expert domain knowledge enables effective, scalable, Just-in-Time feedback on students’ reasoning in authentic course settings. The framework not only substantially boosts performance at scale but also supports conceptual change, as evidenced by students’ shifts from misconceptions to correct understanding over iterative feedback cycles.

title: From Static Context to Calibrated Interactive RL: Mitigating Distribution Shift in Multi-turn Dialogue with Aligned Simulator
summary: A long-standing goal of the research community is to develop highly interactive LLM-based dialogue agents. Recent research focuses on optimizing policies based on fixed offline logs (Static Context RL) or using a prompt-based simulator (Interactive RL). In this work, we theoretically show that both paradigms are fundamentally limited by context distribution shift--a mismatch between dialogue histories observed during training and those encountered in real conversations. This shift compounds quadratically over turns and severely degrades dialogue quality. Specifically, we attribute this shift to two distinct sources: (i) policy-induced shift, arising from training on static histories rather than self-generated trajectories; and (ii) simulator-induced shift, stemming from discrepancies between simulated and real human behaviors. To address these challenges, we propose Calibrated Interactive RL, a unified framework that couples interactive RL with simulator alignment. By aligning the simulator with human interaction patterns, our approach reduces the sim-to-real gap and mitigates compounding distribution shifts. Experiments across multiple dialogue tasks confirm our theoretical analysis: (i) Interactive RL significantly outperforms the Static Context baseline by mitigating policy distribution shift; and (ii) calibrating simulators with our alignment method further bridges the sim-to-real gap, yielding state-of-the-art downstream performance.

AI.tldr: They identify and theoretically analyze a key failure mode in training LLM dialogue agents—context distribution shift—and propose a calibrated interactive RL framework that aligns simulators with real users to mitigate this, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Interactive LLM agents need to perform well in real, multi-turn conversations, but current reinforcement learning approaches either rely on static logs or imperfect simulators. Both lead to a mismatch between training and deployment dialogue histories (context distribution shift), which worsens over multiple turns and degrades quality. The authors want to formally characterize this problem and design a method that remains robust in real user interactions.
AI.method: They provide a theoretical analysis showing that context distribution shift grows roughly quadratically with dialogue length and degrades performance. They decompose the shift into policy-induced shift (from training on fixed histories vs self-generated ones) and simulator-induced shift (from differences between simulated and real users). Building on this, they introduce Calibrated Interactive RL: a unified RL framework that (1) uses interactive RL to reduce policy-induced shift by training on self-generated trajectories, and (2) explicitly aligns/calibrates the simulator to match human interaction patterns, shrinking the sim-to-real gap. The simulator calibration is integrated into the training loop so that the agent and simulator co-adapt toward realistic dialogues.
AI.result: Across several dialogue benchmarks, Interactive RL (without calibration) already outperforms Static Context RL, validating that training on interactive trajectories reduces policy-induced shift. Adding simulator calibration further improves performance and better transfers to real-user evaluations, narrowing the performance gap between simulated and real interactions and setting new state-of-the-art results on these tasks.
AI.conclusion: Static logs and uncalibrated simulators both suffer from compounding context distribution shift, fundamentally limiting dialogue agent quality, especially in long conversations. By analyzing the two main sources of this shift and introducing Calibrated Interactive RL to jointly perform interactive RL and simulator alignment, the authors substantially improve robustness and real-world performance of LLM dialogue agents, demonstrating that carefully calibrated simulators are key for scalable interactive RL training.

title: Probing Minimalist Phase Structure in LLMs: What Universal Dependencies Cannot Represent
summary:
Structural probes train on Universal Dependencies (UD), which does not encode formal-syntactic abstractions such as phase boundaries or phase-internal cohesion. Whether large language models (LLMs) encode these remains an open question that UD-based probing cannot answer by construction. We evaluate structural probes on wh-movement stimuli where UD distances are invariant across conditions by design -- any non-zero effect therefore reflects structure beyond UD. The three conditions -- bare small clause, infinitival, and finite -- are ordered by the number of Minimalist Program (MP) phase boundaries the wh-element crosses.
  Across 13 LLMs from four families, we find a phase-count gradient on a cross-clause pair (12/13 models) and a 13/13 sign asymmetry on a within-clause pair whose UD distance is identical across conditions -- the latter specifically predicted by phase-internal cohesion, an MP abstraction invisible to UD by construction. Activation patching confirms the representations are causally active in 12/13 models. These findings suggest that distributional pretraining can induce representations aligned with formal-syntactic abstractions beyond the reach of annotation-based probing; UD-grounded probes provide a lower bound on syntactic encoding, not an upper bound.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether large language models encode advanced syntactic structures (from the Minimalist Program) that are not captured by Universal Dependencies, showing that models do encode such structure and that UD-based probes underestimate their syntactic knowledge.
AI.motivation: Most structural probing work relies on Universal Dependencies (UD), which captures surface syntactic relations but not deeper formal-syntactic concepts like phase boundaries and phase-internal cohesion from the Minimalist Program. This makes it impossible, by design, to use UD-based probes to test whether LLMs encode these deeper abstractions. The authors want to know whether distributional training alone can lead LLMs to internalize such formal structures and to quantify how much UD-based probing underestimates syntactic encoding in LLMs.
AI.method: The authors construct wh-movement stimuli in which Universal Dependencies (UD) distances are held constant across conditions, so any differences detected by structural probes must reflect structure beyond UD. They design three sentence types—bare small clause, infinitival, and finite—that differ in the number of Minimalist Program (MP) phase boundaries crossed by the wh-element. They apply structural probes to internal representations of 13 LLMs from four model families and examine probe performance across these conditions. They also use activation patching to test whether the probed representations are causally implicated in the models’ behavior, rather than being epiphenomenal features.
AI.result: Across 13 LLMs, they find a robust phase-count gradient on a cross-clause comparison in 12 out of 13 models, indicating that model representations track how many MP phase boundaries the wh-element crosses. For a within-clause comparison where UD distance is exactly the same across conditions, all 13 models show a consistent sign asymmetry predicted by phase-internal cohesion—an abstraction posited in the Minimalist Program but absent from UD. Activation patching further shows that in 12 of 13 models these abstract syntactic representations are causally involved in the model’s processing, not just correlated artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that large language models can and do encode formal-syntactic abstractions, such as phase boundaries and phase-internal cohesion, that go beyond what is represented in Universal Dependencies. This implies that UD-based structural probing only establishes a lower bound on the syntactic information encoded by LLMs, not an upper bound. Distributional pretraining alone is sufficient to induce representations aligned with sophisticated theoretical constructs from the Minimalist Program, challenging the view that neural models only capture shallow or surface-level syntax.

title: The MiniMax-M2 Series: Mini Activations Unleashing Max Real-World Intelligence
summary: We introduce the MiniMax-M2 series, a family of Mixture-of-Experts language models built around the principle that mini activations can unleash maximum real-world intelligence. The flagship M2 contains 229.9B total parameters with only 9.8B activated per token. Designed end-to-end for agentic deployment, the M2 series rests on three components: (i) agent-driven data pipelines producing large-scale, verifiable trajectories across agentic coding and agentic cowork, each grounded in an executable workspace and an artifact-aligned reward; (ii) Forge, a scalable agent-native RL system that adapts to long-horizon agent trajectories, paired with windowed-FIFO scheduling, prefix-tree merging, inference optimization, and a clean training-inference-agent decoupling that supports both white-box and black-box agents; (iii) the latest M2.7 checkpoint takes an early step toward self-evolution -- autonomously debugging training runs and modifying its own scaffold. Across M2 through M2.7, this combination translates a mini-activation footprint into frontier-tier performance on agentic coding, deep search, office-task, and reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: MiniMax-M2 is a Mixture-of-Experts language model family with very large total parameters but small active parameters per token, optimized for agentic tasks.
AI.motivation: To achieve frontier-level performance in real-world, long-horizon agentic tasks (coding, cowork, reasoning) while keeping the per-token compute (activations) small and efficient.
AI.method: They build Mixture-of-Experts models (e.g., M2 with 229.9B params, 9.8B active per token) and pair them with: (i) agent-driven data pipelines generating verifiable trajectories in executable workspaces with reward signals; (ii) Forge, an RL system tailored to agent-native, long-horizon trajectories with scheduling, prefix-tree merging, and training/inference/agent decoupling for both white- and black-box agents; (iii) a self-evolving checkpoint (M2.7) that can autonomously debug training and modify its own agentic scaffold.
AI.result: The M2 through M2.7 models achieve frontier-tier performance on benchmarks for agentic coding, deep search, office tasks, and reasoning, demonstrating that small activation footprints can deliver strong real-world agent performance.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed MoE architecture with agent-native data and RL infrastructure, plus early self-evolution capabilities, can turn mini per-token activations into high real-world intelligence, enabling efficient and powerful agentic language models.

title: Vectors Are Not Neutral: Sensitive-Information Inference from Exported LLM Representations in Summarization
summary: Large language model (LLM) summarization systems may pass compact vector representations of private inputs to downstream retrieval, monitoring, audit, or analytic workflows. Even when source documents remain access-restricted, derived vectors may be handled under different access controls and still support sensitive-information inference, creating a residual information-disclosure risk. We study this issue in clinical discharge-summary generation as a high-stakes case study, using electronic health record (EHR)-recorded race as a controlled sensitive-label audit. We audit two artifacts that a system might retain or expose to downstream components: the final prompt-token hidden state and the mean-pooled prompt representation. Our results show that reducing recoverability of the case-study sensitive label from one exported artifact does not necessarily reduce recoverability from another. As a mitigation case study, we introduce SurfaceLoRA, an exported-vector-targeted parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that uses a gradient-reversal discriminator attached to a designated exported vector. Under a balanced five-way probing protocol, SurfaceLoRA reduces EHR-recorded race recoverability from the targeted final-token artifact toward chance while preserving summarization utility, yet recoverability remains substantially higher from untargeted pooled artifacts. These findings show that privacy auditing and mitigation should be performed on the exact vector artifact retained or exposed to downstream components.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates residual privacy risks when vector representations from LLM-based clinical summarization systems are exposed downstream, showing that sensitive attributes (like EHR-recorded race) can still be inferred, and proposes a mitigation method (SurfaceLoRA) that reduces such leakage for specific exported vectors but not universally.
AI.motivation: To understand and mitigate privacy risks in LLM summarization pipelines, particularly in healthcare, where internal vector representations (even without raw text) can leak sensitive information such as patient race to downstream systems with different access controls.
AI.method: Use clinical discharge-summary generation as a case study and treat EHR-recorded race as a controlled sensitive label. Audit two common exported artifacts—the final prompt-token hidden state and the mean-pooled prompt representation—by training probing models to recover race. Then introduce SurfaceLoRA, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach with a gradient-reversal discriminator attached to a chosen exported vector, and evaluate how it affects sensitive-attribute recoverability and summarization quality under a balanced five-way probing protocol.
AI.result: They find that reducing the ability to infer race from one exported artifact (e.g., the final-token state) does not guarantee reduced inference from another (e.g., mean-pooled representation). SurfaceLoRA succeeds in substantially lowering race recoverability from the targeted final-token artifact toward chance while largely preserving summarization performance; however, race remains much more recoverable from untargeted pooled representations.
AI.conclusion: Privacy risks in LLM-based systems depend on the exact vector artifacts that are stored or exposed, and mitigation strategies must be audited and applied on those specific artifacts rather than assuming global protection. SurfaceLoRA is a promising targeted mitigation for a given exported vector, but it does not eliminate leakage from other representations, highlighting the need for comprehensive, artifact-specific privacy auditing and defenses.

title: Targeted Remasking: Replacing Token Editing with Token-to-Mask Refinement in Discrete Diffusion Language Models
summary: Discrete masked diffusion language models such as LLaDA generate text through iterative denoising, where mask tokens are progressively replaced with predicted tokens. LLaDA2.1 introduced a Token-to-Token (T2T) editing mechanism that accelerates generation by directly replacing committed tokens suspected of being incorrect. However, we identify fundamental limitations of T2T editing: it couples error detection with replacement, pollutes the generation context with potentially incorrect tokens, and introduces a train-inference noise mismatch where systematic model-generated errors differ from the random perturbations seen during training. We propose Token-to-Mask (T2M) remasking, a training-free, drop-in replacement for T2T editing that resets suspected erroneous tokens back to the mask state, allowing the diffusion process to re-predict them under cleaner context. We design and empirically validate three complementary error detection strategies -- probability-based, trigger-mirrored, and temporal-difference-based -- and provide a unified theoretical analysis showing that T2M remasking purifies the generation context, converts systematic inference errors back to the model's native mask noise type, and enables delayed commitment for joint multi-position optimization. Comprehensive experiments across 12 benchmarks spanning knowledge, reasoning, mathematics, coding, and instruction following show that T2M generally improves performance on tasks requiring precise token-level output, with the largest gain on mathematics (+5.92% on CMATH). Error analysis on CMATH reveals that the dominant failure mode is last-mile token corruption -- where correct reasoning produces a corrupted final answer -- and that T2M repairs 59.4% of such cases.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Token-to-Mask (T2M) remasking, a training-free replacement for Token-to-Token (T2T) editing in discrete diffusion language models, which improves text generation—especially in math—by remasking suspected bad tokens instead of directly overwriting them.
AI.motivation: Existing discrete masked diffusion LMs like LLaDA use an iterative denoising process with masks. LLaDA2.1’s Token-to-Token (T2T) editing tries to speed up and improve generation by directly replacing previously fixed tokens deemed erroneous. The authors find that T2T has structural drawbacks: it entangles error detection with replacement actions, contaminates the context with incorrect tokens that then influence future predictions, and creates a mismatch between training (random noise) and inference (systematic model errors). These limitations motivate a cleaner mechanism that can detect and handle errors without introducing such mismatches, while remaining compatible and training-free.
AI.method: They introduce Token-to-Mask (T2M) remasking, which, instead of replacing a suspected erroneous committed token with another token, resets it back to a mask token so that the diffusion process can re-predict it using a cleaner context. T2M is designed as a drop-in, training-free replacement for T2T in discrete diffusion LMs. To decide which tokens to remask, they propose three complementary error detection strategies: (1) probability-based detection using token likelihoods; (2) trigger-mirrored detection that mirrors the model’s own editing triggers; and (3) temporal-difference-based detection that looks at changes over time between iterative predictions. They then provide a unified theoretical analysis showing that T2M purifies the context, converts systematic inference errors back into the same type of mask noise used at training time, and allows delayed commitment so that multiple positions can be jointly optimized across steps.
AI.result: Across 12 benchmarks covering knowledge, reasoning, mathematics, coding, and instruction following, T2M generally improves performance, with the strongest gains in tasks requiring precise token-level outputs. The most notable improvement is in mathematics, achieving a +5.92% absolute performance increase on the CMATH benchmark. A detailed error analysis on CMATH indicates that the primary failure pattern is “last-mile token corruption,” where the reasoning path is correct but the final answer token is wrong. T2M is shown to repair 59.4% of these last-mile corruption cases.
AI.conclusion: T2M remasking is an effective, training-free alternative to T2T editing for discrete masked diffusion language models. By remasking instead of directly replacing suspected bad tokens, it aligns inference-time noise with training-time mask noise, cleans up the generative context, and supports better joint optimization over token sequences. The method yields consistent improvements, especially on mathematically precise tasks, and significantly mitigates last-mile token corruption errors, demonstrating its practical value for precise token-level generation.

title: PolyFusionAgent: A Multimodal Foundation Model and Autonomous AI Assistant for Polymer Property Prediction and Inverse Design
comment: 23 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables; Supplementary material included
summary: Polymer discovery is central to fields ranging from energy storage to biomedicine, but it is hindered by an astronomically large chemical design space and fragmented representations of structure, properties, and prior knowledge. This fragmentation leaves many AI models disconnected from physical and experimental reality, restricting their ability to support directly actionable design decisions. Here we introduce PolyFusionAgent, an interactive framework coupling a multimodal polymer foundation model (PolyFusion) with a tool-augmented, literature-grounded design agent (PolyAgent). PolyFusion aligns complementary polymer views including sequence, topology, 3D geometry, and fingerprints across millions of polymers to learn a shared latent space transferable across chemistries and data regimes, improving thermophysical property prediction and enabling property-conditioned generation of chemically valid, structurally novel polymers beyond the reference design space. PolyAgent closes the design loop by linking prediction and inverse design with evidence retrieval from the polymer literature, proposing, evaluating, and contextualizing hypotheses with explicit precedent in one workflow. Together, PolyFusionAgent enables interactive, evidence-linked polymer discovery combining large-scale representation learning, multimodal chemical knowledge, and verifiable scientific reasoning.

AI.tldr: Introduces PolyFusionAgent, an interactive AI framework for polymer discovery that combines a multimodal polymer foundation model with a literature-grounded design agent.
AI.motivation: Polymer discovery is constrained by the enormous chemical design space and fragmented representations of polymers (sequence, topology, geometry, fingerprints, properties, and literature knowledge). Existing AI models often ignore parts of this information and are weakly connected to physical and experimental reality, limiting their usefulness for real design decisions.
AI.method: Develop PolyFusion, a multimodal foundation model that aligns multiple representations of polymers (sequence, topology, 3D geometry, fingerprints) into a shared latent space using millions of polymer examples, and combine it with PolyAgent, a tool-augmented, literature-grounded design agent. PolyAgent links property prediction and inverse design with automated evidence retrieval from the polymer literature, allowing it to propose, evaluate, and contextualize design hypotheses in one loop.
AI.result: The shared latent space learned by PolyFusion is transferable across different chemistries and data regimes, improving thermophysical property prediction and enabling property-conditioned generation of chemically valid, structurally novel polymers that go beyond the original reference design space. PolyAgent can automatically retrieve and integrate relevant literature evidence to support and contextualize design suggestions.
AI.conclusion: PolyFusionAgent provides an interactive, evidence-linked platform for polymer discovery that unifies large-scale multimodal representation learning with literature-grounded reasoning, thereby closing the loop between prediction, inverse design, and scientific evidence to make polymer design more actionable and reliable.

title: LURE: Live-Usage Replay Evaluations for Reducing Evaluation Awareness
summary: Large language models can recognize when they are being evaluated (evaluation awareness) and behave differently because of that, which undermines the validity of safety and alignment benchmarks. We propose LURE (Live-Usage Replay Evaluations), a method for constructing deployment-like evaluations by replaying realistic agentic interaction trajectories and appending evaluation prompt at the end. We also introduce an automated pipeline for measuring evaluation realism, combining detection of verbalized evaluation awareness and judge-model estimates of the probability of logs being an evaluation, and validate it on a large dataset of deployment and evaluation transcripts. We find that LURE-based evaluations are substantially less distinguishable from deployment than widely used benchmarks and synthetic evaluation generators, and can approach the realism of real conversations with users. We instantiate LURE in scheming, AI safety sabotage, and sycophancy settings. Our results suggest that evaluation realism is a crucial property of alignment benchmarks and should be reported alongside benchmark results, especially when such results are used in safety cases.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that large language models can detect when they are under evaluation and thus behave unnaturally, threatening the reliability of safety benchmarks. It proposes LURE, a method that replays realistic usage logs and appends evaluation prompts, and shows this yields evaluations that look much more like genuine deployment than standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing safety and alignment benchmarks for large language models may be systematically biased because models often recognize benchmark-like prompts and adjust their behavior, e.g., by being extra safe or cautious. This "evaluation awareness" means benchmark scores may overstate real-world safety. The authors want a way to build evaluations that closely resemble real deployment settings so that measured safety better reflects actual behavior in the wild, and they want a principled way to quantify how realistic an evaluation is.
AI.method: They introduce LURE (Live-Usage Replay Evaluations), which constructs evaluation prompts by taking realistic agentic interaction trajectories (e.g., from real user-model conversations or deployment logs) and appending a final evaluation question. They also build an automated realism-measurement pipeline: (1) detecting explicit verbalized evaluation awareness in transcripts, and (2) using a judge model to estimate the probability that a given log is part of an evaluation rather than real usage. They validate this pipeline on a large dataset of both deployment and evaluation transcripts and compare LURE-based evaluations to existing benchmarks and synthetic evaluation generators.
AI.result: Empirically, LURE-based evaluations are significantly harder to distinguish from real deployment interactions than standard benchmarks and common synthetic generators. According to their automated realism metrics, LURE transcripts approach the realism of authentic user conversations, while current benchmarks look obviously evaluative. They demonstrate this in several safety-relevant contexts: scheming behavior, AI safety sabotage, and sycophancy, showing that LURE can be instantiated for diverse alignment concerns.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation realism—the degree to which an evaluation resembles actual deployment—is a key, previously underreported property of alignment benchmarks. LURE offers a practical way to improve realism and to better approximate in-the-wild safety behavior of models. The authors argue that future safety benchmark reports should include realism measurements, particularly when benchmarks are cited as evidence in safety cases or deployment decisions.

title: MobileExplorer: Accelerating On-Device Inference for Mobile GUI Agents via Online Exploration
summary: Mobile graphical user interface (GUI) agents enable AI models to autonomously operate smartphones on behalf of users. However, most existing systems focus primarily on optimizing task accuracy and rely on cloud-hosted models for inference, which introduces privacy concerns and network-dependent latency. As a result, fully on-device deployment of mobile GUI agents remains underexplored. We propose MobileExplorer, a new framework that accelerates on-device inference for vision-based mobile GUI agents via online exploration. The key idea is to exploit the long per-step reasoning time of vision-language models (VLMs) by performing lightweight, parallel exploration of UI elements. During model inference, the agent proactively probes semantically relevant UI elements and records these exploration traces as structured memory. To ensure reliable execution in live mobile environments, we design a two-level rollback mechanism that robustly restores the initial UI state when a fast but naive backtracking strategy fails. The collected exploration traces are then summarized into concise contextual hints and injected into the prompt to enhance the subsequent reasoning step. We evaluate MobileExplorer on multiple off-the-shelf devices using the AndroidWorld benchmark, as well as newly designed, more complex tasks and dynamic on-device environments. MobileExplorer reduces the average number of reasoning steps and end-to-end latency by 23\%, while maintaining or improving task success rates by up to 5\%. A video demonstration of MobileExplorer performance in the real world is available at https://youtu.be/thK7MJmdlvM .

AI.tldr: MobileExplorer is an on-device framework for mobile GUI agents that overlaps VLM reasoning time with lightweight UI exploration to cut latency and steps while keeping or improving task success.
AI.motivation: Existing mobile GUI agents mostly run large models in the cloud, causing privacy issues and network latency, and they optimize for task accuracy rather than on-device efficiency. Fully on-device agents are underexplored, especially for vision-based systems that require heavy per-step reasoning.
AI.method: MobileExplorer runs vision-language model inference on-device and, during each reasoning step, performs parallel, lightweight probing of semantically relevant UI elements. It logs these interactions as structured exploration traces. A two-level rollback mechanism restores the UI if naïve backtracking fails. The exploration traces are summarized into contextual hints that are fed back into the prompt to guide the next reasoning step.
AI.result: On AndroidWorld and additional, more complex and dynamic on-device tasks, MobileExplorer reduces average reasoning steps and end-to-end latency by about 23% while maintaining or improving task success rates by up to 5%, across several off-the-shelf Android devices.
AI.conclusion: Online exploration of UI elements during VLM reasoning, plus robust rollback and trace summarization, can make fully on-device mobile GUI agents significantly faster without sacrificing reliability, demonstrating that high-performance, privacy-preserving on-device deployment is feasible.

title: Conv-to-Bench: Evaluating Language Models Via User-Assistant Dialogues In Code Tasks
summary: The rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has outpaced the scalability of traditional evaluation benchmarks, which remain heavily dependent on labor-intensive expert curation. We address this bottleneck with Conv-to-Bench, a multi-stage framework that automatically transforms authentic multi-turn user-assistant dialogues into structured, verifiable requirement checklists. By leveraging the "instructional evolution" found in real-world conversational logs, our approach deconstructs fragmented user intent into consolidated instructions and binary evaluation criteria. Applied to the programming domain, Conv-to-Bench produces evaluation sets that demonstrate near-perfect alignment with human-authored standards like BigCodeBench, achieving Spearman correlations of up to $ρ$ = 1.000 with significantly lower computational overhead. Validation of the LLM-as-a-judge framework further confirms its reliability, with the primary evaluator achieving substantial agreement with human-verified ground truth ($κ$ = 0.705). Our comprehensive ablation studies reveal that while multi-turn interactions capture the iterative evolution of user intent, instruction-centric extraction provides a more robust foundation. Ultimately, Conv-to-Bench provides a scalable, cost-effective paradigm for maintaining high-fidelity evaluation standards as user-centric AI applications continue to diversify.

AI.tldr: Conv-to-Bench is an automatic framework that converts real multi-turn user–assistant conversations into structured, checklist-style benchmarks for evaluating LLMs, achieving human-level reliability at low cost.
AI.motivation: Traditional LLM benchmarks rely on static, manually curated datasets that are slow and expensive to update, especially as LLM capabilities and user needs rapidly evolve. There is a need for scalable, high-fidelity evaluation suites that reflect authentic user interactions and can be refreshed continuously without heavy expert effort.
AI.method: The paper proposes Conv-to-Bench, a multi-stage pipeline that takes authentic multi-turn user–assistant dialogues and (1) analyzes the instructional evolution in the conversation, (2) consolidates fragmented user intents into explicit task instructions, and (3) derives binary, verifiable requirement checklists suitable for automatic evaluation. The framework uses LLMs (LLM-as-a-judge) to both construct the benchmark items and automatically score model outputs against the generated checklists. It is instantiated and tested in the programming domain and accompanied by ablation studies contrasting multi-turn vs. instruction-centric extraction strategies.
AI.result: In the programming domain, benchmarks generated by Conv-to-Bench are highly consistent with established, human-authored benchmarks such as BigCodeBench, with Spearman rank correlations up to ρ = 1.000 while requiring significantly less computational overhead. The LLM-as-a-judge component shows strong reliability, achieving substantial agreement with human-verified ground truth (Cohen’s κ = 0.705). Ablation studies show that using instruction-centric extraction yields more robust evaluation artifacts than relying solely on raw multi-turn conversational context, although multi-turn histories capture useful iterative refinement of user intent.
AI.conclusion: Conv-to-Bench demonstrates that authentic conversational logs can be systematically converted into reliable, checklist-based benchmarks, enabling scalable and cost-effective evaluation of LLMs. The framework maintains high alignment with expert-created standards while reducing human effort, and it suggests a practical path to continuously updating evaluation suites as user-centric AI applications evolve and diversify.

title: MedGuideX: Internalizing Decision Logic from Executable Guidelines into Large Language Models for Clinical Reasoning
summary: Clinical practice guidelines (CPGs) encode evidence-based decision logic that clinicians apply by evaluating patient variables, conditional criteria, and recommendation rules. However, existing methods often use CPGs as free-text training data or retrieval sources, underutilizing their procedural decision structure. To better exploit this structure, we introduce a guideline-derived training pipeline that transforms CPG recommendations into executable clinical decision logic and uses it to generate factual and counterfactual question-answering data. Theses data teach models both guideline-supported decisions and how decisions change under different patient conditions. Post-training a medical LLM on the generated data yields MedGuideX. Across four clinical reasoning benchmarks, MedGuideX achieves a 10.28% relative improvement in average accuracy. Physician evaluation further shows that MedGuideX better recovers clinician authored reasoning steps and produces physician-preferred rationales in faithfulness, validity, completeness, and clarity. Overall, our results show that executable decision logic from CPGs can be transformed into scalable supervision for building reliable medical LLMs.

AI.tldr: They turn clinical practice guidelines into executable decision logic to generate training data, then fine-tune a medical LLM (MedGuideX), which improves clinical reasoning accuracy and rationale quality.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM methods treat clinical practice guidelines mainly as free text or retrieval sources, failing to exploit their rich procedural decision structures. There is a need for systematic, scalable supervision that reflects how clinicians actually apply guideline logic across different patient conditions, including both what should be done and how recommendations change counterfactually.
AI.method: They design a guideline-derived training pipeline that (1) converts CPG recommendations into executable decision logic with explicit variables, conditions, and rules; (2) uses this logic to generate large-scale factual and counterfactual clinical QA data covering diverse patient presentations and decision pathways; and (3) post-trains a base medical LLM on this synthetic but guideline-grounded dataset to create MedGuideX. They then evaluate MedGuideX on multiple clinical reasoning benchmarks and through physician assessment of model rationales.
AI.result: MedGuideX, the post-trained model, achieves a 10.28% relative gain in average accuracy across four clinical reasoning benchmarks compared with the base model. In physician evaluations, MedGuideX more accurately recovers clinician-authored reasoning steps and its explanations are preferred on dimensions of faithfulness, validity, completeness, and clarity.
AI.conclusion: Executable decision logic extracted from clinical practice guidelines can be systematically transformed into scalable, high-quality supervision data for medical LLMs. This approach yields models with better clinical reasoning performance and more reliable, clinician-aligned rationales, demonstrating that leveraging the procedural structure of guidelines is more effective than using them merely as unstructured text or retrieval material.

title: Alignment Tuning for Large Language Models: A Data-Centric Lens on Alignment Data Pipelines
comment: Accepted at the Findings of ACL 2026
summary: Much of the alignment tuning literature is organized around optimization objectives, while the construction of alignment data is often treated implicitly. In this survey, we adopt a data centric perspective and reframe alignment tuning as a pipeline design problem. We decompose alignment data construction into three interacting stages, response synthesis, preference evaluation, and preference instantiation, and use this framework to organize existing alignment methods into a unified taxonomy. Through this lens, we identify recurring design trade-offs and failure modes observed across prior alignment methods, and distill a set of high level principles that clarify how pipeline design choices influence the resulting optimization signal. Finally, we outline open challenges for alignment data pipelines, including prompt-level alignment, agentic settings, and alignment under evolving objectives.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys alignment tuning from a data-centric, pipeline perspective instead of focusing only on optimization objectives.
AI.motivation: Existing work on alignment tuning mainly focuses on optimization objectives (e.g., RLHF loss functions), while the way alignment data is constructed is often implicit and under-analyzed. This makes it hard to compare methods, understand trade-offs, and reason about failures that actually stem from data pipeline design rather than from the optimization algorithm itself.
AI.method: The authors propose a conceptual framework that reframes alignment tuning as a pipeline design problem and decomposes alignment data construction into three stages: (1) response synthesis (how candidate outputs are produced), (2) preference evaluation (how the quality or preference over responses is assessed), and (3) preference instantiation (how these assessments are turned into concrete training signals). Using this three-stage framework, they systematically review and categorize existing alignment methods into a unified taxonomy, and analyze their design choices and interactions.
AI.result: The survey organizes a wide range of existing alignment methods under the proposed three-stage taxonomy, highlighting recurring design patterns, trade-offs (e.g., cost vs. fidelity, automation vs. reliability), and common failure modes that arise from particular pipeline configurations. It also clarifies how choices in each stage propagate into and shape the final optimization signal used for tuning models.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that alignment outcomes depend critically on the structure and choices of the alignment data pipeline, not just on the optimization objective. It offers high-level principles for designing better alignment pipelines and identifies open challenges, such as handling prompt-level alignment, supporting agentic use cases, and coping with objectives that change over time. This data-centric, pipeline-based view is proposed as a foundation for more systematic and robust alignment tuning in future work.

title: AGORA: Adapter-Grounded Observation-Action Retention for Inference-Free Prompt Compression in LLM Agents
summary: The token-level extractive compressors widely used for general LM context are structurally inappropriate for LLM agents: across 17 (env, backbone, method) cells spanning two independent token-level method families, every cell collapses to mean reward <= 0.05 despite 1.3-13.3x realized compression. We name and characterize this failure mode as action-grammar destruction -- the tokens carrying action semantics (identifiers, brackets, action verbs) are exactly those self-information ranks lowest, so a general-purpose compressor reliably removes them and the environment rejects the residual. The diagnosis points to step-granularity compression. We introduce AGORA, an inference-free step-level compressor combining a structural prompt parser, an always-keep floor for format- and recency-critical content, and a 125M-parameter relevance scorer trained on counterfactual next-action-change labels (~2ms/step, zero per-step LLM toll). Across the compared inference-free and LLM-based methods, AGORA is the only one retaining >= 75% uncompressed performance in 8 of 9 cells (with the lone exception at 73%); a four-way component ablation isolates the structural floor as the dominant quality lever and the learned scorer as the source of 1.0-11.5x adaptive end-to-end compression from a single fixed keep ratio.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that standard token-level compression methods fail badly for LLM agents and introduces AGORA, a step-level compressor that preserves agent performance while still achieving strong context compression.
AI.motivation: Existing token-level extractive compressors are widely used to shorten LLM contexts, but they silently break LLM-based agents in interactive environments: they remove exactly the low-surprisal but action-critical tokens (e.g., identifiers, brackets, verbs), causing actions to be malformed or rejected by the environment. There is a need for a compression method tailored to agents that preserves action grammar and task performance while still saving context length and compute.
AI.method: The authors empirically test token-level compression across 17 environment/backbone/method combinations and identify a universal failure mode they term action-grammar destruction, linked to information-theoretic token rankings. They then propose AGORA, an inference-free step-level compressor. AGORA uses (1) a structural prompt parser to segment and understand the prompt layout, (2) an always-keep floor for structurally and temporally critical content, and (3) a lightweight 125M-parameter relevance scorer trained on counterfactual labels indicating whether removing a step would change the next action. This pipeline runs at ~2 ms per step and does not invoke the main LLM per compression step.
AI.result: Token-level compressors achieve 1.3–13.3× compression but collapse mean reward to ≤0.05 across all 17 tested cells for LLM agents. In contrast, AGORA is the only compared method—among both inference-free and LLM-based compressors—that preserves at least 75% of the uncompressed performance in 8 out of 9 cells, with the remaining cell at 73%. Ablation studies show that the structural always-keep floor is the main contributor to robustness, while the learned relevance scorer is responsible for achieving 1.0–11.5× adaptive end-to-end compression from a single fixed keep ratio.
AI.conclusion: Token-level extractive compression fundamentally conflicts with the needs of LLM agents because it destroys action grammar by preferentially dropping low-surprisal but action-critical tokens. The solution is to shift from token-level to step-level compression, respecting prompt structure and recency. AGORA demonstrates that an inference-free step-level compressor with a structural floor and a small learned relevance scorer can maintain most of the agent's performance while still providing substantial, adaptive compression, establishing a more suitable paradigm for compressing agentic LLM contexts.

title: MicroSpec: Accelerating Speculative Decoding with Lightweight In-Context Vocabularies
summary: Large language models typically employ vocabularies of over 100k tokens, which creates a major computational bottleneck at the final linear projection layer when performing speculative decoding. Current methods for vocabulary pruning depend on either fixed or coarse-grained sub-vocabularies, requiring around 30k active tokens to preserve the quality of the draft model. We introduce MicroSpec, a training-free technique that overcomes this limitation by building a compact, context-sensitive active vocabulary on the fly for every decoding step. Exploiting the natural temporal locality found in language generation, MicroSpec attains high token coverage while reducing the average vocabulary size by more than 40x (down to under 3k tokens), all without any additional trained parameters. To translate this high sparsity into actual speedups on contemporary hardware, we present a co-designed system and algorithm that mitigates the overhead of sparse memory accesses via asynchronous gathering and GPU-resident state management. Acting as a plug-and-play enhancement, MicroSpec reduces draft inference latency by 51.6% on average, achieving an end-to-end speedup of 1.12-1.32x relative to the leading speculative decoding approach EAGLE-2 on various benchmarks, while also surpassing more sophisticated training-based pruning baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes MicroSpec, a training-free, context-sensitive vocabulary pruning technique for speculative decoding that greatly reduces active vocabulary size and speeds up inference.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding in large language models is slowed down by the large final vocabulary projection; existing pruning needs large (≈30k) active vocabularies to keep quality, limiting speedups.
AI.method: MicroSpec dynamically constructs a compact active vocabulary per decoding step using temporal locality in token usage, achieving high token coverage with small vocabularies. It is training-free, adds no parameters, and uses a co-designed algorithm-system stack with asynchronous sparse-gather operations and GPU-resident state to convert sparsity into real hardware speedups.
AI.result: Reduces average active vocabulary size over 40x (to <3k tokens), cuts draft inference latency by 51.6% on average, and yields 1.12–1.32x end-to-end speedup over EAGLE-2 while outperforming training-based pruning baselines.
AI.conclusion: Context-sensitive, training-free vocabulary pruning via MicroSpec can substantially shrink the active vocabulary for speculative decoding and, with systems co-design, deliver practical speedups without sacrificing model quality.

title: Curation and Extraction of Drug-Related Entities from Reddit Platform
comment: Accepted by IEEE International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI 2026)
summary: Physicians learn primarily about illicit drugs from clinical overdose cases, limiting their understanding of real-world usage. Meanwhile, drug users share first-hand experiences online, offering insights into dosage and effects of drugs. To bridge this gap, we introduce ReDose (REddit Drug DOSe and Effect), a dataset of 6,435 Reddit posts on substance use. A board-certified toxicologist primarily annotated both the training and test sets, while two medical science students contributed to the test set, labeling DRUG, DOSE, and EFFECT entities. We benchmarked 6,267 annotations using BERT-based, large language model (LLM)-based, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) models. BiomedBERT achieved an F1-score of 0.843 for DRUG, while Llama-3 70B outperformed GPT-4 (F1 = 0.79 vs. 0.72). EFFECT extraction remains challenging, with GPT-4 achieving a recall of 0.41. ReDose captures patient-curated narratives to advance medical data extraction from social media.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ReDose, a Reddit-based dataset on illicit drug use, annotated for drugs, dosages, and effects, and benchmarks multiple NLP models for information extraction.
AI.motivation: Clinicians mostly learn about illicit drugs from overdose cases, giving a skewed and incomplete picture of how drugs are actually used and experienced in real life. In contrast, drug users share rich, first-hand accounts on platforms like Reddit, including dosage and subjective effects. There was no systematically curated, expert-annotated dataset leveraging these patient-generated narratives for structured extraction of drug, dose, and effect information.
AI.method: The authors collected 6,435 Reddit posts about substance use and created the ReDose dataset. A board-certified toxicologist annotated DRUG, DOSE, and EFFECT entities across training and test sets, with two medical science students helping annotate the test set. They produced 6,267 labeled instances and benchmarked several models: BERT-based models (including BiomedBERT), general-purpose large language models (LLMs) such as Llama-3 70B and GPT-4, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) setups, evaluating entity extraction performance with F1 and recall metrics.
AI.result: BiomedBERT performed best among BERT-based models on DRUG entity extraction with an F1-score of 0.843. Among LLMs, Llama-3 70B outperformed GPT-4 on the task (F1 = 0.79 vs. 0.72). Extracting EFFECT entities proved substantially harder; even GPT-4 achieved only a recall of 0.41, indicating that many effects mentioned in posts were missed. Overall, performance on DRUG and DOSE was stronger than on EFFECT.
AI.conclusion: ReDose is a novel, expert-annotated dataset that leverages Reddit posts to capture detailed, patient-generated narratives about illicit drug use, including substances, dosages, and experienced effects. Benchmarking results show that existing models can effectively identify drugs and, to a lesser extent, dosages, but still struggle with nuanced effect descriptions. The dataset provides a foundation for improving medical information extraction from social media, potentially enhancing clinicians’ understanding of real-world substance use patterns beyond overdose cases.

title: Tail-Aware HiFloat4: W4A4 Post-Training Quantization for Wan2.2
summary: This report describes Tail-Aware HiFloat4, our submission to the low-bit text-to-video generation quantization challenge. Our method adapts the public ViDiT-Q post-training quantization pipeline to Wan2.2 under the HiFloat4 numerical format. We quantize the main linear layers in both Wan2.2 transformer modules with W4A4 HiFloat4 fake quantization, keep numerically sensitive boundary modules in high precision, and introduce an activation-tail-aware percentile calibration module for channel-mask construction. Together with compact PTQ-state restoration, this design reduces the influence of rare calibration outliers while keeping the runtime HiFloat4 arithmetic and sampling pipeline unchanged.

AI.tldr: They present Tail-Aware HiFloat4, a low-bit post-training quantization solution for text-to-video model Wan2.2, focusing on robust activation calibration for tails while preserving the original HiFloat4 runtime.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory and compute costs for large text-to-video models in a quantization challenge, while maintaining model quality under the HiFloat4 low-bit format and not changing the deployed runtime.
AI.method: Adapt the ViDiT-Q post-training quantization pipeline to Wan2.2 using HiFloat4. Quantize main linear layers in transformer modules with W4A4 HiFloat4 fake quantization, keep boundary/sensitive modules in high precision, and introduce an activation-tail-aware percentile-based calibration module that builds channel masks. Use compact PTQ-state restoration to mitigate effects of rare outlier activations, without modifying the existing HiFloat4 arithmetic and sampling pipeline.
AI.result: Achieves effective low-bit quantization of Wan2.2’s transformer layers under HiFloat4 while limiting the negative impact of activation outliers; preserves compatibility with existing HiFloat4 runtime and sampling processes. (Concrete numbers are not in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: Tail-Aware HiFloat4 is a practical, tail-robust PTQ scheme for text-to-video generation under HiFloat4 that selectively quantizes transformer internals, protects sensitive modules, and uses tail-aware calibration and PTQ-state restoration to balance compression with model quality without changing the runtime pipeline.

title: Model Unlearning Objectives Vary for Distinct Language Functions
summary: Large language models (LLMs) learn undesirable properties during pretraining, including dangerous knowledge and toxic text generation. Just as post-training uses different objectives to shape different behaviors, we argue that unlearning methods should be designed for the language function at issue. To study this, we consider two mechanistically distinct unlearning goals, dangerous-knowledge unlearning and toxicity unlearning. For dangerous knowledge, we introduce a cosine-based, meta-learned variant of RMU. For toxicity, we propose a multi-layer objective based on layer-specific probe directions. Across four open-source 7-8B models, our methods achieve strong results, based on distinct training objectives for the two types of unlearning. Overall, our results suggest that unlearning should be studied as a family of problems, analogous to the multiple types of LLM post-training.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes task-specific unlearning methods for large language models, showing that different kinds of unwanted behaviors (dangerous knowledge vs. toxicity) require different unlearning objectives and mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Large language models often acquire harmful capabilities and behaviors during pretraining, such as dangerous know-how and toxic language generation. Existing unlearning or safety methods tend to treat unlearning as a single, generic problem, despite the fact that different unwanted behaviors likely arise from different mechanisms inside the model. The authors want to systematically study whether unlearning should be tailored to the specific "language function" (e.g., factual knowledge vs. style/toxicity) being removed, analogous to how post-training is specialized (e.g., RLHF vs. instruction tuning vs. safety tuning).
AI.method: The authors frame unlearning as behavior-specific and consider two distinct targets: (1) dangerous-knowledge unlearning and (2) toxicity unlearning. For dangerous knowledge, they propose a cosine-based, meta-learned variant of Representation Model Unlearning (RMU), which adjusts representations using a cosine similarity objective optimized via meta-learning. For toxicity, they derive layer-specific probe directions—linear probes that identify toxic features at different layers—and design a multi-layer loss that steers model activations away from those toxic directions while preserving overall capabilities. They evaluate these methods on four open-source 7–8B parameter models and compare their effectiveness in reducing the targeted behaviors while maintaining general performance.
AI.result: On four open-source 7–8B LLMs, the proposed methods substantially reduce the targeted behaviors: the cosine-based meta-learned RMU effectively removes or suppresses dangerous knowledge, while the probe-based multi-layer objective effectively decreases toxic generation. The results indicate that using distinct objectives and mechanisms for each unlearning task leads to stronger performance than a one-size-fits-all approach, with acceptable retention of non-targeted capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Unlearning in LLMs should not be treated as a single, monolithic problem. Different unwanted behaviors—such as dangerous knowledge and toxicity—are rooted in different internal mechanisms and thus benefit from specialized unlearning objectives. By designing and evaluating behavior-specific unlearning methods, the paper demonstrates that tailored approaches can more effectively and selectively remove harmful capabilities, suggesting that future work should view unlearning as a family of related but distinct problems, similar to the various forms of LLM post-training used today.

title: UnityMAS-O: A General RL Optimization Framework for LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
summary:
LLM-based multi-agent systems decompose complex tasks into interacting roles, but most remain manually orchestrated by prompts, tools, and control rules, while agents are rarely optimized through a unified reinforcement learning interface. Existing RL post-training frameworks mainly target single-policy optimization and lack abstractions for user-defined multi-agent workflows, structured interaction, role-specific credit assignment, and configurable parameter sharing.
  We present UnityMAS-O, a general RL optimization framework for LLM-based multi-agent systems. UnityMAS-O treats the complete workflow as the optimization unit, rather than a single response or policy trajectory. It represents workflows through four first-class objects: logical agent roles, graph trajectories, user-defined rewards, and agent--model mappings. This decouples logical agents from physical model parameters, supporting full sharing, full separation, and partial sharing, with rewards assigned at role, turn, and trajectory levels.
  UnityMAS-O extends verl with a Ray-based star-topology runtime. A central controller executes workflows, invokes tools, records structured trajectories, and assembles rewards; model-local worker groups handle rollout, buffering, advantage computation, and distributed PPO-style updates. Users can define agents, workflows, model mappings, and rewards without rewriting the optimization infrastructure.
  We instantiate UnityMAS-O on retrieval-augmented QA, iterative agentic search, and reflective code generation. Across Natural Questions, HotpotQA, and held-out code tasks, multi-agent RL improves manually specified workflows after optimization, with especially large gains for smaller models and strict code all-passed metrics. These results show that UnityMAS-O can serve as a reusable substrate for converting diverse LLM-based multi-agent workflows into trainable multi-agent RL systems.

AI.tldr: UnityMAS-O is a general reinforcement learning framework that optimizes entire LLM-based multi-agent workflows, rather than individual agents, enabling structured multi-agent RL training with flexible parameter sharing and role-specific rewards.
AI.motivation: Most current LLM-based multi-agent systems are manually orchestrated with prompts and tools, and existing RL post-training frameworks mainly target single-policy optimization. They lack abstractions for representing user-defined multi-agent workflows, structured role interactions, and flexible credit assignment, as well as support for configurable parameter sharing between agents. There is a need for a unified RL interface that can directly optimize complex multi-agent LLM workflows.
AI.method: The authors introduce UnityMAS-O, which treats a full multi-agent workflow as the optimization unit. Workflows are represented using four first-class abstractions: (1) logical agent roles, (2) graph trajectories describing interaction structure, (3) user-defined rewards, and (4) agent–model mappings that connect roles to underlying model parameters. This design decouples logical roles from physical models, supporting full, partial, or no parameter sharing. Rewards can be specified at role, turn, and trajectory levels. Implementation-wise, UnityMAS-O extends the verl framework with a Ray-based star-topology runtime: a central controller executes workflows, coordinates tools, logs structured trajectories, and aggregates rewards, while model-local worker groups handle rollout, experience buffering, advantage estimation, and distributed PPO-style updates. Users can plug in their own agents, workflows, mappings, and reward functions without changing optimization code.
AI.result: The framework is instantiated on three types of LLM-based multi-agent applications: retrieval-augmented QA, iterative agentic search, and reflective code generation. On benchmarks including Natural Questions, HotpotQA, and held-out coding tasks, applying multi-agent RL via UnityMAS-O leads to performance improvements over manually specified, non-optimized workflows. Gains are particularly notable for smaller models and on stringent code evaluation metrics such as all-tests-passed criteria.
AI.conclusion: UnityMAS-O provides a reusable, general-purpose substrate for turning diverse LLM-based multi-agent workflows into trainable multi-agent RL systems.
