By modeling the workflow itself as the optimization target and offering flexible abstractions for roles, trajectories, rewards, and parameter sharing, it enables systematic RL optimization of multi-agent LLM systems and yields measurable improvements over hand-crafted orchestration, especially for smaller models and demanding code-generation settings.

title: Towards Error-Free EHRs: Reasoning-Intensive Consistency Verification Between Clinical Notes and Structured Tables in Electronic Health Records
summary: Data consistency between unstructured clinical notes and structured tables in Electronic Health Records (EHRs) is essential for patient safety and clinical decision-making. However, existing work on note-table consistency verification mainly relies on surface-level matching of numeric values or simple events. Such approaches fail to capture the reasoning underlying real-world EHR documentation, including clinical interpretation, event relations, and temporal changes. To address this gap, we introduce EHR-ReasonCon, a reasoning-intensive benchmark for note-table consistency verification. Built on MIMIC-III with expert-guided annotations, it comprises 8,048 entities derived from clinical notes and provides high-quality ground-truth labels. The annotation protocol is supported by specialized table-exploration tools to ensure systematic evidence retrieval and reliable consistency assessment. We also propose EHR-Inspector, an LLM-based framework that segments notes, extracts anchor entities and temporal references, and uses table-exploration tools to verify consistency against structured tables. Evaluated using expert-validated LLM-as-a-judge metrics under harsh and lenient criteria, EHR-Inspector achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple model backbones. Analyses further demonstrate the effectiveness of its components and highlight differences from human verification.

AI.tldr: They build a new benchmark and an LLM-based system to check whether information in clinical notes is consistent with structured EHR tables, focusing on complex clinical reasoning rather than simple value matching.
AI.motivation: Ensuring that information written in free-text clinical notes agrees with the corresponding structured EHR tables is crucial for patient safety and sound clinical decisions. Existing verification methods mostly do shallow matching of numbers or straightforward events and cannot capture real clinical reasoning, such as interpreting lab trends, relating different events, and handling temporal changes in a patient’s status. There is thus a need for a benchmark and methods that test and support reasoning-intensive verification between notes and tables.
AI.method: They create EHR-ReasonCon, a benchmark built from the MIMIC-III dataset with expert-guided annotations. The benchmark includes 8,048 entities extracted from clinical notes, each labeled with high-quality ground-truth consistency judgments relative to structured tables. To support rigorous annotation, they design specialized table-exploration tools that help annotators systematically retrieve evidence and assess consistency. On top of this, they propose EHR-Inspector, an LLM-based framework that: (1) segments clinical notes, (2) extracts anchor clinical entities and temporal references, and (3) interacts with table-exploration tools to verify whether each entity is consistent with the structured tables. Evaluation uses an expert-validated LLM-as-a-judge setup with both strict and lenient scoring criteria.
AI.result: EHR-ReasonCon provides a reasoning-heavy testbed with thousands of annotated entities for note-table consistency verification, surpassing prior resources in complexity and quality. EHR-Inspector, when instantiated with various large language model backbones, achieves state-of-the-art results on this benchmark under both harsh and lenient evaluation criteria. Ablation and component analyses show that each part of EHR-Inspector—note segmentation, entity and temporal extraction, and tool-based table exploration—contributes meaningfully to performance. The analyses also reveal systematic differences between how the model and humans perform verification, indicating distinct reasoning patterns and failure modes.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that verifying consistency between unstructured clinical notes and structured EHR tables requires rich clinical and temporal reasoning that goes beyond simple value matching. EHR-ReasonCon establishes a challenging benchmark for this task, and the proposed EHR-Inspector framework shows that LLMs, when combined with structured tool usage and targeted note processing, can achieve strong performance. The work provides both a dataset and a methodology that can be used to develop and analyze more reliable clinical documentation verification systems, and it highlights remaining gaps between automated and human reasoning in this safety-critical domain.

title: Completion vs Optimality: Policy Gradient in Long-Horizon Cumulative-Damage Problems
summary: Long-horizon decision problems with cumulative damage couple locally attractive actions to globally adverse outcomes. We identify two orthogonal failure modes for policy-gradient methods on this class and propose a decomposition that separates them: \emph{completion} (reaching the terminal horizon rather than exiting via an implicit terminal constraint) and \emph{optimality} (matching the dynamic-programming reference given completion). Under PPO with a linear soft penalty, granting horizon access alone reduces the completion rate: the penalty's equilibrium drives the dominant-activity share to zero, while action-space restriction combined with horizon access achieves completion but leaves an optimality gap ($ΔM_{\text{final}} = 0.271$) that we trace to first-phase greedy commitment at the damage origin. We derive four testable predictions and evaluate them in two separately calibrated environments that share the same abstract structure but differ in domain, horizon, activity set, and calibration data: a 49-step bricklayer career and a 20-season NBA power-forward career. All four predictions replicate qualitatively. The horizon-invariance prediction is met at three of four tested horizons, with the exception at $H = 15$ consistent with the $H^*$ boundary ($H^* \in [6, 14]$ under the NBA parameters).

AI.tldr: The paper studies why policy-gradient methods like PPO fail in long-horizon problems with cumulative damage, and proposes a decomposition into completion and optimality to diagnose these failures, validating predictions in two career-planning style environments.
AI.motivation: In long-horizon decision problems where small, locally rewarding actions cause cumulative damage and bad long-run outcomes, standard policy-gradient RL methods struggle. The authors want to understand and classify these failure modes more precisely, since naive training can lead either to early termination (not reaching the intended horizon) or to suboptimal policies even when the horizon is reached. This has implications for safety and reliability of RL in domains like careers, health, or resource management.
AI.method: They analyze PPO with a linear soft penalty in environments with implicit terminal constraints. They introduce a conceptual decomposition of performance into completion (probability of reaching the terminal horizon instead of failing early) and optimality (how close the policy is to the dynamic-programming optimum conditional on completion). They conduct experiments in two differently calibrated but structurally similar environments: a 49-step bricklayer career and a 20-season NBA power-forward career. They manipulate action-space restrictions and horizon access, derive four testable predictions (including horizon invariance up to a critical horizon H*), and empirically test these predictions across horizons and domains.
AI.result: They show two orthogonal failure modes for PPO: (1) When given horizon access with a linear penalty, the equilibrium shifts so the agent's dominant-activity share goes to zero, lowering completion; (2) With restricted actions plus horizon access, the agent reliably completes the horizon but retains a significant optimality gap (ΔM_final = 0.271), traced to early greedy commitment near the damage origin. All four theoretically derived predictions are qualitatively confirmed in both domains. The horizon-invariance prediction holds at three of four tested horizons; the exception at H = 15 aligns with the theoretically estimated boundary H* ∈ [6, 14] in the NBA setting.
AI.conclusion: Policy-gradient methods like PPO can fail in long-horizon cumulative-damage tasks for two separable reasons: failing to reach the intended horizon (completion) and being suboptimal even when they do (optimality). The proposed completion/optimality decomposition clarifies these mechanisms and helps diagnose when and why PPO underperforms relative to dynamic programming. Their cross-domain experiments support the theory, including a horizon-invariance property that breaks only near a theoretically predicted critical horizon, suggesting the framework is robust and informative for analyzing long-horizon RL behavior.

title: FAB-Bench: A Framework for Adaptive RAG Benchmarking in Semiconductor Manufacturing
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become critical for knowledge-intensive applications, yet evaluating its performance in vertical domains remains difficult due to domain complexity, diverse context scales, and heavy reliance on expert assessments that are costly, inconsistent, and non-scalable. We introduce FAB-Bench, an end-to-end framework for adaptive benchmarking of RAG systems in semiconductor manufacturing. FAB-Bench defines six diagnostic metrics measuring factual accuracy, contextual utilization, completeness, retrieval relevance, technical depth, and reasoning consistency. The framework couples retriever diagnostics with generator-level reasoning analysis across context windows of 4K-32K tokens, quantifying how retrieval precision and generative fidelity co-evolve as contextual scope expands. From over 1,300 generated candidates, we curated a high-quality benchmark of 200 query-answer pairs spanning three synthesis strategies: needle-in-haystack, intra-document multi-topic, and cross-document multi-hop. Systematic evaluation across four LLMs and four RAG frameworks reveals three distinct context-scaling behaviors: logarithmic growth, early saturation, and cold-start dynamics, and identifies attention dilution as the primary mechanism behind performance degradation at extreme context lengths. Cross-framework validation on three additional production RAG systems confirms evaluation portability.

AI.tldr: FAB-Bench is a benchmarking framework to systematically evaluate Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems in the semiconductor manufacturing domain, focusing on how retrieval quality and generation performance change with context length.
AI.motivation: Evaluating RAG systems in complex, vertical domains like semiconductor manufacturing is hard because it usually requires domain experts, is costly, and doesn’t scale well. Existing benchmarks don’t capture nuanced domain needs, varying context sizes, or the joint behavior of retrievers and generators. The authors aim to create a rigorous, scalable, domain-specific benchmark that reveals how RAG systems really behave as context windows grow.
AI.method: They design FAB-Bench, an end-to-end adaptive benchmarking pipeline for RAG. It defines six diagnostic metrics (factual accuracy, contextual utilization, completeness, retrieval relevance, technical depth, reasoning consistency) and jointly evaluates retrievers and generators under varying context window sizes (4K–32K tokens). They construct a curated benchmark of 200 query–answer pairs (from 1300+ generated candidates) that cover three retrieval/generation challenges: needle-in-haystack, intra-document multi-topic, and cross-document multi-hop reasoning. They then systematically test four LLMs and four RAG frameworks with this benchmark, and further validate on three additional production RAG systems to test portability.
AI.result: The experiments reveal three characteristic patterns of how RAG performance scales with context length across systems: logarithmic growth (diminishing returns with larger context), early saturation (performance plateaus quickly), and cold-start dynamics (initial poor performance that improves later). At very large context sizes, performance often degrades, and the main cause is identified as attention dilution—models struggle to focus on the relevant parts of very long contexts. The benchmark is shown to be portable: it can be used to evaluate external production RAG systems and yields consistent diagnostic signals across frameworks.
AI.conclusion: FAB-Bench provides a structured, domain-specific way to evaluate RAG systems in semiconductor manufacturing, exposing fine-grained behaviors of retrievers and generators as context windows scale. It highlights concrete failure modes such as attention dilution at long context lengths and offers diagnostic metrics that can generalize across RAG frameworks. This framework can guide the design and tuning of future RAG systems in similarly complex vertical domains.

title: MemFail: Stress-Testing Failure Modes of LLM Memory Systems
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on external memory systems to remain consistent across long-horizon interactions, but little empirical work has been done to understand the specific failure modes and design choices that these systems present. Existing benchmarks report aggregate question-answering accuracy and treat memory systems as black boxes, making it impossible to attribute an incorrect answer to a particular failure mode of the system. We introduce MemFail, a diagnostic benchmark that isolates the failure modes of modern LLM memory systems. We begin by formalizing memory systems as the composition of three canonical operations -- summarization, storage, and retrieval -- and identify the potential failure modes induced by each. Based on these hypothesized failure modes, we construct five datasets spanning four tasks, each adversarially designed to test a specific operation of a memory system. Using these datasets, we evaluate four state-of-the-art memory systems on MemFail and demonstrate how MemFail can be used to empirically understand the tradeoffs induced by differences in memory system architectures.

AI.tldr: MemFail is a diagnostic benchmark to pinpoint specific failure modes in LLM memory systems by decomposing them into summarization, storage, and retrieval operations and testing each separately.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents increasingly depend on external memory for long, consistent interactions, current evaluations only provide aggregate QA accuracy and treat memory as a black box, making it hard to know why models fail. There is a need for fine-grained diagnostics that reveal which part of the memory pipeline—summarization, storage, or retrieval—is causing errors, so designers can improve and compare architectures in a principled way.
AI.method: The authors formalize LLM memory systems as a composition of three canonical operations: summarization, storage, and retrieval. They hypothesize the failure modes associated with each operation and then construct five adversarially designed datasets over four tasks, with each dataset targeting a specific operation or failure mode. They then run four state-of-the-art memory system architectures on this benchmark to probe how well each component works and where it breaks.
AI.result: When evaluated on MemFail, the four state-of-the-art memory systems show distinct patterns of failures aligned with the benchmark’s targeted operations, revealing concrete trade-offs between different architectural choices. The results demonstrate that seemingly similar systems can differ substantially in their robustness to specific failure modes in summarization, storage, or retrieval.
AI.conclusion: MemFail provides a structured, diagnostic way to evaluate LLM memory systems beyond aggregate QA scores by isolating failures in summarization, storage, and retrieval. This enables empirical analysis of architectural trade-offs and can guide the design and improvement of future memory systems for LLM agents.

title: Elias in the Lighthouse, Again? Diagnosing Low Diversity in LLM Stories
summary: LLM-generated stories are a popular use case, but they show very low variability. We sample 20,000 total stories from four current models using five prompts. We find that 11 words occur in 88.3% of generated stories, with little difference between models. These words include names (Elias, Mara, Elara), settings (lighthouses), and professions (clockmaker, librarian). These tokens do not often occur in published literature nor pre-training data, but they are found in preference data that is likely to have been used by all current models. Surprisingly, these "lighthouse" stories are infrequent when compared with the average post-training story, much of which contains references to copyrighted characters or adult content. This result demonstrates the potentially disproportionate impact of small datasets combined with powerful alignment algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that popular LLMs produce highly stereotyped 'lighthouse' stories, sharing a small set of recurring names, settings, and professions, likely due to shared preference/feedback datasets used in post-training.
AI.motivation: To investigate why LLM-generated stories display low diversity and often converge on similar motifs or content, and to understand the role of post-training preference data in shaping this behavior.
AI.method: The authors sample 20,000 stories from four contemporary language models using five prompts, then analyze word frequencies and compare the presence of recurring tokens with external corpora: published literature, pre-training data, and preference/feedback data used for alignment.
AI.result: They find that 11 specific words (including certain names, settings like lighthouses, and professions like clockmaker and librarian) appear in 88.3% of generated stories across models, despite being rare in published literature and pre-training data. These tokens are, however, prevalent in preference data shared across models. They also find that 'lighthouse' type stories are still less common than post-training stories referencing copyrighted or adult content.
AI.conclusion: A relatively small but shared preference/feedback dataset, combined with strong alignment or RLHF-style algorithms, can strongly bias diverse LLMs toward similar, stereotyped generations, revealing how alignment can unintentionally reduce content diversity and amplify idiosyncrasies from limited feedback data.

title: Mind the Tool Failures: Achieving Synergistic Tool Gains for Medical Agents
summary: Medical AI agents increasingly use external tools for diagnosis, treatment recommendation, and evidence retrieval, yet most existing approaches assume that task-appropriate tools are reliable within their intended scope. This assumption is fragile in real clinical settings, where even relevant tools may fail on challenging instances and lead to unsafe downstream decisions. To address this issue, we study medical tool use under imperfect-tool settings to correct failure instances missed by individual tools. Instance-dependent failure patterns create a gap between the best fixed single tool and an ideal instance-wise selector, which we refer to as the Single-Oracle risk gap. The core challenge is that conventional task-level tool selection cannot realize this gap, as it is inherently bounded by the performance of the best single tool. Motivated by this observation, we therefore account for instance-level heterogeneity and formulate tool use as an instance-level selection problem. Particularly, we propose a GRPO-based reinforcement learning framework with rewards for probabilistic risk minimization and disagreement-aware synergy learning, which promotes instance-level correction of erroneous tool consensus. Furthermore, an entropy-guided sampling strategy is adopted to upweight high-disagreement instances, which provide stronger signals for learning instance-specific tool synergy. These two components complement each other in mitigating instance-level heterogeneity and improving tool synergy. Experiments on two tasks and seven medical benchmarks show that our method consistently achieves robust and stable improvements over a broad range of baselines, highlighting the importance of synergy-aware tool use for reliable medical agentic systems.

AI.tldr: They propose a reinforcement-learning-based framework that selects and combines multiple imperfect medical AI tools on a per-instance basis to reduce diagnostic/treatment risk and improve reliability.
AI.motivation: In real clinical environments, external medical tools (for diagnosis, treatment recommendation, and evidence retrieval) are often imperfect and can fail on difficult cases. Existing approaches usually assume that, within their domain, a chosen tool is reliably correct, so they focus on selecting the right tool at the task level. However, even the right tool can make instance-specific errors, and relying on a single best tool limits performance and safety. There is an unrealized performance gap between always using the best fixed tool and an ideal system that picks the right tool or tool combination for each individual instance. The paper is motivated by closing this instance-wise performance and safety gap for medical AI agents.
AI.method: They first formalize medical tool use as an instance-level selection problem and define the Single-Oracle risk gap between the best fixed tool and an ideal instance-wise selector. To close this gap, they design a reinforcement learning (RL) framework based on GRPO (a policy optimization algorithm). The agent learns, for each input case, how to select and combine tools. The reward has two main components: (1) probabilistic risk minimization, encouraging the agent to choose tool outputs that minimize expected error/risk, and (2) disagreement-aware synergy learning, which explicitly rewards the model for exploiting disagreements among tools to correct their consensus when it is likely wrong. Additionally, they introduce an entropy-guided sampling strategy that upweights high-disagreement instances during training, as these hard cases provide stronger learning signals about when and how to rely on each tool. Together, the RL objective and sampling strategy aim to learn fine-grained, instance-specific tool synergy instead of coarse task-level selection.
AI.result: On two different medical tasks spanning seven public benchmarks, their method outperforms a variety of baseline systems, including standard tool selection and ensembling methods. The improvements are described as robust and stable, meaning they hold across datasets and metrics, and show better handling of difficult instances where existing single-tool or naive multi-tool approaches often fail.
AI.conclusion: Instance-level heterogeneity in medical AI tool performance makes task-level, single-tool selection fundamentally insufficient for safe and reliable clinical agents. By reframing tool use as an instance-level selection and synergy-learning problem, and by using a GRPO-based RL framework with disagreement-aware rewards and entropy-guided sampling, the proposed method can exploit complementary strengths and disagreements among tools. This yields more reliable, risk-aware behavior and demonstrates the importance of synergy-aware tool use in building trustworthy medical agentic systems.

title: Verilog-Evolve: Feedback-Driven and Skill-Evolving Verilog Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have improved Verilog generation from natural-language specifications, but most pipelines still treat generation as isolated sampling followed by functional checking. This is insufficient for practical RTL design, where useful Verilog must be correct, synthesizable, timing-conscious, and friendly to downstream hardware objectives. We present Verilog-Evolve, a feedback-driven framework for versioned Verilog refinement and cross-session skill evolution. For each task, Verilog-Evolve generates diverse minor candidates, evaluates them with executable feedback from functional simulation, Yosys synthesis, ABC timing proxy, and optional GEMM metrics, then promotes the best candidate into a major version under configurable scoring. To improve across tasks, the system maintains modular skill guidance, retrieves skills according to task and feedback context, and evolves candidate skills from logged histories through create/improve/skip decisions and verifier reports. Experiments on VerilogEval and mixed-precision GEMM tasks show that Verilog-Evolve improves final functional success and promotion stability while producing more downstream-friendly RTL under open-source synthesis, timing-proxy, and netlist-level GEMM objectives. Validation-gated skill evolution further improves GEMM downstream quality and achieves the best downstream score and GEMM held-out pass rate among the evaluated skill modes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Verilog-Evolve, a feedback-driven framework that iteratively refines Verilog generated by large language models and learns reusable “skills” across tasks to produce functionally correct, synthesizable, timing-aware RTL that better serves downstream hardware objectives.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based Verilog generation pipelines typically perform one-shot or isolated sampling from natural-language specs and only check functional correctness afterward. This approach is inadequate for real RTL design, which requires code that is not only functionally correct but also synthesizable, timing-conscious, and aligned with downstream objectives such as GEMM performance. There is also a need for systems that can improve over time by learning from prior design attempts and verification feedback across tasks.
AI.method: Verilog-Evolve employs an iterative refinement loop. For each design task, it generates multiple minor Verilog candidates, evaluates them using executable feedback from functional simulation, Yosys synthesis, ABC as a timing proxy, and optionally GEMM-related metrics. Based on a configurable scoring scheme, it promotes the best candidate to a major version. Across tasks, it maintains modular skill modules derived from past solutions and verification logs. A retrieval mechanism selects relevant skills according to the task and feedback context. Skills themselves evolve via create/improve/skip decisions guided by verifier reports, a process gated by validation to ensure that only beneficial skills are kept.
AI.result: On VerilogEval benchmarks and mixed-precision GEMM design tasks, Verilog-Evolve increases final functional success rates and stabilizes the promotion of better candidates compared to baselines. The generated RTL is more favorable for open-source synthesis flows, timing analysis via ABC, and netlist-level GEMM objectives. When skill evolution is further constrained by validation (validation-gated evolution), the system achieves higher downstream GEMM quality, obtaining the best downstream score and highest held-out GEMM pass rate among the tested skill configurations.
AI.conclusion: Feedback-driven, versioned refinement combined with cross-task skill evolution enables LLM-based Verilog generation to better meet practical RTL design requirements. Verilog-Evolve shows that integrating functional, synthesis, timing, and domain-specific feedback into an evolving skill-guided pipeline yields more reliable, downstream-friendly hardware designs and can progressively improve performance across tasks, particularly when skill updates are gated by validation.

title: Towards Feedback-to-Plan Decisions for Self-Evolving LLM Agents in CUDA Kernel Generation
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong empirical gains as self-evolving agents for CUDA kernel generation, driven by feedback-conditioned planning across generations. However, how planning decisions attribute and combine heterogeneous feedback signals remains opaque. Standard end-to-end ablations fail to resolve this question, as iterative planning amplifies early perturbations and conflates feedback effects with trajectory-dependent drift.
  We introduce \texttt{CUDAnalyst}, a unified analysis layer for controlled, generation-level attribution of planning decisions to feedback components via trajectory freezing and selective feedback injection. \texttt{CUDAnalyst} enables stable generation-level evaluation and principled coalitional-style attribution of feedback effects and interactions. Our results show that explicit planning is beneficial only when feedback is aligned, that effective planning emerges from structured multi-feedback interactions, and that high-level plans from stronger reasoning models can partially transfer to weaker ones. These trends hold across reference backbones, representative workloads, and reference induction regimes, indicating that the identified feedback-to-plan structure is robust within the controlled axes studied.

AI.tldr: They propose CUDAnalyst, a framework to attribute how different feedback signals influence planning decisions of LLM agents that generate CUDA kernels, revealing when and how planning and feedback interactions help.
AI.motivation: Although LLM agents can iteratively plan and improve CUDA kernels using multiple feedback signals (e.g., correctness, performance), we don’t understand how each feedback source actually influences planning decisions. Standard ablations are confounded by trajectory drift and early perturbation amplification, so we lack a clean, controlled way to attribute outcomes to specific feedback components and their interactions.
AI.method: They design CUDAnalyst, an analysis layer that freezes full generation trajectories and then selectively injects or withholds individual feedback components at the generation level. This enables controlled experiments where they can attribute planning decisions and performance changes to specific feedback sources, and apply coalitional-style (e.g., Shapley-like) attribution to quantify marginal and interaction effects among multiple feedback channels across models, workloads, and training regimes.
AI.result: Using CUDAnalyst, they find that (1) explicit multi-step planning only helps when available feedback signals are mutually aligned, (2) strong performance gains arise from structured interactions between multiple feedback sources rather than any single one, and (3) high-level plans produced by stronger reasoning models can be partially reused or transferred to guide weaker models. These patterns are consistently observed across different backbone LLMs, CUDA workloads, and reference-induction setups.
AI.conclusion: CUDAnalyst provides a stable and principled way to attribute LLM planning decisions for CUDA kernel generation to heterogeneous feedback signals, overcoming the confounds of standard ablations. The discovered patterns suggest that effective planning depends critically on aligned, interacting feedback channels and that plan-transfer across models is feasible, indicating a robust underlying feedback-to-plan structure within the studied settings.

title: Conceptual Steganography
summary: Language Models (LMs) emit Chains-of-Thought (CoTs) that drive much of their capability. However, the same sequence that carries useful reasoning can also covertly convey messages: a misaligned model may embed covert information in its CoT that slips through human supervision, a form of steganography known as encoded reasoning. Prior LM steganography schemes operate in the token or lexical space, and a content-preserving paraphraser is the canonical and effective defense in recent work. We introduce conceptual steganography, in which each step of a CoT carries information through patterns of high-level reasoning behavior, rather than through lexical choice. Across four model families and two reasoning domains, this backdoor communication channel is shown to be consistently more robust to a strong paraphrase defense than standard keyword approaches, and the encoding of information into CoTs does not affect their utility in the reasoning process. Having raised awareness of this new risk, we then demonstrate that a strategy-aware paraphraser can close much of the channel, highlighting new challenges and recommended defenses for ensuring faithful LLM reasoning in the wild.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and studies “conceptual steganography” in language models, where hidden messages are encoded in the structure and style of chain-of-thought reasoning rather than in specific words, and shows it can bypass standard paraphrasing defenses more effectively than previous token-based schemes.
AI.motivation: Existing language model steganography techniques hide information in the token or lexical space of model outputs. These can often be disrupted by content-preserving paraphrasers, which are a recommended defense in recent work. However, chains-of-thought are rich, structured reasoning sequences that could potentially hide information at a higher level, such as the pattern or style of reasoning itself, which might survive paraphrasing. The authors are motivated to characterize and measure this new risk because it poses a security and alignment challenge: misaligned models could communicate covertly with an attacker through their reasoning traces while appearing benign to human supervisors.
AI.method: The authors define “conceptual steganography,” where each step in a chain-of-thought encodes bits of information through patterns of reasoning behavior (e.g., choice of strategy, ordering of steps, or other high-level reasoning decisions) rather than specific keywords. They implement backdoor communication channels using this mechanism across four different model families and two reasoning domains. They compare the robustness of this conceptual encoding to a strong paraphrasing defense against that of standard keyword- or token-based steganography. They also investigate whether embedding information into CoTs harms the underlying reasoning quality. Finally, they design and evaluate a “strategy-aware” paraphraser that explicitly targets the higher-level structure of reasoning in order to close or narrow this covert channel.
AI.result: Across all tested model families and reasoning domains, conceptual steganography is consistently more robust to strong paraphrasing defenses than conventional keyword-based steganographic schemes: the hidden information survives paraphrasing more reliably. Furthermore, injecting this encoded information into the chain-of-thought does not materially degrade the reasoning utility of the CoT—the models still solve tasks effectively while covertly transmitting data. At the same time, the authors show that a paraphraser designed with awareness of these conceptual strategies can substantially reduce the effective bandwidth of the hidden channel, although it may not eliminate it entirely.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that chains-of-thought introduce a powerful new avenue for covert communication via “conceptual steganography,” where information is encoded in reasoning patterns rather than surface tokens. This threat is more resistant to standard paraphrasing defenses than previously studied approaches and can coexist with high-quality reasoning, making it particularly insidious for safety and oversight. However, defenses that explicitly model and manipulate reasoning strategies—strategy-aware paraphrasers—can significantly constrain this channel. The authors recommend incorporating such structurally aware defenses and rethinking how we supervise and audit chain-of-thought reasoning to ensure faithful, non-covert model behavior in deployment.

title: It's Not the Capability: Harness Sensitivity Is Non-Monotone Across LLM Agent Tiers
summary:
A prevalent assumption in LLM agent deployment holds that more structured harnesses universally improve
  reliability, and that higher-capability models need proportionally less structural guidance -- together
  implying a monotone inverse relationship between model capability tier and optimal harness complexity. We
  test this hypothesis through a controlled 432-run experiment crossing six models across four capability
  tiers with three harness conditions (light, balanced, strict) on HEAT-24, a 24-task synthetic benchmark
  with git-based workspace verification. Our results refute the monotone inverse relationship on two
  fronts. First, for the frontier chat model evaluated (Gemini 2.5 Flash), increased harness verbosity
  lowers VTSR by 29-38 percentage points -- a harness-complexity paradox. Second, for the frontier
  reasoning model evaluated (Qwen3.5-122B, extended thinking enabled), strict harness achieves the highest
  VTSR (91.7%) and the lowest latency, the opposite of the prediction. Within the constrained tier, a 2B
  model (Gemma4:e2B) matches strong-open-tier stability at 91.7% across all harnesses. Because each tier is
  represented by a single model in this study, these results should be interpreted as model-specific
  observations; harness sensitivity appears non-monotone across the models evaluated, and depends
  critically on model type (chat vs. reasoning). We introduce a six-label failure taxonomy showing that
  format_violation dominates capable-model failures while wrong_file dominates low-capability failures, and
  we derive practical tier-aware harness selection guidelines.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically studies how different levels of prompt/harness structure affect LLM agent reliability across model tiers, finding that harness complexity does not monotonically decrease with model capability and can even hurt frontier chat models, while helping frontier reasoning models.
AI.motivation: There is a common belief that stronger models require less prompt structure and that adding more structured harnesses always improves reliability. This assumption guides how practitioners design agent systems, but it has not been rigorously tested across model tiers and types. The paper aims to challenge and empirically evaluate this belief to provide evidence-based guidance for harness design.
AI.method: The authors run a 432-run controlled experiment that crosses six LLMs (spanning four capability tiers: constrained, strong-open, frontier chat, frontier reasoning) with three harness conditions (light, balanced, strict) on HEAT-24, a synthetic 24-task benchmark that uses git-based workspace verification. They measure Verified Task Success Rate (VTSR), latency, and analyze failures via a six-label taxonomy. They compare performance across models and harness complexities to test the hypothesized monotone inverse relationship between capability and optimal harness complexity.
AI.result: The study finds that increased harness verbosity significantly reduces VTSR for the evaluated frontier chat model (Gemini 2.5 Flash) by 29–38 percentage points, contradicting the assumption that more structure always helps. For the evaluated frontier reasoning model (Qwen3.5-122B with extended thinking), the strict harness yields both the best VTSR (91.7%) and the lowest latency, which is opposite to the predicted relationship. In the constrained tier, a 2B model (Gemma4:e2B) achieves strong-open-tier-like stability, with 91.7% VTSR across all harnesses. Failure analysis shows that format violations dominate failures for capable models, while wrong-file errors dominate for low-capability models.
AI.conclusion: The relationship between model capability and optimal harness complexity is non-monotone and strongly model- and type-dependent (chat vs. reasoning). More structured prompts can either harm or help, depending on the model. The authors provide a six-label failure taxonomy and derive practical guidance for selecting harness complexity tailored to the model tier, cautioning against universal rules that more capable models always need less structure or that more structure always increases reliability.

title: Reliable Extraction of Clinical Follow-Up Instructions: A Hybrid Neural-Symbolic Pipeline
summary: Objective. Outpatient notes carry follow-up instructions pairing actions with future times ("MRI brain in two weeks"). Extracting (action, date) pairs supports scheduling and audit, but generative extractors miss the date because linking and arithmetic are implicit in decoding. We test a hybrid neural-symbolic pipeline against direct generation. Methods. We define TestSpecification and TimeSpecification entities and a ScheduledFor relation. BioBERT feeds BIO tagging and a biaffine linker; entities are canonicalized via a 28-action ontology and times normalized to day offsets deterministically. We evaluate on a 2,000-note synthetic outpatient corpus with action-disjoint splits (18 train, 6 OOV-test) against zero-shot GPT-4o-mini and LoRA-fine-tuned LLaMA-3 8B with note-level bootstrap 95% CIs. Results. On 259-note seen and OOV splits the hybrid pipeline achieves Test-Time Pair F1 of 0.997 and 0.986 with 0.00-day MAE. Baselines reach high action F1 (LLaMA-3 0.992; GPT-4o-mini 0.963 seen) but Pair F1 stays at 0.51-0.57 (LLaMA-3) and 0.53 (GPT-4o-mini), CIs non-overlapping with the hybrid. Conclusion. Separating learned entity extraction from deterministic date arithmetic outperforms generation on this benchmark, generalizes to held-out actions, and exposes failure modes. Transfer to real EHR notes is the next validation; a first-pass realism check is in Limitations.

AI.tldr: The paper compares a hybrid neural-symbolic pipeline to generative models for extracting (action, time) follow-up instructions from outpatient notes, showing the hybrid approach dramatically outperforms generative baselines.
AI.motivation: Follow-up instructions in outpatient notes specify what action should be taken and when, but current generative extractors struggle to correctly capture dates because temporal linking and arithmetic are implicit in sequence decoding. Accurately extracting (action, time) pairs is important for scheduling, quality assurance, and audit in healthcare workflows. The authors aim to design and test an approach that explicitly separates entity recognition from date reasoning, hypothesizing this will improve reliability and generalization.
AI.method: The authors introduce a formal representation with TestSpecification and TimeSpecification entities connected by a ScheduledFor relation. They use BioBERT for token-level BIO tagging, followed by a biaffine linker to connect the extracted entities. Recognized actions are mapped to a 28-action ontology, and time expressions are deterministically normalized to day offsets (e.g., "in two weeks" to +14 days). They evaluate on a synthetic corpus of 2,000 outpatient notes with action-disjoint splits (18 actions for training, 6 held out for out-of-vocabulary testing). Performance of this hybrid neural-symbolic pipeline is compared against two generative approaches: zero-shot GPT-4o-mini and a LoRA-fine-tuned LLaMA-3 8B model. Evaluation uses Test-Time Pair F1, mean absolute error (MAE) in days for time normalization, and bootstrap 95% confidence intervals at the note level.
AI.result: On both seen and out-of-vocabulary splits (259 notes in total), the hybrid pipeline attains very high Test-Time Pair F1 scores of 0.997 (seen) and 0.986 (OOV), with a 0.00-day mean absolute error in time normalization. Generative baselines achieve strong action extraction alone (action F1 up to 0.992 for LLaMA-3 and 0.963 for GPT-4o-mini on seen data), but their performance drops substantially when joint (action, time) pairs are evaluated: Pair F1 remains around 0.51–0.57 for LLaMA-3 and 0.53 for GPT-4o-mini. Confidence intervals for these baselines do not overlap with those of the hybrid pipeline, indicating a statistically significant performance gap.
AI.conclusion: A pipeline that separates learned entity recognition from deterministic date arithmetic substantially outperforms end-to-end generative models for extracting (action, time) follow-up instructions in outpatient notes on this synthetic benchmark. The hybrid approach also generalizes well to unseen actions and provides clearer insights into error modes. While these results are promising, validation on real-world electronic health record notes is still needed; the authors note an initial realism assessment in the Limitations section and position real-note transfer as crucial future work.

title: A Dataset of Robot-Patient and Doctor-Patient Medical Dialogues for Spoken Language Processing Tasks
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have brought huge improvements to Artificial Intelligence (AI), which can be applied to general-purpose tasks. However, their application to textual or spoken medical consultations is still an open research problem. This paper proposes MeDial-Speech, a novel speech dataset for training and evaluating Med-AIs that can carry out consultations with patients. It was collected in realistic environments from robot-patient and doctor-patient dialogues, contains 111+ hours of speech data (without data augmentation), and covers four health conditions: Lewy body dementia, heart failure, shoulder pain, and angina. In addition, we propose a dialogue benchmark via sentence selection (with 20 options) to evaluate three state-of-the-art LLMs: GPT-5 mini, DeepSeek-V3, and Claude Sonnet 4. Experimental results reveal that Claude Sonnet 4 is the best in sentence selection, with 71.1% accuracy using manual transcriptions and 74.7% using automatic transcriptions, and that all LLMs are highly overconfident in their probabilistic predictions, regardless of selecting correct or incorrect sentences in medical dialogues. This dataset is free of charge for non-commercial purposes at: https://huggingface.co/datasets/hcuayahu/MeDial-Speech

AI.tldr: Introduces MeDial-Speech, a 111+ hour realistic medical dialogue speech dataset and a sentence-selection benchmark to evaluate LLMs in spoken medical consultations.
AI.motivation: LLMs excel at general-purpose tasks but their effectiveness in textual or spoken medical consultations is underexplored. Realistic, domain-specific speech datasets and quantitative benchmarks are needed to systematically train and evaluate Med-AIs that interact with patients via dialogue.
AI.method: Collect 111+ hours of real-environment speech data from robot-patient and doctor-patient consultations across four conditions (Lewy body dementia, heart failure, shoulder pain, angina). Build a dialogue benchmark where models choose the correct next sentence from 20 candidates. Evaluate three advanced LLMs (GPT-5 mini, DeepSeek-V3, Claude Sonnet 4) using both manual and automatic transcriptions and analyze their prediction confidence/calibration.
AI.result: Claude Sonnet 4 achieves the highest accuracy in sentence selection (71.1% with manual transcripts, 74.7% with automatic transcripts). All tested LLMs exhibit strong overconfidence in their probabilistic predictions, regardless of whether their selected sentence is correct or incorrect, revealing poor calibration in medical dialogue settings.
AI.conclusion: MeDial-Speech provides a substantial, realistic speech resource and an accompanying benchmark for developing and testing Med-AIs for spoken consultations. Current state-of-the-art LLMs, while reasonably accurate in sentence selection, are poorly calibrated and overconfident, indicating that simply deploying them in medical dialogue scenarios is risky and that improved calibration and safety mechanisms are needed. The dataset is publicly available for non-commercial research use.

title: Hubness, Not Anisotropy, Drives Cross-Lingual Retrieval Asymmetry in Multilingual Embedding Models
summary: Multilingual embedding models are deployed under the assumption that cross-lingual retrieval is symmetric: if a query in language A retrieves its translation in language B, the reverse should also hold. In practice it does not. Using a parallel corpus of 6,518 idiomatic and proverbial expressions in English, Bangla, Hindi, and Arabic, embedded by five production-grade encoders (Gemini, Mistral, OpenAI-L, OpenAI-S, Qwen), we formalise this failure as a deficit in mutual nearest-neighbour reciprocity and test a single mechanistic claim: among the geometric pathologies of multilingual spaces, hubness, not anisotropy, centroid drift, or magnitude, is the dominant causal driver. Across five pre-registered experiments with falsification conditions specified in advance, hub mass dominates a joint regression on reciprocity (49.5% dominance share, 1.68x the next predictor; partial R^2 = 0.302 versus 0.003 for anisotropy), while a hub-aware score correction (CSLS) closes 63.5% of the worst-to-best reciprocity gap and yields a mean within-model effect size 130x larger than surgical hub-vector ablation. The latter contrast pinpoints the mechanism: hubness is a pathology of the similarity metric, not of individual hub vectors. We resolve the well-known anisotropy-hubness paradox by showing the two are statistically dissociable, and we recommend replacing cosine similarity with CSLS as the default retrieval metric for multilingual embedding pipelines.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that multilingual retrieval failures between languages are mainly caused by hubness in the embedding space and that using CSLS instead of cosine greatly improves reciprocal cross-lingual retrieval.
AI.motivation: Multilingual embedding models assume symmetrical cross-lingual retrieval, but in practice a translation retrieved from A→B often fails in B→A. Existing explanations invoke several geometric issues (anisotropy, centroid drift, magnitude), but their relative causal impact is unclear, especially in realistic production-grade encoders. This paper aims to identify which geometric factor actually drives the asymmetry and how to fix it in practical systems.
AI.method: The authors build a parallel corpus of 6,518 idiomatic / proverbial expressions in four languages (English, Bangla, Hindi, Arabic) and embed it with five large multilingual encoders (Gemini, Mistral, OpenAI-L, OpenAI-S, Qwen). They formalise cross-lingual symmetry failure as low mutual nearest-neighbour reciprocity between translation pairs. They design five pre-registered experiments, with falsification criteria set in advance, to test whether hubness versus other geometric properties (anisotropy, centroid drift, vector magnitude) best explains reciprocity. They use dominance analysis and partial R² in joint regressions, compare hub-aware rescoring (CSLS) against surgical hub-vector ablation, and examine the statistical relation between anisotropy and hubness.
AI.result: Hub mass is the strongest predictor of reciprocity deficits, contributing about half of the dominance share in regression (49.5%) and a partial R² of 0.302, far exceeding anisotropy (partial R² = 0.003). Applying CSLS, a hub-aware similarity correction, closes 63.5% of the gap between the worst and best reciprocity and produces effect sizes on average 130× larger than ablation of hub vectors. They also show that anisotropy and hubness are statistically dissociable, resolving a known paradox that conflated them.
AI.conclusion: Asymmetries in multilingual cross-lingual retrieval are primarily driven by hubness in the similarity structure rather than by global embedding geometry such as anisotropy or centroid drift. Hubness acts at the level of the similarity metric, not specific hub vectors. Practitioners should replace plain cosine similarity with CSLS as the default retrieval metric in multilingual embedding systems to substantially improve reciprocal translation retrieval.

title: Beyond a Single Direction: Chain-of-Thought Disrupts Simple Steering of Refusal
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) generate chain-of-thought (CoT) traces before producing final outputs, introducing a dynamic internal state that may complicate control mechanisms such as refusal. Unlike instruction-tuned LLMs, where refusal is mediated by a single directional subspace, refusal in large reasoning models (LRMs) additionally depends on the CoT. In DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B, activation steering reverses refusal in only 39% of cases when the CoT is kept fixed, but removing the CoT entirely increases this to 70%, indicating that the CoT actively reinforces refusal. In a two-stage intervention where the model regenerates its CoT under activation steering, refusal is reversed in 94% of cases, while the resulting CoT alone retains 48% of this effect even after steering is removed. This suggests that the CoT can carry and reconstruct the compliance signal independently. These findings indicate that refusal in LRMs is jointly encoded in residual stream activations and CoT. This joint activation makes LRM more robust against activation-level interventions alone, but exposes CoT to a possible alternative surface attack.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how refusals (when a model declines to answer) work in large reasoning models that use chain-of-thought, showing refusal is jointly encoded in both hidden activations and the generated reasoning, making it harder to override via activation steering but opening an attack surface via manipulating the chain-of-thought.
AI.motivation: Existing work on controllability and refusal focuses on standard instruction-tuned LLMs, where refusal can often be controlled via a relatively simple activation-level intervention in a single semantic subspace. Large reasoning models (LRMs) differ because they explicitly generate chain-of-thought (CoT) traces as an internal state before giving an answer. It is unclear how this additional structured internal state interacts with refusal behavior and whether existing activation-steering methods are sufficient for controlling refusals in LRMs. Understanding this is important both for safety (reliable refusals) and for robustness against jailbreaks or steering attacks.
AI.method: The authors empirically study refusal behavior in DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B, a large reasoning model, using activation steering techniques. They compare several settings: (1) applying steering while keeping the existing chain-of-thought fixed, (2) removing the CoT entirely, and (3) a two-stage procedure where they first steer the model while it regenerates its CoT and then evaluate refusal and the residual influence of the new CoT once steering is removed. Across these settings they measure how often steering can flip a refusal into compliance and how much of the compliance signal is preserved in the generated CoT itself.
AI.result: When the chain-of-thought is kept fixed, activation steering only reverses refusals in 39% of cases, showing limited effectiveness. If the CoT is removed, the success rate of steering rises to 70%, demonstrating that the CoT actively supports refusal. In a two-stage setting where the model regenerates its CoT under activation steering, refusals are reversed in 94% of cases, and the resulting CoT alone preserves 48% of this compliance effect even after steering is turned off. This implies that the CoT can independently encode and reconstruct a compliance signal that was initially introduced via activation steering.
AI.conclusion: Refusal behavior in large reasoning models is not localized to a simple activation direction as in many instruction-tuned LLMs, but is jointly encoded in residual stream activations and the generated chain-of-thought. The CoT can absorb, carry, and later reconstruct compliance signals, making LRMs more robust to naive activation-level interventions that ignore the CoT but also creating a new attack surface: manipulating or regenerating chain-of-thought to circumvent refusal. Any safety or control method for LRMs must therefore consider both hidden activations and CoT dynamics, rather than treating refusal as a purely activation-level phenomenon.

title: LATTE: Forecasting Peer Anchored Preference Trajectories for Personalized LLM Generation
summary: Personalized generation with frozen large language models requires a conditioning signal that is both compact and current. Existing personalization methods typically retrieve or summarize user histories in text, or compress them into static latent profiles and soft prompts. These approaches are efficient, but they treat a user's past behavior as an aggregate profile and therefore mix stable identity, recent drift, and item content in the same representation. We propose LAtent Trajectory Tracking and Extrapolation (LATTE), a framework that represents personalization as forecasting a peer anchored relative preference state. For each historical session, LATTE subtracts a time masked baseline formed from comparable users who responded to the same item, producing a state that measures how the target user differs from peers under a shared item context. A lightweight sequence predictor then forecasts the next state in this trajectory, and a State to Token Bridge injects the forecast into a frozen instruction tuned LLM through a single anchored soft token. We provide a latent factor analysis showing when peer anchoring cancels shared item variation and why temporal forecasting trades off stale averages against noisy recent states. Experiments on Amazon Reviews 2023 and MemoryCD show that LATTE consistently outperforms retrieval, summary memory, static latent profiles, difference aware latent profiles, and soft prompt compression baselines. On Amazon Reviews 2023, LATTE improves average ROUGE-L from 0.219 for a static latent profile and 0.245 for the strongest added latent compression baseline to 0.259. Additional pairwise comparisons and diagnostic analyses suggest that the improvement is mainly due to forecasting user-specific trajectory information, rather than merely adding a soft prompt interface.

AI.tldr: LATTE is a personalization framework that models users as trajectories of peer-anchored preference states and forecasts the next state to condition a frozen LLM via a single soft token.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM personalization compresses user history into static or aggregate representations (retrieval, summaries, static profiles, soft prompts), which mix stable identity and recent preference drift, and conflate user-specific behavior with item content. This leads to stale or noisy conditioning signals that are not temporally and contextually disentangled.
AI.method: LATTE constructs, for each user interaction session, a latent state defined as the difference between the target user and a peer baseline: comparable users who interacted with the same item, with their influence time-masked to avoid leakage. This peer anchoring cancels shared item effects and isolates user-specific preferences. A lightweight sequence model predicts the next latent state in the trajectory. A State-to-Token Bridge maps the predicted state into a single anchored soft token that conditions a frozen, instruction-tuned LLM for generation. The paper also performs latent factor analysis to study when peer anchoring is effective and analyzes the trade-off between using long-term averages and recent states for forecasting.
AI.result: On Amazon Reviews 2023 and MemoryCD benchmarks, LATTE consistently outperforms several baselines: retrieval-based personalization, summary memory, static latent profiles, difference-aware latent profiles, and soft-prompt compression. On Amazon Reviews 2023, LATTE raises average ROUGE-L from 0.219 (static latent profile) and 0.245 (best latent compression baseline) to 0.259. Pairwise and diagnostic analyses show that gains are mainly due to trajectory forecasting, not just the existence of a soft-prompt interface.
AI.conclusion: Personalization for frozen LLMs can be more effective when modeled as forecasting user-specific trajectories in a peer-anchored latent space rather than aggregating histories into static profiles or summaries. Peer anchoring decouples user preference from item content, and temporal forecasting provides a compact, up-to-date conditioning signal that can be injected into an instruction-tuned LLM via a single soft token to yield better personalized generation.

title: The Attribution Blind Spot: Detecting When Language Models Rely on Memory Rather Than Retrieved Context
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation promises to ground language model outputs in external evidence, yet the field has no reliable way to verify whether retrieved context actually governs generation -- a prerequisite for any high-stakes deployment. The standard assumption, that context-consistent output implies context-governed output, breaks when the retrieved document overlaps with the model's pretraining data: the model can produce faithful-looking text entirely from parametric memory, and both pathways yield indistinguishable output. We name this failure the attribution blind spot and introduce Computational Reality Monitoring (CRM) to address it. CRM operationalizes a principle adapted from cognitive science's reality monitoring framework: comparing internal representations with and without context reveals membership-conditioned representational divergence that output-level monitors systematically miss. CRM does not certify which source an individual generation used; it detects whether pretraining exposure leaves a measurable internal trajectory signature, establishing a necessary substrate for source attribution. Across nine model variants spanning three families, this divergence concentrates in architecture-specific layer patterns, receives converging support from block-level noise intervention, and generalizes across tasks and datasets while collapsing on domain-confounded benchmarks. The attribution blind spot is measurable and partially addressable: internal representations carry a diagnostic signal invisible at the output level, establishing a foundation for systems whose internal awareness of evidence provenance governs their external behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a core failure in retrieval-augmented generation: models can appear to use retrieved context while actually relying on memorized pretraining data, and output alone cannot reveal this. It proposes Computational Reality Monitoring (CRM), a method that inspects internal representations with and without context to detect whether pretraining leaves a distinct internal signature, thereby laying groundwork for reliable source attribution in language models.
AI.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is supposed to make language models depend on external documents for their answers, which is critical for trust, safety, and updatability. However, there is currently no robust way to verify that a model’s output is truly governed by the retrieved context instead of its own parametric memory. This is especially problematic when the retrieved evidence overlaps with pretraining data, because then the model can generate context-consistent answers purely from memory, making it impossible to distinguish sources based on output alone. The authors aim to expose and characterize this "attribution blind spot" and to provide a principled method to detect when internal parametric knowledge is influencing generation despite the presence of external context.
AI.method: The authors define the "attribution blind spot" where models can produce outputs that match retrieved context while actually relying on memorized pretraining data. To address this, they introduce Computational Reality Monitoring (CRM), inspired by cognitive science’s reality monitoring. CRM compares a model’s internal representations (hidden states/activations) during generation under two conditions: with retrieved context and without it. By analyzing the membership-conditioned divergence between these trajectories, CRM detects whether pretraining exposure leaves a systematic internal signature. They apply CRM across nine variants from three model families, analyze which layers show the strongest divergence, and validate findings via block-level noise interventions and evaluations across diverse tasks, datasets, and domain-confounded benchmarks.
AI.result: CRM reveals that the attribution blind spot is real and quantifiable: internal representations diverge in ways that correlate with whether the model has seen the content in pretraining, even when the outputs look the same. This divergence is not uniform: it clusters in architecture-specific layer patterns, indicating that certain layers are more involved in encoding pretraining-derived knowledge versus context-driven reasoning. Noise interventions at the block level support the causal relevance of these layers for the divergence signal. CRM’s diagnostic signal generalizes across different tasks and datasets, but it weakens or collapses in benchmarks where domain confounds make attribution intrinsically ambiguous.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that source attribution in retrieval-augmented generation cannot rely solely on output behavior because of an "attribution blind spot" caused by overlapping pretraining and retrieved data. Internal activations contain a diagnostic signal about whether pretraining knowledge is influencing generation, which CRM can capture even when outputs are indistinguishable. Although CRM cannot certify the source of any single output, it provides a necessary substrate for attribution and a foundation for building systems in which a model’s internal awareness of evidence provenance can be used to govern its external behavior in a more reliable way.

title: Granuscore: A Reference-Free Measure of Granularity for Text Analysis and Question Answering
summary: Natural language conveys information at varying levels of granularity, from fine-grained references to broad descriptions. While granularity is fundamental to human communication, existing measures mostly capture surface detail or sentence specificity. We introduce Granuscore, a reference-free measure of granularity that leverages structural properties of a hierarchical embedding space. Granuscore reliably recovers hierarchical orderings on the Granola-EQ dataset and captures expected differences in granularity across discourse contexts. Across domains, we further show that Granuscore explains non-linear variation in sentence specificity beyond sentence length. Finally, we apply Granuscore to four question-answering benchmarks and analyze how granularity differs for questions, gold answers, and model outputs across response outcomes. The analysis reveals consistent differences in model behavior and provides a principled lens for characterizing the difficulty of QA datasets. Together, the results position Granuscore as a scalable, broadly applicable tool for analyzing granularity in text.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Granuscore, a new reference-free metric to quantify how fine- or coarse-grained text is, using a hierarchical embedding space.
AI.motivation: Existing measures of textual granularity mainly capture surface details or crude sentence specificity and fail to reflect the deeper, hierarchical nature of information in language. The authors want a principled, scalable way to measure how fine- or coarse-grained a piece of text is, in order to better analyze discourse, specificity, and behavior of NLP systems, especially in QA tasks.
AI.method: They design Granuscore, a metric built on structural properties of a hierarchical embedding space, rather than on references or shallow surface cues. They validate it by checking whether it recovers known hierarchical orderings in the Granola-EQ dataset, and by testing whether it captures expected granularity differences across discourse contexts and domains. They then apply the metric to multiple QA benchmarks, comparing scores for questions, gold answers, and model outputs, and relating these scores to response outcomes.
AI.result: Granuscore successfully recovers hierarchical orderings on the Granola-EQ dataset and detects intuitive differences in granularity across discourse situations. It accounts for non-linear variation in sentence specificity that is not explained by sentence length alone. In QA benchmarks, it reveals consistent and interpretable differences in the granularity of gold answers vs. model outputs and connects these to performance differences and dataset difficulty.
AI.conclusion: Granuscore is an effective, scalable, and broadly applicable reference-free tool for measuring textual granularity. It aligns with hierarchical structure in data, provides explanatory power beyond simple factors like length, and offers a principled lens for analyzing model behavior and the difficulty of QA datasets.

title: LiveK12Bench: Have Large Multimodal Models Truly Conquered High School-level Examinations?
summary: Advanced Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have demonstrated impressive performance in K-12 reasoning tasks, exhibiting great promise as intelligent tutors. Realizing this potential requires models to navigate real-world examinations effectively, yet most existing benchmarks fail to capture the complexity of authentic testing environments. Specifically, most datasets are static, prone to data contamination, and are often confined to restricted modalities, disciplines, and evaluation criteria. To address these issues, we introduce LiveK12Bench, a dynamic, holistic, multi-disciplinary benchmark designed to evaluate the reasoning abilities of LMMs in realistic examination scenarios. LiveK12Bench comprises 2K+ verified questions spanning Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, and Biology, sourced from the latest real-world exam papers and designed to grow over time. Our framework features several core innovations: 1) featuring an automated pipeline that continuously ingests and parses the latest examination papers to mitigate data leakage; and 2) proposing a novel `Mock Exam' evaluation scheme, which assesses the ability to complete end-to-end exams autonomously with accurate and efficient reasoning paths. Extensive experiments on 12 LMMs reveal that advanced models suffer substantial performance degradation under exam-realistic constraints: GPT-5's score drops from 79 to 53 (out of 100) when process rigor and efficiency are jointly evaluated. Our findings expose critical vulnerabilities, such as sensitivity to complex visual layouts, highlighting the gap between idealized reasoning capabilities and true educational readiness. Both code and dataset are publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LiveK12Bench, a dynamic, real-exam-style benchmark to evaluate large multimodal models’ K–12 reasoning in realistic conditions, revealing significant performance drops under exam-like constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing K–12 benchmarks for large multimodal models are mostly static, susceptible to training-data contamination, and limited in modalities, disciplines, and evaluation schemes. They do not faithfully represent real-world exam conditions, where models must handle fresh questions, complex layouts, multiple subjects, and end-to-end exam workflows. The authors aim to build a benchmark that better reflects authentic examination environments to more accurately measure LMMs’ readiness as intelligent tutors or test-takers.
AI.method: They construct LiveK12Bench, a continuously growing benchmark of 2K+ questions from up-to-date real K–12 exam papers in Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, and Biology. The pipeline automatically collects and parses the latest exams to reduce leakage and keep the benchmark current. They also introduce a “Mock Exam” evaluation scheme where models must autonomously complete full exams, emphasizing not only final accuracy but also the rigor and efficiency of reasoning processes under realistic constraints (e.g., visual layouts, timing/format). Twelve state-of-the-art LMMs are systematically evaluated on this benchmark.
AI.result: Across 12 LMMs, the authors observe notable performance degradation when shifting from idealized, per-question evaluation to exam-realistic conditions that jointly assess reasoning rigor and efficiency. For example, a strong model’s score (GPT-5) drops from 79/100 to 53/100 under the Mock Exam scheme. The experiments identify issues such as susceptibility to complex visual layouts and difficulties sustaining accurate reasoning across full-length exams.
AI.conclusion: LiveK12Bench better captures the demands of real-world K–12 examinations for large multimodal models, uncovering a significant gap between their benchmarked reasoning ability and practical educational readiness. The benchmark and Mock Exam protocol highlight vulnerabilities—especially to complex visual formatting and end-to-end exam constraints—and provide a more realistic, evolving testbed for future model development and evaluation. The authors release the code and dataset to support ongoing research and improvement in educational LMMs.

title: Bounded Path Context: A Controlled Study of Visible Path History in LLM-Based Knowledge Graph Question Answering
summary: LLM-based knowledge-graph question answering (KGQA) delegates graph traversal to language models, turning each question into a sequence of local relation-selection decisions repeated across beams and hops. A common but untested default is to serialize the complete partial path into every routing prompt, even though the controller already maintains this path as exact symbolic state. Bounded Path Context (BPC) decouples these two roles: the controller retains full paths in symbolic memory for answer extraction and audit, while the relation-selection prompt exposes only the question, the current entity, outgoing relation candidates, and at most the last K hops. A controlled sweep over K -- fixing graph neighborhoods, beam budget, depth, decoding, and answer-extraction format -- shows that bounded histories match or exceed full-history prompting on complete WebQSP and CWQ test sets with Qwen3.5-9B-AWQ: K=1 achieves 0.487 answer-set F1 on WebQSP versus 0.472 for full history, and K=0 reaches 0.287 on CWQ versus 0.274, with 9.7% and 12.1% fewer input tokens respectively. At the 4B scale, K=1 remains the strongest setting on both benchmarks. Per-example analysis reveals that 71-84% of examples are unaffected by history length, while the affected cases expose when prior hops disambiguate versus distract. These results suggest that path serialization length is better treated as a tunable interface variable than as a default assumption in LLM-based graph controllers.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how much of a reasoning path needs to be serialized into prompts for LLM-based knowledge-graph question answering and finds that short, bounded path histories can match or outperform full-history prompting while saving tokens.
AI.motivation: Most LLM-based KGQA systems serialize the full traversed path into every prompt step, assuming this is necessary for accurate relation selection. This increases token usage and latency, and it is unclear whether long histories actually help. The paper aims to test this assumption and determine whether shorter, bounded path histories suffice without hurting—and possibly improving—performance.
AI.method: They propose Bounded Path Context (BPC), where an external controller keeps the exact full path symbolically, but the LLM used for relation selection only sees: (1) the question, (2) the current entity, (3) outgoing relation candidates, and (4) at most the last K hops in the path. They run controlled experiments sweeping K (including K=0,1 and full history) while holding constant graph neighborhoods, beam size, search depth, decoding strategy, and answer-extraction format. They evaluate on the WebQSP and CWQ KGQA benchmarks using Qwen3.5-9B-AWQ and a 4B model, comparing answer-set F1 and token counts, and analyze per-example sensitivity to history length.
AI.result: On WebQSP with Qwen3.5-9B-AWQ, K=1 achieves 0.487 answer-set F1, outperforming full-history prompting at 0.472 while using 9.7% fewer input tokens. On CWQ, K=0 attains 0.287 F1 versus 0.274 for full history, with 12.1% fewer tokens. At the 4B model scale, K=1 remains the best-performing setting on both datasets. Per-example analysis shows that 71–84% of questions have identical outcomes regardless of history length, and that among the remaining cases, longer histories sometimes help disambiguate but also frequently distract the model.
AI.conclusion: Full path serialization is not inherently necessary for LLM-based KGQA controllers and can even be suboptimal. Treating path-history length as a tunable interface parameter, with bounded context (such as K=0 or K=1), can match or surpass full-history performance while reducing token usage. This reframes prompt design for graph traversal as an interface optimization problem rather than a fixed design choice, enabling more efficient and sometimes more accurate KGQA systems.

title: Composition Collapse: Stable Factual Knowledge Does Not Imply Compositional Reasoning
summary: Post-training is routinely evaluated through aggregate benchmark scores that treat multi-hop reasoning as a single capability -- as if a model that answers more questions correctly must be better at assembling facts. We show that this assumption can be misleading: recipes with statistically indistinguishable atomic knowledge produce composition behaviour separated by over 40 percentage points, a phenomenon we call composition collapse: the systematic failure to assemble stably-known facts into chains, invisible to aggregate metrics. We introduce a double-gate protocol that changes the estimand from an aggregate compositionality gap to residual composition failure conditioned on stable atomic access, decomposing post-training gains into three independent channels: atomic stability, residual composition, and critical depth. On a benchmark of temporal factual chains spanning depths 2--11 across four post-training recipes, this decomposition reveals that post-training objectives shift composition capability in directions that aggregate metrics mask, and suggests that claims about multi-hop reasoning improvement should be accompanied by atomic-gate-controlled composition metrics. Diagnostic probes further show that a substantial share of measured composition failure reflects generation-time computation constraints rather than permanent inability to compose.

AI.tldr: They study why models that look equally good on atomic facts can differ a lot in multi-hop reasoning, introduce a new evaluation protocol, and show hidden weaknesses in composition that standard metrics miss.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training evaluation uses aggregate scores that conflate atomic factual knowledge with the ability to compose that knowledge in multi-hop reasoning. This can hide important differences: models with similar factual knowledge can have very different abilities to chain facts together. The authors want a more precise way to measure and decompose multi-hop reasoning performance, to understand what post-training is really improving.
AI.method: They define and analyze a phenomenon called composition collapse, where models systematically fail to compose facts they individually know. To study this, they propose a double-gate evaluation protocol that conditions on the model’s stable access to atomic facts. This changes the estimand from a raw compositionality gap to residual composition failure given atomic knowledge.
