Using temporal factual chain benchmarks with depths from 2 to 11 and four different post-training recipes, they decompose performance into three parts: atomic stability (how reliably atomic facts are accessed), residual composition (how well facts are chained when known), and critical depth (how performance degrades with chain length). They also apply diagnostic probes to distinguish failures due to generation-time computation constraints from true representational inability to compose.
AI.result: They find that different post-training recipes with statistically indistinguishable atomic knowledge can have composition behavior that differs by over 40 percentage points. The decomposition shows that post-training can improve or hurt composition even when aggregate metrics suggest similar or improved performance. It reveals shifts along the three channels (atomic stability, residual composition, critical depth) that raw benchmarks mask. Probes indicate that a significant portion of what appears as composition failure is actually due to inference-time or generation-time computation limits, not a permanent lack of compositional capability in the model.
AI.conclusion: Aggregate benchmark scores are insufficient to characterize multi-hop reasoning. Composition collapse can remain hidden unless evaluation explicitly conditions on stable access to atomic facts. The proposed double-gate protocol and decomposition provide a clearer picture of how post-training affects reasoning, and the authors argue that future claims about multi-hop reasoning improvements should report atomic-gate-controlled composition metrics. Moreover, some apparent failures to compose may be mitigated by addressing generation-time computation constraints rather than changing the underlying representations.

title: Why Prompt Optimization Works, and Why It Sometimes Doesn't: A Causal-Inspired Edit-Level Analysis
summary: Automated prompt optimization methods (e.g., DSpy, TextGrad) can substantially improve the performance of large language model (LLM), however, their generalization ability across different tasks remains underperformed. In practice, the superiority of the optimized prompt on one benchmark often fails to transfer to another, and this limitation persists even when switching across different LLM backbones. To investigate the underexplored sources of heterogeneity in prompt performance, we conduct a causal inference-inspired observational analysis of optimized prompts across a diverse set of optimization frameworks, LLM backbones, and NLP benchmarks. To achieve the goal, we build upon the propensity-adjusted associational analysis together with multiple complementary representations of prompt edits, where the consistent task-conditioned edits patterns are identified. We find that complexity-increasing and meta-instructional edits are negatively associated with mathematical and multi-hop reasoning performance, whereas step-by-step and meta-cognitive edits improve logical and sequential reasoning tasks. These effects are robust across cognitive-load annotations, surface-level text features, and edit-motif analyses, and can generalize across optimization frameworks. Overall, these results indicate that prompt optimization failures arise from systematic interactions between edit families and task characteristics rather than random optimization artifacts, providing feature-level characterization of optimizer behavior and motivating future task-conditioned optimizer design.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why automatically optimized prompts for LLMs often fail to generalize across tasks, identifying systematic relationships between types of prompt edits and task characteristics, and suggesting task-conditioned optimizer design.
AI.motivation: Although automatic prompt optimization methods can significantly boost LLM performance on specific benchmarks, their gains usually do not transfer well to new tasks or even to other models. This poor generalization suggests underexplored, systematic sources of heterogeneity in how different prompt edits interact with task types. The authors aim to understand these sources by moving beyond black-box evaluation of whole prompts and instead analyzing what kinds of edits are being made, and how these edits causally relate to performance across diverse tasks and optimization frameworks.
AI.method: The authors conduct an observational, causal-inference-inspired study of prompts produced by various automatic optimization frameworks across multiple LLM backbones and NLP benchmarks. They use propensity-adjusted associational analysis to control for confounding factors when relating specific prompt edit types to task performance. They define and extract multiple complementary representations of prompt edits (e.g., complexity-increasing, meta-instructional, step-by-step, meta-cognitive), then look for consistent task-conditioned edit patterns and their association with performance. Robustness checks involve controlling for cognitive-load annotations, surface-level text statistics, and recurring edit motifs.
AI.result: They identify systematic relationships between categories of prompt edits and task performance. Edits that increase complexity or add meta-instructions tend to hurt performance on mathematical and multi-hop reasoning tasks. In contrast, edits that encourage step-by-step reasoning or meta-cognitive reflection improve performance on logical and sequential reasoning tasks. These effects hold across different prompt optimization frameworks, LLM backbones, and are robust to controls for cognitive load, shallow text features, and motif-level analyses. The observed patterns generalize beyond any single optimizer or model.
AI.conclusion: Prompt optimization failures are not random artifacts of search procedures but stem from structured interactions between families of prompt edits and specific task characteristics. The paper provides a feature-level characterization of optimizer behavior, showing which edit types help or hurt which task categories. This understanding motivates the development of future prompt optimizers that are explicitly task-conditioned, selecting or constraining edit families based on task type to achieve more reliable generalization across benchmarks and models.

title: What Makes Chain-of-Thought Work at Probe Time? Local Co-occurrence Rather Than Global Derivation
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting reliably improves language-model accuracy, but which properties of a rationale text drive the improvement is poorly understood. Prior work has largely studied generation-time behavior. We instead ask a probe-time question: given a fixed rationale in context, what in that text changes the answer? We identify two complementary sources of the gain. First, even a globally word-shuffled rationale substantially outperforms the no-rationale baseline, indicating a strong lexical activation effect. More importantly, the additional gain from structured text appears to arise less from sentence-level logical ordering and more from short-range token adjacency. Preserving contiguous windows of just $n^\star{=}2$--$3$ tokens recovers most of the remaining gain toward full CoT performance. Supporting experiments rule out copying of explicit answer declarations or answer values, as well as full grammatical realization, as primary drivers. Further generalization experiments show that the qualitative pattern remains stable across multiple model families, parameter scales, and datasets. These results support a local co-occurrence activation (LCA) account of probe-time CoT, in which the observed gains appear to arise primarily from lexical activation and short-range token co-occurrence rather than sentence-level logical derivation.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why chain-of-thought (CoT) prompts help language models, finding that most benefits come from lexical activation and short-range token co-occurrence rather than full logical reasoning.
AI.motivation: Although CoT prompting is known to improve language model performance, it is unclear what aspects of the rationale text actually cause better answers. Prior studies focus on generation-time behavior and implicitly assume sentence-level logical reasoning is key. The authors want to disentangle which properties of a fixed rationale—its words, order, local vs global structure—are responsible for performance gains.
AI.method: The authors take an analysis-at-probe-time approach: instead of changing how rationales are generated, they fix a given rationale and manipulate its structure before feeding it to the model. They compare original CoT rationales with various perturbed versions, including globally word-shuffled text, rationales that preserve only short contiguous token windows, and other variants that disrupt sentence-level order and grammar. They also perform control experiments to eliminate trivial mechanisms such as copying explicit answers or answer values. This is tested across multiple model families, sizes, and datasets to evaluate robustness.
AI.result: They find that even a globally word-shuffled rationale gives a large performance boost over no rationale, showing a strong lexical activation effect: the mere presence of relevant words helps. Additional gains from preserving structure are mostly explained by short-range token adjacency, not full sentence-level logical order. Keeping contiguous windows of only 2–3 tokens is enough to recover most of the remaining gap to full CoT performance. Experiments rule out answer copying and full grammaticality as main factors, and the same qualitative behavior appears across different models and tasks.
AI.conclusion: The study supports a local co-occurrence activation (LCA) account of CoT prompting at probe time. CoT’s benefits largely stem from lexical activation and short-range token co-occurrence patterns rather than from models performing genuine sentence-level logical reasoning over the rationale. This challenges interpretations of CoT gains as clear evidence of step-by-step logical derivation inside current language models.

title: AI evaluation may bias perceptions: The importance of context in interpreting academic writing
summary: This paper examines how estimates of AI use in scientific writing can be biased when evaluation methods ignore contextual differences across countries and fields. Using large-scale data on journal publications from Dimensions, we construct AI-likeness benchmarks based on differences between human-written and LLM-rephrased abstracts. We show that a pooled benchmark may confound pre-existing stylistic variation with AI-generated text, producing substantial distortions across country-field groups even in pre-LLM publications. In contrast, country-field-specific benchmarks attenuate such distortions and provide a more credible baseline for comparison. Applying these methods to publications in 2025 reveals that the pooled benchmark systematically overestimates AI use in certain countries and fields while underestimating it in others. These findings highlight the importance of context-aware measurement for accurate and equitable evaluation of AI use in science.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that naive, pooled AI-detection benchmarks misinterpret existing stylistic differences across countries and disciplines as AI use, and proposes context-specific benchmarks that give fairer, more accurate estimates of AI-assisted scientific writing.
AI.motivation: As AI tools become common in scientific writing, there is growing interest in measuring their use across countries and fields. However, existing measurement approaches often ignore that writing styles vary naturally by region and discipline, which can cause false signals of AI use and biased cross-group comparisons. The authors aim to design an evaluation approach that distinguishes genuine AI signals from pre-existing stylistic variation so that assessments of AI use in science are accurate and equitable.
AI.method: The authors use large-scale journal publication data from the Dimensions database. They generate AI-likeness benchmarks by taking human-written abstracts and creating LLM-rephrased versions, then comparing linguistic patterns between the two. They first build a pooled benchmark that ignores country and field, and then construct country-field-specific benchmarks that account for contextual variation. They analyze how these different benchmarks classify both pre-LLM-era and 2025 publications, quantifying distortions in estimated AI use across country-field groups.
AI.result: The pooled, context-agnostic benchmark confounds natural stylistic differences (e.g., across countries and scientific fields) with AI-like writing, producing large apparent differences in “AI use” even for pre-LLM publications where actual AI use should be negligible. In contrast, country-field-specific benchmarks substantially reduce these spurious disparities and yield a more reliable baseline. When applied to 2025 publications, the pooled benchmark is shown to systematically overestimate AI use in some countries and fields and underestimate it in others, relative to the context-aware benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Context-free AI-detection benchmarks in scientific writing can be seriously biased because they mistake legitimate cross-country and cross-field stylistic variation for AI-generated text. Building country- and field-specific benchmarks yields more credible, equitable estimates of AI use over time and across groups. The paper concludes that future efforts to monitor AI in science must incorporate contextual information into their measurement strategies to avoid distorted and potentially unfair evaluations.

title: Helicase: Uncertainty-Guided Supply Chain Knowledge Graph Construction with Autonomous Multi-Agent LLMs
summary: LLM-based multi-agent systems have been widely adopted for knowledge retrieval and report generation, synthesizing known information through web search and textual reasoning. However, many critical information tasks in supply chains are not simple one-shot queries: they are structural inference problems requiring multi-hop reasoning across complex, fragmented web resources. Questions such as \textit{``Which Tesla components use lithium from Australian mines?''} have no answer in any single document; answers must be computationally synthesized through the autonomous construction and analysis of dynamic knowledge graphs assembled from fragmented, heterogeneous sources. Moreover, such discovery processes must be uncertainty-aware: decisions depend not only on answers but on calibrated confidence in their reliability, traceable to source quality and reasoning consistency. To address this capability gap, we propose \textit{Helicase}, an autonomous multi-agent LLM system for uncertainty-guided supply chain knowledge graph construction. \textit{Helicase} decomposes high-level supply-chain queries into executable investigation plans, coordinates specialized web-search, reasoning, and coding agents through iterative verification loops, and incrementally constructs query-specific supply chain knowledge graphs with per-fact uncertainty annotations. Its three-layer uncertainty framework tracks uncertainty at the action, trajectory, and memory layers, enabling both structural inference and calibrated confidence assessment. To evaluate autonomous reasoning across the full complexity spectrum, we introduce SCQA (Supply Chain Query Assessment), a benchmark of 80 supply chain queries organized into four quadrants spanning single-hop to multi-hop inference under both high and low data visibility.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Helicase, an autonomous multi-agent LLM system for constructing supply-chain knowledge graphs with uncertainty estimates, addressing complex multi-hop supply-chain queries.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems work well for single-shot knowledge retrieval but struggle with complex, structural inference tasks in supply chains that require multi-hop reasoning over fragmented, heterogeneous web sources. Additionally, these tasks demand explicit handling of uncertainty and traceability to support high-stakes decisions, which current systems lack.
AI.method: The authors design Helicase, an autonomous multi-agent LLM framework that decomposes high-level supply-chain questions into investigation plans, orchestrates web-search, reasoning, and coding agents, and builds query-specific supply chain knowledge graphs. It uses iterative verification loops and a three-layer uncertainty framework that tracks uncertainty at the action, trajectory, and memory levels, producing per-fact uncertainty annotations.
AI.result: Helicase is evaluated using SCQA, a new benchmark of 80 supply chain queries spanning single-hop to multi-hop inference and varying data visibility. The system demonstrates the ability to perform structural inference and provide calibrated confidence assessments for supply-chain questions; specific numerical results are not given in the abstract but the implication is that it performs well across the benchmark’s complexity quadrants.
AI.conclusion: Helicase narrows the gap between current LLM-based retrieval systems and the needs of real-world supply-chain intelligence by enabling autonomous, uncertainty-aware construction of dynamic knowledge graphs for complex queries. The SCQA benchmark further provides a structured way to test multi-hop, uncertainty-aware reasoning in supply-chain contexts.

title: Evidence Absence Is Not Evidence Insufficiency: Diagnosing NEI Construction Artifacts in Fact Verification
summary: Evidence absence is not evidence insufficiency, but fact verification benchmarks can make them observationally similar. The Not Enough Information (NEI) label is often operationalized through different evidence conditions, and that choice silently determines what a verifier learns and what its score can hide. We introduce NEI-CAP, a construction-aware diagnostic protocol for insufficient-evidence evaluation. Each NEI example carries the construction family that produced it; NEI-CAP audits shortcut cues, validates hard cases through human adjudication, and tests whether competence transfers across constructions. We instantiate the protocol in SciFact-style scientific verification, with FEVER and HoVer as bounded external controls. Across these settings, NEI competence does not transfer reliably: models trained on shortcut-prone constructions fail to recognize semantically related insufficient evidence, and mixed-construction training narrows but does not close the gap. Fixed-claim diagnostics further show that the evidence condition shifts confidence in the reference Support/Refute label, not only NEI recall, so an aggregate NEI score can hide which problem a model has actually solved.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that how we define and construct “Not Enough Information” (NEI) cases in fact‑verification benchmarks critically shapes what models learn, and that NEI skills do not reliably transfer across different constructions.
AI.motivation: Fact verification benchmarks use an NEI label for claims where available documents do not justify a Support or Refute decision. However, datasets implement NEI with different evidence-construction rules (e.g., missing documents, partially relevant documents, semantically related but non‑verifying evidence). These hidden design choices can create shortcut cues and make a model’s NEI score misleading: high performance may reflect learning superficial patterns tied to specific constructions rather than robust understanding of insufficient evidence. The authors want a systematic way to expose and evaluate these issues.
AI.method: They propose NEI-CAP, a “construction-aware” diagnostic protocol for NEI evaluation. Each NEI instance is tagged with the construction family that generated it (e.g., particular patterns of evidence insufficiency). The protocol (1) audits shortcut cues associated with each construction family, (2) uses human adjudication to validate genuinely hard NEI cases, and (3) tests cross-construction generalization: whether NEI competence learned from one construction transfers to others. They apply NEI-CAP to scientific fact verification in a SciFact-like setting and use FEVER and HoVer datasets as controlled external benchmarks. They run training and evaluation regimes including single-construction training, mixed-construction training, and fixed-claim diagnostics with varying evidence conditions.
AI.result: Models exhibit poor transfer of NEI competence across construction families. Systems trained on shortcut-prone NEI constructions fail to identify NEI cases that are semantically related but constructed differently. Training on a mixture of constructions improves but does not fully close the generalization gap. Fixed-claim diagnostics reveal that changing the evidence condition (how NEI is instantiated) not only affects NEI recall, but also shifts models’ confidence in the Support vs. Refute labels for the same claim, indicating entanglement between NEI handling and core label decisions.
AI.conclusion: The way NEI examples are constructed in fact-verification datasets crucially determines what models actually learn. NEI performance does not represent a single, transferable competence: models latch onto construction-specific shortcuts and fail to generalize to other forms of insufficient evidence, even under mixed training. Moreover, NEI design influences models’ Support/Refute behavior, meaning that aggregate NEI scores cannot reliably indicate whether a model has truly mastered recognizing insufficient evidence. Construction-aware protocols like NEI-CAP are needed to diagnose, interpret, and improve NEI evaluation in verification benchmarks.

title: Multi-Stakeholder LLM Alignment: Decomposing Estimation from Aggregation
summary: Multi-stakeholder tasks require one output to satisfy users with conflicting preferences. Holistic LLM judges conflate utility estimation and utility aggregation, yielding unstable implicit weights. We show empirically and theoretically that this aggregation-specific \emph{weighting noise} can create large score shifts when stakeholder satisfaction is dispersed; in our experiments, these weight-induced shifts also increase with stakeholder count. We propose \textsc{DecompR}: counterfactual-calibrated weights are fixed from query structure before candidate scoring, while per-role utilities are estimated independently, removing candidate-dependent weight drift and reducing estimation noise.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses instability in large language model (LLM) judgment for multi-stakeholder settings due to noisy, candidate-dependent weighting of stakeholder utilities, and proposes a method (DecompR) that fixes aggregation weights based on query structure and separately estimates utilities per stakeholder role.
AI.motivation: In multi-stakeholder scenarios (e.g., content that must satisfy users with conflicting preferences), current holistic LLM judging approaches mix up two distinct steps: estimating how satisfied each stakeholder is (utility estimation) and combining these into a single score (utility aggregation). This conflation leads to unstable and implicit stakeholder weights that can change with each candidate output. Such instability becomes more severe when stakeholder satisfaction is diverse and when more stakeholders are involved, leading to unfair or unreliable evaluations.
AI.method: The authors introduce DecompR, a decomposition-based evaluation framework. It first determines fixed aggregation weights from the query structure using a counterfactual calibration procedure, before seeing specific candidate answers. Then it independently estimates utilities for each stakeholder role for a given candidate. This separation removes candidate-dependent drift in stakeholder weights and reduces overall estimation noise in the aggregated score.
AI.result: Empirical experiments and theoretical analysis show that noise specifically caused by aggregation (weighting noise) can cause large changes in overall scores when stakeholder satisfaction is spread out. The experiments further show that these score shifts grow as the number of stakeholders increases. When applying DecompR, the method successfully stabilizes the aggregation by fixing weights ahead of time and independently estimating per-role utilities, thereby reducing candidate-dependent weight drift and lowering noise in the final evaluation scores.
AI.conclusion: Holistic LLM judges are unreliable for multi-stakeholder tasks because they entangle utility estimation with utility aggregation, leading to noisy and unstable stakeholder weights, especially as stakeholders and preference dispersion increase. DecompR, by decoupling these steps and using counterfactual-calibrated, structure-derived weights, provides a more stable and lower-noise evaluation mechanism in such settings.

title: The Labyrinth and the Thread: Rethinking Regularizations in Sequential Knowledge Editing for Large Language Models
summary: Sequential editing of structured knowledge in large language models allows targeted factual updates without retraining, yet existing methods often rely on complex regularization or constraint mechanisms whose necessity remains unclear. In this work, we systematically investigate the mechanisms underlying effective and stable sequential editing. Specifically, we first analyze the empirical success of AlphaEdit and establish, via a rigorous optimization analysis, the formal equivalence between one-time and sequential editing. Building on this insight, we generalize the equivalence to a broader class of editing objectives, demonstrating that stability emerges naturally from properly accounting for accumulated editing constraints, rather than from specialized regularization or null-space operations. We empirically confirm that many commonly used regularization strategies are unnecessary for reliable sequential updates. Furthermore, we extend our framework to handle conflicting edits, ensuring robust and consistent behavior under contradictory updates. Ultimately, our work provides Ariadne's thread through the labyrinth of sequential editing, charting a path toward simpler, more interpretable, and dependable knowledge updates. Our code is available at https://github.com/Wangzzzzzzzz/OTE-SE-Alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why sequential knowledge editing in large language models can be done stably and effectively without complex regularization, showing that properly formulating the optimization problem makes one-time and sequential editing formally equivalent and naturally stable, and extends this framework to handle conflicting edits.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for sequentially updating facts in large language models can work well but often depend on complicated regularization or constraints (e.g., null-space projections), whose real necessity and underlying mechanisms are not well understood. There is a need for a principled understanding of what actually makes sequential editing stable and effective, and whether simpler, more interpretable approaches are possible.
AI.method: The authors start from a detailed optimization analysis of AlphaEdit, a successful sequential editing method, and prove a formal equivalence between one-shot (batch) editing and step-by-step sequential editing under certain formulations. They then generalize this equivalence to a wider family of editing objectives, explicitly accounting for how editing constraints accumulate across steps. This analysis reveals that stability arises from incorporating these accumulated constraints, not from specialized regularizers or null-space operations. They empirically test various regularization strategies and evaluate conditions under which they are unnecessary. Finally, they extend the theoretical and algorithmic framework to scenarios with conflicting edits and propose mechanisms to maintain robust, consistent model behavior when updates contradict each other.
AI.result: The analysis shows that, under the proposed framework, sequential and one-time editing are formally equivalent, implying that stable sequential editing does not fundamentally require complex regularization or null-space projections. Experiments corroborate that many popular regularization strategies can be removed without sacrificing reliability of sequential updates. The extended framework successfully manages conflicting edits, preserving robustness and consistency in the model’s behavior despite contradictory updates. The authors provide an implementation at the referenced GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: Stability in sequential knowledge editing comes from correctly formulating and accumulating editing constraints at each step, rather than from elaborate regularization mechanisms. This yields a unified theoretical view where one-shot and sequential editing coincide for a broad class of objectives. As a consequence, knowledge editing pipelines can be made simpler, more interpretable, and still dependable, and can be systematically extended to handle even conflicting edits within the same framework.

title: On the Detection of Commutative Factors in Factor Graphs: Necessary and Sufficient Conditions
summary: Exploiting the indistinguishability of objects in a probabilistic graphical model such as a factor graph is key to lifted probabilistic inference algorithms and allows for tractable probabilistic inference problems with respect to domain sizes. A central building block for the exploitation of indistinguishable objects in factor graphs is the identification of commutative factors, i.e., factors whose output values are invariant under permutations of input values assigned to a subset of their arguments. In this paper, we revisit the theoretical foundations underlying the state-of-the-art algorithm to detect commutative factors. Specifically, we show that in its current form, the state-of-the-art algorithm relies on a central theorem that is mistakenly regarded as a sufficient condition to identify commutative factors, while it actually only implies necessary condition. Consequently, the state of the art might, as we show in this paper, deliver incorrect results. To fix the flaws currently present in the state of the art, we prove a slightly modified version of the aforementioned theorem, which serves as a necessary condition to identify commutative factors. Moreover, we present a corrected version of the state-of-the-art algorithm, which keeps its efficiency while ensuring correctness and introduce a complementary algorithm with tighter worst-case bounds.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes and corrects the theory and algorithms used to detect commutative factors in factor graphs for lifted probabilistic inference, fixing a previously accepted but flawed theorem and algorithm, and proposing efficient, correct alternatives.
AI.motivation: Lifted probabilistic inference exploits symmetries (indistinguishability of objects) in factor graphs to make inference tractable for large domains. A key symmetry is commutativity of factors, but the current state-of-the-art detection algorithm is based on a theorem incorrectly treated as a sufficient condition. This can cause wrong detection of commutative factors and therefore incorrect lifted inference. The paper is motivated by the need to re-examine the theoretical foundations, expose these flaws, and provide sound and efficient alternatives.
AI.method: The authors perform a theoretical re-analysis of the central theorem used in the state-of-the-art commutative-factor detection algorithm, formally showing that it only provides a necessary, not sufficient, condition. They then prove a slightly modified theorem that is sound as a necessary condition, derive a corrected detection algorithm that preserves the efficiency of the original but guarantees correctness, and design an additional complementary algorithm with improved worst-case complexity bounds. The work is primarily mathematical and algorithmic, focused on proof and complexity analysis rather than empirical evaluation.
AI.result: They demonstrate that the previously accepted theorem does not justify using the existing algorithm as a sufficient test for commutativity, and they give explicit cases where the old algorithm produces incorrect results. They then provide: (1) a revised theorem that correctly captures necessary conditions for commutativity; (2) a corrected version of the state-of-the-art detection algorithm that remains efficient but is theoretically sound; and (3) a new complementary algorithm with tighter worst-case performance guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The established theoretical basis for detecting commutative factors in factor graphs was flawed, leading to potentially incorrect lifted probabilistic inference. By repairing the theorem and deriving corrected and complementary algorithms, the paper restores soundness to this crucial step while maintaining, and in some cases improving, computational efficiency. This strengthens the theoretical foundations and practical reliability of lifted probabilistic inference methods that rely on commutative-factor detection.

title: NestedKV: Nested Memory Routing for Long-Context KV Cache Compression
summary: Long-context language models are limited by the memory footprint of the key-value (KV) cache. Existing training-free KV compression methods usually rank tokens by one importance signal -- attention, recency, layer-wise allocation, or key distinctiveness -- which becomes brittle when useful context is globally distinctive, locally episodic, or immediately relevant. We introduce NestedKV, a key-only KV cache compression method inspired by the Continuum Memory System in Nested Learning. NestedKV maintains global, block-level, and sliding-window key anchors, scores tokens by multi-time-scale cosine anomaly, and combines the resulting rankings with a training-free outer learner using head-adaptive mixing and surprise-gated token routing. The score is paired with adaptive per-head budgets and requires no training or LLM modification. Across RULER (4k--32k), LooGLE, LongBench, LongBench-E, InfiniteBench, and MMLU-Pro on Qwen3 and Llama-3.2 models, NestedKV is strongest when the retained cache is small. On Qwen3-4B, it improves over KeyDiff by up to 19.10 points on RULER and 19.29 on LongBench at $r=0.75$; at $r=0.95$, it retains 37.32 on LongBench versus 17.55 for KeyDiff.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes NestedKV, a training-free method to compress the KV cache in long-context LLMs by keeping only keys and using a multi-scale, adaptive importance scoring mechanism that outperforms prior KV compression methods, especially under tight memory budgets.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLMs are constrained by the memory usage of the KV cache, which grows with sequence length. Existing training-free KV compression approaches typically rely on a single importance heuristic (e.g., attention scores or recency) and therefore struggle when relevant information appears in diverse temporal patterns: globally rare but important, locally clustered, or very recent. A more robust, general KV compression mechanism is needed to handle heterogeneous patterns of useful context without retraining or modifying LLMs.
AI.method: NestedKV is a key-only KV cache compression method inspired by the Continuum Memory System in Nested Learning. It maintains three nested sets of key anchors: global anchors over the whole context, block-level anchors, and sliding-window anchors. Tokens are scored by a multi-time-scale cosine anomaly measure that detects unusual or informative keys across these different temporal scales. The method then combines rankings from these scales using a training-free outer learner with head-adaptive mixing (per-attention-head weighting) and surprise-gated token routing (preferring tokens that are more anomalous or informative). It uses adaptive per-head KV budgets and does not require any model retraining or architectural changes.
AI.result: On multiple long-context benchmarks (RULER 4k–32k, LooGLE, LongBench, LongBench-E, InfiniteBench, and MMLU-Pro) and across Qwen3 and Llama-3.2 models, NestedKV consistently performs best when the KV cache is heavily compressed. For Qwen3-4B, compared to the strong baseline KeyDiff, NestedKV improves scores on RULER by up to 19.10 points and on LongBench by up to 19.29 points at a cache retention ratio r=0.75. At a more aggressive compression r=0.95, NestedKV still retains a score of 37.32 on LongBench, whereas KeyDiff drops to 17.55, showing significantly better robustness under tight memory constraints.
AI.conclusion: NestedKV offers an effective, training-free way to compress the KV cache in long-context LLMs by selectively retaining only the most informative keys across multiple temporal scales. Its nested anchor structure, multi-time-scale anomaly scoring, and head-adaptive, surprise-gated token selection yield substantially better performance than prior KV compression techniques under strict memory budgets, without modifying or retraining the underlying models. This makes it a practical solution for deploying long-context LLMs on memory-constrained hardware.

title: TADDLE: A Tool-Augmented Agent for Detecting Deficient LLM-Generated Peer Reviews
summary: LLM-generated peer reviews are increasingly common at major venues, yet their deficiencies are hard to detect because they are uniformly fluent and well-structured. Existing work either classifies authorship without judging quality, or scores quality with features designed for human-written reviews; no prior system detects deficiencies in LLM-generated reviews at the level of individual defect types. To bridge the gap, we introduce TADDLE, a Tool-Augmented Agent for Detecting Deficient LLM-Generated Peer Reviews, together with the first expert-annotated benchmark for this task. Our benchmark comprises 1,800 reviews on 50 ICLR 2025 papers, multi-label-annotated by 18 domain experts against a taxonomy of six defect categories (plus a non-deficient label). TADDLE decomposes detection into four specialized analysis tools -- Verify, Correct, Complete, and Transform -- orchestrated by an agent; an integrator synthesizes their outputs into binary and multi-label classifications via two-stage semi-supervised learning. Extensive experiments show that TADDLE performs strongly on both binary detection and the multi-label classification task. We release the benchmark and code at https://github.com/AquariusAQ/TADDLE.

AI.tldr: The paper presents TADDLE, a tool-augmented agent to detect specific defect types in LLM-generated peer reviews, along with the first expert-annotated benchmark for this task.
AI.motivation: LLM-generated peer reviews are now widely used in major venues, but their flaws are hard to notice because they look fluent and well-structured. Existing research either only identifies whether a review is LLM-written or not, or evaluates review quality using features tailored to human reviews, without pinpointing concrete defect types in LLM-generated reviews. There is a lack of systems and datasets that can diagnose these reviews at a fine-grained, defect-category level.
AI.method: The authors build TADDLE, a tool-augmented agent that breaks down defect detection into four specialized tools: Verify, Correct, Complete, and Transform. These tools analyze different aspects of a review. An agent coordinates the tools, and an integrator combines their outputs using a two-stage semi-supervised learning framework to produce both binary (deficient vs. non-deficient) and multi-label classifications over a taxonomy of six defect types plus a non-deficient label. They also construct an expert-annotated benchmark of 1,800 reviews on 50 ICLR 2025 papers, labeled by 18 domain experts according to the defect taxonomy.
AI.result: TADDLE demonstrates strong performance on both binary detection of deficient reviews and multi-label classification across the six defect categories on the new benchmark dataset. The benchmark and implementation are made publicly available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The work fills a gap in evaluating LLM-generated peer reviews by introducing both a specialized detection system and the first fine-grained, expert-annotated benchmark for review defects. TADDLE’s tool-augmented, agent-based architecture effectively identifies multiple defect types, enabling more reliable use and assessment of LLM-generated peer reviews in scientific venues.

title: An In-Vitro Study on Cross-Lingual Generalization in Language Models
summary: Cross-lingual transfer in language models is difficult to study in natural corpora because lexical overlap, morphology, data imbalance, and tokenization are entangled. We introduce an in-vitro framework with two procedurally generated languages that share the same ontology, typed grammar, and compositional structure, but differ in surface realization. This lets us independently vary lexical distance, minority-language proportion, tokenizer training regime, and vocabulary size, while evaluating transfer on a masked minority-language condition whose lexical forms are never observed during training. Across 700 controlled runs, we find that transfer is governed less by tokenizer balance or raw lexical similarity than by whether tokenization preserves reusable cross-lingual substructure. Smaller vocabularies often improve masked transfer by keeping words decomposable into shared fragments, whereas larger vocabularies can turn forms into language-specific atoms. We further show that transfer emerges as a staged process: grammatical and type-level competence precede masked lexical generalization. Finally, we attempt to explain this mechanism through tokenizer bridges and show that bridge strength correlates strongly with masked reachability.

AI.tldr: The paper creates a controlled synthetic setup to study cross-lingual transfer and shows that transfer depends mainly on tokenization preserving shared substructure, with smaller vocabularies often helping, and that grammatical transfer precedes lexical transfer.
AI.motivation: Studying cross-lingual transfer with real languages is confounded by intertwined factors like lexical overlap, morphology, tokenization, and data imbalance, making it hard to identify what truly drives transfer. The authors want a clean, controllable testbed to disentangle these factors and understand the mechanisms of transfer in language models.
AI.method: They design two procedurally generated languages that share the same abstract ontology, typed grammar, and compositional structure but differ in their surface word forms. With this synthetic setup, they systematically manipulate lexical distance between the languages, proportion of minority-language data, tokenizer training regime, and vocabulary size. They then run about 700 controlled experiments training language models and evaluate cross-lingual transfer on a masked-language objective where the minority-language word forms are never directly observed during training. They also analyze how tokenizers create shared subword units ("bridges") between the languages and quantify how these relate to transfer performance.
AI.result: They find that cross-lingual transfer is driven less by balanced tokenization or raw lexical similarity and more by whether the tokenizer preserves reusable cross-lingual substructure. Smaller vocabularies tend to improve masked transfer by forcing words to be decomposed into shared subword fragments, whereas larger vocabularies often make words into language-specific atomic units that hinder transfer. They also observe that transfer unfolds in stages: models first acquire grammatical and type-level competence before successfully generalizing lexically in the masked minority language. Quantitative analysis shows a strong correlation between the strength of tokenizer "bridges" and the model's ability to reach masked minority-language forms.
AI.conclusion: Effective cross-lingual transfer in language models depends critically on tokenization that maintains shared substructure between languages, rather than simply on vocabulary size or lexical similarity. Smaller, more decompositional vocabularies can enhance transfer, while overly large vocabularies can harm it by eliminating cross-lingual subword overlap. Transfer appears to proceed in stages from structural/grammatical competence to lexical generalization. The concept of tokenizer bridges provides a mechanistic explanation, as the strength of these bridges aligns closely with how well models can reach and predict unseen minority-language forms.

title: From Norms to Indicators (N2I-RAG): An Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Legal Indicator Computation
summary: Computing legal indicators from normative texts is a key task in legal monitoring and policy evaluation, but presents significant challenges due to the complexity, scale, and interpretive nature of legal language, as well as the variability in available document quality. Existing natural language processing techniques and generative models can assist in legal analysis, but often suffer from high risk of hallucinations and lack the interpretability and evidence grounding required for reliable indicator computation. This paper presents N2I-RAG (From Norms to Indicators), an agentic retrieval-augmented generation framework designed to automate the computation of legal indicators in a transparent and traceable way. We integrate adaptive retrieval, llm-based agents, and validation mechanisms in a modular pipeline, where each component performs a defined role in filtering, retrieving, and assessing evidence, and in producing binary legal outcomes linked to identifiable legal provisions. The framework emphasizes traceability by requiring explicit explanations of intermediate decisions and final indicator assignments. We evaluate N2I-RAG using an in-house constructed French marine environmental law corpus that includes both scanned and digital sources. Comparative experiments with multiple language model families demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms baseline systems, and generalizes well when tested on 2 different bans. The results indicate that agentic retrieval-augmented generation can bridge open-text legal language and standardized indicator computation, offering a foundation for transparent and scalable legal observatories.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces N2I-RAG, an agent-based retrieval-augmented generation framework that computes legal indicators from complex normative texts in a transparent and traceable way, outperforming baseline NLP and LLM approaches on French marine environmental law tasks.
AI.motivation: Legal monitoring and policy evaluation require reliable computation of legal indicators from normative texts. However, legal language is complex, large-scale, and interpretive, and source documents can be noisy (e.g., scanned). Existing NLP and generative models help with legal analysis but are prone to hallucinations, and they often lack interpretability and clear evidence grounding, which are essential for trustworthy legal indicators. The paper aims to address these reliability and transparency gaps.
AI.method: The authors propose N2I-RAG, an agentic retrieval-augmented generation pipeline. It uses adaptive retrieval over a mixed-quality legal corpus and orchestrates multiple LLM-based agents, each with a clearly defined role: filtering documents, retrieving and assessing relevant legal evidence, and translating this evidence into binary legal outcomes (indicators) tied to specific legal provisions. The system incorporates validation and requires explicit explanations of intermediate steps and final decisions, enhancing traceability and interpretability.
AI.result: Using an in-house French marine environmental law corpus containing both scanned and digital texts, the authors run comparative experiments across several language model families. N2I-RAG consistently surpasses baseline approaches in computing legal indicators and shows good generalization when applied to two different legal bans, even under varying document conditions.
AI.conclusion: Agentic retrieval-augmented generation, as instantiated by N2I-RAG, can effectively connect open-text legal norms to standardized legal indicators in a transparent, evidence-grounded, and scalable manner. This provides a promising basis for building legal observatories capable of monitoring compliance and supporting policy evaluation, while mitigating hallucinations and improving interpretability compared to standard LLM and NLP baselines.

title: The Need for an External Observer Formalizing the Sufficiency Gap: A Mathematical Extension of Mixture Identifiability and Contextual Grounding in Sequence Models
summary: We construct a binary mixed-regime process with one deterministic textual regime and one random regime governed by an unobserved latent state. Even an ideal infinite-capacity sequence predictor that exactly recovers the text-only marginal law can become overconfident when the observed prefix is compatible with the wrong latent regime. The resulting entropy difference is not an ordinary optimization error; it is a sufficiency gap caused by marginalization over an unobserved state. We then formalize retrieval, tool use, and external grounding through an auxiliary binary signal with fidelity $γ\in [1/2,1]$. The resulting Bayesian update yields a contextual dominance threshold: a corrective signal reverses the posterior odds induced by the textual history exactly when its fidelity exceeds the text-only posterior weight assigned to the misleading regime. This threshold reduces, but does not generally eliminate, the sufficiency gap; complete closure requires perfect revelation of the relevant latent state or an equivalent verification mechanism. The analysis clarifies why temperature scaling cannot restore missing context, why grounding mechanisms must be both informative and learnably usable by the model, and why autonomous sequence models require structurally decoupled observers or verifiers in high-stakes domains.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how sequence models can become overconfident due to hidden latent regimes and how auxiliary signals like retrieval or tools can mitigate, but not always remove, this issue.
AI.motivation: To understand a fundamental source of overconfidence and miscalibration in sequence models that arises even when they perfectly model text, due to unobserved latent states governing the data-generating process.
AI.method: They construct a mixed-regime generative process with one deterministic text regime and one random latent-state regime, analyze the behavior of an ideal sequence predictor under this process, and then introduce an auxiliary binary signal with controllable fidelity to formalize retrieval, tool use, and external grounding. Using Bayesian analysis, they derive conditions under which the auxiliary signal can correct misleading textual context.
AI.result: They show that a predictor that is perfect on the text-only marginal can still be badly overconfident when the text is compatible with the wrong latent regime, creating an irreducible entropy gap (“sufficiency gap”). They derive a contextual dominance threshold: the auxiliary signal corrects the posterior only if its fidelity exceeds the posterior probability the text assigns to the misleading regime, and they prove that this only reduces, but generally does not remove, the sufficiency gap unless the latent state is perfectly revealed or verifiable.
AI.conclusion: Overconfidence in sequence models can stem from structural insufficiency of text-only context rather than mere optimization errors. Grounding via tools, retrieval, or external signals helps only if those signals are sufficiently informative and can be effectively used by the model; simple techniques like temperature scaling cannot fix missing context. For high-stakes applications, autonomous sequence models should be paired with structurally separate observers or verifiers that can access and reveal the relevant latent state.

title: Developing a Totally Unimodular Linear Program for Optimal Conformance Checking: When and Why It Complements A*
comment: Author-accepted manuscript accepted for publication in Expert Systems with Applications. Code and experiment scripts are available at: https://github.com/Izack-Cohen/unimodular-conformance-checking. Version corresponding to the accepted paper: v1.0.0
summary:
Alignment-based conformance checking is the state-of-the-art approach for comparing observed process executions with normative process models. The standard exact solution relies on an A*-based heuristic search, which can exhibit exponential runtime in the presence of long traces or substantial deviations.
  This paper introduces a reformulation of alignment-based conformance checking as a totally unimodular linear program (LP) defined on the reachability graph of the synchronous product. By exploiting the underlying network-flow structure, the proposed formulation guarantees the existence of an integral optimal extreme-point solution through LP relaxation, thereby avoiding the combinatorial overhead associated with integer variables and branch-and-bound search.
  We conduct an extensive empirical evaluation on more than 2.1 million conformance checking instances derived from real-world and synthetic benchmark datasets. The results show that A* and the LP approach exhibit complementary performance characteristics: the former performs best on short, well-conforming traces, while the LP formulation provides substantial speedups for longer traces with deviations, precisely where conformance checking is most informative. Based on these findings, we derive simple algorithm-selection guidelines that combine both approaches, achieving average runtime savings of 38.6% with 96% selection accuracy compared to always using A*.

AI.tldr: The paper reformulates alignment-based conformance checking as a totally unimodular linear program over the synchronous product’s reachability graph, enabling efficient LP-based computation that complements traditional A* search and yields significant runtime gains when combined via simple algorithm-selection rules.
AI.motivation: Alignment-based conformance checking via A* search can be exponentially slow for long traces or when there are many deviations from the model, which are exactly the cases where conformance information is most valuable. There is a need for an alternative formulation that avoids combinatorial explosion while preserving exactness and that performs better on difficult instances.
AI.method: The authors recast the optimal-alignment problem as a linear program defined on the reachability graph of the synchronous product of model and log. They exploit the problem’s inherent network-flow structure and prove that the constraint matrix is totally unimodular, which ensures that solving the LP relaxation with standard LP solvers yields integral (alignment) solutions without integer variables or branch-and-bound. They then empirically compare this LP-based approach to the classical A*-based method on over 2.1 million instances and analyze performance patterns. Finally, they design simple algorithm-selection rules that decide per instance whether to use A* or LP.
AI.result: The LP formulation yields integral optimal alignments directly from LP relaxation, eliminating the need for combinatorial integer search. In experiments over more than 2.1 million instances from real and synthetic logs, the A* approach is faster for short, well-conforming traces, whereas the LP formulation is substantially faster for long traces with deviations. Using simple selection rules to choose between A* and LP per instance achieves 96% accuracy in picking the faster method and reduces average runtime by 38.6% compared to always using A*.
AI.conclusion: Alignment-based conformance checking can be effectively solved via a totally unimodular LP on the synchronous product reachability graph, providing a robust alternative to A* search. The LP and A* approaches have complementary strengths, and a straightforward algorithm-selection strategy that combines them yields significant overall runtime savings while preserving exactness. This makes conformance checking more scalable and practical, especially on long and highly deviating traces where it is most insightful.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Verification of LLM Outputs for Data-Sensitive Domains (extended preprint)
comment: Extended preprint version of accepted technical communication at KI 2026. 22 pages, 3 figures
summary: LLMs deployed in high-stakes domains face fundamental reliability challenges: hallucinations, inconsistencies, and privacy vulnerabilities introduce unacceptable risks where errors carry legal, financial, or safety consequences. This paper presents a hybrid verification architecture combining formal symbolic methods with neural semantic analysis to provide complementary guarantees for LLM-generated content. This architecture employs logical reasoning for input verification, leveraging completeness properties to provide decidable guarantees on structured requirements. For output validation, embedding-based semantic similarity detects contextual hallucinations where formal methods lack expressiveness. This separation is realized in a parallel, actor-based pipeline, addressing limitations of prompt-based self-verification approaches, which inherit the distributional biases that produce hallucinations. The proposed architecture and type-aware verification method are validated with HAIMEDA, a real-world medical device damage assessment reporting system developed through Action Design Research. Evaluation shows hallucination detection rates of over 83% for structured entities and 72% for semantic fabrications, with a 30% reduction in report creation time, demonstrating that neuro-symbolic architectures can provide principled safeguards for LLM deployment in data-sensitive domains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hybrid neuro-symbolic verification architecture to detect and mitigate LLM hallucinations and reliability issues in high-stakes, data-sensitive domains, validated in a medical device damage assessment system.
AI.motivation: LLMs used in high-stakes domains such as medicine, law, or finance suffer from hallucinations, inconsistencies, and privacy vulnerabilities. In these domains, errors are costly and unacceptable, yet existing prompt-based self-verification approaches are limited because they inherit the same biases that cause hallucinations and lack strong guarantees. The authors aim to create a more principled, reliable way to verify LLM behavior to make them safer for deployment in such sensitive contexts.
AI.method: The authors propose a hybrid verification architecture that combines formal symbolic methods with neural semantic analysis. Input verification uses logical reasoning and type-aware, structured constraints, exploiting completeness and decidability properties to guarantee that inputs meet formal requirements. Output validation uses embedding-based semantic similarity to detect contextual hallucinations and semantic fabrications beyond the reach of rigid formal logic. The architecture is implemented as a parallel, actor-based pipeline rather than a single prompt-based loop, thus separating concerns and reducing shared biases. The approach is instantiated and studied in HAIMEDA, a medical device damage assessment reporting system developed via Action Design Research.
AI.result: In the HAIMEDA system, the proposed architecture achieves hallucination detection rates greater than 83% for structured entities (i.e., clearly specifiable fields) and 72% for more subtle semantic fabrications. Additionally, the system yields a 30% reduction in report creation time, indicating both safety and efficiency benefits in a realistic deployment setting.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that neuro-symbolic verification architectures—specifically, those combining formal symbolic input validation with neural semantic output checking in a parallel, actor-based pipeline—can substantially improve the reliability and efficiency of LLMs in data-sensitive, high-stakes domains. The demonstrated performance in the medical device assessment context suggests that such architectures offer principled safeguards that exceed the capabilities of prompt-based self-verification alone.

title: Rethinking the Multilingual Reasoning Gap with Layer Swap
summary: Recent reasoning Large Language Models produce a chain-of-thought (CoT) predominantly in English, even when prompted in non-English languages. Prior work suggests that forcing the CoT to remain in the input language (\emph{native reasoning}) substantially degrades performance relative to allowing the model to reason in English before answering in the input language (\emph{English-pivoted reasoning}). However, most studies of this native reasoning gap rely on inference-time interventions or limited native-language training data. We revisit this comparison at a larger scale and under comparable supervision. We construct long multilingual reasoning datasets across six languages (English, French, German, Spanish, Chinese and Swahili); fine-tune specialists in both native and English-pivoted regimes on top of \texttt{Qwen/Qwen3-8B-Base}, and evaluate across mathematics, science, general knowledge, and code. In this setting, the average native reasoning gap shrinks to 1.9--3.5\% across the five non-English languages, considerably smaller than previously reported. Weight-space analysis of the native specialists reveals aligned fine-tuning updates in the middle layers and divergence in the outer layers. This points to a largely language-agnostic reasoning core surrounded by language-specific layers. Exploiting this structure, we introduce a Layer Swap: transferring the English specialist's stronger reasoning mid-layers into each native specialist, closing most of the native reasoning gap across the five non-English languages while preserving CoT in the target language. We release all models and datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why multilingual LLMs tend to reason in English and quantifies how much performance is lost when forcing reasoning in the native (non-English) language, then proposes a method (Layer Swap) to keep native-language chains-of-thought while recovering most of the lost performance.
AI.motivation: Reasoning-focused LLMs tend to produce chain-of-thought in English even for non-English prompts, suggesting that English may be a privileged reasoning language. Previous work reports a large performance drop when models are constrained to reason in the native language, but these studies often depend on inference-time tricks or small, unbalanced training sets. The authors want to re-examine this "native reasoning gap" under fairer conditions: comparable large-scale supervision across languages and controlled fine-tuning, and to understand what in the network causes English to be such an effective pivot for reasoning.
AI.method: 1) Build long chain-of-thought training datasets in six languages (English, French, German, Spanish, Chinese, Swahili) spanning math, science, general knowledge, and coding tasks. 2) Take a base model (Qwen3-8B-Base) and fine-tune two types of specialists per language: (a) native specialists that both reason and answer in the target language; (b) English-pivoted specialists that reason in English but answer in the target language. 3) Evaluate these specialists on multilingual benchmarks to measure the performance difference (native reasoning gap). 4) Perform weight-space analysis comparing native specialists across languages to the English specialist, especially looking at layer-wise similarity to identify shared vs language-specific components. 5) Based on findings, design and test a Layer Swap procedure: replace the mid-layers of each native specialist with those from the English specialist, keeping outer layers native, and measure its effect on performance and language of CoT.
AI.result: Under carefully matched large-scale supervision, the average performance difference between native reasoning and English-pivoted reasoning is much smaller (1.9–3.5% across French, German, Spanish, Chinese, Swahili) than previously reported. Weight analysis shows that mid-layers of native specialists are highly aligned across languages and with the English specialist, while early/late layers diverge, suggesting a shared, language-agnostic reasoning core with language-specific peripheries. Using Layer Swap—transplanting the English specialist’s mid-layers into native specialists—recovers most of the remaining gap while still generating chains-of-thought in the native language. All resulting models and datasets are released.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that reasoning in LLMs is largely language-agnostic at the representational core, with language-specific processing concentrated in outer layers. When training data and supervision are balanced, the penalty for native-language reasoning is modest rather than large. By selectively sharing mid-layer weights (Layer Swap) from a strong English reasoning specialist, one can substantially improve native-language reasoning performance without sacrificing native-language chains-of-thought. This both challenges prior claims of a large intrinsic native reasoning deficit and offers a practical approach to building better multilingual reasoning models, supported by open-sourced models and datasets.

title: LELA: An End-to-end LLM-based Entity Linking Framework with Zero-shot Domain Adaptation
summary: Entity linking is a key component of many downstream NLP systems, yet existing approaches are often tied to the specific target knowledge bases and domains, limiting their real world application. In this paper, we extend LELA, a modular and domain-agnostic LLM-based entity disambiguation method, into a practical Python library that integrates zero-shot Named Entity Recognition (NER) -thereby providing a complete end-toend pipeline for entity-linking in real-world usage. We provide experimental results validating LELA's performance and robustness across diverse entity linking settings. In our demo, users can play with the system on their own input texts.

AI.tldr: They present a practical Python library that implements LELA, a modular, domain-agnostic LLM-based entity linking pipeline with built-in zero-shot NER, and show it works robustly across settings.
AI.motivation: Existing entity linking approaches are tightly coupled to specific knowledge bases and domains, which restricts their applicability in real-world, heterogeneous settings. There is a need for a flexible, domain-agnostic solution that can be easily integrated into downstream NLP systems and used on arbitrary user text without heavy domain-specific engineering.
AI.method: They extend LELA—an LLM-based, modular, domain-agnostic entity disambiguation framework—into a usable Python library. The library combines zero-shot Named Entity Recognition and entity disambiguation into a single end-to-end pipeline. The system is designed to plug into different knowledge bases and domains with minimal adaptation. They evaluate the library experimentally across a variety of entity-linking configurations and also provide an interactive demo interface for user input texts.
AI.result: Experimental results show that the extended LELA system maintains strong performance and robustness across diverse entity-linking settings, suggesting that the modular, domain-agnostic design generalizes well beyond a single KB or domain. The system is usable as a practical tool rather than just a research prototype.
AI.conclusion: The work turns LELA into a practical, end-to-end, domain-agnostic entity linking solution in Python by integrating zero-shot NER and robust disambiguation. It demonstrates that LLM-based, modular approaches can deliver strong and generalizable entity-linking performance suitable for real-world deployments, and offers a demo so users can explore it on their own text.

title: KARMA: Karma-Aligned Reward Model Adaptation
summary: Human communication depends on implicit social signals where effectiveness is shaped by tone, context, and conversational norms rather than semantic content alone. We introduce KARMA (Karma-Aligned Reward Model Adaptation), a framework for LLM learning of context-sensitive conversational behavior from large-scale social interaction data. KARMA trains a reward model on Reddit conversations to predict response valuation conditioned on context, and uses this signal to fine-tune language models via reinforcement learning to improve performance on pragmatics-mediated tasks. Critically, we find that the highest performing reward model does not lead to better downstream model alignment: a reward model relying exclusively on conversational context was a worse predictor of Reddit karma but yielded substantially better downstream performance. We evaluate the effects of KARMA applied to a downstream model with and without direct exposure to the social media data. The resulting models show improved pragmatics-mediated behaviors with largely mitigated undesirable side effects. Factuality is consistently diminished by KARMA across all conditions, including when the downstream model has no direct exposure to Reddit data, suggesting that this tension is embedded in the reward signal itself rather than introduced by noisy training data.

AI.tldr: The paper presents KARMA, a framework that trains reward models on Reddit conversations to adapt LLMs for better context-sensitive, pragmatic conversational behavior, revealing a tension between social alignment and factuality.
AI.motivation: Human communication effectiveness depends heavily on implicit social and pragmatic signals, not just literal semantics, and current LLMs lack robust, context-sensitive conversational behavior aligned with real social interaction norms. The authors want to harness large-scale social media signals (Reddit karma) to teach models pragmatics-mediated behavior while studying how such social reward signals affect alignment, side effects, and factuality.
AI.method: The authors propose KARMA (Karma-Aligned Reward Model Adaptation), which (1) trains reward models on Reddit conversations to predict a response’s valuation (karma) given its conversational context, (2) uses these learned reward models as objectives in a reinforcement learning fine-tuning stage for LLMs, and (3) systematically varies how much the reward model relies on context versus other features. They then apply these reward models to adapt downstream language models, both with and without direct exposure of those models to Reddit data, and evaluate impacts on pragmatics-mediated tasks, side effects, and factual accuracy.
AI.result: They find that the reward model which best predicts Reddit karma is not the one that yields the best downstream behavior. Instead, a reward model that relies only on conversational context, though worse at predicting karma, leads to substantially better performance on pragmatics-mediated tasks. KARMA-adapted models show improved pragmatic conversational behaviors and reduced undesirable side effects. However, across all tested settings, KARMA consistently lowers factual accuracy, including when the downstream model never directly sees Reddit data, indicating the misalignment between social approval signals and factuality is built into the reward model signal itself.
AI.conclusion: Social interaction signals such as Reddit karma can be used via KARMA to improve LLM pragmatic and context-sensitive conversational behavior, but optimizing for such social reward signals introduces a systematic trade-off with factuality. The best reward model for predicting human social approval is not necessarily the best for desirable downstream alignment, and care must be taken when using social feedback as a reward objective, as it may inherently encode tensions between social alignment and truthfulness.

title: Generating Robust Portfolios of Optimization Models using Large Language Models
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 LM4Plan Workshop
summary: Mathematical optimization is a powerful tool for structured decision-making across domains such as resource allocation and planning. Formulating optimization models faithful to reality, though, remains a significant bottleneck as it typically demands both domain expertise and optimization knowledge that are often scarce. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) promise to bridge this gap, enabling the generation of candidate optimization models from natural language descriptions. However, there is no guarantee that any single LLM-generated model is reliable, and existing approaches that output only one model are therefore risky. In this work, we propose a novel algorithm that generates a portfolio of optimization models, designed to be robust to the limitations of LLMs. Our method exploits the observation that a single LLM can play two distinct roles $\unicode{x2014}$ as a stochastic generator and as a reasoning evaluator $\unicode{x2014}$ and proposes a unified framework that leverages both capabilities in a complementary manner. We provide theoretical guarantees showing that, as long as either the generator or the evaluator is well-aligned with human preferences, the portfolio is guaranteed to contain high-quality candidates, enabling a principled human-in-the-loop process in which a decision-maker can review multiple candidates before committing to one. We further validate our approach empirically, demonstrating strong performance across a range of optimization modeling tasks.

AI.tldr: They propose a method that uses an LLM both to generate and to evaluate multiple optimization models from natural language, producing a portfolio of candidates instead of a single risky model, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Building realistic mathematical optimization models from natural language is hard because it requires scarce domain and optimization expertise. Single LLM-generated models are unreliable, and existing methods that output only one model are risky, so there is a need for a safer, more robust way to use LLMs for optimization modeling.
AI.method: They design an algorithm that treats one LLM as having two roles: (1) a stochastic generator that proposes diverse optimization model candidates from a natural language description; and (2) a reasoning-based evaluator that scores or ranks these candidates. The algorithm produces a portfolio of models by repeatedly sampling, evaluating, and selecting candidates under a unified framework that leverages both roles. They also develop a theoretical analysis proving quality guarantees on the portfolio given assumptions about alignment of either the generator or evaluator with human preferences, and then test the approach on various optimization modeling tasks.
AI.result: Theoretically, they show that if either the generator or the evaluator is sufficiently aligned with human preferences, the produced portfolio will include high-quality optimization models, suitable for human selection. Empirically, they validate that their portfolio-generation method performs well across multiple optimization modeling benchmarks, outperforming or being more reliable than approaches that output only a single model.
AI.conclusion: Using LLMs to create a portfolio of optimization models, and leveraging the same LLM both as a stochastic generator and reasoning-based evaluator, yields a robust, theoretically grounded, and empirically effective framework for translating natural language problem descriptions into optimization models, enabling safer human-in-the-loop decision-making.

title: From Snippets to Semantics: Rethinking Evidence Granularity for Multilingual Fact Verification
summary: Multilingual fact verification requires evidence that is both relevant and sufficiently complete for reliable factuality prediction. However, existing systems often rely on search snippets, sentence-level evidence, or locally segmented passages, which can miss decisive context and produce fragmented evidence. To overcome these limitations, we propose SEEK, a Semantic Evidence Extraction with an adaptive chunKing framework that constructs coherent evidence chunks from full fact-checking articles by identifying semantic topic transitions and preserving local verification context. The constructed chunks are encoded using a multilingual encoder and then multilingual LLMs are finetuned using LoRA adapter for veracity prediction. Experiments on X-FACT and RU22Fact show that SEEK improves macro-f1 by up to 10% over semantic chunking, 19% over sentence chunking, and 20% over search-snippet baselines. Evidence completeness and significance analyses further show that SEEK preserves richer verification context and enables more reliable multilingual fact-checking.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SEEK, a multilingual fact verification framework that builds coherent semantic evidence chunks from full articles to improve veracity prediction, significantly outperforming snippet-, sentence-, and naive semantic-chunk-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing multilingual fact verification systems often rely on search snippets, individual sentences, or small local passages as evidence. These units can be fragmented and miss important context, leading to incomplete or unreliable factuality judgments. The authors aim to address the problem of evidence incompleteness and lack of coherent context in multilingual fact-checking, especially when using long, full-length fact-checking articles as sources of evidence.
AI.method: The authors propose SEEK, a Semantic Evidence Extraction with adaptive chunKing framework. SEEK processes full fact-checking articles to identify semantic topic transitions and segment them into coherent evidence chunks that preserve local verification context. These chunks are then encoded with a multilingual encoder. On top of these representations, multilingual large language models are finetuned using LoRA adapters to perform veracity prediction for fact verification claims. The key technical contribution is the adaptive, semantics-aware chunking mechanism that balances completeness and coherence of evidence.
AI.result: On two multilingual fact verification datasets, X-FACT and RU22Fact, SEEK yields substantial performance gains in macro-F1 score: up to 10% improvement over prior semantic chunking methods, 19% over sentence-level chunking, and 20% over search-snippet-based baselines. Additional analyses on evidence completeness and significance indicate that the chunks produced by SEEK contain richer and more decisive verification context than alternative evidence selection methods.
AI.conclusion: SEEK effectively addresses evidence fragmentation in multilingual fact verification by constructing coherent, context-preserving semantic chunks from full fact-checking articles. This leads to more reliable veracity prediction and notable performance improvements over existing baselines, demonstrating that better evidence structuring and context preservation are key to advancing multilingual fact-checking systems.

title: ORCA: An End-to-End Interactive Copilot for Optimized Root Cause Analysis
summary: Causal analysis is a crucial task in many domains, including manufacturing, social science, and medicine. However, despite recent progress, the conceptual and methodological complexity of causal methods makes them largely inaccessible to domain experts.
