This gap prevents experts from leveraging these advances and hinders researchers who lack access to real-world data for validation. To bridge this divide, we introduce ORCA, a copilot for end-to-end causal analysis. ORCA orchestrates agents to understand the user's goals and guide them through the most appropriate causal analysis workflow, from fully automatic to highly user-guided execution. It features causal discovery, causal effect estimation, explainability and Root-Cause-Analysis (RCA). ORCA evaluates and compares performance, generates key metrics and diagrams, and generates insights through structured reports. We highlight its effectiveness across several real-world use-cases.

AI.tldr: ORCA is a copilot system that makes complex causal analysis accessible to domain experts by orchestrating multiple agents to support end-to-end workflows.
AI.motivation: Causal analysis is powerful but difficult to use for non-specialists, and researchers often lack access to real-world data for validating new methods. There is a need for a system that lowers the barrier to applying and evaluating causal methods in practical domains.
AI.method: The authors design ORCA, a multi-agent copilot that interacts with users to elicit goals and then selects and runs an appropriate causal workflow. ORCA integrates modules for causal discovery, causal effect estimation, explainability, and root-cause analysis, and automatically produces metrics, visualizations, and structured reports for the user.
AI.result: ORCA successfully conducts end-to-end causal analyses across various real-world use cases, automatically comparing model performance and delivering interpretable outputs such as metrics, diagrams, and narrative insights.
AI.conclusion: A multi-agent copilot like ORCA can substantially bridge the gap between advanced causal inference methods and domain experts, enabling more widespread, validated, and explainable use of causal analysis in practice.

title: Quality Without Usefulness: LLM-Generated XAI Narratives as Trust Heuristics Rather Than Decision Aids
summary: Prior work shows that Large Language Models (LLMs) can transform Explainable AI (XAI) outputs into Natural Language Explanations (NLEs) that score highly on quality metrics such as plausibility, coherence, and comprehensibility. But does explanation quality translate to practical usefulness? We investigate this question in a time-series energy forecasting domain through five controlled experiments (2,730 judgments across 60 test instances), each operationalising a distinct facet of usefulness studied in the XAI literature. Holding NLE quality constant at the high levels established by a prior factorial study, we find that NLEs do not improve task accuracy on any of the five tasks, while inflating self-reported confidence. A placebic control shows that this confidence boost is driven by text presence rather than content. In an out-of-distribution detection task, NLEs reduce the LLM judge's ability to flag unreliable predictions, providing false reassurance that masks model failure. We characterise these findings as the Quality-Usefulness Gap and argue that evaluation of the XAI-to-NLE pipeline must extend beyond text-quality metrics to downstream task performance.

AI.tldr: The paper finds that high-quality natural language explanations generated from XAI outputs do not improve users’ task performance, and can even be harmful by overinflating confidence and masking model failures.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs can turn technical XAI outputs into fluent, plausible natural language explanations, it is unclear whether these high-quality explanations actually help humans make better decisions. Prior work focused on text-level quality metrics, not on real task usefulness.
AI.method: The authors run five controlled user experiments in a time-series energy forecasting setting, with 2,730 human judgments over 60 test instances. They keep the NLEs’ surface quality fixed at high levels (as established in earlier work), and systematically test different facets of “usefulness” defined in the XAI literature (e.g., accuracy, calibration, out-of-distribution detection). They also include a placebic control condition where text is present but content is meaningless, to separate the effect of text presence from the effect of explanation content.
AI.result: Across all five tasks, the presence of high-quality NLEs does not improve task accuracy. At the same time, NLEs consistently increase self-reported confidence. The placebo text condition shows that the confidence boost comes from the mere presence of text rather than its substantive content. In an out-of-distribution detection task, NLEs degrade the LLM judge’s ability to recognize unreliable predictions, creating false reassurance and hiding model failures.
AI.conclusion: There is a substantial gap between standard explanation quality metrics for NLEs and their actual usefulness for downstream decision-making—the Quality-Usefulness Gap. Evaluations of XAI-to-NLE pipelines should go beyond text quality and focus on practical impact on human and model performance, with attention to risks like overconfidence and failure-masking.

title: Boosting Knowledge Graph Foundation Models via Enhanced Negative Sampling
summary: Knowledge graphs (KGs) have become the core backbone of numerous downstream tasks such as question answering and recommender systems. However, despite all this, KGs are often very incomplete. To perform zero-shot knowledge graph completion in unseen KGs, which have different relational vocabularies from those used for pre-training, KG foundation models (KGFMs) receive a wide range of attention. Existing KGFMs often perform training using random negative triples, which are constructed by replacing the head or tail entity of a positive triple with a random entity. However, these negative triples are often constructed with limited quality, providing weak supervision for KGFM training. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective adaptive negative sampling approach, KMAS, to enhance existing KGFMs. KMAS constructs hard negative triples through the updated relation embeddings generated from the existing KGFM's relation encoder. To further adaptively align with the evolving capability of the KGFM during the training process, KMAS adjusts the ratio of hard negative triples dynamically throughout the whole training process: after a warmup phrase, it increases the ratio linearly and then decreases linearly. Extensive experiments are conducted over 44 data sets. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed negative sampling method can enhance many SOTA KGFMs without requiring excessive additional time or memory consumption.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes KMAS, an adaptive hard negative sampling strategy that improves training of knowledge graph foundation models (KGFMs) for zero-shot KG completion.
AI.motivation: Knowledge graphs are widely used but often incomplete. For zero-shot completion on unseen KGs with different relations, KGFMs are trained, yet they typically rely on simple random negative sampling, which yields low-quality negatives and weak supervision. There is a need for a more informative negative sampling strategy that can better exploit the model’s evolving understanding of relations without high computational cost.
AI.method: KMAS is an adaptive negative sampling method integrated into existing KGFMs. It uses the relation encoder of a KGFM to generate updated relation embeddings and, based on them, constructs hard negative triples—triples that are more challenging for the current model to distinguish from positives. During training, KMAS dynamically adjusts the proportion of such hard negatives: after an initial warm-up phase with fewer hard negatives, the ratio increases linearly to a peak and then decreases linearly, aligning the negative sampling difficulty with the model’s changing capability.
AI.result: Across 44 datasets, integrating KMAS into various state-of-the-art KGFMs consistently improves performance on zero-shot knowledge graph completion. These gains come with minimal additional computational overhead in terms of time and memory compared to standard random negative sampling.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, model-aware hard negative sampling via KMAS enhances the training of KGFMs for zero-shot KG completion. By leveraging relation embeddings and dynamically scheduling the hardness and ratio of negative samples through training, KMAS strengthens supervision and improves performance without significant extra resource cost, and can be plugged into multiple existing KGFMs.

title: EmoDistill: Offline Emotion Skill Distillation for Language Model Agents in Adversarial Negotiation
summary: Post-trained LLMs are often optimized to align responses with human preferences, making them safe, polite, and conversationally appropriate. In adversarial negotiation, however, this alignment can become a vulnerability: emotionally framed language may steer agents toward the counterparty's interests. Using GoEmotions-based affective prompting, we show that emotion substantially shifts negotiation outcomes, suggesting that emotion is a strategic action channel rather than a surface style. Thus, we introduce \textbf{EmoDistill}, an offline framework for distilling emotional negotiation skills into language model agents. EmoDistill decomposes emotional strategy into emotion selection and emotion expression: an Implicit Q-Learning (IQL) selector learns \emph{which} emotion to express, while a Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)-based policy learns \emph{how} to express it through Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Judge Policy Optimization (JPO). Across four emotion-sensitive, high-stakes negotiation domains, SLM policies trained under the EmoDistill framework achieve the highest utility, outperforming vanilla SLM/LLM baselines and IQL-only emotion selection. Ablations show that emotion conditioning is essential, and transfer studies demonstrate generalization across domains, unseen counterparties, and trained-vs-trained tournaments. Overall, EmoDistill learns skills from offline agent-to-agent interactions, avoiding costly online negotiation during training.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EmoDistill, a framework to teach negotiation agents to strategically use emotions, significantly improving their negotiation outcomes over standard language models.
AI.motivation: Post-trained LLMs are aligned to be safe and polite, which becomes a weakness in adversarial negotiations where emotional language can be exploited to steer models toward the opponent’s interests. The authors want to understand and harness emotion as a strategic tool in negotiation, rather than a superficial style, and to do so without costly online interaction.
AI.method: First, the authors use GoEmotions-based affective prompting to show that injecting different emotions into prompts systematically shifts negotiation outcomes, establishing emotion as a strategic channel. They then propose EmoDistill, an offline distillation framework that separates emotional negotiation strategy into (1) emotion selection and (2) emotion expression. An Implicit Q-Learning (IQL) selector learns which emotion to deploy in a given negotiation state. A separate policy, implemented via LoRA adapters on top of a base language model, learns how to express the chosen emotion using Supervised Fine-Tuning on negotiation data and Judge Policy Optimization (JPO) to refine behaviors based on judgment feedback. All training uses offline agent–agent negotiation logs rather than live interaction.
AI.result: In four high-stakes, emotion-sensitive negotiation domains, small language model (SLM) policies trained with EmoDistill achieve higher negotiation utility than: (a) baseline SLMs/LLMs without explicit emotional strategy, and (b) variants that only use IQL for emotion selection without emotion-conditioned expression. Ablation studies confirm that conditioning the policy on the selected emotion is crucial for performance. Transfer experiments show that the learned emotional negotiation skills generalize across domains, to new counterparties, and in tournaments where trained agents play against each other.
AI.conclusion: Emotion in negotiation is not merely stylistic but a powerful strategic channel that can be learned and exploited by language model agents. By decoupling emotion selection from emotion expression and training entirely offline on interaction logs, EmoDistill produces negotiation agents that achieve superior outcomes while avoiding the expense and risk of online training. The framework suggests a principled way to imbue LMs with controllable, strategic use of affect in adversarial settings.

title: BatteryMFormer: Multi-level Learning for Battery Degradation Trajectory Forecasting
summary: Early battery degradation trajectory forecasting (BDTF), which predicts the full-life state-of-health trajectory from early operational data, is critical for battery optimization, manufacturing, and deployment. Battery degradation data exhibit two key characteristics. First, degradation data present a multi-level structure, including regularities shared within aging conditions and trajectory patterns shared across batteries. Second, degradation-related variations in voltage-current profiles are often localized to specific state-of-charge (SOC) intervals. Existing approaches often fail to explicitly model these characteristics. To bridge this gap, we propose BatteryMFormer, a multi-level Transformer for early BDTF. BatteryMFormer integrates (1) an aging-condition-aware decoder that injects aging-condition priors via aging-condition-informed queries and aging-condition-aware attention, (2) a meta degradation pattern memory that learns and retrieves trajectory prototypes to guide long-horizon forecasting, and (3) a dual-view encoder that jointly captures temporal dynamics and SOC-localized variations from voltage and current time series. Extensive experiments on four battery domains show that BatteryMFormer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, marking a significant step toward reliable BDTF. Our code is available at https://github.com/Ruifeng-Tan/BatteryMFormer.

AI.tldr: Introduces BatteryMFormer, a Transformer-based model for early forecasting of full battery state-of-health trajectories from limited early-life data, leveraging multi-level structure and SOC-localized degradation patterns to outperform existing methods.
AI.motivation: Early and accurate forecasting of battery degradation trajectories is crucial for optimizing battery usage, manufacturing decisions, and deployment planning. However, current methods do not adequately capture two important characteristics of degradation data: (1) multi-level structure where there are both condition-specific regularities and cross-battery trajectory patterns, and (2) localized degradation-related changes in voltage-current profiles confined to specific SOC intervals. This gap limits the reliability and accuracy of long-horizon battery health prediction from early-life data.
AI.method: The paper proposes BatteryMFormer, a multi-level Transformer architecture tailored for battery degradation trajectory forecasting from early operational data. It comprises three main components: (1) an aging-condition-aware decoder that incorporates aging-condition priors through specialized queries and attention mechanisms that are informed by the aging conditions; (2) a meta degradation pattern memory that learns prototype degradation trajectories from data and retrieves them during inference to guide long-horizon predictions; and (3) a dual-view encoder that jointly processes voltage and current time series to capture both global temporal dynamics and degradation variations that are localized to specific SOC ranges.
AI.result: Across four different battery datasets/domains, BatteryMFormer consistently achieves better forecasting performance than state-of-the-art baseline methods for early battery degradation trajectory prediction. The empirical results demonstrate improved accuracy and robustness for long-horizon state-of-health trajectory forecasting when trained on early-cycle data.
AI.conclusion: BatteryMFormer effectively models the multi-level structure and SOC-localized patterns present in battery degradation data, enabling more reliable early-life forecasting of full state-of-health trajectories. The approach represents a significant advance over existing methods and moves the field closer to dependable, data-driven battery degradation prediction for practical applications. The authors also release code to facilitate reproduction and further research.

title: SeDT: Sentence-Transformer Decision-Transformer Conditioning for Multi-Turn Conversation Reliability
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve impressive performance when a task is fully specified in a single turn, yet the same models lose up to 39% of that performance when the identical task is revealed incrementally across multiple turns, a phenomenon documented at scale as Lost in Conversation. Crucially, this collapse is almost entirely a reliability failure; the best case, the aptitude only falls 16%, while the unreliability more than doubles (+112%). We argue that the root cause is structural, a flat conversation history assigns equal implicit weight to every prior turn, giving the model no signal to distinguish a critical constraint from incidental dialog. We present SeDT Sentence-transformer Decision-Transformer, a training-free inference-time method that resolves this by importing return-to-go conditioning from offline reinforcement learning. SeDT annotates each conversation shard with a cumulative relevance score derived from three complementary semantic, lexical, and positional signals and presents the full annotated history to the model at the final turn, without weight changes, without training data, and without discarding context. Evaluated on the Lost-in-Conversation benchmark in three LLMs and three generation tasks, SeDT outperforms the sharded baseline in all nine model-task combinations, with gains up to +37.7% in mean performance P and simultaneous reductions in unreliability in seven of the nine combinations. In short, telling the model which past turns matter is sufficient to substantially recover the performance lost in conversation.

AI.tldr: They identify and fix a reliability drop in LLMs when tasks are given across multiple turns instead of all at once, by reweighting conversation history at inference time using relevance scores.
AI.motivation: LLMs perform much worse when instructions are revealed gradually in multi-turn conversations even if the final task is identical, due mainly to reliability issues. Existing approaches treat all prior turns equally in the context window, giving models no indication of which parts of the history are crucial for solving the task. There is a need for a method that can restore single-turn performance in multi-turn settings without retraining or discarding potentially useful context.
AI.method: They propose SeDT (Sentence-transformer Decision-Transformer), an inference-time, training-free method inspired by return-to-go conditioning in offline reinforcement learning. SeDT computes a cumulative relevance score for each conversational shard based on semantic similarity, lexical overlap, and positional features, effectively estimating how important each turn is to the final response. It then annotates each shard in the history with these scores and feeds this annotated full history to an unmodified LLM at the final turn, without changing weights or needing additional training data.
AI.result: On the Lost-in-Conversation benchmark, using three different LLMs and three generation tasks, SeDT consistently outperforms the naive sharded multi-turn baseline in all nine model-task pairs. The method yields performance improvements up to +37.7% in mean performance and reduces unreliability in most cases (seven out of nine), while only modestly affecting best-case aptitude.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly signaling the relative importance of past conversation turns through relevance-conditioned annotations is enough to recover much of the performance LLMs lose in multi-turn conversational settings. This suggests that the main problem is not lack of capability but lack of structural guidance about which context is critical, and that inference-time, training-free approaches can meaningfully improve reliability in dialog-like use cases.

title: Traceable Knowledge Graph Reasoning Enables LLM-Assisted Decision Support for Industrial VOCs in the Steel Industry
summary: Key knowledge for steel-industry volatile organic compounds (VOCs) governance is scattered across unstructured scientific literature, making it difficult to integrate process, pollutant, and control-technology evidence and increasing the risk of hallucination when general large language models (LLMs) answer low-frequency industrial questions. Here we developed Chat-ISV, a knowledge graph (KG) enhanced multi-agent Q&A system that parses a curated steel-industry VOCs literature corpus, constructs a Neo4j KG with 27180 nodes and 81779 semantic edges, and combines prompt-constrained extraction, chunk-centered topology optimization, multi-agent routing, source-backtracking retrieval, local literature retrieval, open-domain knowledge access, and interactive subgraph visualization. Benchmark tests and 400 expert blind evaluations showed that topology optimization reduced isolated nodes from 57% to 4.08% and that Chat-ISV achieved high factual reliability, with 96.93% precision, 72.63% recall, an F1-score of 0.830, and a mean score of 1.69/2.00. By converting fragmented environmental-engineering literature into traceable, queryable, and decision-support-oriented knowledge, Chat-ISV establishes a scalable environmental-informatics paradigm for reliable LLM deployment and intelligent pollution-control decision support in specialized industrial domains.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Chat-ISV, a knowledge-graph-enhanced multi-agent Q&A system that organizes scattered literature on VOCs in the steel industry to provide reliable, source-traceable, decision-support answers.
AI.motivation: Key knowledge about VOCs governance in the steel industry is dispersed across unstructured literature, making it hard to systematically connect processes, pollutants, and control technologies. This fragmentation hampers expert decision-making and causes general LLMs to hallucinate on low-frequency, specialized industrial questions.
AI.method: The authors curate a domain-specific corpus on steel-industry VOCs and automatically construct a Neo4j-based knowledge graph with 27,180 nodes and 81,779 semantic edges. They design a multi-agent Q&A architecture that uses prompt-constrained information extraction, chunk-centered topology optimization of the KG, multi-agent routing for queries, source-backtracking and local literature retrieval for grounding, open-domain knowledge access for complementary information, and interactive subgraph visualization to expose the underlying evidence.
AI.result: Topology optimization of the KG significantly improved graph connectivity, lowering isolated nodes from 57% to 4.08%. In benchmark tests and 400 expert blind evaluations, Chat-ISV reached 96.93% precision, 72.63% recall, an F1-score of 0.830, and a mean expert rating of 1.69 out of 2, indicating high factual reliability of its answers.
AI.conclusion: Chat-ISV effectively converts fragmented environmental-engineering literature into a structured, queryable, and traceable knowledge resource tailored for steel-industry VOCs governance. The system demonstrates that a KG-enhanced, multi-agent LLM framework can provide reliable, decision-support-oriented Q&A in highly specialized industrial domains, offering a scalable paradigm for environmental informatics and intelligent pollution-control decision support.

title: Psychological Constructs in Shared Semantic Space
summary: Psychological constructs are often measured in separate instruments, datasets, and research traditions, which makes direct comparison difficult. This paper proposes a framework for making such constructs semantically commensurate by representing and comparing them as directions in a shared word-embedding space. Using Supervised Semantic Differential, we estimate construct-specific semantic gradients from text-outcome associations and project them onto theoretically motivated reference axes. As an initial test case, we use Valence, Arousal, and Dominance (VAD) as an affective coordinate system. First, we recover interpretable VAD directions from English word-level affective norms. Second, we project semantic gradients for 27 GoEmotions categories into this space and recover the expected organization of emotions, especially along valence and arousal. Third, we apply the same procedure to Big Five personality domains and facets derived from IPIP-NEO-300 item-factor associations. Domain-level placements are broadly coherent, while facet-level results are more exploratory because they rely on sparse questionnaire text. The results suggest that embedding spaces can support construct-level comparison across otherwise incommensurable psychological measurements, provided that semantic placements are assessed for stability and interpretability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method to compare different psychological constructs by embedding them into a shared semantic space using word embeddings.
AI.motivation: Psychological constructs such as emotions and personality traits are typically measured with different instruments, scales, and datasets, making it hard to compare them directly or integrate findings across research traditions. The authors aim to create a unified framework that allows these diverse constructs to be compared in a common semantic space, thus improving construct-level comparison and theoretical integration.
AI.method: The authors use a framework they call Supervised Semantic Differential. They treat constructs as directions (semantic gradients) in a shared word-embedding space, estimated from associations between text (e.g., item wording, emotion labels) and measured outcomes (e.g., affective ratings, factor loadings). These construct-specific directions are then projected onto reference axes defined by a theoretically grounded affective coordinate system: Valence, Arousal, and Dominance (VAD). This allows them to examine where different constructs fall in the VAD space and how they relate to each other.
AI.result: First, they show that they can recover meaningful and interpretable Valence, Arousal, and Dominance directions from existing English word-level affective norm data. Second, they project semantic gradients for 27 GoEmotions categories into this VAD space and find that the organization of emotions matches theoretical expectations, particularly along valence and arousal dimensions. Third, they apply the same procedure to Big Five personality domains and facets using IPIP-NEO-300 items; domain-level positions in VAD space are broadly coherent with theory, while facet-level positions are more tentative due to limited textual information. Overall, the empirical results demonstrate the feasibility of using embedding spaces to compare disparate psychological constructs.
AI.conclusion: Word-embedding spaces, combined with the Supervised Semantic Differential approach, can provide a common semantic framework for comparing psychological constructs that are otherwise measured with incommensurate instruments. This approach works well when semantic signals are strong and stable (e.g., for broad emotion categories and personality domains), but is more exploratory when based on sparse or noisy textual cues (e.g., facets). The authors conclude that embedding-based construct comparison is promising, provided that results are carefully evaluated for stability and interpretability.

title: Can Broad Biomedical Knowledge be Contextualized into Scenario-Grounded Propositions?
summary: Biomedical discovery often requires connecting broad biomedical knowledge with specific experimental or clinical data. Background knowledge suggests relevant mechanisms but is usually too general to map directly onto dataset variables, while data-driven patterns can be dataset-specific and hard to interpret mechanistically. We study this missing link as knowledge contextualization: transforming broad biomedical knowledge into evidence-supported, scenario-grounded propositions that domain experts can inspect, replay, and validate. We propose SCENE, a bi-level multi-agent framework that treats knowledge contextualization as iterative search. The upper level converts broad knowledge into search directions and grounds them in the dataset schema. The lower level executes these directions through multi-objective optimization to identify concrete propositions that balance evidential strength and data support. Feedback between the two levels progressively refines the search. We evaluate SCENE in two settings: discovering patient subgroups with heterogeneous treatment benefits in clinical trial scenarios, and identifying context-specific biological responses in LINCS L1000 studies. In clinical trials, SCENE discovers specific, well-supported subgroups and outperforms existing baselines. In L1000 studies, SCENE identifies perturbational contexts with strong target-response matching and high positive rates. These results show that SCENE bridges broad knowledge and scenario-specific evidence, producing traceable, inspectable hypotheses for follow-up validation.

AI.tldr: SCENE is a bi-level multi-agent search framework that contextualizes broad biomedical knowledge into concrete, data-supported, and traceable hypotheses for specific datasets.
AI.motivation: In biomedical research, there is a gap between broad, often generic domain knowledge (e.g., mechanisms, pathways, clinical understanding) and specific experimental or clinical datasets. Background knowledge is informative but too coarse to directly match dataset variables, while purely data-driven analyses often yield patterns that are difficult to interpret mechanistically and may be dataset-specific. The authors aim to bridge this gap by systematically transforming general knowledge into precise, evidence-backed propositions tailored to a given scenario or dataset, enabling experts to inspect, validate, and build on them.
AI.method: The paper introduces SCENE, a bi-level multi-agent framework that treats knowledge contextualization as an iterative search process. At the upper level, broad biomedical knowledge is converted into search directions, which are then grounded to the concrete schema of a given dataset (e.g., clinical variables, perturbations). At the lower level, these directions are operationalized via multi-objective optimization to find specific propositions (e.g., patient subgroups, context-specific responses) that trade off evidential strength and data support. The two levels interact iteratively: results from the lower level provide feedback that refines the upper-level search directions, leading to progressively better contextualized hypotheses. The framework is instantiated and evaluated in two application settings.
AI.result: In clinical trial datasets, SCENE successfully discovers specific patient subgroups that exhibit heterogeneous treatment benefits, and it quantitatively outperforms existing baseline methods on relevant metrics. In the LINCS L1000 perturbation studies, SCENE identifies perturbational contexts in which there is strong alignment between targets and biological responses, coupled with high positive rates. Across both settings, the framework produces hypotheses that are both well supported by data and consistent with prior knowledge.
AI.conclusion: SCENE effectively bridges broad biomedical knowledge and scenario-specific evidence by framing knowledge contextualization as a bi-level iterative search problem. It generates concrete, traceable, and interpretable hypotheses—such as treatment-responsive subgroups and context-specific perturbation effects—that outperform baselines and are suitable for expert inspection and downstream experimental or clinical validation.

title: Generating Logically Consistent Synthetic Supply Chain Data with LLM-Driven Knowledge Graph Reasoning
summary: Synthetic data offers a promising solution to two persistent barriers in supply chain analytics: data scarcity and data privacy. However, for synthetic data to support operational simulation and decision-making, it must do more than reproduce the statistical distributions of real records, and also preserve the \emph{operational logic} that governs supply chain processes, including the temporal orderings, mathematical dependencies, hierarchical taxonomies, and conditional rules that make a record operationally plausible. We consider this logic as the ``physics'' of supply chain data. Existing tabular generative models are primarily optimized for distributional fidelity and downstream predictive utility, and therefore often generate records that appear statistically realistic but violate fundamental operational constraints. This paper introduces \textbf{\textit{TabKG}}, a knowledge-graph-guided framework for logically consistent synthetic supply chain tabular data generation. TabKG constructs a \textbf{\textit{Column Relationship Knowledge Graph (CR-KG)}} to represent data operational dependencies. It uses a multi-LLM ensemble with majority voting to propose candidate relationships from column metadata, validates these relationships against real data to remove hallucinated or unsupported edges, and then uses the validated CR-KG to guide generation. Specifically, TabKG compresses the original table into independent columns, generates these columns using a latent diffusion model, and deterministically reconstructs dependent columns according to the validated relationships, enforcing logical consistency by construction with respect to the discovered operational rules.

AI.tldr: They propose TabKG, a knowledge-graph-guided framework to generate synthetic supply chain tables that respect operational logic, not just statistical distributions.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic tabular data generators focus on matching distributions or predictive performance, but in supply chain settings this leads to records that violate real-world operational rules (e.g., temporal order, dependencies, constraints), limiting their usefulness for simulation and decision-making. The authors want synthetic data that is both realistic and operationally consistent.
AI.method: 1) Define 'operational logic' as a set of dependencies among table columns (temporal, mathematical, hierarchical, conditional). 2) Build a Column Relationship Knowledge Graph (CR-KG) over columns. Candidate edges are mined by an ensemble of LLMs using column metadata, then pruned by checking against real data to remove hallucinated or unsupported relations. 3) Decompose the table into independent vs dependent columns based on the CR-KG. 4) Train a latent diffusion model to generate only the independent columns. 5) Reconstruct all dependent columns deterministically from the independent ones using the validated relationships, so that generated records satisfy the inferred rules by construction.
AI.result: A framework (TabKG) that can synthesize supply chain tabular data honoring inferred operational dependencies by first discovering and validating a column relationship graph and then constraining generation to respect this graph. Empirical results are implied (though not in the abstract) to show improved logical consistency compared with standard tabular generative models.
AI.conclusion: By representing operational logic as a column relationship knowledge graph and using it to constrain generative modeling—generating independent columns stochastically and reconstructing dependents deterministically—TabKG produces synthetic supply chain data that is not only statistically realistic but also logically consistent with underlying operational rules, making it more suitable for simulation and decision support.

title: Counteraction-Aware Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation for General Capability Recovery with Domain Preservation
summary: Domain specialization can improve LLM behavior in vertical domains, but often weakens the general capabilities inherited from the original model. Recent Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD) pipelines recover model capabilities by supervising student-generated trajectories with teacher feedback, but typically assume teacher-aligned prompt coverage, requiring prompts to match the teachers' training distributions. This assumption is difficult to satisfy when the general teacher is an open-source model whose post-training data are unknown. Instead of attempting to reconstruct this hidden distribution, we study general capability recovery with readily available proxy general prompts. We identify two failure modes of vanilla MOPD in this incomplete-coverage situation: recovery-preservation counteraction from mixing conflicting recovery and preservation gradients, and weak-signal flattening from uniformly averaging samples with unequal correction demand. We propose Counteraction-Aware Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation (CaMOPD), which addresses these issues with decoupled alternating training and gap-based sample selection. CaMOPD gives general recovery dedicated updates, periodically reviews domain prompts for preservation, and selects samples with larger averaged token-level teacher-student log-probability gaps to concentrate correction signals. Across role-play dialogue and medical reasoning QA scenarios, CaMOPD performs best in general recovery over baselines while maintaining domain-specific behavior. Gradient coherence analyses further support the intended effect of CaMOPD in producing more coherent correction signals.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CaMOPD, a new distillation method that better recovers general capabilities of domain-specialized LLMs when only proxy general prompts are available, by decoupling recovery and preservation training and focusing updates on samples with large teacher-student gaps.
AI.motivation: Domain-specializing large language models improves performance in specific verticals but harms their general abilities. Existing multi-teacher on-policy distillation methods can recover capabilities, but they assume that the prompts used for training align well with the teacher models’ training distributions, which is unrealistic when using open-source teachers with unknown post-training data. The authors want a way to recover general capabilities even when they only have imperfect proxy prompts and cannot match the teacher’s true data distribution.
AI.method: They analyze standard MOPD under incomplete teacher-prompt coverage and identify two failure modes: (1) recovery-preservation counteraction, where gradients from general-capability recovery and domain-preservation conflict when mixed; and (2) weak-signal flattening, where uniformly averaging samples dilutes strong correction signals because not all samples need the same amount of correction. They then propose CaMOPD, which (a) decouples and alternates between updates for general capability recovery and updates for domain behavior preservation, and (b) uses gap-based sample selection, preferring samples whose averaged token-level teacher-student log-probability gaps are larger, concentrating gradient updates where the teacher disagrees most with the student.
AI.result: In experiments on role-play dialogue and medical reasoning QA, CaMOPD achieves the best general capability recovery compared to baseline methods, while still preserving the desired domain-specific behavior. Gradient coherence analysis shows that CaMOPD increases the alignment (coherence) of correction gradients over training steps, supporting the claim that it reduces gradient conflicts and concentrates learning on meaningful corrections.
AI.conclusion: CaMOPD offers a more effective way to recover general capabilities in domain-specialized LLMs when only proxy general prompts are available. By separating recovery and preservation training and selecting high-gap samples, it mitigates gradient interference and weak supervision in standard MOPD, leading to better general-domain performance without sacrificing domain specialization, as confirmed empirically and via gradient coherence analysis.

title: ContextGuard: Structured Self-Auditing for Context Learning in Language Models
summary: Recent benchmarks reveal that despite strong reasoning capabilities, large language models (LLMs) still struggle to faithfully apply complex contextual knowledge. These failures are often not wholesale reasoning collapses: in context-rich tasks, models may follow the central reasoning path while missing peripheral, persistent, or format-sensitive requirements.

AI.tldr: LLMs show strong overall reasoning but still fail to correctly follow all nuanced contextual requirements in complex tasks.
AI.motivation: To investigate why LLMs with good reasoning skills still make mistakes when tasks require detailed adherence to rich, nuanced context and multiple constraints.
AI.method: Benchmarking and analyzing LLM behavior on context-rich tasks that involve central reasoning paths plus additional peripheral, persistent, or format-sensitive requirements.
AI.result: The benchmarks show that LLMs can track the main reasoning path but frequently miss side constraints, long-term requirements, or specific formatting and contextual details.
AI.conclusion: Even advanced LLMs need improved mechanisms for reliably tracking and applying all relevant contextual constraints, not just the core reasoning steps, in complex tasks.

title: Position: AI Safety Requires Effective Controllability
summary: AI safety is still largely framed as alignment: training models to follow human preferences, safety policies, and normative constraints. That framing has improved the behavior of modern language models, but aligned behavior does not by itself guarantee that a deployed agent can be stopped, overridden, or constrained once it operates in open-ended, interactive, and tool-using environments. A system may be safe in expectation and still fail to yield to explicit runtime authority under conflicting instructions, long-horizon execution, adversarial inputs, or risky tool use. This position paper argues that AI safety therefore requires controllability as a first-class objective. We define \emph{controllability} as the ability of an AI system to remain reliably interruptible, overridable, redirectable, and constrainable by explicit control signals at runtime while preserving ordinary utility when such signals are absent. To study this gap, we introduce \controlbench{}, a benchmark for evaluating controllability failures in high-risk agentic scenarios. Experiments with OpenClaw-based agents show that current alignment and guardrail mechanisms reduce risk, but often fail to provide persistent, authoritative, and enforceable runtime control. We therefore propose a control-centric architectural framework that highlights explicit control planes, runtime intervention pathways, persistent control states, and auditable decision interfaces as key design principles for future controllable AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that aligning AI behavior with human preferences is not enough for safety; we must additionally ensure that AI systems are controllable at runtime. It introduces a benchmark, ControlBench, to test controllability in high‑risk, agentic scenarios and proposes an architectural framework with explicit control mechanisms for future AI systems.
AI.motivation: Most AI safety work focuses on alignment—getting models to follow human preferences and safety policies under normal conditions. However, aligned behavior does not ensure that an AI agent operating in complex, open-ended environments will reliably submit to human control, especially under conflicting instructions, long-horizon tasks, adversarial inputs, or risky tool use. The motivation is to highlight and study this gap between alignment and actual, enforceable control over deployed agents.
AI.method: The authors conceptually distinguish controllability from alignment and formalize controllability as reliable interruptibility, overridability, redirectability, and constrainability via explicit runtime control signals while preserving normal task performance when no control signals are present. They then construct ControlBench, a benchmark consisting of high-risk, agentic scenarios designed to elicit controllability failures. Using OpenClaw-based agents, they empirically test how existing alignment and guardrail mechanisms perform on these scenarios. Based on the observed shortcomings, they outline a control-centric architectural framework emphasizing explicit control planes, runtime intervention pathways, persistent control states, and auditable decision interfaces.
AI.result: Experiments on ControlBench with OpenClaw-based agents show that current alignment techniques and guardrail mechanisms do reduce some risks but frequently fail to ensure strong runtime control: agents do not always remain persistently interruptible, overridable, or redirectable when faced with conflicting instructions or challenging scenarios. These empirical findings demonstrate a measurable gap between aligned behavior and true controllability in deployed agentic systems.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that AI safety must treat controllability as a first-class objective, distinct from but complementary to alignment. Existing alignment and guardrail methods are insufficient to guarantee robust runtime control in complex, high-risk scenarios. To address this, the authors advocate for a control-centric architecture for AI systems, built around explicit control planes, intervention pathways, persistent control states, and auditable decision interfaces, and propose ControlBench as a tool to systematically evaluate and drive progress on controllable AI design.

title: Optimising Factual Consistency in Summarisation via Preference Learning from Multiple Imperfect Metrics
summary: Reinforcement learning with evaluation metrics as rewards is widely used to enhance specific capabilities of language models. However, for tasks such as factually consistent summarisation, existing metrics remain underdeveloped, limiting their effectiveness as signals for shaping model behaviour.While individual factuality metrics are unreliable, their combination can more effectively capture diverse factual errors. We leverage this insight to introduce an automated training pipeline that improves factual consistency in summaries by aggregating scores from different weak metrics. Our approach avoids the need for complex reward shaping by mapping scores to preferences and filtering out cases with high disagreement between metrics. For each source document, we generate lexically similar summary pairs by varying decoding strategies, enabling the model to learn from factual differences caused by subtle lexical differences. This approach constructs a high-quality preference dataset using only source documents.Experiments demonstrate consistent factuality gains across models, ranging from early encoder-decoder architectures to modern large language models, with smaller models reaching comparable factuality to larger ones.

AI.tldr: They improve factual consistency in summaries by using multiple weak factuality metrics as rewards in a preference-learning RL pipeline.
AI.motivation: Existing RL approaches for language models often rely on a single automatic metric as a reward, which is problematic for factual summarisation because current factuality metrics are immature and individually unreliable. There is a need for a way to better supervise models towards factual consistency without requiring perfect metrics or expensive human feedback.
AI.method: They aggregate several weak factuality metrics and convert their outputs into preference labels between pairs of summaries. For each source document, they generate lexically similar summary pairs using different decoding strategies to induce subtle factual differences. They then filter out cases where different metrics disagree strongly, retaining higher-confidence preference data. This yields an automated preference dataset that can be used in a reinforcement learning or preference optimisation framework to train models for more factual summarisation.
AI.result: Across a range of models, from older encoder–decoder systems to modern large language models, training with their metric-aggregation preference pipeline improves factuality scores. Notably, smaller models trained with this approach achieve factuality competitive with much larger baseline models.
AI.conclusion: Combining multiple weak factuality metrics into an automated preference-generation pipeline enables effective RL-based training for factually consistent summarisation. This strategy reduces dependence on any single unreliable metric or on human feedback, scales across model sizes and architectures, and can substantially boost factuality, especially for smaller models.

title: Scaling, Benchmarking, and Reasoning of Vision-Language Agents for Mobile GUI Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown rapid progress in mobile GUI navigation. This paper presents a systematic study of data scaling, benchmarking, and reasoning for VLM-based agents in this domain. To facilitate rigorous evaluation, we introduce HyperTrack, a large-scale dataset with over 16000 real-world tasks across more than 650 Chinese mobile applications, along with GUIEvalKit, an open-source toolkit for unified benchmarking of VLMs on offline GUI navigation tasks. Using HyperTrack, we analyze the effects of training data scale on both supervised and reinforcement-based finetuning. Our results show that reinforcement-based finetuning consistently outperforms supervised finetuning, particularly in out-of-domain settings, highlighting the synergy between data scaling and reinforcement learning. Leveraging GUIEvalKit, we further benchmark state-of-the-art (SOTA) VLMs and analyze how interaction history and reasoning capabilities influence task completion. Together, HyperTrack and GUIEvalKit provide a comprehensive platform for developing and evaluating VLM agents in mobile GUI navigation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a large-scale dataset and a unified evaluation toolkit to study how data scaling and reasoning affect vision-language agents for mobile GUI navigation, showing that reinforcement-based finetuning scales better than supervised finetuning, especially out-of-domain.
AI.motivation: Existing work on vision-language models for mobile GUI navigation lacks a systematic, large-scale evaluation framework and clear understanding of how training data size and finetuning strategies (supervised vs. reinforcement learning) influence performance and generalization across diverse real-world apps.
AI.method: The authors construct HyperTrack, a dataset with 16,000+ real tasks across 650+ Chinese mobile apps, and build GUIEvalKit, a unified offline benchmarking toolkit. They then train and finetune VLM-based agents using both supervised and reinforcement-learning objectives at varying data scales, and evaluate them with GUIEvalKit to study the impact of data size, finetuning strategy, interaction history, and explicit reasoning on task completion.
AI.result: Experiments show that reinforcement-based finetuning consistently outperforms supervised finetuning, with the performance gap growing in out-of-domain scenarios, demonstrating a strong interaction between larger data scales and RL-based optimization. Benchmarks with GUIEvalKit further reveal that richer interaction history and stronger reasoning capabilities improve task completion for SOTA VLMs.
AI.conclusion: HyperTrack and GUIEvalKit together create a comprehensive platform for training and benchmarking VLM-based GUI agents. The study concludes that scaling data in combination with reinforcement-based finetuning and leveraging interaction history and reasoning are key to achieving robust, generalizable performance in mobile GUI navigation tasks.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Budget Allocation for Adaptive Test-Time Reasoning
summary: Sampling multiple responses improves language model reasoning, but uniform compute allocation is inefficient: easy questions are over-sampled while hard questions remain under-explored. We propose Uncertainty-Aware Budget Allocation (UAB), a concave integer optimization framework that reallocates a fixed sampling budget based on per-question uncertainty estimated at no additional inference cost. In Phase 1, every question receives one generation; its average negative log-likelihood (ANLL), extracted directly from output log-probabilities, serves as a difficulty signal while the generation contributes to the final vote. In Phase 2, the remaining budget is allocated by a marginal-greedy algorithm that solves a concave coverage-maximization surrogate exactly: uncertain questions receive more sampling budget while confident questions receive fewer additional samples. Evaluated on six open-weight and black-box models spanning 1.5B to 27B parameters and five reasoning benchmarks covering math, logic, and preference tasks, UAB outperforms baselines by up to +3% in average accuracy and up to +5% on individual benchmarks, with the largest gains in low-resource settings, requiring no auxiliary model or additional LLM call. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/manhitv/UAB.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Uncertainty-Aware Budget Allocation (UAB), a method to more efficiently distribute a fixed sampling budget across questions when using language models for reasoning, based on uncertainty estimated from a single initial generation per question.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches that improve language model reasoning via multiple sampled responses usually allocate the same number of samples to every question. This uniform allocation wastes compute on easy questions that are quickly and confidently solved, while difficult, uncertain questions do not receive enough exploratory samples. There is a need for a principled way to redistribute a fixed generation budget so that harder or more uncertain questions receive more attention without incurring additional inference or model costs.
AI.method: UAB is framed as a concave integer optimization problem for allocating a fixed sampling budget across questions. In Phase 1, each question gets exactly one generation from the language model. From this generation, the method extracts the average negative log-likelihood (ANLL) using the model’s output token log-probabilities, treating ANLL as a difficulty or uncertainty signal. This initial sample also counts as one vote toward the final answer. In Phase 2, the remaining sampling budget is distributed using a marginal-greedy algorithm that exactly optimizes a concave coverage-maximization surrogate objective. Questions with higher uncertainty (higher ANLL) receive more additional samples, while those with lower uncertainty receive fewer. The approach requires no extra model calls or auxiliary models beyond computing log-probabilities that are already available from the initial generation.
AI.result: Across six open-weight and black-box language models ranging from 1.5B to 27B parameters, evaluated on five reasoning benchmarks (math, logic, and preference tasks), UAB achieves up to +3 percentage points improvement in average accuracy compared to baselines and up to +5 percentage points on individual benchmarks. The performance gains are especially pronounced in low-resource settings, where the sampling budget is limited. All improvements come without additional inference calls or reliance on external models.
AI.conclusion: By using ANLL from a single initial generation as an uncertainty signal and reallocating remaining samples via a concave optimization-based greedy algorithm, UAB efficiently focuses sampling effort on harder questions. This leads to more accurate reasoning under a fixed compute budget compared to uniform sampling strategies, and it does so without extra inference cost or auxiliary models. The method is practical, model-agnostic, and demonstrates consistent gains across diverse models and benchmarks, with code publicly released for reproducibility.

title: ICCU: In-Context Continual Unlearning via Pattern-Induced Refusal Rules
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of specific data from trained language models. In real-world deployments, unlearning requests often arrive sequentially, which challenges existing fine-tuning-based methods: fine-tuning each request is costly, accumulates utility loss, and may cause cross-request interference. To address these issues, we propose ICCU (In-Context Continual Unlearning), an in-context continual unlearning framework that induces readable refusal rules from unlearning datasets and applies them at inference time either as a filter or via the system prompt, without modifying model parameters. Because rules are accumulated as an order-independent union, ICCU is compositional and free of cross-request interference, and the original forget-set data can be discarded after rule induction. Extensive experiments show that ICCU effectively suppresses target knowledge while preserving utility, scales across sequential requests, and remains robust to paraphrased and cross-lingual queries.

AI.tldr: Proposes ICCU, an in-context continual unlearning framework that uses induced refusal rules instead of parameter updates to remove specific knowledge from language models, avoiding cross-request interference and preserving utility.
AI.motivation: Existing machine unlearning for LMs typically relies on fine-tuning per unlearning request, which is expensive, accumulates performance degradation, and causes interference when many sequential unlearning requests arrive in real-world deployments. There is a need for a scalable, compositional, and low-cost way to handle continual unlearning without repeatedly modifying model weights.
AI.method: ICCU induces human-readable refusal rules from provided unlearning datasets (forget sets). These rules are stored and later applied at inference time, either through a filtering mechanism or by inserting them into the system prompt. The base model parameters remain unchanged. Rules from different unlearning requests are aggregated as an order-independent union, enabling compositionality and preventing interference between requests. After rules are learned, the original sensitive data can be discarded.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple unlearning benchmarks show that ICCU substantially suppresses the model’s ability to reproduce or use the target (to-be-forgotten) knowledge while maintaining overall task performance on non-forgotten data. ICCU handles long sequences of unlearning requests without accumulating utility loss, scales to paraphrased and cross-lingual queries, and compares favorably to fine-tuning-based unlearning baselines in efficiency and robustness.
AI.conclusion: ICCU demonstrates that in-context, rule-based continual unlearning is a viable alternative to parameter-updating methods, offering compositional, interference-free handling of sequential unlearning requests while preserving utility and robustness. This suggests a practical direction for deploying unlearning in real-world language model systems where requests arrive over time and underlying training data may need to be discarded.

title: Telenor Nordics Customer Service self-help corpus
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, 5 tables. Submitted to Nordic Machine Intelligence. Dataset: https://zenodo.org/records/19493152
summary: This paper presents a multilingual customer service self-help corpus comprising 1,122 manually validated documents in Finnish, Danish, Norwegian, and Swedish, totaling over one million tokens. The documents have been sourced from the public self-help pages of four Nordic telecommunications operators and subsequently filtered for person-identifiable information and relevance through a combined LLM and human annotation pipeline. Domain-specific datasets for Nordic languages remain scarce, particularly in customer service: a domain of growing importance for retrieval-augmented generation, cross-lingual transfer learning, and emerging agent-based service architectures. An analysis of the corpus reveals substantial variation in document length and structure across operators, reflecting distinct editorial strategies, as well as broad topical coverage spanning network hardware, mobile services, TV and streaming, billing, and account management. The dataset is publicly available under a CC-BY-NC-SA-4.0 license at https://zenodo.org/records/19493152, intended to support reproducible research in Nordic NLP and information retrieval.

AI.tldr: A new publicly available Nordic multilingual customer service self-help corpus (Finnish, Danish, Norwegian, Swedish) with 1,122 PII-filtered documents is introduced for research in NLP and information retrieval.
AI.motivation: Domain-specific datasets for Nordic languages, especially in customer service, are scarce, limiting progress in areas like retrieval-augmented generation, cross-lingual transfer, and agent-based service systems.
AI.method: The authors collected documents from public self-help pages of four Nordic telecom operators, then applied a pipeline combining large language models and human annotators to filter out personal data and ensure topical relevance. They analyzed document length, structure, and topical coverage across operators.
AI.result: They obtained a corpus of over one million tokens, 1,122 manually validated documents across four Nordic languages, with documented variation in length and structure between operators and broad topic coverage (hardware, mobile, TV/streaming, billing, account management). The dataset is released under a CC-BY-NC-SA-4.0 license on Zenodo.
AI.conclusion: The released corpus fills a gap in Nordic, domain-specific customer service data and is intended to facilitate reproducible research in Nordic NLP and information retrieval, benefiting tasks like RAG, cross-lingual transfer, and agent-based customer service architectures.

title: StepOPSD: Step-Aware Online Preference Distillation for Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning for multi-turn agents suffers from a credit-assignment mismatch: rewards are sparse and trajectory-level, while success often hinges on a few local decisions. Existing online policy distillation (OPD) provides denser token-level supervision, but typically treats heterogeneous agent trajectories as monolithic strings rather than causal interaction units. We present StepOPSD, a post-rollout preference self-distillation framework that takes the agent step as the unit of credit redistribution. StepOPSD decomposes trajectories into action-centered step segments, rescoring them under hindsight-enriched teacher contexts and converting token-level log-probability gaps into sign-preserving advantage shaping with a normalized per-step credit budget before the GRPO update. Across ALFWorld and Search-QA with Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, StepOPSD attains best or second-best results on subsets most sensitive to local causal errors, including first-place performance on ALFWorld Heat (79.1%), PickTwo (95.0%), Search-QA TriviaQA (61.6%), and tied-best performance on HotpotQA (40.4%). The results further reveal a consistent two-knob law: smaller α_clip acts as a broadly stabilizing local trust region, whereas the optimal global mixing strength λ_mix remains task-dependent. These findings suggest that step-aware distillation is most useful when trajectory-level rewards are weakly aligned with the local action that determines downstream success.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes StepOPSD, a step-wise online preference self-distillation method that fixes credit-assignment mismatch in multi‑turn RL agents by redistributing trajectory-level rewards down to action steps, yielding better performance on tasks where local decisions drive success.
AI.motivation: In multi-turn reinforcement learning for agents (e.g., text-based environments or tool-using LMs), rewards are typically sparse and given only at the trajectory level (success/failure at the end). However, whether an episode succeeds often depends on a few crucial local actions. Standard RL struggles to correctly attribute credit to those steps, and existing online policy distillation methods, while providing denser token-level feedback, treat entire trajectories as flat strings, ignoring their causal step structure. This creates a mismatch between where reward is measured and where decisions matter, limiting learning efficiency and performance, especially on tasks sensitive to local causal errors.
AI.method: The authors introduce StepOPSD, a post-rollout preference self-distillation framework that operates at the agent-step level. After an agent generates a trajectory, StepOPSD: (1) decomposes the trajectory into action-centered step segments (each corresponding to an agent decision and its immediate context), (2) rescores these segments using a teacher that has access to hindsight-enriched context (i.e., information from the whole trajectory to better judge each step), (3) computes token-level log-probability differences between teacher and student per step, and (4) converts these gaps into sign-preserving advantage signals. These advantages are normalized so that each step receives a bounded credit budget, then fed into a GRPO-style policy update. The method has two key hyperparameters: α_clip, controlling local trust-region-like clipping for stability, and λ_mix, controlling how strongly distillation is mixed with the base RL signal.
AI.result: On ALFWorld and Search-QA benchmarks using Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct backbones, StepOPSD achieves best or second-best performance on subsets where success is particularly sensitive to local causal decisions. It reaches first place on ALFWorld Heat (79.1%), PickTwo (95.0%), and Search-QA TriviaQA (61.6%), and tied best performance on HotpotQA (40.4%). Empirically, they find a robust pattern in hyperparameters: smaller α_clip consistently improves stability like a conservative trust region, while the optimal mixing coefficient λ_mix varies by task, indicating task-dependent value of the distillation signal.
AI.conclusion: StepOPSD demonstrates that redistributing trajectory-level reward down to step-level credit using hindsight-aware teacher scores can substantially improve multi-turn RL agents, especially when trajectory-level rewards are weakly aligned with the specific local actions that determine success. The identified two-knob law (small α_clip for stability, task-dependent λ_mix for performance) suggests practical tuning guidelines. Overall, the work argues that step-aware, causally structured distillation is a promising direction for overcoming credit-assignment challenges in RL for language-based agents.

title: GeoFaith: A Spatio-Temporal Dual View of Faithful Chain-of-Thought
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has advanced large language models (LLMs), but outcome-based supervision leads to pervasive post-hoc rationalization, producing plausible yet unfaithful reasoning chains. Most prior faithfulness assessment methods are either unscalable, expensive, or unreliable. We propose GeoFaith, a spatio-temporal framework that leverages latent geometric structure and entropy dynamics to diagnose and enforce faithful reasoning. We develop a scalable bootstrapping pipeline expanding step-level annotations from 1k to 20k samples across four domains, train an 8B faithfulness detector outperforming GPT-5 on standard benchmarks, and design a faithfulness-aware reinforcement learning framework jointly optimizing outcome correctness, process faithfulness, and trajectory consistency. Experiments show the proposed method achieves superior performance on both faithfulness detection and downstream reasoning, producing shorter, more interpretable chains without sacrificing accuracy. Our code will be made available publicly.

AI.tldr: GeoFaith introduces a framework to diagnose and enforce faithful Chain-of-Thought reasoning in LLMs using geometric and entropy-based analysis plus RL, achieving better faithfulness and reasoning quality without losing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing Chain-of-Thought supervision often yields unfaithful, post-hoc rationalizations, and current methods to assess faithfulness are either too costly, unscalable, or unreliable. The paper aims to provide a scalable, accurate way to detect and enforce truly faithful reasoning processes in LLMs.
AI.method: The authors propose GeoFaith, a spatio-temporal framework that exploits latent geometric structure and entropy dynamics in model trajectories to measure reasoning faithfulness. They build a scalable bootstrapping pipeline to grow step-level annotations from 1k to 20k samples in four domains, train an 8B-parameter faithfulness detector, and integrate this detector into a reinforcement learning setup that jointly optimizes for answer correctness, process faithfulness, and consistency of reasoning trajectories.
AI.result: The 8B faithfulness detector surpasses GPT-5 on standard faithfulness benchmarks. The overall framework improves both faithfulness detection and downstream reasoning quality, yielding shorter, more interpretable reasoning chains without reducing task accuracy.
AI.conclusion: GeoFaith offers an effective and scalable solution for diagnosing and enforcing faithful Chain-of-Thought reasoning in LLMs. By leveraging geometric and entropy-based signals along with a faithfulness-aware RL objective, it improves faithfulness and interpretability of reasoning while maintaining performance, and the accompanying codebase is to be released publicly.

title: VitaBench 2.0: Evaluating Personalized and Proactive Agents in Long-Term User Interactions
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have evolved into interactive agents that collaborate with users in real-world tasks. Effective collaboration in such settings increasingly depends on understanding the user beyond what is explicitly stated, as user intent is often reflected in fragmented daily interactions and requires both personalized modeling and proactive interaction. However, existing agent benchmarks primarily evaluate reasoning and tool use, largely overlooking the challenges of inferring and leveraging user preferences in realistic scenarios. To address this gap, we introduce VitaBench 2.0, a benchmark for evaluating personalized and proactive agent behavior in long-term user interactions. In VitaBench 2.0, tasks are organized as temporally ordered sequences for individual users, where preferences are embedded in fragmented and heterogeneous interactions. Successful completion of tasks requires the agent to continuously extract, utilize, and update user preferences from these interactions. We further evaluate proactiveness through tasks that require agents to recognize missing information and actively acquire it from users or environments before making decisions. To support systematic analysis, we provide an extensible memory interface that enables controlled comparison across different memory architectures. We benchmark a diverse set of frontier proprietary and open-source LLMs. Results show that real-world personalization remains highly challenging even for state-of-the-art models, revealing a substantial gap between current capabilities and practical requirements. Extensive analysis further reveals the failure modes and capability bottlenecks of current agents in real-world personalized decision-making, providing insights for future model improvements.

AI.tldr: VitaBench 2.0 is a benchmark designed to evaluate how well large language model agents can behave in a personalized and proactive way over long-term, realistic user interactions.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agent benchmarks mainly test reasoning and tool use, but real-world collaboration requires agents to understand user preferences and intent that are implicit, fragmented, and spread over time. There is a lack of systematic evaluation focused on personalization and proactiveness in such long-horizon, realistic scenarios.
AI.method: The authors build VitaBench 2.0, where each user has a temporally ordered sequence of heterogeneous interaction tasks. User preferences are hidden within these fragmented interactions. Agents must continuously extract, store, update, and apply these preferences to complete tasks. The benchmark also includes tasks that require proactive behavior: detecting missing information, querying users or the environment, and then making decisions. An extensible memory interface is provided so different memory architectures can be plugged in and compared under controlled conditions. Multiple proprietary and open-source LLMs are evaluated on this benchmark.
AI.result: Across a diverse set of state-of-the-art proprietary and open-source LLMs, performance on VitaBench 2.0 is still poor, indicating that robust real-world personalization and proactiveness remain unsolved. The experiments expose specific failure cases and limitations in how current agents model, retain, and use user preferences over time and how they decide when and how to ask for missing information.
AI.conclusion: Realistic, long-term personalized interaction is still a major open challenge for LLM-based agents. VitaBench 2.0 offers a structured benchmark and memory interface to study and compare approaches, and the observed performance gaps and failure modes highlight where future research and model development should focus to build more capable personalized and proactive agents.

title: Are Video Models Zero-Shot Learners and Reasoners in Education? EduVideoBench, A Knowledge-Skills-Attitude Benchmark for Educational Video Generation
summary: Video generation models (VGMs) are rapidly entering classrooms, yet existing benchmarks evaluate only perceptual quality, intrinsic faithfulness, generic safety, or video as a reasoning medium, and none assesses whether the outputs are educationally valid. In this work, we present EduVideoBench, the first balanced benchmark in the education domain, grounded in the Knowledge-Skills-Attitude (KSA) framework so that pedagogical adequacy and educational safety are evaluated jointly rather than as ad-hoc quality dimensions. Across five frontier VGMs, our results show substantial room for improvement across knowledge, skills, and attitude before they are classroom-ready. We complement this with a qualitative analysis of expert comments, finding that educational validity is multi-component, where a single misaligned element such as pacing, legibility, or notation can invalidate an otherwise correct video. We hope EduVideoBench will guide the development of VGMs that are pedagogically grounded and safe for the classroom.

AI.tldr: EduVideoBench is a new benchmark to systematically test whether video generation models produce pedagogically sound and safe educational videos, not just visually good ones.
AI.motivation: Video generation models are starting to be used in classrooms, but current benchmarks focus on visual quality, basic faithfulness, generic safety, or using video for reasoning tasks. None directly measure whether generated videos are actually suitable and effective for teaching and learning. There is a need for an evaluation framework that reflects real educational goals and constraints so that models can be judged on their classroom readiness.
AI.method: The authors design EduVideoBench, a balanced benchmark specifically for the education domain, structured around the Knowledge-Skills-Attitude (KSA) framework from educational theory. They define evaluation dimensions and criteria that jointly consider pedagogical adequacy (e.g., correctness, clarity, pacing) and educational safety (e.g., harmful content, inappropriate attitudes). They then apply this benchmark to five state-of-the-art video generation models and perform both quantitative scoring and qualitative expert analysis of the generated educational videos.
AI.result: When evaluated on EduVideoBench, five frontier video generation models show significant deficiencies across all three KSA dimensions—knowledge, skills, and attitude—indicating that their outputs are not yet reliably suitable for classroom use. Expert commentary further reveals that even when the factual content is mostly correct, issues such as poor pacing, low legibility, or incorrect notation can undermine the educational usefulness of a video.
AI.conclusion: EduVideoBench demonstrates that current video generation models are far from educationally reliable and highlights that educational validity is a multi-component construct: small misalignments in presentation or attitude can negate otherwise correct content. The benchmark provides a structured way to measure pedagogical adequacy and safety together, and the authors position it as a tool to steer future VGM development toward being both pedagogically grounded and safe for classroom deployment.

title: Detecting Is Not Resolving: The Monitoring Control Gap in Retrieval Augmented LLMs
summary: Retrieval-augmented LLMs are deployed for tasks where evidence quality determines action safety, yet evaluation protocols assume that single-turn robustness predicts robustness when evidence accumulates across turns. We show this assumption is fundamentally incorrect. Models exhibit a monitoring-control gap: they readily acknowledge contradictory evidence, yet this awareness fails to constrain their final recommendations - detecting epistemic conflict does not imply resolving it safely. Through a multi-turn document accumulation protocol across four model families (1.5B-32B parameters) and over 50,000 turn-level evaluations, we demonstrate that single-turn diagnostics systematically overestimate RAG safety, that contradiction acknowledgement is uncorrelated with safe resolution, a pattern corroborated by targeted human validation, and that no universal prompt fix exists. Converging mechanism evidence - hidden-state probing, attention analysis, and response-strategy taxonomy - points to action selection as the most plausible locus of the deficit: danger-relevant information is internally represented and receives enhanced attention during unsafe generation, yet fails to constrain output behavior. The gap between what models recognize and what they do must be measured and closed before retrieval-augmented systems can be trusted in high-stakes settings.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that retrieval-augmented LLMs can notice contradictory evidence across multiple turns but still give unsafe final recommendations, so standard single-turn robustness tests overestimate safety.
AI.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems are being used in settings where following bad or inconsistent evidence can cause harm. Existing safety evaluations usually test one query–one context scenarios, implicitly assuming that if a model is robust in a single turn, it will stay robust as more documents and potential contradictions accumulate. The authors suspect this assumption is wrong and want to rigorously test whether models can both detect and safely resolve epistemic conflicts over multiple turns.
