title: PashtoTTS-Bench: automated screening for low-resource non-Latin-script text-to-speech
summary: Text-to-speech (TTS) evaluation for low-resource non-Latin-script languages can fail when it relies on a single ASR round-trip word error rate (WER). A system may produce no audio, speak a neighbouring language, preserve target script text only in an ASR transcript, or sound unnatural to native listeners. We introduce INSV (Intelligibility, Naturalness, Script fidelity, and Verification), a reporting framework that separates these cases. This paper reports INSV-A, the automated screening subset: synthesis completion, ASR WER/CER, transcript Script Fidelity Rate, and audio language identification. Native MOS and phonetic annotation are specified but not claimed in this release. We instantiate INSV-A as PashtoTTS-Bench, a dated benchmark for Pashto TTS. The April-May 2026 run evaluates Edge GulNawaz, Edge Latifa, OmniVoice clone, OmniVoice auto, and an Urdu negative control on 200 FLEURS and 200 filtered Common Voice 24 prompts. Under the independent omniASR_CTC_300M_v2, OmniVoice auto has the lowest WER (24.1% FLEURS, 27.4% CV24), followed by Edge GulNawaz (32.8%, 39.5%), Edge Latifa (35.6%, 47.7%), and OmniVoice clone (45.4%, 34.8%). WER below the natural-speech baseline reflects clean synthetic audio and should not be read as better than native speech. Whisper Large V3 returns 0.0% Pashto labels on checked Pashto TTS audio, while MMS-LID-4017 and SpeechBrain VoxLingua107 separate Pashto outputs from the Urdu control. The release provides provider metadata, per-sentence scores, LID audits, failure logs, and scripts for adding systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces INSV, a richer evaluation framework for low‑resource, non‑Latin-script TTS, and instantiates its automated part (INSV-A) as a Pashto TTS benchmark showing system ranking and LID/tool limitations.
AI.motivation: Standard TTS evaluation with a single ASR round‑trip WER is unreliable for low‑resource, non‑Latin-script languages because it cannot distinguish important failure modes: missing audio, wrong language output, text only preserved in ASR transcripts, or speech that is unintelligible or unnatural to native listeners. There is a need for a structured framework that decomposes these aspects so researchers can more accurately assess system performance and track progress, especially for Pashto and similar languages where tooling is weak and benchmarks are lacking.
AI.method: Define the INSV framework with four dimensions: Intelligibility, Naturalness, Script fidelity, and Verification. In this paper, implement only the automated screening subset, INSV-A, which includes: (1) checking whether synthesis completed, (2) computing ASR WER and CER with an independent Pashto ASR model (omniASR_CTC_300M_v2), (3) measuring Script Fidelity Rate from ASR transcripts to ensure output preserves target script, and (4) running audio language identification using multiple LID models (Whisper Large V3, MMS-LID-4017, SpeechBrain VoxLingua107). Instantiate this as PashtoTTS-Bench, a dated benchmark. Evaluate several systems (Edge GulNawaz, Edge Latifa, OmniVoice clone, OmniVoice auto, and an Urdu negative control) on 200 FLEURS and 200 filtered Common Voice 24 Pashto prompts, and release metadata, per-sentence scores, LID audits, failure logs, and scripts to facilitate adding new systems.
AI.result: On PashtoTTS-Bench under omniASR_CTC_300M_v2, OmniVoice auto achieves the lowest WER (24.1% on FLEURS and 27.4% on CV24), followed by Edge GulNawaz (32.8%, 39.5%), Edge Latifa (35.6%, 47.7%), and OmniVoice clone (45.4%, 34.8%). Some synthetic systems attain WER lower than a natural-speech baseline, interpreted as an artifact of cleaner synthetic audio rather than superior intelligibility to native speech. For language identification, Whisper Large V3 fails to label any checked Pashto TTS audio as Pashto (0.0% Pashto labels), whereas MMS-LID-4017 and SpeechBrain VoxLingua107 successfully distinguish Pashto system outputs from the Urdu negative control. The benchmark infrastructure captures detailed run metadata, sentence-level metrics, and failure patterns.
AI.conclusion: A single ASR WER metric is inadequate for evaluating low-resource, non-Latin-script TTS. The proposed INSV framework, and specifically its automated INSV-A subset instantiated as PashtoTTS-Bench, offers a more nuanced and practical evaluation by separating intelligibility, naturalness, script fidelity, and verification dimensions. The initial Pashto benchmark demonstrates measurable differences among current systems, highlights tool limitations (e.g., Whisper’s LID failure), and provides a reusable, extensible infrastructure with transparent data and scripts to support ongoing comparative evaluation and future expansion to include native listener MOS and phonetic annotation.

title: Prompt Injection Detection is Regime-Dependent: A Deployment-Aware Evaluation with Interpretable Structural Signals
summary: Prompt injection poses a critical threat to the safe deployment of large language models, yet existing detection approaches are typically evaluated under limited settings that do not reflect real-world operating constraints. In this work, we present a deployment-aware evaluation of prompt injection detection using a multi-model and multi-regime experimental framework. We compare lexical, semantic, structural, and transformer-based detectors across multiple out-of-distribution settings, repeated data splits, and both ranking and thresholded deployment metrics. We introduce interpretable structural signals that capture hierarchy overrides, system prompt spoofing, role redefinition, and evasion patterns, and assess their contribution both within sparse models and in combination with strong encoder baselines. Our results show that detection performance is highly regime-dependent and sensitive to threshold selection, with no single model dominating across all settings. Transformer-based models achieve the strongest overall performance, while structural signals provide modest but consistent gains in certain regimes and improve low false positive rate behaviour in harder scenarios. These findings highlight the gap between ranking performance and deployment effectiveness and underscore the importance of evaluating prompt injection defences under realistic operational constraints. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates prompt injection detectors under realistic deployment conditions, showing that performance varies greatly across settings and that no single model is best everywhere, though transformer-based methods generally perform strongest.
AI.motivation: Prompt injection is a major security risk for large language models, but existing detection methods are usually tested in simplified, narrow setups that do not match real-world constraints. There is a need for a comprehensive, deployment-aware evaluation to understand how different detectors actually perform when subjected to varied and challenging conditions.
AI.method: The authors build a multi-model, multi-regime experimental framework to evaluate a range of detectors (lexical, semantic, structural, and transformer-based) across diverse out-of-distribution scenarios, multiple random data splits, and both ranking-based and thresholded deployment metrics. They also design interpretable structural features that encode hierarchy overrides, system prompt spoofing, role redefinition, and evasion strategies, and test these signals both in sparse models and when combined with strong encoder-based baselines.
AI.result: Detection performance is found to be highly dependent on the evaluation regime and the chosen decision thresholds, with no single detector consistently outperforming the others in all settings. Transformer-based detectors generally give the best overall performance. The proposed structural signals yield modest but reliable improvements in certain regimes and help achieve better low–false positive rate performance in difficult scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a significant mismatch between good ranking scores in standard evaluations and true deployment effectiveness for prompt injection detection. It emphasizes that defences must be assessed under realistic operational constraints and that incorporating structural signals can usefully complement strong transformer-based detectors, especially when low false positive rates are crucial.

title: Cast a Wider Net: Coordinated Pass@K Policy Optimization for Code Reasoning
comment: Code reasoning; pass@K optimization; coordinated planning; verifiable rewards; strategy diversity
summary: Repeated sampling with a verifier is the standard way to allocate test-time compute for code generation, with pass@$K$ as the canonical metric. Yet the standard policy class draws $K$ independent samples from a single answer distribution, so attempts often collapse onto near-duplicate reasoning paths and waste the budget on redundant rollouts. This failure is costly in competitive programming, where many problems admit multiple distinct algorithmic strategies and pass@$K$ requires only one correct attempt. We propose Coordinated Pass@$K$ Policy Optimization (CPPO), which turns pass@$K$ generation into joint exploration over strategies: a planner emits a tuple of $K{=}4$ alternative high-level methods, and a shared solver attempts one solution per method. CPPO trains this joint policy with a multiplicative planner reward, $R_{\mathrm{plan}} = J_ψ\cdot R_{\mathrm{out}}$, assigning credit only to valid strategy tuples that lead to verifier-confirmed pass@$K$ success. Across APPS, CodeContests, and LiveCodeBench-v6, CPPO improves pass@$4$ over direct sampling, planning baselines, planner-only SFT, and pass@$K$-oriented RL under the same $K{=}4$ solver-attempt budget, with statistically significant gains on six of nine model--benchmark cells. The largest single gain is $+0.16$ on Qwen3.5-9B LiveCodeBench-v6 over the strongest baseline, PKPO ($0.588 \rightarrow 0.748$; paired bootstrap, $p < 0.05$).

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new training framework, CPPO, that coordinates multiple diverse solution strategies for code generation to better use a fixed pass@K budget, significantly improving pass@4 across several competitive programming benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard pass@K code generation draws K independent samples from one answer distribution, often producing near-duplicate solutions that waste compute. In competitive programming, many problems admit several different algorithmic strategies, and pass@K only needs one of them to succeed. There is a need for a method that explicitly encourages diverse, complementary strategies within a fixed attempt budget to increase the chance at least one solution passes.
AI.method: CPPO (Coordinated Pass@K Policy Optimization) reframes pass@K generation as joint exploration over strategies. A planner module outputs a tuple of K=4 distinct high-level solution methods for a given problem. A shared solver module then attempts one concrete solution per planned method. Training uses a multiplicative planner reward, R_plan = J_ψ·R_out, which gives credit only when a full tuple of strategies leads to a verified pass@K success. This encourages coordinated, diverse strategies that collectively maximize pass@K.
AI.result: Evaluated on APPS, CodeContests, and LiveCodeBench-v6, CPPO consistently improves pass@4 compared to several baselines: naive direct sampling, other planning-based approaches, planner-only supervised fine-tuning, and RL methods designed for pass@K. Under the same K=4 solver-attempt budget, CPPO achieves statistically significant gains in six out of nine model–benchmark combinations. The largest observed improvement is a +0.16 absolute increase in pass@4 on Qwen3.5-9B on LiveCodeBench-v6 over the strongest baseline PKPO (from 0.588 to 0.748, with p < 0.05 by paired bootstrap).
AI.conclusion: Jointly planning diverse strategies and assigning reward at the tuple level is more effective than independently sampling solutions from a single policy for pass@K-based code generation. CPPO more efficiently uses a fixed test-time budget by coordinating exploration over distinct algorithmic methods, leading to robust and statistically significant pass@4 improvements across multiple competitive programming benchmarks and models.

title: PersLitEval: Fine-grained Benchmark and Evaluation of LLMs on Persian Literature Questions
summary: Despite impressive multilingual capabilities, large language models (LLMs) remain poorly evaluated on literary knowledge in non-English languages. We introduce PersLitEval, a benchmark of 4,514 Persian literature multiple-choice questions across eight fine-grained categories spanning spelling, literary devices, grammar, vocabulary, word formation, and conceptual understanding, sourced from materials for the Konkur university entrance examination. We evaluate six LLMs across ten prompting strategies, revealing striking category-level disparities across three tiers of task difficulty: models reach higher accuracy on conceptual similarity tasks but struggle with formal linguistic analysis, with spelling and word formation proving the hardest across all models. Prompting strategy has a significant impact on performance, with explained few-shot examples yielding the best results, particularly on formal linguistic categories. An error analysis identifies three failure modes: semantic comprehension gaps, formal linguistic knowledge gaps, and counting/enumeration errors, suggesting that different categories require different improvement strategies.

AI.tldr: They build and use a new Persian literature multiple-choice benchmark (PersLitEval) to systematically evaluate LLMs’ non-English literary knowledge and analyze their failure modes and prompting sensitivities.
AI.motivation: Existing multilingual evaluations of LLMs focus mostly on English or on general tasks, leaving a gap in assessing fine-grained literary and linguistic knowledge in non-English languages like Persian. There is a need to understand how well LLMs handle domain-specific, high-stakes exam-style questions and where they fail, both overall and by category and difficulty tier.
AI.method: The authors construct PersLitEval, a dataset of 4,514 Persian literature multiple-choice questions covering eight fine-grained categories, drawn from Konkur exam prep materials. They then evaluate six different LLMs under ten distinct prompting strategies. They analyze performance at the category level and across three difficulty tiers and conduct an error analysis to identify recurring failure modes.
AI.result: Across models, accuracy is higher on conceptual similarity questions but lower on formal linguistic analysis, with spelling and word formation being the most challenging categories. Prompting choice matters substantially: prompts with explained few-shot examples generally yield the best performance overall and particularly improve results on formal linguistic tasks. The analysis reveals three main types of errors: gaps in semantic comprehension, gaps in formal linguistic knowledge, and mistakes in counting/enumeration.
AI.conclusion: PersLitEval exposes that current LLMs’ Persian literary competence is uneven: they can handle conceptual similarity questions relatively well but struggle with formal linguistic aspects like spelling and morphology. Carefully designed prompts with explanatory few-shot examples can mitigate some weaknesses, especially on formal categories, but the identified failure modes indicate that different aspects—semantic understanding, linguistic knowledge, and basic enumeration—require targeted improvement strategies in model training and evaluation.

title: Evaluating the Relevance of Uncertainty Estimators for LLM Hallucination
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are prone to hallucinations, i.e., statements unsupported by the input or training data, hindering reliable deployment. In parallel, numerous uncertainty estimation (UE) methods have been proposed to quantify model confidence and are often implicitly treated as proxies for model failure. However, the relationship between uncertainty and hallucinations remains insufficiently characterized. We present a systematic empirical study of the association between uncertainty estimators and hallucinations in LLMs. Rather than assuming this association, we evaluate directly when and to what extent it holds. We consider a diverse set of uncertainty estimators, including information-theoretic, sampling-based, and reflexive estimators, and examine their behavior across hallucination settings. Our experiments cover both intrinsic hallucinations (violations of input faithfulness) and extrinsic hallucinations (unsupported claims relative to training data), using four complementary benchmarks, including RAGTruth and HalluLens. We find that the association is highly variable and often weak, depending on the hallucination type and the LLM under evaluation. These results challenge the use of uncertainty as a direct signal of hallucination and clarify when it provides actionable information.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically studies how well various uncertainty estimation methods in LLMs align with hallucinations and finds that this association is highly variable and often weak.
AI.motivation: LLMs frequently hallucinate, producing unsupported statements that limit their reliability. Many uncertainty estimation techniques have been proposed and are informally used as indicators of model failure, but it is unclear how reliably they actually signal hallucinations. The paper aims to clarify and quantify this relationship instead of assuming it.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic empirical evaluation of multiple uncertainty estimators—including information-theoretic metrics, sampling-based methods, and reflexive (self-evaluation) approaches—across several hallucination scenarios. They examine both intrinsic hallucinations (unfaithful to input) and extrinsic hallucinations (unsupported by training data), using four benchmarks such as RAGTruth and HalluLens, and compare uncertainty signals to actual hallucination occurrences for different LLMs.
AI.result: Across the benchmarks and models, the correlation between uncertainty estimates and hallucinations is inconsistent and often weak. The effectiveness of uncertainty as an indicator of hallucination depends strongly on the hallucination type, the specific uncertainty method, and the underlying LLM.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty estimates cannot be universally treated as reliable proxies for hallucinations in LLMs. Practitioners should be cautious about using uncertainty as a direct hallucination signal and instead consider when and where particular estimators offer useful, actionable information. The study delineates conditions under which uncertainty is more or less informative for hallucination detection.

title: Attribute-Based Diagnosis of LLM Alignment with Hate Speech Annotations
summary: Hate speech annotation is costly, subjective, and prone to annotator disagreement, making large-scale dataset construction challenging. We systematically analyze how well large language models (LLMs) align with human judgments across ten theoretically grounded subjective attributes, such as dehumanization, violence, and sentiment, evaluating both small and large variants of Llama 3.1 and Qwen 2.5. Our analysis reveals a consistent split across all models: behaviorally explicit dimensions (insult, humiliate, attack-defend) correlate strongly with human annotations, while evaluative dimensions (respect, sentiment, hate speech) are systematically inverted. Demographic persona conditioning reduces model confidence without improving alignment. Building on these insights, we propose combining attribute-level LLM predictions via a confidence-weighted Ridge regression to reconstruct continuous hate speech scores from the Measuring Hate Speech corpus, achieving $R^2$ of up to 0.71 and outperforming direct prompting baselines, demonstrating that structured attribute decomposition recovers a richer and more human-aligned signal than end-to-end label prediction alone.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well large language models align with human judgments on hate speech by decomposing hate speech into multiple subjective attributes and then recombining them to predict continuous hate scores, showing this structured approach outperforms direct label prediction.
AI.motivation: Hate speech annotation is expensive, subjective, and subject to annotator disagreement, making it difficult to build large, high-quality datasets. At the same time, LLMs are increasingly used for content moderation, but their alignment with nuanced human judgments about different aspects of hate speech is poorly understood. The authors want to analyze where LLMs agree or disagree with humans across specific dimensions of hatefulness and explore whether leveraging LLMs in a structured way can approximate rich, continuous human hate speech scores without needing extensive new annotations.
AI.method: The authors evaluate several LLMs (small and large variants of Llama 3.1 and Qwen 2.5) on ten theoretically motivated subjective attributes related to hate speech, such as dehumanization, violence, insult, humiliation, respect, sentiment, and hate speech itself. They compare model outputs to human annotations along these dimensions to measure alignment patterns and investigate demographic persona conditioning. Based on the observed attribute-level patterns, they build a confidence-weighted Ridge regression model that takes the set of LLM-predicted attributes as input features and predicts continuous hate speech scores from the Measuring Hate Speech corpus. They compare this structured, attribute-based prediction to simple direct prompting baselines where the LLM predicts hate speech scores or labels end-to-end.
AI.result: They find a consistent pattern across all tested LLMs: models align well with humans on behaviorally explicit attributes like insult, humiliation, and attack-defend, but perform poorly and even inversely on evaluative attributes like respect, sentiment, and overall hate speech. Persona conditioning of the models reduces the confidence of their responses but does not improve alignment with human annotations. The proposed confidence-weighted Ridge regression that combines attribute-level predictions achieves an R^2 of up to 0.71 on the continuous hate speech scores of the Measuring Hate Speech corpus, outperforming direct prompting approaches.
AI.conclusion: LLMs do not uniformly align with human judgments of hate speech: they are better at identifying explicit hostile behaviors than at matching evaluative, normative judgments such as overall hatefulness or respect, and naïve persona conditioning does not fix this misalignment. However, decomposing hate speech into multiple interpretable attributes and then recombining LLM predictions with a simple regression model can recover a more human-aligned, continuous hate speech signal than directly asking LLMs for hate speech labels or scores. This suggests that structured attribute modeling is a promising direction for leveraging LLMs in hate speech moderation and related tasks, while highlighting the need to carefully distinguish between behavioral and evaluative dimensions of harmful content.

title: Share More, Search Less: Collaborative Parallel Thinking for Efficient Test-Time Scaling
summary: Test-Time Scaling (TTS) enhances the reasoning capabilities of large language models by allocating additional inference compute to explore the solution space. However, existing parallel TTS methods typically keep branches isolated during search: intermediate discoveries remain branch-private and cannot guide other branches in time. This information isolation causes substantial redundant exploration, as branches repeatedly rediscover information already found elsewhere and require more search steps to collect complete decision information needed to reach correct answers. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{Collaborative Parallel Thinking (CPT)}, a training-free inference framework that enables search-time information sharing across parallel branches. CPT extracts compact intermediate information from ongoing branches, maintains a deduplicated query-level information pool, and broadcasts pool entries through the input context, allowing each branch in subsequent search steps to reuse discoveries made by other branches rather than rediscover the same information. Empirically, experiments on HMMT and AIME benchmarks show that CPT establishes a stronger accuracy--latency Pareto frontier than strong baselines across rollout budgets and model scales, highlighting search-time collaboration as an effective direction for efficient parallel TTS.

AI.tldr: Introduces Collaborative Parallel Thinking (CPT), an inference-time framework that lets parallel test-time reasoning branches share intermediate information, reducing redundant exploration and improving the accuracy–latency trade-off for large language models.
AI.motivation: Existing parallel Test-Time Scaling (TTS) methods run multiple reasoning branches independently. Their intermediate findings are not shared, so branches repeatedly rediscover the same facts or partial solutions. This information isolation wastes compute and requires more search steps to reach correct answers, limiting the effectiveness and efficiency of TTS.
AI.method: CPT is a training-free inference framework that augments parallel search with cross-branch collaboration. During inference, it: (1) extracts compact intermediate information (e.g., key facts, partial derivations) from ongoing branches; (2) aggregates these into a deduplicated, query-level information pool; and (3) periodically feeds entries from this pool back into each branch via the input context. Thus, later steps in each branch can condition on discoveries made by other branches instead of re-deriving them. The approach is evaluated across different rollout budgets and model scales.
AI.result: On HMMT and AIME reasoning benchmarks, CPT achieves a better accuracy–latency Pareto frontier than strong baseline TTS approaches, meaning it reaches higher accuracy for the same or lower inference cost, or equivalently, requires less compute for similar accuracy levels. These gains hold across varying numbers of rollouts and different model sizes.
AI.conclusion: Search-time collaboration among parallel branches is a powerful and efficient enhancement to test-time scaling. By sharing and reusing intermediate discoveries across branches without additional training, CPT reduces redundant search and yields more favorable accuracy–latency trade-offs, suggesting collaborative search as a promising design principle for future TTS methods.

title: Tracing Computation Density in LLMs
summary: Transformer-based large language models (LLMs) are comprised of billions of parameters arranged in deep and wide computational graphs, but it is not clear that they exploit their full capacity for all inputs. We introduce the s-Trace method to efficiently estimate the subgraph of size s that best approximates a full model output. With this method, we find the computation in a variety of LLMs to be organized in two distinct phases. A small subgraph mostly composed of early-layer nodes can reconstruct the head of the full model output distribution. Adding further nodes, mostly located in later layers and increasingly consisting of attention heads, leads to incremental refinements in approximating the full output distribution. We find moreover that the amount of necessary computation per input correlates with model uncertainty, and that sparser subgraphs encode shallow statistics, such as unigram frequency. Overall, our results suggest a consistent modular organization in effective LLM computation, with a sparse early-layer core providing a rough prediction that is further refined through denser computations in later layers.

AI.tldr: They propose a method (s-Trace) to find the smallest subgraph of an LLM that approximates its output, revealing that LLM computation has a modular, two-phase structure: a sparse early-layer core gives a rough prediction, and later layers refine it.
AI.motivation: LLMs have billions of parameters and very deep, wide computation graphs, but it is unknown whether and how all this capacity is actually used for each input. The authors want to understand the internal organization and effective sparsity of LLM computation: which parts of the network matter for a given prediction, how much computation is truly necessary, and what different components are responsible for (e.g., rough guesses vs. refinements).
AI.method: They introduce s-Trace, an efficient procedure that, for a chosen size s, identifies a subgraph (subset of nodes/operations) whose computation best approximates the full model’s output distribution. Using this method across different LLMs, they analyze which layers and components (e.g., attention heads) are selected at different values of s, and how well these subgraphs reproduce the full output distribution and its uncertainty.
AI.result: Using s-Trace, they find that LLM computation decomposes into two phases. First, a small, mostly early-layer subgraph can already reconstruct the head (top-probability region) of the output distribution. Second, adding more nodes—primarily from later layers and increasingly attention heads—gradually improves the match to the full distribution. They also show that more uncertain model outputs require larger effective subgraphs, and that very sparse subgraphs mainly capture shallow statistics such as token frequency.
AI.conclusion: LLMs exhibit a consistent, modular computational structure: a sparse, early-layer core computes a rough, shallow prediction, and later, denser computations—especially in attention heads—iteratively refine it. This suggests that, although LLMs are massively overparameterized in principle, their effective computation for each input is structured and can often be well-approximated by relatively small, interpretable subgraphs whose size depends on the model’s uncertainty.

title: ExTax: Explainable Disinformation Detection via Persuasion, Emotion, and Narrative Role Taxonomies
summary: The democratization of LLMs has accelerated the generation and circulation of highly fluent disinformation, making traditional syntax-semantic verification increasingly insufficient. Such deception rarely relies solely on surface-level falsity; instead, it often combines persuasive rhetoric, emotional manipulation, and narrative role construction to influence readers' interpretations through multiple cognitive pathways. However, existing detectors typically emphasize isolated signals -- such as syntax, external knowledge, persuasion, or affective cues -- and therefore struggle to capture the multi-faceted manipulative intents underlying disinformation or provide human-auditable explanations. To address this gap, we present \textbf{ExTax}, a taxonomy-aligned framework for explainable disinformation detection. ExTax unifies persuasive rhetoric, emotional manipulation, and narrative roles into a 17-dimensional taxonomic space, covering 6 persuasive-rhetoric strategies, 5 emotional-manipulation methods, and 6 narrative-role categories. It elicits attributes from multiple frontier LLMs, reconciles their disagreements through Entropy-driven Dynamic Label Smoothing, and fuses the resulting taxonomic representations with contextual encodings via Heterogeneous Multi-Head Attention, grounding each prediction in an interpretable manipulation profile. Across five cross-domain and cross-genre benchmarks, ExTax achieves an overall Macro $F_1$ of $0.8456$, outperforming state-of-the-art deep learning and LLM-based baselines. It also remains robust under severe genre imbalance, where the strongest deep baseline degrades from $0.9454$ to $0.6194$.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ExTax, a taxonomy-aligned, explainable framework for detecting disinformation by modeling persuasive rhetoric, emotional manipulation, and narrative roles in a unified feature space, achieving strong and robust performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing disinformation detectors mainly focus on isolated signals such as syntax, factuality, persuasion markers, or affective cues, and thus fail to capture the complex, multi-dimensional manipulative intent behind modern, LLM-generated disinformation. Moreover, they provide limited human-auditable explanations for why content is considered manipulative. There is a need for a detection framework that can jointly model different manipulation strategies and yield interpretable, taxonomy-based explanations.
AI.method: The authors introduce ExTax, a 17-dimensional taxonomy that integrates 6 persuasive-rhetoric strategies, 5 emotional-manipulation methods, and 6 narrative-role categories. They prompt multiple frontier LLMs to elicit these manipulation attributes from text, then resolve inconsistencies between LLM outputs using Entropy-driven Dynamic Label Smoothing. The resulting taxonomic feature vectors are combined with contextual encodings (likely from neural language models) via a Heterogeneous Multi-Head Attention mechanism, producing predictions that are explicitly grounded in a manipulation profile over the taxonomy.
AI.result: On five cross-domain and cross-genre benchmarks, ExTax obtains an overall Macro F1 score of 0.8456, surpassing state-of-the-art deep learning and LLM-based baselines. It shows particular robustness under severe genre imbalance conditions, where competitive deep learning baselines suffer large performance drops (e.g., from 0.9454 to 0.6194), whereas ExTax maintains strong performance.
AI.conclusion: By unifying rhetoric, emotion, and narrative-role signals into a coherent taxonomy and coupling them with an explainable prediction pipeline, ExTax more effectively detects and characterizes disinformation than prior approaches. Its taxonomy-aligned profiles offer interpretable evidence of manipulation strategies and maintain robust performance across domains and genres, suggesting a promising direction for explainable, LLM-aware disinformation detection systems.

title: BhashaSetu: A Data-Centric Approach to Low-Resource Machine Translation
summary: We present BhashaSetu, a linguistically enriched English--Marathi parallel dataset addressing persistent data limitations in low-resource neural machine translation (NMT). Marathi, spoken by over 95 million people, remains underrepresented in high-quality parallel corpora across diverse domains. Our dataset comprises 2.78 million sentence pairs from heterogeneous sources including news, politics, healthcare, literature, and culture, with stemmed and lemmatized representations to support morphology-aware analysis. We benchmark multiple state-of-the-art translation models using BLEU, spBLEU, chrF++, and TER metrics, and conduct parameter-efficient fine-tuning of NLLB-200-distilled-600M using LoRA. A key finding from our ablation: corpus-level deduplication is the single largest preprocessing contributor to downstream quality (removing it reduces performance by 1.17 BLEU and 2.21 chrF++), demonstrating that disciplined cross-source corpus hygiene is a low-cost, high-impact intervention for low-resource, morphologically rich languages. The dataset is publicly released to promote reproducible and linguistically informed low-resource NMT research.

AI.tldr: Introduces BhashaSetu, a large, linguistically enriched English–Marathi parallel corpus and shows that corpus-level deduplication greatly improves NMT quality.
AI.motivation: Low-resource neural machine translation for Marathi suffers from a lack of large, high-quality, domain-diverse, and linguistically annotated parallel data, limiting progress and reproducibility for this widely spoken but under-resourced language.
AI.method: The authors construct a 2.78M sentence-pair English–Marathi parallel corpus from multiple domains (news, healthcare, literature, culture, etc.), add stemmed and lemmatized forms for morphology-aware work, benchmark several state-of-the-art NMT models with BLEU, spBLEU, chrF++, and TER, and perform parameter-efficient LoRA fine-tuning of NLLB-200-distilled-600M, including an ablation on preprocessing choices such as corpus-level deduplication.
AI.result: They obtain competitive translation performance on English–Marathi, and the ablation shows that removing corpus-level deduplication causes a notable drop (−1.17 BLEU, −2.21 chrF++), identifying deduplication as the most impactful single preprocessing step for improving downstream NMT quality.
AI.conclusion: BhashaSetu is a publicly released, linguistically enriched English–Marathi corpus that supports morphology-aware and reproducible low-resource NMT research, and rigorous cross-source deduplication is highlighted as a simple but high-impact practice for morphologically rich, low-resource languages.

title: FalAR: A Large-scale Speaker-Annotated European Portuguese Speech Corpus of Parliamentary Sessions
summary: State-of-the-art performance for Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) largely depends on the availability of large-scale labeled corpora. This creates a demand for increased data collection efforts, particularly for under-represented languages and dialectal varieties. Due to having considerably fewer speakers (around 11 million), European Portuguese (EP) is overshadowed by Brazilian Portuguese (BP) (around 200 million speakers) in currently available large-scale speech data resources, resulting in under-performing speech-based systems for EP users. To address this gap, and following similar data collection efforts for other languages, we present FalAR, a large-scale, speaker-annotated speech corpus of European Portuguese parliamentary sessions. Spanning approximately 20 years, FalAR comprises 5,800 hours of speech data. In addition, 4,850 hours have speaker identity annotations, for a total of 1,180 speakers with associated metadata including age, gender, political affiliation, and parliamentary role. The corpus was built using a state-of-the-art EP CAMÕES ASR model for transcription-reference alignment. In this paper, we describe the data collection process, together with the main characteristics of the FalAR corpus. Furthermore, we evaluate the trade-off between data quantity and alignment accuracy on ASR performance, with our experiments demonstrating that incorporating FalAR as pre-training data yields up to 14% relative WER improvement over baseline models.

AI.tldr: They build and release FalAR, a 5,800-hour European Portuguese parliamentary speech corpus with rich speaker metadata, and show it significantly improves ASR performance when used for pre-training.
AI.motivation: ASR systems need large labeled datasets, but European Portuguese is under-resourced compared to Brazilian Portuguese, leading to worse ASR quality for EP users. There is a need for a large-scale, publicly available EP speech corpus with speaker information to close this performance gap.
AI.method: They collected and processed about 20 years of European Portuguese parliamentary session recordings, performing segmentation, alignment, and transcription using a state-of-the-art EP CAMÕES ASR model. They annotated speaker identities and associated metadata (age, gender, political affiliation, parliamentary role) for most of the data. They then conducted ASR experiments to assess how adding this corpus for pre-training, and varying alignment quality vs. quantity, affects word error rate.
AI.result: FalAR contains 5,800 hours of EP speech, with 4,850 hours labeled with speaker identities for 1,180 speakers. Using FalAR as additional pre-training data for ASR gives up to 14% relative WER improvement compared with baselines that do not use it. They also characterize corpus properties and examine the trade-off between using more but noisier aligned data vs. less but cleaner data.
AI.conclusion: A large, speaker-annotated EP parliamentary speech corpus can be constructed semi-automatically using existing ASR models and parliamentary archives. The resulting FalAR dataset significantly improves EP ASR when used for pre-training, helping close the resource gap for this language. The work suggests that similar approaches can be used for other under-resourced language varieties, and that balancing data size and alignment quality is important for maximizing ASR gains.

title: Large Language Model-Powered Query-Driven Event Timeline Summarization in Industrial Search
summary: Understanding how events evolve over time is essential for search engines handling queries about trending news. We present QDET (Query-Driven Event Timeline Summarization), a production system deployed on Baidu Search that constructs focused event timelines to explain specific query events. Unlike traditional topic-centric approaches that aim for comprehensive coverage, QDET identifies and organizes sub-events closely relevant to the query from noisy candidate sets formed by millions of documents retrieved daily. QDET incorporates two key innovations: (1) multi-task supervised fine-tuning with three auxiliary tasks-temporal ordering, causal judgment, and timeline completion-that enable compact models to match the performance of much larger general-purpose models in specialized domains; (2) reinforcement learning-based event concise summarization that enforces strict length constraints while maintaining semantic quality, achieving 88.2% length compliance and outperforming 671B-scale models by 7.7 points in constraint satisfaction. Our fine-tuned 7B parameter model achieves 76.2% F1 score on timeline summarization, slightly surpassing the zero-shot performance of DeepSeek-R1-671B (76.1% F1) while using only 1% of its parameters-demonstrating that domain-specific optimization enables production-ready models with comparable quality at drastically reduced computational costs. Online A/B tests on Baidu Search validate real-world effectiveness, showing 5.5% CTR improvement, 4.6% longer dwell time, and 4.4% deeper exploration compared to single-task baselines. We further demonstrate that timeline understanding transfers to heat prediction, confirming effective knowledge transfer to downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: QDET is a production system at Baidu that builds query-focused event timelines for news search using a compact 7B model fine-tuned with auxiliary tasks and RL for concise summaries, matching or surpassing much larger models while improving user engagement metrics.
AI.motivation: Search engines need to explain how news events evolve over time in response to user queries, but existing topic-centric or general-purpose models struggle to extract and organize only the most query-relevant sub-events from massive, noisy web corpora under strict length constraints and production efficiency requirements.
AI.method: They design QDET, which (1) retrieves massive daily document collections, forms noisy candidate sub-events, and uses a 7B LLM fine-tuned via multi-task supervision on four tasks: main timeline summarization plus three auxiliaries—temporal ordering, causal judgment, and timeline completion—to improve event structure understanding; and (2) applies reinforcement learning-based summarization that optimizes for strict length constraints while preserving semantic quality, letting the model generate concise event descriptions that respect production limits.
AI.result: The fine-tuned 7B model reaches 76.2% F1 on timeline summarization, slightly exceeding the zero-shot performance of a 671B model (76.1% F1) while using only ~1% of the parameters, and its RL summarizer satisfies length constraints 88.2% of the time, outperforming the 671B model by 7.7 points on this metric; in online A/B tests on Baidu Search, QDET yields 5.5% higher click-through rate, 4.6% longer dwell time, and 4.4% more page exploration compared with single-task baselines, and the learned timeline understanding successfully transfers to a downstream heat prediction task.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific, multi-task fine-tuning combined with RL for length control enables a relatively small 7B model to deliver production-quality, query-focused event timelines that rival or surpass much larger general models, improving both offline summarization metrics and online user engagement while also providing transferable event-structure knowledge for related tasks like trend/heat prediction.

title: QUACK: Questioning, Understanding, and Auditing Communicated Knowledge in Multimodal Social Deduction Agents
summary: Social deduction games have become a popular testbed for probing reasoning, deception, coordination, and belief modeling in Large Language Model (LLM) agents. However, most environments are scored only by game outcomes such as win rates and largely remain to text-only interaction, making it difficult to tell whether an agent's language is actually grounded in what it perceived and did, or to identify the failure modes underlying its behavior. To address this gap, we introduce QUACK, an open-source environment and evaluation framework for auditing the grounding of agent language in multimodal social reasoning. QUACK evaluates agents at three levels: game outcomes, behavioral trajectories, and utterance-level consistency. Its core Statement Verification Pipeline reconstructs each agent's ground-truth trajectory from engine logs and checks every discussion claim against it, automatically flagging spatial hallucination, unsupported accusation, deception collapse, and language-action inconsistency. Evaluating three frontier VLMs in both homogeneous and cross-model adversarial settings, we find that even the strongest agent hallucinates 15.1% of its verifiable spatial claims and makes over half of its accusations without grounded evidence. We release the full engine, evaluation framework, toolkit, and logs at https://github.com/AAAAA-Academia-Attractions/QUACK.

AI.tldr: QUACK is an open-source multimodal social deduction game framework to audit how well LLM/VLM agents’ language is grounded in what they perceive and do, going beyond win-rate metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing social deduction testbeds for LLM agents mostly rely on coarse outcome metrics (e.g., win rates) and text-only interaction. This makes it unclear whether agents’ statements are actually grounded in the underlying game state and their own actions, and it obscures specific failure modes such as hallucinated descriptions or ungrounded accusations. The authors want a principled way to inspect, quantify, and compare how faithfully agents’ language reflects their perceptions, beliefs, and behaviors in a richer, multimodal environment.
AI.method: They build QUACK, an open-source environment and evaluation framework for multimodal social deduction games. QUACK evaluates agents on three levels: (1) game outcomes; (2) detailed behavioral trajectories; and (3) utterance-level consistency with ground truth. A central Statement Verification Pipeline reconstructs each agent’s true action and perception trajectory from environment logs, then checks every discussion statement against this trajectory. It automatically detects and labels several categories of mismatch and failure: spatial hallucinations, accusations not supported by evidence, breakdowns in deliberate deception, and inconsistencies between language and actual actions.
AI.result: In experiments with three state-of-the-art Vision-Language Models (VLMs), evaluated under both homogeneous (same model for all players) and cross-model adversarial settings, they find substantial grounding failures. Even the strongest agent hallucinates 15.1% of spatially verifiable claims and issues more than half of its accusations without sufficient grounding in evidence. These quantitative results illustrate systematic problems in how current agents connect language to game state and behavior.
AI.conclusion: QUACK provides a fine-grained, multimodal benchmark and toolkit for auditing the grounding of agent language in social reasoning tasks. It reveals that strong modern VLM agents still frequently hallucinate about spatial aspects of the environment and make many ungrounded accusations, despite sometimes achieving reasonable game outcomes. The framework, code, tools, and logs are fully released to facilitate further research on grounded language, deception, and multi-agent reasoning in complex environments.

title: E3: Issue-Level Backtesting for Automated Research Critique
summary: We present E3, an automated review assistant that augments reviewers and engineering teams by identifying decision-relevant technical concerns in research papers. For each concern, E3 reports its nature, its location, its bearing on the contribution, and the analysis or evidence that would resolve it, covering unsupported claims, missing ablations, weak baselines, hidden assumptions, threats to validity, and leakage risks. To evaluate E3 without contamination confounds we adopt an issue-level backtesting protocol: the corpus is restricted to papers postdating the training cutoff of every automated source, and for each paper a meta-judge that observes only anonymised reviews labels every issue-source pair as Caught, Partial, or Missed. Applied to 100 ICLR 2026 papers and 4598 judged issue rows, comparing E3 against the ICLR human reviews and two prompt-matched LLM baselines built on gpt-5.4 from OpenAI and claude-opus-4-6 from Anthropic, with meta-judge gpt-5.5, E3 attains the highest recall on every aggregate metric. Partial-inclusive recall reaches 90.2 percent, which is 15.5 points over GPT, 17.1 points over Claude, and 29.2 points over the human reviews, and strict recall preserves the ordering at 65.8 percent. On concerns raised by the human reviewers, E3 recovers 89.6 percent; on concerns the human reviewers missed it surfaces 1635 additional rows admitted into the judged union, 406 above the next-best source. Corpus, baseline prompts, judge prompt template, and evaluation code are released.

AI.tldr: E3 is an automated review assistant that detects decision-relevant technical concerns in research papers and outperforms both humans and strong LLM baselines in recall on these issues.
AI.motivation: Peer review often misses important technical issues such as unsupported claims, missing experiments, and hidden assumptions, especially as submission volume grows. Existing automated tools and generic LLM assistance are not systematically evaluated at the level of individual issues, and may be affected by data contamination when assessed on past papers. The paper aims to build an assistant specifically optimized for surfacing decision-relevant concerns and to rigorously measure how well it complements or exceeds human reviewers and generic LLMs.
AI.method: The authors design E3, a system that scans research papers and identifies specific technical concerns. For each concern it outputs structured information: what the concern is, where it appears in the paper, how it affects the main contribution, and what evidence or analysis would resolve it. Categories include unsupported claims, missing ablations, weak baselines, hidden assumptions, threats to validity, and leakage risks. For evaluation, they propose an issue-level backtesting protocol that avoids training-data contamination by using only papers that postdate the training cutoff of all automated systems. A meta-judge LLM, given only anonymized human and system reviews, labels each (issue, source) pair as Caught, Partial, or Missed. They apply this to 100 ICLR 2026 papers, creating 4598 judged issue rows, and compare E3 with ICLR human reviews and two prompt-matched LLM baselines built on recent OpenAI and Anthropic models, with a stronger LLM as the meta-judge.
AI.result: Across 100 ICLR 2026 papers and 4598 labeled issue rows, E3 achieves the best recall on all aggregate metrics versus human reviewers and two strong LLM baselines. Partial-inclusive recall is 90.2%, beating GPT by 15.5 percentage points, Claude by 17.1 points, and human reviews by 29.2 points. Under stricter criteria, E3 still leads with 65.8% recall. On issues that humans did raise, E3 recovers 89.6% of them. Additionally, on issues missed by humans, E3 surfaces 1635 extra concerns that are accepted into the meta-judge’s union set, 406 more than the next-best source.
AI.conclusion: E3 substantially improves the coverage of decision-relevant technical concerns in research papers, both capturing most issues that humans identify and adding many they miss, while outperforming strong general-purpose LLM baselines. The proposed issue-level backtesting protocol offers a contamination-resistant way to evaluate automated reviewing tools. By releasing the corpus and evaluation artifacts, the work supports more rigorous future development of AI-assisted peer review systems.

title: On the Hidden Costs of Counterfactual Knowledge Training in LLM Unlearning
summary: Counterfactual tuning (CFT) has emerged as a promising paradigm for Large Language Model (LLM) unlearning by training models to generate alternative fictitious knowledge in place of undesired content. However, in this work, we find that this paradigm still underperforms other paradigms in some aspects, and identify two previously overlooked pitfalls underlying this gap: (1) knowledge conflict, where mutual inconsistencies within counterfactual corpora induce conflicting gradients that disrupt parameter optimization, and (2) hallucination spillover, where fitting false targets instills a persistent fabrication bias, inflating hallucination rates on unrelated domains. To systematically diagnose these issues, we introduce RWKU+, an extended benchmark equipped with novel trade-off metrics and gradient-level diagnostic tools. Our work further discusses the limitations and overhead of the paradigm, aiming to provide insights and actionable guidance for more rigorous LLM unlearning research.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques counterfactual tuning (CFT) for LLM unlearning, revealing key pitfalls and offering a new benchmark, RWKU+, with diagnostics to better understand and improve unlearning methods.
AI.motivation: As LLMs become widely deployed, the ability to 'unlearn' specific information (e.g., incorrect, private, or harmful data) is crucial. Counterfactual tuning has been proposed as a clean way to replace undesired knowledge with fictitious alternatives, but its actual behavior and trade-offs are not fully understood, and it can lag behind other unlearning methods. The authors are motivated to understand why CFT underperforms and to systematically expose and measure its hidden issues.
AI.method: The authors empirically dissect the behavior of counterfactual tuning by formulating and analyzing two major failure modes: knowledge conflict (gradient conflicts from mutually inconsistent counterfactual data) and hallucination spillover (a global bias toward fabrication). They then build RWKU+, an extended benchmark that incorporates new trade-off metrics to jointly measure unlearning effectiveness, knowledge retention, and hallucination, along with gradient-level diagnostic tools to study training dynamics and conflicts.
AI.result: The study shows that CFT, despite its conceptual appeal, suffers from performance gaps relative to other unlearning paradigms due to the identified pitfalls. Knowledge conflict is shown to hinder effective parameter updates, while hallucination spillover increases hallucination in domains beyond the unlearned content. RWKU+ successfully surfaces these issues, enabling fine-grained evaluation and revealing concrete limitations and overheads of CFT-based unlearning.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual tuning alone is not a silver bullet for LLM unlearning; it can introduce subtle but serious problems, including conflicting learning signals and a global tendency to hallucinate. By providing RWKU+ with new metrics and diagnostic tools, the paper encourages more careful, rigorous evaluation of unlearning methods and suggests that future work should explicitly account for and mitigate these pitfalls when designing unlearning strategies.

title: LLMs Are Already Good Tutors: Training-Free Prompt Optimization for Pedagogical Math Tutoring
summary: Aligning LLMs for math tutoring typically requires RL-based training with multi-GPU infrastructure. We investigate whether training-free prompt optimization-evolving only the system prompt via API calls-can serve as a practical alternative. We adapt 7 published methods and propose 5 education-specialized methods, evaluating these 12 methods under 5 conditions on 2 OOD benchmark suites. All 12 best-per-method configurations surpass the strongest RL-trained baseline (R_total = 0.633), and our ParetoGrad achieves the best Pareto balance across post-test solve rate, leak control, and helpfulness, rather than dominating any single component. Behavioral analysis with an 82-code educational codebook reveals that training-free methods rely on teaching-knowledge patterns at 2-3x the rate of RL-trained models, with a compensating ~10 percentage-point reduction in intent-level scaffolding. We also find a task-dependent reasoning mode effect consistent across training-free and RL-based paradigms. Our approach enables efficient development of pedagogically aligned LLM tutors with prompts alone and minimal compute.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that carefully optimized system prompts, without any additional training, can match or beat RL-trained math-tutor LLMs on multiple benchmarks while using far less compute.
AI.motivation: Aligning LLMs as math tutors typically relies on reinforcement learning with large compute budgets, which limits practicality and rapid iteration. The authors want to know if a cheaper, training-free approach—optimizing only the system prompt through API calls—can achieve comparable or better educational alignment and performance, including correctness, leak control, and helpfulness.
AI.method: They adapt 7 existing prompt-optimization methods and introduce 5 new, education-focused ones, for 12 methods total. These prompts are evolved or tuned purely via API calls (no weight updates) and evaluated in 5 experimental conditions on 2 out-of-distribution benchmark suites for math tutoring. They compare against a strong RL-trained baseline and use a detailed 82-code educational behavior codebook to analyze tutoring behaviors such as teaching-knowledge use and scaffolding, along with a Pareto-style analysis (ParetoGrad) balancing multiple objectives: solve rate, leak control, and helpfulness.
AI.result: All 12 methods, when using their best configuration, exceed the performance of a strong RL-trained baseline (R_total = 0.633). The proposed ParetoGrad method provides the best trade-off across post-test solve rate, leak control, and helpfulness, though it does not maximize any single metric individually. Behavioral analysis shows training-free prompt-optimized tutors use teaching-knowledge patterns 2–3 times more than RL-trained models but show about a 10-percentage-point decrease in intent-level scaffolding. They also observe a consistent, task-dependent reasoning mode effect across both training-free and RL-based models.
AI.conclusion: Training-free prompt optimization that modifies only the system prompt can produce math-tutor LLMs that match or outperform RL-trained models on key educational metrics, with far lower computational cost. These methods shift tutoring style toward heavier use of explicit teaching knowledge and somewhat less intent-level scaffolding. The results suggest that effective, pedagogically aligned LLM tutors can be developed efficiently using prompt engineering alone, making large-scale RL training optional rather than necessary for many educational deployments.

title: MiRD: Reliable Set-Valued Prediction for Open-Ended Question Answering via Miscoverage Risk Decomposition
summary: Reliable set-valued prediction provides a principled way to mitigate hallucinations in open-ended question answering (QA), yet existing conformal approaches typically rely on a fragile premise: finite sampling must already produce at least one admissible candidate, or calibration examples violating this condition are discarded. In this paper, we introduce MiRD, a two-stage framework that decomposes overall miscoverage into sampling failure and conditional selection failure. In Stage I, MiRD establishes an expectation-level marginal upper bound on the probability that finite sampling produces no admissible answer under a fixed budget. In Stage II, conditioned on sampling success, MiRD calibrates a conformal selection threshold using admission-correlated nonconformity scores defined over the full calibration set, thereby preserving calibration-set integrity. Across three open-ended QA datasets and eight models, MiRD controls sampling risk, conditional selection risk, and overall miscoverage, while yielding tighter first-stage bounds than PAC-style alternatives and more adaptive prediction sets than successful-only calibration.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MiRD, a two-stage framework for reliable set-valued prediction in open-ended QA that explicitly separates and controls sampling failure and selection failure, improving calibration and adaptivity over existing conformal approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing conformal prediction methods for open-ended QA assume that finite sampling will always produce at least one admissible answer for calibration examples. When this fails, such examples are often discarded, compromising reliability and calibration-set integrity. There is a need for a framework that can explicitly account for both the risk that sampling fails to produce any correct answer and the risk that the selection procedure fails given that sampling did succeed.
AI.method: MiRD decomposes overall miscoverage into two components: (1) sampling failure, where a limited number of samples contains no admissible answer, and (2) conditional selection failure, where the conformal selection mechanism fails despite successful sampling. Stage I derives an expectation-level marginal upper bound on the probability that finite sampling yields no admissible answer under a specified sampling budget, yielding a bound on sampling risk. Stage II, conditioned on cases where at least one admissible answer is sampled, performs conformal calibration using admission-correlated nonconformity scores on the full calibration set, without discarding any calibration examples. This yields a calibrated selection threshold that controls conditional selection risk. Together, the two stages yield control over overall miscoverage while maintaining calibration-set integrity.
AI.result: Empirically, on three open-ended QA datasets and eight language models, MiRD is shown to effectively control sampling risk, conditional selection risk, and the resulting overall miscoverage. The first-stage bounds on sampling failure probability are tighter than those obtained from PAC-style methods, and the resulting prediction sets are more adaptive—i.e., smaller or more informative—than those produced by approaches that calibrate only on successful sampling instances.
AI.conclusion: MiRD offers a principled, two-stage approach to reliable set-valued prediction in open-ended QA that overcomes a key weakness of prior conformal methods: the need to discard calibration examples where sampling fails. By separately bounding sampling failure and selection failure and calibrating on the full dataset, MiRD achieves stronger theoretical guarantees and better empirical performance, yielding tighter bounds and more adaptive prediction sets than existing alternatives.

title: LitSeg: Narrative-Aware Document Segmentation for Literary RAG
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances Large Language Models (LLMs) by incorporating external knowledge, particularly for long-tail domains such as literary works. However, the critical step of document segmentation in RAG remains largely underexplored. Existing strategies are typically semantically blind and overlook the complicated narrative structures of literary works, often resulting in fragmented plots and unclear references that severely hinder retrieval and generation performance. To address this, we propose LitSeg, a novel narrative-theory-guided segmentation framework. By employing multi-stage prompting, LitSeg explicitly extracts valid events, untangles narrative threads, clarifies narrative structures, and locates turning points to inform segmentation. To alleviate the computational overhead of multi-stage inference with large-scale models, we further introduce LitSeg-Lite, a lightweight single-pass chunker fine-tuned on LitSeg-generated data via a two-stage training strategy, distilling the complex process into a single inference pass. Extensive experiments demonstrate that with structurally independent text chunks, our methods significantly improve retrieval accuracy and context relevance over baselines, ultimately enhancing downstream QA performance, while ablation studies validate the efficacy of narratological guidance and data distillation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LitSeg, a narrative-theory-guided segmentation framework for RAG on literary texts, and a distilled lightweight version LitSeg-Lite, both improving retrieval and QA performance by creating structurally independent text chunks.
AI.motivation: RAG systems struggle with long-tail domains like literary works because existing document segmentation methods ignore narrative structure, leading to fragmented plots and unclear references that hurt retrieval and generation quality. There is a need for segmentation that respects narrative structure while remaining computationally feasible.
AI.method: The authors design LitSeg, which uses multi-stage prompting with LLMs guided by narrative theory to: extract events, disentangle narrative threads, clarify narrative structure, and locate turning points, then use these signals to segment texts. To reduce the cost of multi-stage inference, they create LitSeg-Lite, a lightweight single-pass chunker fine-tuned on data produced by LitSeg using a two-stage training strategy, effectively distilling the multi-step reasoning into one pass.
AI.result: Experiments show that both LitSeg and LitSeg-Lite generate structurally independent chunks that substantially improve retrieval accuracy and context relevance compared to baseline segmentation methods. These segmentation improvements translate into better downstream question answering performance. Ablation studies confirm that narratological guidance and the data distillation process are key contributors to the gains.
AI.conclusion: Narrative-theory-guided segmentation is effective for RAG on literary works. LitSeg and its distilled variant LitSeg-Lite yield better-structured chunks that improve retrieval and QA, and the study highlights the importance of incorporating narratological signals and knowledge distillation into segmentation frameworks for complex narrative texts.

title: Formalization of Malagasy conjugation
summary: This paper reports the core linguistic work performed to construct a dictionary-based morphological analyser for Malagasy simple verbs. It uses the Unitex platform and comprised the contruction of an electronic dictionary for Malagasy simple verbs. The data is encoded on the basis of morphological features. The morphological variations of verb stems and their combination with inflectional affixes are formalized in finite-state transducers represented by editable graphs. 78 transducers allow Unitex to generate a dictionary of allomorphs of stems. 271 other transducers are used by the morphological analyser of Unitex to recognize the stem and the affixes in conjugated verbs. The design of the dictionary and transducers prioritizes readability, so that they can be extended and updated by linguists.

AI.tldr: The paper describes how the authors built a dictionary-based morphological analyzer for Malagasy simple verbs using Unitex and finite-state transducers.
AI.motivation: There was a need for a robust, extensible computational tool to analyze the morphology of Malagasy verbs, a language that is less resourced and whose verbal morphology is complex. Existing tools or resources were insufficient, so a new dictionary-based analyzer was required.
AI.method: The authors used the Unitex platform to build an electronic dictionary of Malagasy simple verbs, encoding entries with detailed morphological features. They modeled stem alternations and the combination of verb stems with inflectional affixes using finite-state transducers, represented as human-readable graphs. 78 transducers are dedicated to generating allomorphic stem forms, and 271 additional transducers are used in the analyzer to recognize stems and affixes in conjugated forms. The design emphasizes clarity and maintainability so linguists can easily extend and revise the resources.
AI.result: They produced a working dictionary-based morphological analyzer within Unitex that can generate allomorphs of Malagasy verb stems and automatically recognize stems and affixes in conjugated verbs. The analyzer relies on 78 transducers for generation and 271 for analysis, along with an electronic dictionary organized by morphological features.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that a readable, extensible finite-state, dictionary-based approach can effectively handle Malagasy simple verb morphology within the Unitex platform, and that the resulting resources (dictionary and transducers) can be maintained and expanded by linguists over time.

title: Grounding Text Embeddings in Stakeholder Associations
summary: Text embeddings are widely used to analyse large corpora of complex texts. However, it is unclear whether the embeddings capture the same semantic distances as the human experts using them. Ensuring alignment between embedding representations and human intentions is essential for valid analyses. We present the Stakeholder Grounding Exercise, a method for making expert associations explicit and grounding embedding model results in human understanding. In our primary case study on Danish policy issues, we find that neural text embeddings are substantially less reliable than human experts (19-26 pp gap), and that this misalignment propagates to downstream clustering performance (Spearman $ρ=0.9$ between exercise ranking and cluster quality). A secondary study on US Federal AI use cases replicates the gap (16pp) in English, using a digital protocol and a different community of experts -- demonstrating that the gap is not an artefact of a single instrument or domain. The Stakeholder Grounding Exercise offers a practical method for assessing whether embedding models capture the semantic distinctions that matter most to domain experts.

AI.tldr: They introduce a method (Stakeholder Grounding Exercise) to check if text embeddings reflect expert-defined semantic distances, finding sizable gaps between model and expert judgments in two case studies.
AI.motivation: Although text embeddings are widely used to analyze complex text corpora, it is uncertain whether they capture the same semantic relationships and distinctions that matter to the human experts who rely on them. This misalignment could undermine the validity of downstream analyses and decisions, especially in high‑stakes domains like policy and AI governance. A systematic way is needed to make expert associations explicit and to benchmark embedding models against these human intuitions.
AI.method: They propose the Stakeholder Grounding Exercise, in which domain experts explicitly express how similar or different they consider various texts or issues to be, creating a human-based semantic distance benchmark. They then compare these expert-derived distances to those inferred from neural text embeddings, and examine how discrepancies affect downstream tasks such as clustering. The method is applied first to Danish policy issues, then replicated in a separate study on US Federal AI use cases using a different protocol and expert group.
AI.result: In the Danish policy case study, neural text embeddings are found to be substantially less reliable than human experts, with a 19–26 percentage point gap in alignment. This misalignment strongly predicts lower clustering performance, evidenced by a high Spearman correlation (ρ=0.9) between the expert-based ranking from the exercise and cluster quality. In the US Federal AI use case study, a similar performance gap of about 16 percentage points is observed, even though the language (English), instrument (digital protocol), and expert community differ, suggesting the effect is robust.
AI.conclusion: The Stakeholder Grounding Exercise provides a practical framework for evaluating whether text embedding models capture the semantic distinctions that experts care about. The findings show that commonly used embeddings can be meaningfully misaligned with expert judgments, and that this misalignment has tangible negative effects on downstream analytical tasks such as clustering. Therefore, embedding-based analyses should be systematically grounded in stakeholder expertise rather than assumed to reflect relevant semantics by default.

title: MAIGO: Mitigating Lost-in-Conversation with History-Cleaned On-Policy Self-Distillation
summary: Large language models often solve tasks from a fully specified prompt but degrade when the same requirements unfold over multiple turns, known as the lost-in-conversation (LiC) gap. We trace part of this degradation to self-contamination: intermediate assistant replies enter later context and carry early deviations forward. Motivated by this mechanism, we propose MAIGO, an on-policy self-distillation method that reduces this contamination using history-cleaned references from the model's own policy. For middle turns, MAIGO removes prior assistant replies while preserving the user-visible sharded prefix; for answer turns, it distills from paired full-view references conditioned on the completed user-side dialogue. A reliability weight downweights middle-turn samples that disagree with the clean reference. MAIGO requires no verifier rewards, state labels, or inference-time scaffolding. Under the LiC paired-view protocol with deterministic verifiers, MAIGO improves Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct SHARDED accuracy from 52.8 to 66.1 and the SHARDED/FULL ratio from 66.5% to 84.1%, while keeping FULL accuracy within 2.3 points. These results show that self-contamination is a trainable component of the LiC gap.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and mitigates the “lost-in-conversation” gap in multi-turn dialogues by addressing self-contamination from the model’s own intermediate replies, proposing MAIGO, an on-policy self-distillation training method that cleans history and significantly improves multi-turn reliability without hurting single-turn performance.
AI.motivation: Large language models often perform well when given fully specified, single-shot prompts but their performance degrades when equivalent requirements are spread across multi-turn conversations. This lost-in-conversation (LiC) gap is problematic for realistic interactive use of LLMs. The authors hypothesize that a key contributor is self-contamination, where the model’s earlier imperfect replies get re-ingested as context and propagate errors. They want to understand and reduce this trainable source of degradation without relying on expensive external supervision or complex runtime scaffolding.
AI.method: The authors introduce MAIGO, an on-policy self-distillation technique that trains the model to be robust to its own prior conversational turns. During training under a LiC paired-view protocol, they construct two types of references from the model’s current policy: (1) for middle turns, they create history-cleaned references by removing prior assistant messages while preserving the user-visible “sharded” prefix, thus preventing the model from conditioning on its own potentially flawed outputs; (2) for final answer turns, they generate full-view references conditioned on the complete user-side dialogue. The model is trained to match these references via self-distillation, and a reliability weight is introduced to downweight middle-turn samples whose observed behavior diverges from the clean reference, indicating unreliability. The method is fully on-policy and does not require external verifiers, hand-labeled dialogue states, or special inference-time prompting or scaffolds.
AI.result: Applied to Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and evaluated under the LiC paired-view setup with deterministic verifiers, MAIGO substantially improves multi-turn robustness. Specifically, SHARDED accuracy (performance when only partial conversation context is available) increases from 52.8 to 66.1. The ratio of SHARDED to FULL performance—an indicator of the LiC gap—improves from 66.5% to 84.1%, showing the model’s multi-turn behavior is much closer to its ideal single-shot capability. Importantly, FULL accuracy decreases by at most 2.3 points, indicating that gains in conversational robustness come with minimal cost to standard single-turn performance.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that self-contamination from a model’s own intermediate responses is a significant and trainable factor in the lost-in-conversation gap. By cleaning conversational history during training and using on-policy self-distillation with reliability weighting, MAIGO improves the consistency and reliability of LLMs in multi-turn dialogues without requiring external rewards or complex runtime mechanisms. These results suggest that targeted training against self-contamination can substantially close the LiC gap while preserving strong single-turn performance.

title: Beyond Binary: Speech Representations Across the Cognitive Score Hierarchy
summary: This study examines the relationship between speech representations and the hierarchical structure of cognitive assessment in mild cognitive impairment. Utilizing 5,754 German neuropsychological assessment recordings, we evaluate six cognitive tasks across three score levels: task, domain, and global levels. We compare hand-crafted acoustic features with self-supervised learning (SSL) embeddings. Results show that although SSL representations generally outperform hand-crafted features at lower levels, this trend reverses for MCI classification. Furthermore, task-specific constraints influence performance: tasks with greater response freedom exhibit performance dilution as hierarchical levels increase, suggesting ``specialist'' representations, whereas the performance of highly structured tasks increases toward higher levels, suggesting ``generalist'' representations. These findings show links between task constraints and assessment hierarchy in automated clinical speech analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how different types of speech representations (hand-crafted vs. self-supervised) relate to the hierarchical structure of cognitive assessments in people with mild cognitive impairment (MCI).
AI.motivation: Automated analysis of clinical speech is promising for detecting and monitoring cognitive decline, but it is unclear how various speech representations align with the hierarchical structure of cognitive assessments (task, domain, global) and whether these representations behave consistently across tasks with different levels of constraint and freedom. Understanding this relationship can improve model design and interpretation in MCI assessment.
