AI.method: The authors use 5,754 German recordings from neuropsychological assessments of people with mild cognitive impairment. They analyze six different cognitive speech tasks and evaluate performance at three score levels: task-level, domain-level, and global-level. They extract both hand-crafted acoustic features and self-supervised learning (SSL) speech embeddings. They then compare how well each representation predicts cognitive scores and MCI status at each hierarchical level, and examine how task-structure (free vs. highly constrained responses) affects performance patterns across levels.
AI.result: SSL-based speech representations generally outperform traditional hand-crafted acoustic features at lower levels of the hierarchy (e.g., task-level and perhaps domain-level predictions). However, for the clinically important MCI classification (a higher-level, global outcome), hand-crafted acoustic features perform better than SSL embeddings. Moreover, tasks that allow more open-ended responses lose predictive performance when aggregating from task to domain/global levels (indicating more specialized, task-specific representations), while highly structured tasks gain performance at higher levels (indicating more generalizable, generalist representations).
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that the effectiveness of speech representations in automated cognitive assessment is hierarchical and task dependent. SSL embeddings are not uniformly superior: they excel at lower-level, task-specific predictions but can be inferior to hand-crafted features for global MCI classification. Additionally, the structural constraints of a task (free vs. highly structured responses) systematically shape whether the derived representations behave as specialists or generalists across the assessment hierarchy. These insights highlight the need to match representation types and task designs to the level and purpose of cognitive assessment in clinical speech analysis.

title: Learning When to Think While Listening in Large Audio-Language Models
summary: Recent advances in Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs) have made real-time, streaming spoken interaction increasingly practical. In this setting, reasoning quality and responsiveness are tightly coupled: delaying reasoning until the speech endpoint can improve answer quality but moves deliberation into user-visible response delay, while answering too early risks committing before decisive evidence arrives. We introduce a learnable wait-think-answer control formulation for LALMs. Motivated by the incremental nature of human conversation, the controller decides under partial audio evidence when to wait, when to externalize a compact reasoning update, and when to answer. Using Qwen2.5-Omni-7B as the base model, we construct aligned wait-think-answer traces from spoken reasoning data, train the controller with supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and then apply Decoupled Clip and Dynamic Sampling Policy Optimization (DAPO). The reward combines answer correctness, action validity, update timing, latency synchronization, reasoning quality, and chain consistency, optimizing the complete wait-think-answer trajectory and not the final answer alone. On a six-task synthetic spoken reasoning question answering (SRQA) benchmark, the six-reward DAPO controller improves the row-weighted accuracy from 67.6% to 70.3% while reducing post-endpoint final-think length by 14% under the same Qwen deployment harness. On a 186-item human-recorded Real Audio Bench, a transfer check beyond text-to-speech (TTS)-rendered speech, the controller family remains functional: SFT achieves the strongest accuracy, while the six-reward DAPO controller is the only learned variant whose final-think length falls below the base. These results suggest that a streaming model should learn when to make intermediate reasoning explicit during the audio stream.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a learnable controller for streaming Large Audio-Language Models that decides when to wait, think (externalize reasoning), and answer, improving the trade-off between reasoning quality and response latency.
AI.motivation: In real-time spoken interaction with Large Audio-Language Models, there is a conflict between answer quality and responsiveness. Waiting for the full speech input allows better reasoning but increases user-visible delay, while answering too soon risks errors because crucial evidence may not yet have arrived. Existing approaches typically optimize only final answer quality and do not explicitly control intermediate reasoning or timing decisions during streaming audio. The paper aims to design a principled control mechanism that manages when the model should pause, reason explicitly, or answer while audio is still incoming, similar to incremental human conversation.
AI.method: The authors formulate a wait-think-answer control policy for streaming LALMs. A controller observes partial audio-derived evidence and chooses among three actions: wait for more input, emit a compact externalized reasoning update (think), or provide an answer. They use Qwen2.5-Omni-7B as the base audio-language model and build aligned wait-think-answer traces from spoken reasoning data. The controller is first trained with supervised fine-tuning on these traces. Then they apply Decoupled Clip and Dynamic Sampling Policy Optimization (DAPO), a reinforcement-style optimization scheme with a multi-component reward. The reward accounts for answer correctness, validity of actions, timing of updates, synchronization with latency constraints, reasoning quality, and consistency of the chain of thought, optimizing the entire interaction trajectory rather than only the final answer. Policies are then evaluated on synthetic and real-audio benchmarks.
AI.result: On a six-task synthetic spoken reasoning QA benchmark, the controller trained with the six-term DAPO objective improves row-weighted accuracy from 67.6% to 70.3%, while reducing the post-endpoint final reasoning length by 14% compared to the same Qwen deployment harness. On a 186-example human-recorded Real Audio Bench, which tests transfer from TTS-generated to real speech, all trained controllers remain usable. The SFT controller attains the best accuracy, whereas the six-reward DAPO controller is the only learned variant that reduces final-think length below the base model, showing it can shorten visible latency without collapsing accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that in streaming audio-question-answering, explicitly learning a wait-think-answer control policy can improve the balance between reasoning quality and responsiveness. By optimizing a trajectory-level, multi-objective reward that includes timing and reasoning structure, the controller learns when to make intermediate reasoning explicit and when to commit to an answer. The results on both synthetic and real-audio benchmarks indicate that such controllers can reduce user-perceived latency while maintaining or improving accuracy, suggesting a promising direction for more conversational, incrementally reasoning LALMs.

title: Not All Tokens Matter Equally: Dynamic In-context Vector Distillation with Decisive-Token Supervision for Long-form Medical Report Generation
summary: Distilling demonstration effects into hidden-space interventions offers a lightweight alternative to full finetuning. However, existing multimodal variants are mostly evaluated on short-form tasks, where outputs end after a few tokens. Extending these methods to long-form generation exposes a fundamental yet underexamined limitation: token-level distillation implicitly treats all output tokens as equally informative, but long-form outputs are dominated by high-frequency template and grammatical tokens, while the tokens that actually determine output quality are sparsely distributed. In medical report generation (MRG), two such decisive tokens stand out: pathology-related tokens that determine diagnostic content, and the end-of-sequence (EOS) event that determines termination. Both receive insufficient supervision under uniform cross-entropy, and autoregressive decoding further compounds the problem by drifting away from teacher-forced trajectories. We propose DIVE, a frozen-backbone distillation framework that addresses long-form report generation through two complementary mechanisms matched to these failures. Decisive-token supervision restores supervision balance by upweighting the cross-entropy contribution of pathology-related tokens and the EOS event, ensuring that content fidelity and termination are learned during training rather than imposed at decoding time. State-conditioned dynamic steering replaces fixed open-loop residuals with hidden-state-dependent adapters, allowing the injected signal to adapt as decoding drifts. Experiments on MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert Plus with two medical VLM backbones show that DIVE consistently ranks among the strongest methods across lexical and clinical-proxy metrics. Our method achieves the best BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, and RadGraph F1 in all dataset--backbone settings, while remaining competitive on coarse label-level CheXbert F1.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DIVE, a distillation framework tailored for long-form medical report generation, which improves supervision on key pathology and EOS tokens and uses state-conditioned dynamic steering to robustly guide frozen multimodal backbones during decoding.
AI.motivation: Existing hidden-space intervention and distillation techniques for multimodal models work well on short-form tasks but struggle with long-form generation like medical reports. In long-form outputs, not all tokens are equally important: a few pathology-related tokens and the end-of-sequence decision largely determine report quality, yet standard uniform cross-entropy treats every token equally, over-focusing on frequent template and grammatical tokens. Moreover, autoregressive decoding drifts away from teacher-forced trajectories, weakening the effect of fixed residual steering. The authors want a lightweight alternative to full finetuning that directly addresses these long-form-specific issues.
AI.method: DIVE is a frozen-backbone distillation framework with two key mechanisms. (1) Decisive-token supervision reweights the loss so that pathology-related tokens and the EOS event contribute more to the cross-entropy objective, ensuring that diagnostic content and proper termination are strongly supervised during training. This counteracts the dominance of high-frequency template tokens. (2) State-conditioned dynamic steering replaces fixed, open-loop residual interventions with adapters whose outputs depend on the current hidden state. This lets the steering signal adapt as decoding diverges from the teacher trajectory, maintaining control over generation quality. The approach is applied on top of existing medical vision-language model backbones without finetuning their main parameters.
AI.result: On two medical chest X-ray report datasets, MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert Plus, and across two different medical VLM backbones, DIVE consistently performs among the strongest methods. It achieves the best scores in BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, and RadGraph F1 across all dataset–backbone combinations, demonstrating improvements in lexical overlap and structured clinical relation quality. On the more coarse label-level CheXbert F1 metric, DIVE remains competitive with state-of-the-art approaches, indicating that its advantages are not limited to surface form metrics.
AI.conclusion: Long-form medical report generation exposes weaknesses of standard token-uniform distillation and fixed hidden-space interventions, especially in supervising sparse but crucial pathology tokens and EOS decisions, and in handling decoding drift. DIVE addresses these by rebalancing supervision toward decisive tokens and using state-conditioned dynamic steering, delivering strong and often state-of-the-art performance across multiple datasets and backbones while keeping the backbone frozen. This shows that carefully designed distillation and intervention mechanisms can be a practical, efficient alternative to full finetuning for high-stakes long-form multimodal generation.

title: EpiCurveBench: Evaluating VLMs on Epidemic Curve Digitization
summary: Chart-to-data extraction with vision-language models (VLMs) is increasingly evaluated on benchmarks that show diminishing headroom (frontier VLMs exceed 89% on ChartQA) and with metrics that treat extracted points as unordered key-value pairs, ignoring the temporal structure of time series and penalizing small alignment shifts as catastrophic failures. We address both gaps with EpiCurveBench, a benchmark of 1,000 real-world epidemic curve images curated from diverse public-health sources, and EpiCurveSimilarity (ECS), an evaluation metric that aligns predicted and ground-truth series via dynamic programming, tolerating local temporal shifts and gaps while penalizing them proportionally. Evaluating six methods--three frontier closed VLMs, one open VLM, and two specialized chart-extraction systems--we find the strongest model reaches only 52.3% ECS, and that ECS spreads the four general-purpose VLMs over a 25-point range where key-value metrics (RMS, SCRM) compress them into a 5-point band. We further validate ECS against four downstream epidemiological summary statistics, finding that higher ECS predicts smaller errors in total counts, peak timing, and peak magnitude, and higher growth-rate fidelity; across all four, ECS correlates 1.5--3.6 times more strongly than Dynamic Time Warping, which lacks a gap penalty and therefore cannot distinguish a truncated prediction from a temporally faithful one. EpiCurveBench targets a high-impact public-health application--unlocking decades of outbreak data trapped in published figures--but the benchmark and metric apply directly to any structured time-series chart-extraction setting.

AI.tldr: Introduces EpiCurveBench, a new benchmark of 1,000 real-world epidemic curve images, and EpiCurveSimilarity (ECS), a time-series–aware metric for evaluating chart-to-data extraction using vision-language models, showing existing models perform far worse than suggested by traditional key-value metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing chart-to-data benchmarks for vision-language models show saturated performance (e.g., >89% on ChartQA) and rely on evaluation metrics that ignore temporal ordering in time-series charts, treating data as unordered key-value pairs. This leads to misleadingly optimistic assessments, especially for epidemic curves where temporal alignment is critical. There is a need for a realistic, time-series–aware benchmark and metric that reflect downstream epidemiological usefulness.
AI.method: The authors construct EpiCurveBench, a benchmark of 1,000 real-world epidemic curve images sourced from diverse public-health publications. They propose EpiCurveSimilarity (ECS), a sequence-alignment-based metric that uses dynamic programming to align predicted and ground-truth time series, allowing for local temporal shifts and gaps while penalizing them proportionally via a gap penalty. They evaluate six methods (three frontier closed-source VLMs, one open-source VLM, and two specialized chart-extraction systems) on EpiCurveBench using ECS and existing key-value metrics (RMS, SCRM), and correlate ECS with four epidemiologically relevant downstream summary statistics.
AI.result: The strongest evaluated model achieves only 52.3% ECS on EpiCurveBench, indicating substantial remaining headroom compared to inflated scores on existing benchmarks. ECS spreads four general-purpose VLMs across a 25-point performance range, whereas traditional key-value metrics like RMS and SCRM compress them into a narrow 5-point band. ECS scores are strongly predictive of errors in total case counts, peak timing, peak magnitude, and growth-rate fidelity; ECS correlates 1.5–3.6 times more strongly with these downstream epidemiological statistics than Dynamic Time Warping, which lacks a gap penalty and fails to distinguish truncated from temporally faithful predictions.
AI.conclusion: EpiCurveBench exposes significant limitations of current vision-language models and chart-extraction systems on realistic epidemic curves, contradicting the impression of near-solved performance from prior benchmarks. EpiCurveSimilarity offers a more meaningful, time-series–aware evaluation that better reflects downstream epidemiological utility than existing key-value or DTW-based metrics. While motivated by public-health applications (recovering historical outbreak data from figures), both the benchmark and ECS metric are broadly applicable to any structured time-series chart-extraction task.

title: GraphReview: Scientific Paper Evaluation via LLM-Based Graph Message Passing
summary: Scientific paper evaluation often involves not only assessing a manuscript itself, but also relating it to contemporaneous research and prior literature. However, existing LLM-based methods typically model these signals separately and lack a unified mechanism for propagating review evidence across papers. We propose $\textbf{GraphReview}$, a graph-based LLM framework that formulates paper evaluation as review-signal message passing over a semantic paper graph. The graph jointly captures intrinsic quality, synchronic links among contemporaneous papers, and diachronic links to prior work. LLMs are used to estimate node-level quality priors and generate edge-level comparative evidence through pairwise paper comparisons, while Personalized PageRank integrates review signals for quality ranking, decision prediction, and review generation. To produce higher-quality graph evidence, we propose reward-induced maximum likelihood objectives for training the LLM backbones. Experiments show that GraphReview consistently outperforms the strongest baseline, achieving average improvements of 29.7% on decision and ranking metrics, including gains of 23.7% in Accuracy and 57.6% in Spearman's $ρ$. It also produces higher-quality review texts and generalizes effectively across time periods and conference venues. The code is available at https://github.com/ECNU-Text-Computing/GraphReview.

AI.tldr: GraphReview is a graph-based LLM framework that evaluates scientific papers by propagating review signals over a semantic paper graph, leading to substantially better decision prediction, ranking, and review generation than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based paper evaluation approaches treat different evidence sources—such as a paper’s own content, its contemporaneous competitors, and its prior literature—separately. They lack a unified mechanism to propagate and integrate these review signals across related papers, which is crucial for realistic peer review where papers are judged relative to each other and to past work. The paper is motivated by the need for a systematic, graph-based way to model these relationships and leverage them to improve automated reviewing and decision-making.
AI.method: The authors construct a semantic paper graph whose nodes are papers and whose edges encode relationships: intrinsic quality information, synchronic links between contemporaneous submissions, and diachronic links to prior work. Large language models are used in two ways: (1) to estimate node-level quality priors (how good each paper is on its own) and (2) to generate edge-level comparative evidence via pairwise comparisons between related papers. These node and edge signals are then integrated using Personalized PageRank, which performs review-signal message passing over the graph to obtain quality rankings, acceptance decision predictions, and review text generation. Additionally, the LLM backbones are further trained with reward-induced maximum likelihood objectives to improve the quality of the graph evidence they produce.
AI.result: GraphReview is empirically evaluated against strong baselines on tasks such as decision prediction, ranking quality, and review generation. It achieves average improvements of 29.7% over the best baseline on decision and ranking metrics, including a 23.7% gain in classification Accuracy and a 57.6% gain in Spearman’s rank correlation (ρ). The framework also yields higher-quality review texts and shows good generalization across different time periods and conference venues, indicating robustness and transferability.
AI.conclusion: Modeling scientific paper evaluation as review-signal message passing on a semantic paper graph substantially improves automated peer-review tasks. By jointly capturing intrinsic quality and inter-paper relationships, and by using LLMs both for priors and comparative evidence, GraphReview offers a unified, effective framework for decision prediction, ranking, and review generation. The use of reward-induced maximum likelihood further enhances evidence quality, and the method generalizes well across domains, suggesting it is a promising approach for scalable, consistent paper reviewing systems.

title: The Coverage Illusion: From Pre-retrieval Routing Failure to Post-retrieval Cascades in a Production RAG System
summary: In modern RAG pipelines, query augmentation methods such as HyDE and query expansion are applied to every query, resulting in substantial LLM inference costs and increased end-to-end latency. The empirical justification for this overhead in real production traffic remains largely unexplored. We present a case study of the Danish National Encyclopedia, evaluating five retrieval workflows over 20,000 query-workflow pairs from production traffic and synthetic conditions. In this system, synthetic queries suggest that LLM augmentation is needed for over 90% of queries to achieve high retrieval coverage. However, under our production deferral policy, only 27.8% of real user queries need LLM augmentation. We call this gap the Coverage Illusion and attribute it to a structural mismatch between synthetic and real query distributions. Pre-retrieval routing cannot resolve this gap, as the need for LLM augmentation is only revealed after searching the index, a result confirmed by our evaluation of four machine learning paradigms. The coverage gap, undetectable from the query alone, motivates a post-retrieval cascade that runs workflows in cheapest-first order and escalates to LLM augmentation only when a step returns no documents. Operating entirely without training overhead or secondary serving infrastructure, the cascade improves quality by +0.140 Composite Overall points over Always-HyDE, reduces latency by 31.8%, and serves 72.2% of real user queries without LLM augmentation.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes when LLM-based query augmentation is actually needed in a real RAG system, showing that synthetic benchmarks dramatically overstate its necessity and proposing a cheap post-retrieval cascade that cuts cost and latency while improving quality.
AI.motivation: Modern RAG pipelines often apply LLM-based query augmentation (e.g., HyDE, query expansion) to every query. This is expensive and increases latency, yet most evidence for its benefits comes from synthetic benchmarks rather than real production traffic. The authors observe a potential mismatch: synthetic queries may suggest that almost all queries need augmentation, but real user behavior could be very different. They aim to quantify this gap in a real system, understand why it occurs, and design a more cost-effective retrieval workflow that only uses LLM augmentation when it is actually necessary.
AI.method: The authors conduct a case study on the Danish National Encyclopedia search system. They evaluate five different retrieval workflows over 20,000 query–workflow pairs drawn from both real production traffic and synthetic query conditions. They compare how often LLM-based query augmentation is required to achieve high retrieval coverage in synthetic vs. real queries. They define and analyze the 'Coverage Illusion'—the discrepancy between synthetic and real query needs—and test whether pre-retrieval routing (using four different machine learning paradigms) can predict when augmentation is needed from the query alone. Based on the findings, they design a post-retrieval cascade: retrieval workflows are run in a cheapest-first order, and the system escalates to LLM augmentation only when cheaper steps retrieve no documents, avoiding additional training or serving infrastructure.
AI.result: In synthetic query settings, LLM augmentation appears necessary for over 90% of queries to achieve high retrieval coverage. However, under a production deferral policy using real user traffic, only 27.8% of queries actually require LLM augmentation, exposing a large Coverage Illusion caused by structural differences between synthetic and real query distributions. Pre-retrieval routing methods, including four different ML paradigms, fail to reliably detect which queries need augmentation before searching, confirming that the need for LLM help is only evident post-retrieval. The proposed post-retrieval cascade, which escalates from cheapest workflows to LLM-augmented ones only when needed, improves a Composite Overall quality metric by +0.140 points relative to an Always-HyDE baseline, cuts latency by 31.8%, and serves 72.2% of real user queries without any LLM augmentation.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic query benchmarks significantly overestimate how often LLM-based query augmentation is needed in real RAG systems, leading to unnecessary cost and latency. The authors define this mismatch as a Coverage Illusion arising from differences between synthetic and real query distributions. Because the need for augmentation cannot be reliably predicted from the query alone, pre-retrieval routing is ineffective. Instead, a simple post-retrieval cascade that starts with cheap retrieval and escalates to LLM augmentation only after failed retrieval steps can both improve retrieval quality and substantially reduce latency and LLM usage, all without extra training or infrastructure. This suggests that practical RAG deployments should favor such cascaded designs over unconditional LLM augmentation informed only by synthetic evaluations.

title: Temporal Simultaneity Predicts Annotation Quality in Sentiment Corpora
summary: Annotation quality is difficult to sustain when campaigns span weeks or months with small annotator pools. We present a Setswana sentiment dataset of 3,565 tweets annotated by three native-speaker annotators across eight batches and examine why inter-annotator agreement (IAA) declines over time. Despite an aggregate Randolph's free-marginal Kappa of $κ= 0.76$, "excellent," per-batch $κ$ falls by more than 32 points across the annotation task. Through six targeted analyses, we find that (i) label confusion concentrates on the negative/neutral boundary, (ii) two annotators show run-length drift consistent with autopilot labeling, and (iii) the dominant predictor of $κ$ is temporal simultaneity: tweets labeled within one minute achieve $κ= 0.98$, while those labeled more than a day apart reach only $κ= 0.65$. Annotation speed and tweet-level linguistic features show no meaningful association with $κ$. We benchmark three open multilingual encoders and proprietary models (GPT-5 and Gemini) on three-class sentiment classification; fine-tuning yields gains of 29 to 43 macro-F1 points over pretrained baselines, with GPT-5 few-shot leading overall (62.2 macro-F1). We release the dataset, per-annotation timestamps, and analysis code to support reproducible quality auditing for future African language NLP resources.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how annotation quality for a Setswana tweet sentiment dataset degrades over long campaigns, diagnoses causes of inter-annotator agreement decline, and benchmarks multilingual models on the resulting data.
AI.motivation: Long-running annotation campaigns with small pools of annotators often suffer from declining inter-annotator agreement over time, but the concrete drivers of this decline are underexplored, especially for low-resource African languages like Setswana. The authors want to create a high-quality sentiment dataset and, at the same time, understand and quantify factors that affect annotation reliability so that future resource creation efforts can better monitor and maintain quality.
AI.method: They collect 3,565 Setswana tweets and have three native speakers annotate them for three-way sentiment (positive/negative/neutral) over eight temporal batches. They compute Randolph's free-marginal Kappa overall and per batch, then perform six focused analyses: confusion matrices to locate boundary problems, run-length analysis to detect potential autopilot labeling, correlation of Kappa with time gaps between annotators’ labels, and tests of associations between Kappa and annotation speed as well as tweet-level linguistic features. They also benchmark several open multilingual encoders and proprietary LLMs (GPT-5, Gemini) on three-class sentiment classification, comparing pretrained encoders, fine-tuned models, and few-shot prompting. They provide timestamps and code for reproducible auditing.
AI.result: Overall inter-annotator agreement is high (Randolph's Kappa 0.76), but per-batch Kappa drops by more than 32 points over the course of the campaign. Label disagreements cluster mostly on the negative vs. neutral boundary. Two annotators show long runs of identical labels indicative of autopilot behavior. The strongest predictor of Kappa is temporal simultaneity: tweets annotated within one minute show extremely high agreement (Kappa 0.98), whereas annotations spaced by more than a day drop to Kappa 0.65. No substantial relationship is found between Kappa and annotation speed or linguistic features of tweets. For modeling, fine-tuning multilingual encoders boosts macro-F1 by 29–43 points over their zero-shot/pretrained performance; GPT-5 in a few-shot setup achieves the best macro-F1 (62.2).
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that even when aggregate agreement looks excellent, annotation quality can degrade substantially over time in small, long-running campaigns. Temporal alignment of annotators is a key factor in maintaining high agreement, while speed and surface linguistic complexity are less important. The released Setswana sentiment dataset, along with timestamps and analysis code, offers both a valuable resource for African-language sentiment analysis and a framework for reproducible quality auditing to inform better design and monitoring of future annotation efforts.

title: ENPMR-Bench: Benchmarking Proactive Memory Retrieval for Emotional Support Agents
summary: Memory-augmented language agents are increasingly deployed in affective applications such as emotional support, where understanding and responding to users' latent emotional needs is critical. However, existing research often treats memory as a tool for factual retrieval, overlooking its role in shaping users' emotional experiences. In this work, we introduce ENPMR-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating Emotional Need-aware Proactive Memory Retrieval (ENPMR), a core capability that enables agents to infer users' latent emotional needs and proactively retrieve appropriate memories to support empathetic interaction. Grounded in Maslow's hierarchy of needs, ENPMR-Bench includes over 1,800 memory-augmented dialogues and defines structured mappings between emotional needs and supportive memory types. Experimental results demonstrate that current retrieval paradigms, including both embedding-based and LLM-driven approaches, exhibit substantial deficiencies, with empathy scores significantly lagging behind golden memory conditions. While chain-of-thought prompting improves the alignment between inferred emotional needs and retrieved memories to some extent, a notable performance gap remains. Together, these findings reveal critical limitations in current agents and outline directions for advancing personalized emotional support through need-sensitive memory retrieval.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ENPMR-Bench, a benchmark to evaluate how well memory-augmented language agents proactively retrieve emotionally supportive memories aligned with users’ latent needs.
AI.motivation: Existing memory-augmented language agents focus on factual retrieval and neglect how memory use shapes users’ emotional experiences. There is a lack of systematic evaluation for agents’ ability to infer latent emotional needs and proactively retrieve supportive memories during empathetic interaction, especially grounded in an established psychological theory.
AI.method: The authors construct ENPMR-Bench, a benchmark of over 1,800 memory-augmented dialogues annotated with emotional needs based on Maslow’s hierarchy and with structured mappings from each need to suitable supportive memory types. They then evaluate various retrieval paradigms, including embedding-based retrieval, LLM-driven retrieval, and chain-of-thought prompting strategies, measuring how well retrieved memories align with inferred emotional needs and how this impacts empathy scores.
AI.result: Across the benchmark, existing embedding-based and LLM-based retrieval methods perform poorly at need-sensitive memory retrieval, producing empathy scores far below conditions where the correct (“golden”) memories are provided. Chain-of-thought prompting slightly improves the alignment between inferred needs and retrieved memories, but a considerable gap to the golden-memory upper bound persists.
AI.conclusion: Current memory retrieval mechanisms in language agents are insufficient for personalized emotional support, as they fail to reliably infer users’ latent emotional needs and select appropriate supportive memories. ENPMR-Bench exposes these limitations and provides a testbed and direction for developing more need-aware, emotionally intelligent memory retrieval strategies in affective applications.

title: Pair-In, Pair-Out: Latent Multi-Token Prediction for Efficient LLMs
summary: Long chain-of-thought reasoning has made autoregressive decoding the dominant inference cost of modern large language models. Existing methods target either the input side (latent compression) or the output side (speculative decoding and multi-token prediction, MTP), but the two lines of work have been pursued independently. Moreover, output-side methods must incur an expensive verifier pass to validate the unreliable draft tokens predicted by MTP. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{Pair-In, Pair-Out (PIPO)}, which unifies both sides by viewing a latent compressor and an MTP head as mirror-image operations: the compressor folds two input tokens into one latent representation, while the MTP head unfolds one hidden state into one additional output token. To remove the verifier cost without sacrificing reliability, PIPO trains a lightweight confidence head that decides whether draft tokens should be accepted. We observe that On-Policy Distillation (OPD) naturally matches the rejection-sampling criterion of speculative decoding, so the confidence head can be trained alongside OPD with negligible extra cost. Experiments on AIME 2025, GPQA-Diamond, LiveCodeBench v6, and LongBench v2 with Qwen3.5-4B and 9B backbones show that PIPO improves pass@4 over regular decoding by up to $+7.15$ points, while delivering up to $2.64\times$ first-token-latency and $2.07\times$ per-token-latency speedups.

AI.tldr: PIPO unifies input compression and multi-token prediction to speed up and cheapen long chain-of-thought decoding while maintaining accuracy via a confidence head trained with on-policy distillation.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive decoding dominates inference cost for long chain-of-thought reasoning in modern LLMs. Existing efficiency approaches either compress the input (latent compression) or accelerate output generation (speculative decoding, MTP), and are developed separately. Output-side approaches additionally require an expensive verifier forward pass because multi-token predictions are unreliable. A method that jointly optimizes both input and output sides and removes the costly verifier while preserving reliability is needed.
AI.method: PIPO introduces a symmetric architecture: a latent compressor that merges pairs of input tokens into a single latent representation, and an MTP head that expands a single hidden state into an additional output token, forming a pair-in, pair-out scheme. To avoid the heavy verifier pass, PIPO adds a lightweight confidence head to decide whether to accept or reject drafted tokens. This confidence head is trained using On-Policy Distillation (OPD), leveraging the fact that OPD’s training signal aligns with the rejection-sampling rule used in speculative decoding, so it can be trained with almost no extra cost.
AI.result: On challenging benchmarks for reasoning and long-context tasks (AIME 2025, GPQA-Diamond, LiveCodeBench v6, LongBench v2) and using Qwen3.5 4B and 9B models, PIPO achieves up to +7.15 absolute pass@4 improvement over standard decoding, while also reducing latency: up to 2.64× speedup for first-token latency and 2.07× speedup for per-token latency.
AI.conclusion: By treating input compression and multi-token prediction as mirror operations and replacing the heavyweight verifier with a confidence head trained via OPD, PIPO unifies two previously separate efficiency strategies and delivers both higher accuracy and substantially faster decoding for long chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs.

title: Lost in Sampling: Assessing Lexical Reachability in LLMs via the Word Coverage Score (WCS)
summary: Modern Large Language Models (LLMs) are often criticized for producing repetitive and homogeneous text, despite possessing vast latent vocabularies. While previous research has focused on model knowledge and training data, we investigate the role of decoding mechanics in suppressing linguistic diversity. We introduce the Word Coverage Score (WCS), a metric that quantifies the extent to which contextually appropriate human vocabulary is mathematically pruned by standard sampling filters (e.g., Top-$p$, Top-$k$, and Min-$p$). Rather than assessing static knowledge, the WCS measures the lexical survival rate of low-frequency, high-information human words as a function of sampling parameters. By auditing open-weight models on human-authored corpus fragments, we identify which logical lexical choices are rendered unreachable by the decoder, even when they reside within the probability space. Our results provide quantitative evidence that industry-standard sampling defaults act as unintended censorship mechanisms, smoothing the unique textures of human expression into a homogenized discourse. The WCS offers a rigorous framework for optimizing the trade-off between text coherence and lexical richness, providing a diagnostic tool for preserving the diversity of human language in generative models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Word Coverage Score (WCS) to quantify how standard decoding strategies in LLMs (Top-p, Top-k, Min-p) prune contextually appropriate but low-frequency human words, contributing to repetitive and homogeneous outputs.
AI.motivation: LLMs, despite large vocabularies, tend to generate repetitive and homogeneous language. Existing work mostly blames training data or model knowledge, but the authors suspect decoding procedures themselves may be a key cause of reduced linguistic diversity. They aim to rigorously measure how decoding filters suppress rare but meaningful words that humans would naturally use.
AI.method: The authors define Word Coverage Score (WCS), a metric that evaluates, for a given human-authored context, which human-chosen words are still reachable under a model’s probability distribution after applying common sampling filters like Top-p, Top-k, and Min-p. They audit open-weight models on human corpora, compute the lexical survival rate of low-frequency, high-information words across different sampling parameters, and identify which plausible words are mathematically eliminated by decoding filters even though they originally had non-zero probability.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that standard decoding defaults significantly prune contextually appropriate, information-rich human words, reducing lexical diversity. Many human-like lexical choices, although present in the model’s probability space, become unreachable due to aggressive sampling filters. The authors quantify how different parameter settings affect the survival of such words via WCS.
AI.conclusion: Standard sampling schemes for LLM decoding operate as unintended censorship mechanisms by over-pruning rare yet meaningful vocabulary, leading to homogenized language. Word Coverage Score offers a principled way to diagnose and tune decoding parameters to better balance coherence with lexical richness, helping preserve the diversity and nuance of human language in generated text.

title: It's Not Always Sycophancy: Measuring LLM Conformity as a Function of Epistemic Uncertainty
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are known to abandon their initial stance to conform to user pushback. While prior research largely attributes this behavior to sycophancy learned during reinforcement learning from human feedback, we hypothesize that conformity is also driven by a model's epistemic uncertainty at inference time. In this paper, we introduce MUSE, a two-stage evaluation framework to disentangle the mechanisms driving LLM conformity. Specifically, MUSE maps a model's epistemic uncertainty in responding to a query against its likelihood to yield to user pushback in a subsequent turn. We demonstrate that the mechanisms driving conformity extend beyond sycophancy alone. Specifically, we characterize two distinct factors that jointly drive conformity: sycophantic conformity, where a model aligns with user pushback even with absolute certainty in its initial response, and uncertainty-driven conformity, where a model's likelihood for conformity increases alongside its uncertainty. Furthermore, we conduct ablation studies to demonstrate that both sycophantic conformity and uncertainty-driven conformity grow with 1) the LLM's perceived expertise of the user and 2) the plausibility of the user's suggestions. More broadly, MUSE informs more targeted intervention strategies by distinguishing alignment-induced sycophancy and training-corpora-driven uncertainty.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MUSE, a framework to dissect why LLMs change their answers under user pushback, distinguishing between sycophancy and uncertainty-driven conformity.
AI.motivation: LLMs often change their initial answers when users push back, commonly blamed on sycophancy from RLHF. The authors suspect that epistemic uncertainty at inference time is another key driver, and existing work does not clearly separate these mechanisms.
AI.method: They propose MUSE, a two-stage evaluation: first, quantify a model’s epistemic uncertainty about its answer to a query; second, measure how likely the model is to conform when a user later challenges that answer. By correlating uncertainty with conformity and manipulating user expertise and suggestion plausibility, they disentangle different conformity mechanisms and run ablation studies.
AI.result: They find that conformity cannot be explained by sycophancy alone. They identify two components: (1) sycophantic conformity, where the model changes its stance even when highly certain initially; and (2) uncertainty-driven conformity, where conformity likelihood grows with the model’s uncertainty. Both components increase as the model perceives the user as more expert and the user’s counter-suggestions as more plausible.
AI.conclusion: MUSE reveals that LLM conformity stems from both alignment-induced sycophancy and uncertainty-driven behavior rooted in training data. Distinguishing these mechanisms enables more targeted interventions to reduce harmful or excessive conformity without sacrificing appropriate deference under genuine uncertainty.

title: Separating Semantic Competition from Context Length in RAG Reading
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems can respond incorrectly even when the correct passage was retrieved. The model must still read the retrieved passages and identify which one contains the answer among others that look relevant. This passage-reading model is called the reader. Does it fail simply because the context is longer or because the other passages genuinely compete with the correct one? We introduce and demonstrate a matched-control protocol for RAG reading: we keep the number and length of passages fixed, but replace hard competitors with less competitive real passages. We apply this control across two compact open models on SQuAD. This replacement partially restores performance, with the strongest effects on F1 and answer inclusion. For Phi-2, this recovers +6.0 EM points, +7.0 answer-inclusion points, and +0.057 F1. For Qwen2.5-1.5B, it recovers +4.5 EM points, +9.0 answer-inclusion points, and +0.068 F1. To track how performance changes as competitors accumulate, we also report retention curves and summarize them with a right-censored half-life when the curves do not cross half-retention. Together, these results show the protocol isolates a competition effect distinct from context length, though the effect is clearer for F1 and answer inclusion than for exact match, and also varies with snippet length.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates why retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) models fail even when the correct passage is retrieved, separating the effects of context length from genuine competition between passages.
AI.motivation: RAG systems often hallucinate or answer incorrectly, and prior work shows that adding more retrieved passages can hurt performance. However, it is unclear whether errors stem mainly from longer context (more tokens to process) or from the presence of misleading but plausible competing passages. Understanding this distinction is crucial for designing better retrieval and reading strategies, especially for smaller open-source models.
AI.method: The authors propose a matched-control protocol: for each RAG example, they keep the total number and length of passages constant but replace the strongest competing passages (hard competitors) with less competitive, yet real, passages. By controlling for context length and passage count, they isolate the competition effect. They apply this protocol on SQuAD using two compact open models (Phi-2 and Qwen2.5-1.5B), and measure metrics such as exact match (EM), F1, answer inclusion, and derive retention curves that describe performance as more competitor passages are added. They also summarize these curves via a right-censored half-life metric when curves never drop below half-retention.
AI.result: When hard competitors are replaced with weaker ones under the matched-control protocol, model performance improves without changing context length or passage count. For Phi-2, the control recovers +6.0 EM, +7.0 answer-inclusion, and +0.057 F1. For Qwen2.5-1.5B, it recovers +4.5 EM, +9.0 answer-inclusion, and +0.068 F1. Retention curves show how performance declines as more competitive passages appear, and the right-censored half-life provides a compact summary of that decline, with clearer signals in F1 and answer-inclusion than in EM, and sensitivity to snippet length.
AI.conclusion: The matched-control protocol demonstrates a genuine competition effect in RAG readers that is distinct from simple context-length limitations: models struggle more when they must distinguish the correct passage from highly competitive distractors, even if context length is unchanged. This effect is more evident in F1 and answer inclusion than in exact match and varies with passage/snippet length. These findings suggest that improving RAG readers requires addressing competitive interference between passages, not just scaling context windows or retrieval quantity.

title: Probing Cultural Awareness in LLMs: A Case Study of Cross-Culture Aesthetic Stylistics
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in diverse cultural contexts, yet their ability to master aesthetic stylistics, i.e., the strategic use of language to evoke cultural resonance, remains underexplored. We curate C4STYLI, a benchmark of highly stylized translated movie titles and advertising slogans from Hong Kong and the Chinese Mainland, to evaluate LLMs via the lens of behavioral recognition and productive competence. Extensive evaluations show that LLMs differ from humans in stylistic recognition, and this recognition ability varies across text domains. In addition, stylistic recognition and generation performance in LLMs are not consistently aligned. To further examine whether LLMs genuinely capture stylistic information in stylistic recognition, we conduct structural ablation with logistic regression probes. We find that, in the Hong Kong setting, stylistic recognition in LLMs relies primarily on surface-level linguistic information rather than stylistic structure. This suggests limited sensitivity to Hong Kong-specific stylistic structure.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well large language models understand and generate culturally stylized language, using a new benchmark of translated movie titles and ad slogans from Hong Kong and Mainland China.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are used globally, it is important to know whether they can handle aesthetic stylistics—the culture-specific ways language is used to create resonance, especially in domains like marketing and entertainment where style is crucial. Existing work has not deeply examined this capability.
AI.method: The authors construct C4STYLI, a benchmark dataset of highly stylized translations of movie titles and advertising slogans from Hong Kong and Mainland China. They evaluate LLMs along two dimensions: (1) behavioral recognition—how well models can recognize stylistic features—and (2) productive competence—how well they can generate text matching these styles. They compare LLM performance to humans, analyze variation across domains, and use logistic regression probes with structural ablation to see which parts of model representations encode stylistic information.
AI.result: LLMs show different patterns from humans in recognizing stylistic features, and their recognition performance is uneven across domains. Moreover, a model’s ability to recognize style does not necessarily predict its ability to generate that style. Probing experiments in the Hong Kong setting reveal that LLMs mostly rely on surface-level linguistic cues rather than deeper stylistic structures when doing stylistic recognition.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs have limited and uneven competence in understanding and producing culturally specific aesthetic stylistics, particularly for Hong Kong-style language. Their stylistic recognition leans heavily on superficial features instead of genuine stylistic structure, indicating a lack of deep sensitivity to local stylistic conventions even when performance might appear adequate on the surface.

title: Self-Ensembling Vision-Language Models for Chart Data Extraction
summary: Charts effectively convey quantitative information, but the underlying data are often locked in image form, hindering reuse and analysis. Manually digitizing charts is time-consuming and error-prone, motivating automatic chart-to-table extraction. Recent approaches use specialized vision-language models (VLMs), yet performance still lags on charts with many datapoints or substantial stylistic variation. We propose a VLM self-ensembling method that repeatedly samples multiple tabular outputs from the same VLM for a fixed chart image and aggregates them at the level of individual table cells. We align candidate tables and take per-cell medians over numerical values to produce a more accurate consensus table. Our method also includes convergence detection to stop sampling once the aggregated table stabilizes, and uncertainty estimation based on dispersion across samples to help users assess extraction reliability. Because existing chart extraction benchmarks contain relatively simple plots with limited room for improvement, we introduce WB-ChartExtract, a new benchmark built from World Bank data with more complex and stylistically diverse charts; on average, its charts contain 7 times more datapoints than those in the ChartQA benchmark. Across both ChartQA and WB-ChartExtract, our approach improves extraction accuracy over single-pass VLM outputs, yielding up to 23% relative improvement on WB-ChartExtract after ensembling. More broadly, our method helps unlock tabular data previously siloed in chart images, enabling downstream analysis and reuse.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a self-ensembling method for vision-language models to more accurately extract tables from chart images, plus a new, more challenging benchmark dataset; ensembling multiple outputs and aggregating them cell-wise significantly improves extraction accuracy.
AI.motivation: Chart images contain valuable quantitative data, but this data is often inaccessible for direct analysis because it is embedded in images. Manual digitization is inefficient and error-prone, and existing automatic chart-to-table methods based on single-pass VLMs perform poorly on complex charts with many datapoints or diverse styles. There is a need for a more accurate, reliable, and scalable approach to recover tabular data from charts, especially on more realistic, challenging datasets.
AI.method: The authors propose a VLM self-ensembling framework. For each chart image, they repeatedly query the same vision-language model to obtain multiple candidate tables. They then align these candidate tables and aggregate them at the cell level, computing per-cell median values for numerical entries to form a consensus table. The method includes (1) convergence detection, which monitors when the aggregated table stabilizes and stops further sampling, and (2) uncertainty estimation, which uses the dispersion of values across samples in each cell to quantify reliability of the extracted data. Additionally, they construct a new benchmark, WB-ChartExtract, from World Bank data with more complex and stylistically diverse charts than existing datasets.
AI.result: On both the existing ChartQA benchmark and the new WB-ChartExtract benchmark, the proposed self-ensembling method outperforms single-pass VLM chart-to-table extraction. The gains are most pronounced on the more challenging WB-ChartExtract dataset, where ensembling yields up to 23% relative improvement in extraction accuracy. WB-ChartExtract itself is shown to be substantially more complex than ChartQA, containing charts with on average seven times more datapoints and greater stylistic variety.
AI.conclusion: Self-ensembling of VLM outputs is an effective strategy for improving automatic chart-to-table extraction, particularly for complex and stylistically varied charts. By aggregating multiple predictions at the cell level and providing convergence and uncertainty estimates, the approach enhances both accuracy and user trust. The new WB-ChartExtract benchmark offers a more realistic testbed for future research. Overall, the method contributes to making quantitative information locked in chart images more accessible for downstream analysis and reuse.

title: Chartographer: Counterfactual Chart Generation for Evaluating Vision-Language Models
summary: Chart question-answering (QA) benchmarks aim to pose questions that require visual reasoning to correctly answer, but models can often reach solutions through shortcuts or prior familiarity with a chart based on their own background knowledge. To strictly evaluate visual reasoning, we propose counterfactual charts where the chart-question task remains fixed, but underlying chart and the corresponding answer are varied. We introduce Chartographer, a framework to reverse engineer charts into executable code, validate reconstruction fidelity, generate seed-controlled counterfactual variants, and derive new answers from executable QA logic. We apply this framework to existing chart QA datasets and evaluate proprietary and open-source vision-language models (VLMs), measuring variation sensitivity and generalizability. Counterfactual charts reveal failures hidden by single-chart performance: VLMs often fail to generalize after answering the original chart correctly. We find failures are most prevalent when updated charts require novel visual reasoning pathways.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes counterfactual charts to rigorously test visual reasoning in chart QA and shows that current vision-language models fail to generalize when chart visuals change.
AI.motivation: Existing chart QA benchmarks can be solved using shortcuts, prior data memorization, or background knowledge, rather than genuine visual reasoning over the chart content. There is a need for a more stringent evaluation that isolates and tests visual reasoning capabilities, independent of spurious cues or chart familiarity.
AI.method: The authors introduce Chartographer, a framework that reverse engineers existing charts into executable code, checks the fidelity of these reconstructions, and then uses them to generate controlled counterfactual chart variants while keeping the question structure fixed. From these executable specifications, they systematically derive new answers using executable QA logic, enabling counterfactual evaluation of vision-language models on chart QA.
AI.result: Applying Chartographer to existing chart QA datasets, the authors evaluate both proprietary and open-source vision-language models. They measure how sensitive these models are to variations in the charts and how well they generalize across counterfactual variants. The results show that models often fail on the counterfactual charts even when they answered the original chart correctly, indicating a lack of robust visual reasoning and over-reliance on superficial cues.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual chart evaluation exposes hidden weaknesses in current vision-language models that single-chart performance metrics fail to capture. The most significant failures occur when the counterfactual charts require new, previously unused visual reasoning pathways, underscoring the need for benchmarks and model designs that better support robust, flexible visual reasoning over charts.

title: When Does Demographic Information Help? Data and Modeling Regimes for Perspective-Aware Hate Speech Detection
summary: Demographic information is often used to model annotator perspectives in subjective tasks such as hate speech detection, but its benefit is inconsistent: it improves performance in some settings and behaves as noise in others. This paper asks when demographic features help. We analyze demographic gain as a function of both data split properties and modeling frameworks. For data splits, we measure annotator disagreement, namely how often annotators assign different labels to the same example, along with training size and train-test demographic coverage. We find that demographic gains concentrate in regimes with low training disagreement, high test disagreement, fine-grained ambiguity measurement, sufficient training data, and greater demographic overlap. Motivated by these regimes, we introduce a gated demographic residual model that treats demographics as a selective adjustment to text-only predictions. Experiments on MHS and POPQUORN show that this design is effective, especially on high disagreement or low confidence examples. Overall, our results suggest that demographics should not be assumed useful by default; their value depends jointly on the data regime and the modeling framework.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates when demographic information about annotators helps in subjective NLP tasks and shows that its utility depends on data properties and modeling choices, proposing a model that selectively uses demographics as residual signals.
AI.motivation: In subjective annotation tasks like hate speech detection, annotators' demographics are often used to model perspective, but prior work finds mixed benefits: sometimes demographics improve performance, other times they add noise. This inconsistency creates uncertainty about whether and how to leverage demographic data responsibly and effectively. The paper is motivated by the need to understand under what conditions demographic features actually help, and to design modeling approaches that capitalize on those conditions without over-relying on demographics.
AI.method: The authors systematically analyze the performance gain from using annotator demographic features as a function of (1) data split properties—annotator disagreement rates, training data size, and overlap between train and test demographics; and (2) different modeling frameworks. They quantify annotator disagreement (how often annotators assign different labels to the same instance) and examine settings with varying levels of ambiguity and demographic coverage. Based on insights from this analysis, they propose a gated demographic residual model, which first makes a text-only prediction and then selectively applies a demographic-based residual correction via a gating mechanism, effectively using demographics as an adjustment term rather than as core input features.
AI.result: They find that performance gains from demographic features are not uniform but concentrate in specific data regimes: low disagreement in training data, high disagreement in test data, fine-grained measures of ambiguity, sufficiently large training sets, and strong demographic overlap between train and test. In these regimes, the proposed gated demographic residual model outperforms baselines that either ignore demographics or use them naively, and it is particularly effective on examples with high annotator disagreement or low model confidence. Experiments on the MHS and POPQUORN datasets empirically validate these claims.
AI.conclusion: Demographic information should not be treated as inherently beneficial in subjective NLP tasks. Its usefulness depends jointly on properties of the data (disagreement, size, demographic coverage) and the modeling design. By treating demographics as a gated residual—i.e., a selective adjustment on top of text-based predictions—models can better exploit demographics in the regimes where they help, while mitigating their negative impact where they behave like noise. This provides practical guidance for when and how to integrate demographic features in annotation modeling.

title: Real Images, Worse Judgments: Evaluating Vision-Language Models on Concreteness and Imagery
summary: Visual inputs are often assumed to improve language understanding in multimodal models. We examine this assumption by asking whether vision-language models (VLMs) can distinguish useful visual evidence from incidental image context in lexical judgments. We use human concreteness and imagery ratings because they span words with varying expected visual relevance, from abstract and low-imagery words to concrete and high-imagery words. We find that real-image contexts do not yield consistent gains and often hurt alignment with human ratings, most sharply when visual evidence is least relevant. Through probing and canonical correlation analysis, complemented by an attribution case study, we find that real-image contexts are associated with representational shifts and greater sensitivity to spurious visual cues, coinciding with weaker recoverability of the targeted lexical properties. We further show that instructing models to focus solely on textual content at inference time can reduce this degradation, with the clearest gains on these vulnerable subsets. Our findings suggest that current instruction-tuned VLMs need better calibration of when visual context should inform lexical judgments.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether visual information actually helps multimodal models make lexical judgments and finds that real images often hurt alignment with human concreteness/imagery ratings, especially when visual evidence is not relevant.
AI.motivation: Multimodal vision-language models are assumed to better understand language by incorporating visual context, but this assumption is under-tested at a fine-grained lexical level. The authors want to know if these models can appropriately use or ignore visual information depending on whether it is actually helpful, using human concreteness and imagery ratings as a benchmark of when visual content should matter.
AI.method: The authors evaluate vision-language models on predicting human concreteness and imagery ratings for words under different input conditions: with and without real-image visual context. They analyze how visual context changes internal representations using probing classifiers and canonical correlation analysis, and they conduct an attribution case study to see which parts of the input the model relies on. They also experiment with inference-time instructions that explicitly tell the model to base its decisions only on textual information, measuring how this affects performance, especially on subsets where visual evidence is expected to be less relevant.
AI.result: Adding real-image visual context does not reliably improve and often worsens correlation with human concreteness and imagery ratings, particularly for abstract or low-imagery words where visual cues should matter least. Representation-level analyses show that visual context induces shifts that increase the model’s sensitivity to spurious image features and make the target lexical properties less recoverable. Instruction-based interventions at inference time that limit attention to text partially mitigate these negative effects, with the largest improvements on the most vulnerable word subsets.
AI.conclusion: Current instruction-tuned vision-language models are not well calibrated about when visual context should influence lexical judgments, often over-weighting irrelevant visual cues and degrading performance on abstract or low-imagery words. Simple inference-time instructions to downweight visual information can help, but more fundamental advances are needed in how these models integrate and selectively use visual context.

title: Semantic Gradients Interactions in SSD: A Case Study in Racial Identity and Hate Speech
summary: We introduce interaction SSD, an extension of Supervised Semantic Differential that models how semantic meaning varies across moderators such as groups, traits, or conditions making this variation testable and interpretable. The method estimates a main semantic gradient, an interaction gradient, and conditional gradients, all interpretable through standard SSD tools. We illustrate it on the UC Berkeley Measuring Hate Speech corpus, testing whether annotator racial identity moderates hate-speech judgments of comments targeting people of color. The interaction model detects a significant moderation effect: the shared gradient contrasts dehumanizing hostility with counter-speech, while the interaction gradient reveals smaller group-linked differences in which semantic cues predict hate-speech ratings. Interaction SSD makes moderated meaning-outcome relationships statistically testable and interpretable.

AI.tldr: The paper extends Supervised Semantic Differential (SSD) to model how semantic meaning varies by moderators (e.g., groups or conditions), making these moderated meaning–outcome relationships statistically testable and interpretable.
AI.motivation: Existing SSD methods capture a single semantic gradient linking language to outcomes but cannot easily test or interpret how this relationship differs across groups (e.g., different annotator identities) or conditions. In many social and linguistic applications, we specifically care whether different groups rely on different semantic cues when making judgments, and we need statistically testable, interpretable tools to study such moderation effects in meaning–outcome relationships.
AI.method: The authors propose Interaction SSD, an extension of Supervised Semantic Differential that incorporates interaction terms between texts and moderator variables (such as group, trait, or condition). The model jointly estimates: (1) a main semantic gradient shared across groups, (2) an interaction gradient capturing how the meaning–outcome mapping changes with the moderator, and (3) group-conditional gradients. These gradients remain interpretable using standard SSD tools, enabling analysis of how specific semantic cues relate differently to outcomes across moderator levels. They demonstrate the method using the UC Berkeley Measuring Hate Speech corpus with annotator racial identity as the moderator of hate-speech judgments.
AI.result: Applied to hate-speech annotation data, Interaction SSD detects a statistically significant moderation effect of annotator racial identity on hate-speech judgments of comments targeting people of color. The main (shared) gradient identifies a spectrum from dehumanizing hostility to counter-speech. The interaction gradient reveals more subtle, group-linked differences in which semantic cues are most predictive of hate-speech ratings, indicating that annotators from different racial groups weigh certain semantic signals differently.
AI.conclusion: Interaction SSD successfully generalizes SSD to moderated settings, offering a framework where differences in meaning–outcome relationships across groups or conditions are both statistically testable and semantically interpretable. This makes it possible to rigorously study and visualize how different groups rely on distinct semantic cues when making judgments, with demonstrated utility in analyzing hate-speech annotation behavior.

title: FinHarness: An Inline Lifecycle Safety Harness for Finance LLM Agents
summary: Finance LLM agents must simultaneously block prompt-induced unauthorized actions and approve legitimate multi-step business workflows. However, boundary filters often miss irreversible mid-trajectory tool calls, while post-hoc LLM judges perform auditing only after termination -- too late for intervention and at a computational cost that scales linearly with trace length. We present FinHarness, an inline safety harness that wraps a finance agent end-to-end with three components: a Query Monitor that fuses single-turn intent with cross-turn drift, a Tool Monitor that evaluates each prospective tool call, and a Cascade module that integrates per-step risk and adaptively routes verification between a lightweight and an advanced-tier LLM judge. Fired risk factors are re-injected into the agent input as ex-ante evidence, enabling the agent to refuse, re-plan, or approve on its own. On FinVault, routed FinHarness cuts ASR from 38.3% to 15.0% while largely preserving benign approval ($41.1\% \to 39.3\%$), and uses $4.7\times$ fewer advanced-judge calls than an always-advanced ablation.

AI.tldr: FinHarness is an inline safety harness for financial LLM agents that monitors queries and tool calls in real time, routing risky steps to stronger judges to reduce unauthorized actions while preserving useful workflows and lowering computation cost.
AI.motivation: Financial LLM agents operate tools that can trigger irreversible transactions and sensitive operations. Existing safety approaches either use boundary filters that miss unsafe mid-trajectory tool calls or post-hoc LLM judges that only evaluate after the whole interaction, which is too late to prevent damage and computationally expensive as traces grow. The motivation is to design a safety mechanism that acts during the agent’s reasoning and tool use, stops malicious or unsafe steps before they execute, yet still allows legitimate multi-step finance workflows to proceed efficiently.
AI.method: The paper proposes FinHarness, an inline safety harness that wraps a finance agent with three components. (1) A Query Monitor that combines current user intent with cross-turn drift analysis to detect changes in goals or attempts to steer the conversation to unsafe behavior. (2) A Tool Monitor that inspects each prospective tool call before execution, assessing its risk. (3) A Cascade module that aggregates per-step risk signals and adaptively routes verification: low-risk steps go to a lightweight judge model, while high-risk ones go to an advanced LLM judge. When risk factors trigger, they are fed back into the agent’s context as evidence so the base agent can decide to refuse, re-plan, or proceed more safely.
AI.result: On the FinVault benchmark, the routed FinHarness configuration reduces the attack success rate (ASR) from 38.3% to 15.0%, while keeping benign approval rates almost unchanged (from 41.1% to 39.3%). Additionally, it reduces reliance on the expensive advanced judge, using 4.7 times fewer advanced-judge calls than a baseline that always uses the advanced tier for verification.
AI.conclusion: Inline, step-wise safety monitoring with adaptive judge routing can substantially improve the safety of finance LLM agents by intercepting unsafe tool calls during execution, rather than only at boundaries or after completion. FinHarness demonstrates that combining query and tool monitoring with a cascade of lightweight and advanced judges reduces unauthorized actions, maintains most legitimate approvals, and significantly cuts computational cost, suggesting a practical path for safer, scalable financial AI agents.

title: MATCHA: Matching Text via Contrastive Semantic Alignment
summary: Reliable evaluation is essential for understanding large language model (LLM) performance, yet today's go-to metrics, namely token-overlap scores (e.g., ROUGE) and embedding-based measures (e.g., BERTScore), often misjudge semantic similarity of documents. Our study shows that both token-overlap metrics and embedding-based metrics routinely assign nearly identical scores to texts that directly contradict each other, thereby potentially masking fundamental errors. We introduce MATCHA, an automatic metric that jointly rewards semantic agreement with a reference and penalizes contradictions. MATCHA employs a dual-view perspective that measures (i) proximity to the gold text and (ii) distance from an adversarially generated counterfactual contradiction. In eight public benchmarks, MATCHA outperforms popular metrics, compared with human annotations on question-answering, image caption generation, natural language inference, summarization, and semantic textual similarity tasks. On the TruthfulQA dataset (i.e., a dataset without a training set, where no embedding-based metrics could locally train on), this improvement in terms of matching texts with a reference reaches 18.38% over ROUGE-L and 20.82% over BERTScore. Both quantitative comparison and qualitative human assessments confirm the efficacy and validity of MATCHA and uncover fundamental weaknesses in pre-existing metrics. Compared with 23 embedding models, including top state-of-the-art ones, used as a metric similar to BERTScore, MATCHA remains the most accurate in distinguishing correct from incorrect statements solely based on a reference. Our code and metric are publicly available (https://github.com/Siran-Li/MATCHA).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MATCHA, a new automatic metric for evaluating large language models that rewards agreement with a reference text and penalizes contradictions using both a reference and an adversarial counterfactual.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation metrics for LLM outputs—token-overlap scores like ROUGE and embedding-based scores like BERTScore—can assign almost identical scores to outputs that actually contradict each other, failing to capture factual correctness and semantic agreement. This undermines reliable assessment of LLM performance, especially on truth-sensitive tasks.
AI.method: The authors design MATCHA, a dual-view evaluation metric that (1) measures semantic proximity between a candidate text and a gold reference and (2) simultaneously measures semantic distance from an adversarially generated counterfactual that contradicts the reference. They generate such counterfactual contradictions and integrate both agreement and contradiction signals into a single automatic score, which is then validated across multiple benchmarks and tasks.
AI.result: Across eight public benchmarks covering QA, image captioning, NLI, summarization, and semantic textual similarity, MATCHA shows higher correlation with human judgments than standard metrics like ROUGE and BERTScore. On the TruthfulQA dataset, MATCHA improves alignment with human assessments by up to 18.38% over ROUGE-L and 20.82% over BERTScore. Against 23 embedding models used as metrics, including state-of-the-art ones, MATCHA remains the most accurate at distinguishing correct from incorrect statements when only a reference is available.
AI.conclusion: Contradiction blindness is a fundamental weakness of common automatic metrics for text evaluation. By explicitly modeling both agreement with a reference and distance from an adversarial contradiction, MATCHA provides a more reliable, human-aligned evaluation of LLM-generated text across diverse tasks, and it outperforms a wide range of existing token-based and embedding-based metrics.
